cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Blue Cliff Record
cb:div.head: Preface to the Blue Cliff Record
cb:div.p:
The life-vein of ultimate sanctity; the great pivotal device of the successive patriarchs; a numinous prescription that changes one’s bones; a marvelous art for nourishing the spirit. It
was the master Xuedou who possessed the true eye that surpasses schools and transcends standards, who lifted up the correct command and did
not reveal the customary style. Wielding the tongs and hammer that boil Buddhas and forge patriarchs, he cast verses that bring forth a patch-robed monk’s upward
nose. A silver mountain, an iron wall—who would dare bore into it? A mosquito biting an iron ox—hard to even get one’s teeth
into it. If one does not meet a great master-craftsman, how could one fathom its hidden subtlety? There was also the old man Foguo, who, while residing at Blue
Cliff, was asked for guidance by students who were confused. The old man, moved with compassion, plucked out and brought forth the deep
sources, dissected the underlying principles, and pointed straight ahead in the full light of day—how could this be mere quick cleverness? The hundred public
cases, threaded together from the beginning like a single string; a whole squad of old fellows, examined one by one and all passed through.
You must know that the Zhao jade originally has no flaw; Xiangru only hoodwinked the King of Qin. The ultimate Way truly
lies beyond words. The lineage master let down compassion to rescue what was going astray; if one sees it like this, only then does one know the thoroughgoing
old granny. But if one clings to phrases and sinks into words, one cannot avoid extinguishing the Buddha-seed. Fortunately,Universal Illumination
by personally drawing close to the master’s seat, one gets to hear what one has never heard. Dharma friends gathered it into a brief compilation; with my crude incompetence I narrate its
beginning and end. This was in the Jianyan era, wushen year, on the last day of late spring. A bhikshu in training, heir in the patriarchal line,
respectfully writes this preface.Universal Illumination

Since the Sutra in Forty-Two Sections entered China, people first knew there was a Buddha. From Bodhidharma to
the Sixth Patriarch transmitting the robe, only then did there begin to be phrases and sayings. “Originally there is not a single thing” became the Southern School.
“Constantly, diligently wipe it clean” became the Northern School. Thus there came to be Chan verses on old cases circulating
in the world. Their followers also had the method of overturning cases: scolding Buddha, cursing the patriarchs—nothing they would not do. Among them,
there were some who deeply grasped the living method of our poets. Yet as for what is called the First Principle—what use has it
for phrases? Xuedou and Yuanwu, with the urgent heart of an old granny; Dahui already burned it with a single torch.
Zhang Wei Mingyuan of Yuzhong rekindled dead ashes and printed it anew—this too is what one might call the urgent heart of an
old granny, is it not? In the fourth year of Dade, gengzi, on the eighth day of the fourth month, guichou,
Fang Hui Wanli of Ziyang Mountain wrote this preface.

The Blue Cliff Collection was composed by the great master Yuanwu. His chief disciple, the master Dahui,
then burned and discarded the book. The various dharmas of the world all dread attachment.
For a son of Śākya, nothing is more worthy of reverence than the Buddha; yet there are times when he is even cursed. This is because there is “me”
and no “other”—it proceeds from me, not from the other. If one abandons oneself and chases after things, one will inevitably come to
lose oneself. The mind is one with the Way; the Way is one with all things. It fills the great void—where
could one go that is not the Way? But ordinary people, looking at it, can see what they see, and yet do
not see what they do not see. They seek it from others, and others speak of it—like Dongpo’s
explanation by metaphor: conjecturing back and forth, the farther one goes, the more one misses. Even our Master, embodying the Way,
still wished to be without words—how much more for the Buddha’s teaching, as a supramundane dharma: how could it be sought through letters,
through language? Even so, there are also things that cannot be dispensed with. The wise are few
and the foolish are many; those who have learned are few and those who have not learned are many. The Great Canon’s more than five thousand
volumes were all set up for future ages. If one could truly forget words, old Śākyamuni
should have kept his mouth shut—why all this endless chatter? The principles under heaven indeed include
what does not depart from the ordinary, yet transcends the surface of the ordinary. Though it may seem easy
to know, in truth it is not easy to know. If one does not seek it from others, then for one’s whole life one cannot
obtain it. In ancient times, people renowned in the world were either the best among a thousand, or outstanding among ten thousand.
The Tai’a sword is the sharp sword under heaven: climb a mountain and it slays tigers
and leopards; enter the water and it cuts down flood-dragons. People’s knowledge of it ends there. Yet the ancients
had those who were skilled in using it: fighting from the city walls, they swung it with the wind at their back, and the three armies
suffered great defeat, blood flowing red for a thousand li. How could one, with only one’s own limited
capacity, exhaust all its possibilities with doubt? Since I heard there was such a book, I sought it with utmost urgency.
The Zhang family of Yuzhong began anew to carve the woodblocks and came to consult me; I then assisted and brought it to completion.
I also inscribed its opening. In the ninth year of Dade, yisi year, on an auspicious day in the third month, Yu
Cen Xiuxiu, layman; Zhou Chi of Liaocheng, wrote this at a lodging by Guan Bridge in Qiantang.

Someone asked: between the making and the destroying of the Blue Cliff Collection, which is right? I said: both are right. The tooth-worn old man
came from the West and transmitted the seal of mind directly, without establishing letters—this is surely so. Yet as for discussions of bloodline and returning to emptiness,
who, in fact, made them? The ancients said, “Not in words, not apart from words”—
these are truly knowing words. If it already enables everyone, at the moment of raising a curtain, hearing a board, holding up a finger, or stubbing a toe,
to settle the great matter—what place have words? Since the “flower held up and a faint smile,”
after the gate-pole fell, the moment one touches phrases, without words there is no way to transmit. This
too is something that cannot be abandoned. It has been said that the books of the ancestral teaching, those called public cases,
began in the Tang and flourished in the Song; their origins go back far. The two characters are originally a worldly term
from clerical documents in secular law. Their uses are three. First: when wall-gazing practice is fulfilled and roaming on foot is finished, the star of nirvana is hard to discern,
and one easily falls into the ways of the wild fox; those with eyes examine and distinguish it.
With one scolding and one shout, it must reveal actual attainment—like an old clerk judging guilt from the case records, seeing the inside
all at once, leaving out not a single confession. Second: when one has just come from Lingnan and has not yet swallowed the waters of the West River,
the byways where sheep are lost easily bring tears; the needle that points to the sea must face south. With compassionate heart,
one guides and receives them; one blow, one mark—making them realize and awaken—like the chief judge enforcing the law,
overturning a wrongful verdict and pulling people out of death. Third: when one’s transgressions in the fields are deep and the matter of tethering the donkey is weighty,
one’s resolve in learning the game of go must be single, and the color that dyes silk easily grieves. A great good spiritual friend
entrusts and charges them, making their mind die on the meditation cushion; with each movement, with each inquiry—
like an office issuing posted regulations, making people read the statutes and know the law. As soon as an evil thought arises, it is immediately
put to sleep and extinguished—this is the third. It provides registers, makes case files, sets out the mechanisms and situations, and serves as regulations,
so how is it different, at first, from the world’s so-called golden rules, jade statutes, and other clear, reverent manuals?
Why did the patriarchs establish them as public cases and leave them to the monasteries? Their intent
may have taken up something like this. But since the latter days of the Dharma, people seek the wondrous mind in scraps of wound-paper, and hand over the true
Dharma to mouth-talk; even if they exhaust the ghosts and spirits, they still cannot leave the ledger. Clinging to others’ doorways, they let themselves
be called “sir.” The sword is gone but the boat is still notched; the rabbit has fled but the stump will not move.
Their belly is full of tangled vines; they can ask a thousand turns. Yet with the great matter of life and death, it has no connection
at all. When the bell rings and the water-clock runs out, what use will it be? Alas—an antelope hangs its horns; it cannot
be sought by forms and traces. And those who study well, though of lesser faculties—how could they merely follow step for step, hurry
whenever the other hurries? Knowing this, the hearts of both old men are right. Yuanwu’s heart, looking after children and
grandchildren, was strong; therefore he again lifted up Xuedou’s verses. Dahui’s heart, saving from burning and rescuing from drowning, was strong;
therefore he established the destruction of the Blue Cliff Collection. The Śākya teaching speaks a whole Great Canon, yet in the end it says,
“I have not spoken a single word.” How could it be deceiving me? Yuanwu’s heart is the heart of the Śākya teaching
when it speaks sutras; Dahui’s heart is the heart of the Śākya teaching that forbids speaking. Yu,
Ji, and Yanzi would all be the same if placed in the other’s position. Pushing it or pulling it, the point is simply to get the cart to move.
After more than two hundred years, Zhang Mingyuan of Yuzhong again carved the blocks, to prolong its transmission.
Is this the ancestral teaching returning to spring, or do worldly circumstances have their fixed number? Yet the circulation of this book
bears very great consequence. If one sees water and takes it for the sea, or recognizes the pointing finger as the moon, it is not only Dahui
who would worry; Yuanwu too would then remove the sticking and loosen the bonds. Someone of old
wrote a poem on a portrait, saying: plainly, on the paper, Master Zhang; with all one’s strength one calls out in a loud voice,
yet he does not answer. If you wish to look into this book, first take up this saying. In the Dade era, jiachen, on the full moon of the fourth month.
Written by the Old Man of the Three Teachings.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Scroll One, by the master Foguo Yuanwu

cb:div.p:
The master resided at Lingquan Chan Monastery on Mount Jiashan in Lizhou
Commentary and chanting on the essential sayings of Master Xuedou Xian’s verses on old cases

The master’s instruction said: seeing smoke beyond a mountain, you already know it is fire. Seeing horns over a wall, you immediately know
it is an ox. Raise one and illuminate three; weigh opportunity down to a scruple and a liang—this is the everyday tea and
rice of the patch-robed monk. But when it comes to cutting off all streams, surging in the east and sinking in the west, going against or along, roaming freely, giving and taking
at will—right at such a time, tell me: whose place of conduct and practice is this? Look
closely at Xuedou’s tangled vines.

[1] Raised: Emperor Wu of Liang asked the great master BodhidharmaTalking about this muttering fellow,, “What is the holy truth,
the first principle?” Bodhidharma said, “Vast and empty, with no holiness.” The emperor said, “Who is it facing me?” Bodhidharma said, “I do notwhat kind of donkey-tethering stake is that?You would think it was all that special. An arrow flies past Silla. How deadly!
Plain as day.His face full of shame and panic. Forcing himself to be awake, sure enough, he cannot grope his way to it.
know.” The emperor did not accord with him; Bodhidharma then crossed the river and reachedBah! Come again and it is not worth half a coin.What a pity. Yet it is a bit closer.
Later, the Emperor of Wei brought it up and asked Zhigong. Zhigong said, “Does Your Majesty recognize this man?” The emperor said, “I do not.” Zhigong said,This wild-fox demon cannot avoid a bout of muddle, going from west to east, from east to west.A poor child
remembering old debts; bystanders have eyes.Along with the Venerable Zhigong, drive him out of the country, that will do.
Give him thirty blows. Bodhidharma is here!It turns out Emperor Wu could take up the Bodhidharma case.
“This is the Great Being Avalokiteśvara. He transmitted the Buddha’s mind-seal.” The emperor regretted it.Wildly pointing and annotating; the arm does not bend outward.
So he sent an envoy to invite him back. Zhigong said, “Do not say that Your MajestySure enough, he cannot hold it. He says he is muttering.
has dispatched envoys to fetch him, even if the whole nation went—Someone in the eastern house dies, someone in the western house helps wail. Fine too, drive him out of the country all at once.
he still would not return.”Venerable Zhigong also deserves thirty blows. He does not know that beneath his heels great light is released.

Bodhidharma saw from afar that this land had people with Mahāyāna capacity, so he sailed across the sea and, in time,
arrived. He transmitted only the mind-seal, opened and revealed the deluded path, established no words and letters, and pointed directly to the human
mind: see your nature and become Buddha. If you see it like this, you have a share of freedom and do not follow
any language’s turns. Fully embodied and already complete, then later you can face Emperor Wu
in dialogue, and also see where the Second Patriarch settled the mind. Naturally you will see it—without calculating emotional dust.
Cut it off with a single stroke—clean and unbound. Why divide into right and wrong again, and argue over what is
gained or lost? Even so, how many can do it? Emperor Wu once donned the robe himself and personally
lectured on the Radiant Prajñā scripture, moving heaven to rain down flowers and making the ground turn to gold. He distinguished
the Way and served the Buddha, issued proclamations to the realm, built temples, ordained monks, and practiced according to the teachings. People called
him the “Son of Heaven with the Buddha-mind.” When Bodhidharma first met Emperor Wu, the emperor asked, “I have built temples and ordained
monks—what merit is there?” Bodhidharma said, “No merit.” Right away it was like foul water poured over his head.
If you penetrate this “no merit” word, I’ll allow you to personally meet Bodhidharma. But tell me—
building temples and ordaining monks—why is there no merit at all? Where does this meaning lie?
The emperor, along with Dharma Master Louyue, Great Master Fu, and Crown Prince Zhaoming, upheld discussions of the true
and the conventional two truths. According to the teachings, the ultimate truth clarifies “not being,” and the conventional truth clarifies
“not non-being.” Ultimate and conventional are not two—this is the first meaning of the noble truth. This is the teaching school’s
most subtle and most profound point. The emperor picked up this utmost principle and asked Bodhidharma, “What
is the first meaning of the noble truth?” Bodhidharma said, “Vast and open—no holiness.” All patch-robed monks under heaven
cannot jump out of it. Bodhidharma cut him off with one stroke. Nowadays how many people misunderstand it,
then go on playing with “spirit and soul,” bulging their eyes and saying, “Vast and open—no holiness.” They’re pleased there’s no
involvement. The former master, the Fifth Patriarch, once said: if someone penetrates just this “vast and open—no holiness,”
they return home and sit steady. If they are the kind who just tangle up vines, there’s no harm in smashing their
lacquer bucket. Bodhidharma was extraordinary in this, so it is said: penetrate one phrase, and a thousand
phrases, ten thousand phrases are penetrated all at once. Naturally you can sit through it and hold it firm. The ancients said,
“Grinding bones to powder and shattering the body still would not repay it; one clear phrase surpasses a hundred billion.” Bodhidharma split
his head open and gave him a squeeze—yet how much still leaked out. The emperor did not awaken, and instead, with
views of self and other, asked again, “Who is it that stands before me?” Bodhidharma was compassionate to an extreme, and again
said, “I do not know.” It left Emperor Wu’s eyes and mind fixed, moving yet not knowing where to land. What
words could speak here? At this point, whether there is something or nothing, if you pick it up it cannot be borne. Master Duan had
a verse: “One arrow, as usual, brings down a falcon; add another arrow and you’ve already
been too generous. He returns directly to sit before Mount Shaoshi; let the ruler of Liang not speak of inviting him again.” It goes on:
“Who would want to invite him?” The emperor did not accord with it, so Bodhidharma quietly left the country. The old fellow could only feel dispirited.
He crossed the river to Wei. At that time Emperor Xiaoming of Wei was on the throne, of this northern people’s
Tuoba clan; only later was it called “China.” When Bodhidharma arrived there, he also did not
go out to meet anyone. He went straight past to Shaolin, faced the wall for nine years, and received the Second Patriarch. There they called
him the “Brahman of wall-gazing.” Later, Emperor Wu of Liang asked Zhigong. Zhigong said, “Does Your Majesty
recognize this man?” The emperor said, “I do not.” Tell me—what was said to Bodhidharma: was it
the same or different? It seems alike, yet it seems not; it is, yet it is not. Many people misunderstand and say: earlier
Bodhidharma was answering him with Chan; later Emperor Wu was replying to Zhigong. Thus
it is “recognizing” recognition—so it has no involvement. Back then, when Zhigong asked like this, tell me
how should one respond? Why not kill him with a single blow, to avoid seeing all this muddling?
Emperor Wu instead honestly admitted, “I do not.” Zhigong, seeing the moment, acted and said,
“This is the Great Being Avalokiteśvara, transmitting the Buddha’s mind-seal.” The emperor regretted it and then sent envoys to fetch him.
How fitting—and how not. If at the time, once he said, “This is the Great Being Avalokiteśvara transmitting the Buddha’s
mind-seal,” it would also have been fine to banish him from the country—still a bit better. People say Zhigong, in the
thirteenth year of Tianjian, passed away. Bodhidharma only came in the first year of Putong. They are separated by
seven years—so why say they met at the same time? This must be a mistaken tradition. According to
what is recorded in the tradition, we will not debate this now. We only need to know the main outline. Tell me:
Bodhidharma is Avalokiteśvara; Zhigong is Avalokiteśvara—then which one is the true
Avalokiteśvara? If it is Avalokiteśvara, why are there two? Not only two—
they form groups and squads. Later, in Northern Wei, Vinaya Master Guangtong and Tripiṭaka Bodhiruci
debated with the master. The master rebuked them for clinging to characteristics and pointing at mind, and for their narrow, cramped measure. They themselves
could not bear it, and competed in raising harmful intent, repeatedly administering poison. By the sixth time, his karmic ties of teaching
were complete and he had found a person to transmit the Dharma to, so he no longer tried to save himself. He sat upright and passed away. He was buried at
Dinglin Monastery on Bear Ear Mountain. Later, Song Yun of Northern Wei, on an embassy, met the master in the Congling Mountains, carrying
a single sandal in his hand as he went. Emperor Wu, recalling him, composed an inscription himself, saying: “Alas! To see
and not see; to meet and not meet; to encounter and not encounter—now and ancient alike. I resent
it, I hate it.” And he further praised: “When mind exists, through vast kalpas one remains stuck as an ordinary man. When mind
does not exist, in an instant one ascends wondrous awakening.” Tell me—where is Bodhidharma right now?
You’ve already missed him and don’t even know it.

The noble truth is vast and open—how could one ever discern it? Who is it that stands before me?An arrow flies past Silla. Hm?It has passed. What is hard to discern?
Who again says, “I do not know”? Because of this,Come again and it is not worth half a coin. Off he goes like that again.Among three or four. Bah!
he secretly crossed the river—how could thorns and brambles not grow?Unable to pierce others' nostrils, yet pierced by others. Blue heaven, blue heaven! What a sorry excuse for a man.
The whole nation pursued him, but he did not return; through a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, they vainly remember—do not remember, do not remember.Beneath the heels it is already several zhang deep.A twofold koan. What use is it to chase after it? Where is it?
Where is a great man's resolve?Switching hands, he pounds his chest and makes an announcement to empty space.
A clear wind circles the earth—what limit could it have?What is he saying? Doing business in a ghost cave.Sure enough. Big and small, Xuedou rolls into the grass.
The master looked around to left and right and said, “Is there still a Patriarch here?” Then he said himself, “There is—call him here to wash this old monk’s feet.”You wait for that fellow, still doing
this coming and going,muddled and confused.Give him thirty more blows and drive him out; even that is not
excessive. Doing this coming and going, he is still a bit closer.

Now, judging by Xuedou’s verse on this case, it is like someone skilled at dancing with the Tai’a sword.
He circles and coils within empty space, naturally not offending blade or edge. If you lack this kind
of skill, the moment you pick it up you are hurt by the edge and your hand is cut. If you have eyes to see, watch
him lift and set down, praise and blame. Using only four lines, he wipes it clean and pins down a single
gong’an. In general, verses on old cases are just Chan spoken by a roundabout route—grasping the main outline of the ancient and
settling the case, that’s all. Xuedou gave him a squeeze; right at the start he says: “The
noble truth is vast and open—how could one ever discern it?” Under his first line, Xuedou plants this
line—no harm calling it extraordinary. But tell me: in the end, how do you discern it? Even if you have iron
eyes and copper pupils, you still cannot grope it out. Here, can emotional consciousness be used to divine and
measure it? Therefore Yunmen said: like striking sparks from stone, like a flash of lightning. This bit
does not fall into mental contrivance, thought, or imagining. If you wait until you open your mouth, what can it accomplish?
When calculating thoughts arise, the hawk has already flown past Silla. Xuedou says: you patch-robed monks under heaven—
how could you ever discern it? Who stands before me? Add this “still says ‘I do not know’ ”—this is Xuedou
being far too grandmotherly, helping people layer upon layer. Tell me: “vast and open” and “I do not know”—
are they the same or different? For one who is done, it is understood without speaking. For
one who is not done, they will surely split it into two pieces. Everywhere, people ordinarily say:
Xuedou re-lifted it once more. They do not realize: the four-line verse already exhausts the whole case. After that,
out of compassion, he versified the events: “Because of this he secretly crossed the river—how could thorns
and brambles not grow?” Bodhidharma originally came to this land to loosen stickiness and remove bindings for people, to pull nails and
extract wedges, to clear away brambles. Why then say “brambles grow”? Not only back then—among you
people, right under your heels even now, it is already several fathoms deep. “The whole nation pursued him, but he did not return.”
“Through a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, they vainly remember.” How mortifying—no man of resolve! Tell me: where is Bodhidharma
right now? If you see Bodhidharma, then you see where Xuedou finally helps people. Xuedou
feared people would chase emotional views, so he turned the pivotal hinge and brought out his own
understanding, saying: “Do not remember; a clear wind circles the earth—what limit could it have?” Since you should not remember—
what about the matter right under your heels—how is it? Xuedou says: right now, here, the earth is
encircled by a clear wind—throughout heaven above and earth below, what limit could there be? Xuedou picks up the affairs of a thousand ages, ten thousand
ages and tosses them before your face. It was not only that in Xuedou’s time there was no limit—among you
people too, what limit is there? And then he feared people would get stuck right here, so again
Employing expedient means, he shouted, “Is there still a patriarch here?” He answered himself, “There is.” Xuedou
has arrived here, and it’s not amiss to help people. His bare sincerity comes in pieces. And he says again, “Call him here
to wash this old monk’s feet.” That cuts down a man’s dignity too much. Yet back then it was fine—treating him
with proper hands and feet. Tell me, where is Xuedou’s intent? Right here—call him
a donkey and it is; call him a horse and it is; call him a patriarch and it is. How
does the name miss the mark? Time and again they call him “Xuedou” and send the patriarch off. Fortunately there’s no entangling
involvement. Tell me—after all, how is it? Only the old barbarian is allowed to know; the old barbarian is not allowed
to understand.

The pointer says: Heaven and earth are cramped; sun, moon, and stars go dark all at once. Even if the blows were like
raindrops and the shouts like rushing thunder, it still wouldn’t match what belongs to the supreme vehicle. Even
the Buddhas of the three times can only know it themselves; the patriarchs of successive generations cannot bring it up at all.
The whole Tripitaka and its commentaries do not reach it. Even a clear-eyed patch-robed monk cannot save himself. When you reach
here, how do you ask for instruction? Say the word “Buddha” and you drag mud and carry water. Say the word
“Zen” and your face fills with shame. Those seasoned adepts need not be mentioned. Beginners and later students
must investigate straight through.

[2] Case: Zhaozhou addressed the assembly, saying: “The supreme Way is not difficult;What is this old fellow doing? Do not strike up this tangle of vines.Neither hard nor easy.
it only dislikes choosing and rejecting. As soon as there are words, that is choosing and rejecting; that isWhat is right before your eyes? The Third Patriarch is still here.
clarity. This old monk is not within clarity—do you still cherish it or not?” At that timeTwo ends, three faces; show off less. When fish move, the water turns muddy; when birds fly, feathers fall.The thief's body
is already exposed. Where is this old fellow going?Ruined. Still, there is one or half of one.
a monk asked, “Since you are not within clarity, what is there to cherish?” Zhaozhou said, “I don’t know either.” The monk said, “Master,Fine, give him one
squeeze; the tongue presses against the palate.Squeeze this old fellow to death; retreat three thousand.
since you don’t know, why then did you say you are not within clarity?” Zhaozhou said, “Ask and you’ll get it.” The monk bowed and withdrew.Watch where he runs off to. Chase him and make him
climb a tree.Thanks to this one move. This old bandit

Master Zhaozhou would commonly raise this topic, simply: “It only dislikes choosing and rejecting.” This
comes from the Third Patriarch’s Inscription on Faith in Mind: “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and rejecting. Only do not
hate or love, and it will be clear of itself. As soon as there is right and wrong, that is choosing and rejecting; that is clarity.”
If you understand it like that, you’ve already missed it. Cutting and nailing, gluing and sticking—what use is that? Zhaozhou
said: “That is choosing and rejecting; that is clarity.” Nowadays, those who investigate Zen and inquire into the Way—if they are not in choosing and rejecting,
they sit in clarity. “This old monk is not within clarity—do you all still
cherish it or not?” Since you people are not within clarity, tell me—where is
Zhaozhou? Why then does he tell people to cherish? The former master, the Fifth Patriarch,
once said: “With hands hanging down, he comes as if passing by you.” How do you understand that? Tell me—what
is the place of hands hanging down? Recognize the intent at the hook’s tip—do not take the fixed North Star for it. This
monk stepped forward—he was indeed something special. He grabbed Zhaozhou at the empty spot and pressed him hard.
“Since you are not within clarity, what is there to cherish?” Zhaozhou still did not use the staff
or the shout—he only said, “I don’t know either.” If it weren’t for this old fellow, being pressed like that
would often make one forget what came before and lose what comes after. Luckily this old fellow has a free turning place,
so he answered him like this. Nowadays, Zen followers—when questioned they also say, “I
don’t know, I don’t understand.” But what can be done? Same road, different tracks. This monk had a special
point—only then could he ask: “Master, since you don’t know, why then did you say you are not
within clarity?” An even better pressing. If it were someone else, they’d often be unable to explain it away.
Zhaozhou was a genuine hand—he simply told him, “Ask and you’ll get it.” The monk bowed and withdrew.
This monk still could do nothing to this old fellow; he could only swallow his anger and hold his tongue. This
is a great master of the school—he won’t debate mystery or subtlety with you, won’t debate “occasion” or “state.” He only
meets people with the matter at hand. Hence the saying: if you curse at him, he’ll let you have the last word; if you spit,
he’ll let you splash water. People don’t realize it: this old fellow, all his life, did not meet people with staff and shout—
only with ordinary speech. And yet no one under heaven can do anything with him. Because
all his life he had none of those calculations. Therefore he can pick up and use things sideways and upside down, go against the current
or with it, and attain great freedom. People today don’t grasp it; they only keep saying, “Zhaozhou
doesn’t answer; he doesn’t explain for people,” not realizing they miss it right in front of their faces.

The supreme Way is not difficult—at the edge of words, at the edge of phrases. One has many kinds; two has no twofold. At the horizon the sun rises, the moon sets; before the railing the mountains are deep, the water cold. When the skull’s knowinghas a triple koan. His mouth full of frost, what is he saying?When fish move, the water turns muddy. Split into seven pieces and eight fragments.
Smearing it up.Better to separate it. It is just the same; what end is there?How can it bear it?
Four, five, six, seven. Why strike up entangling vines?Present it face to face. Boundless above the head, boundless beneath the feet. Absolutely
avoid raising the head or lowering the head.Once dead, never alive again. Do you still feel your hairs bristle?
is exhausted, what joy could stand? A dragon’s cry in a withered tree—the thaw has not yet dried.Staring with eyes wide open inside the coffin. The layman Lu is his fellow participant.
Hard, hard—Bah! A withered tree blooms again. Bodhidharma roams the eastern land.Deviant Dharma is hard to support. Turn it upside down and speak: what place is this? Say hard, say easy.
choosing and rejecting, clarity—sir, you see for yourself.Blind! You would think it was because of others; luckily you can see for yourself. It has nothing to do with this mountain monk.

Xuedou knew where it landed, so he composed the verse like this. “The supreme Way is not difficult,” and then he followed it with
“at the edge of words, at the edge of phrases.” Raise one corner and you don’t use the other three to turn it back. Xuedou said,
“One has many kinds; two has no twofold.” It’s like the three corners turning back the one. Tell me—what
place is the edge of words and the edge of phrases? Why does “one” have many kinds, and “two”
have no twofold? If you don’t have eyes, where will you grope? If you penetrate
these two lines, then as the ancients said, you beat it into one piece—yet you still see: mountains
are mountains, water is water; long is long, short is short; heaven is heaven, earth is
earth. Sometimes you call heaven “earth”; sometimes you call earth “heaven.” Sometimes you call mountains
“not mountains,” call water “not water.” In the end, how do you go on steadily? Wind
comes and trees move; waves rise and boats lift. Spring is born, summer grows; autumn harvests, winter stores. One kind
of level heart—vanishing and ending of itself—and these four lines of the verse are cut off at once. Xuedou
had surplus talent, so he split it apart and wrapped it up, calculating it out. But this is just
putting a head on top of a head, saying: “The supreme Way is not difficult. At the edge of words, at the edge of phrases. One has many kinds. Two has
no twofold.” Though there aren’t that many matters, when the sun rises at the horizon, the moon then sets; before the railing,
when the mountains are deep, the water is cold. When you reach here, words are at the edge, phrases are at the edge; every head
is the Way; each thing is wholly real. Isn’t this forgetting both mind and objects, beaten into one piece
in that place? Xuedou’s head is too solitary and steep; in the end he also lets out quite a lot. If
you investigate through and see through, it naturally resembles the finest taste of ghee.
If emotional interpretation has not been forgotten, you will see it split into seven pieces and eight fragments; you certainly cannot understand talk like this.
“When the skull’s knowing is exhausted, what joy could stand? A dragon’s cry in a withered tree—the thaw has not yet dried.” This
is exactly the crossing point. The monk asked like this; Zhaozhou answered like this. Zhaozhou
said: “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and rejecting. As soon as there are words, that is choosing and rejecting; that is
clarity. This old monk is not within clarity—do you still cherish it or not?” At that time
a monk then asked: “Since you are not within clarity, what is there to cherish?” Zhaozhou
said: “I don’t know either.” The monk said: “Master, since you don’t know, why then did you say
you are not within clarity?” Zhaozhou said: “Ask and you’ll get it.” The monk bowed and withdrew. This is
an old case of questioning the Way. Xuedou dragged it over and strung it together, using a verse
for “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and rejecting.” People today don’t understand the ancients’ intent; they only
keep biting words and chewing phrases—when will that ever end? Only a master with thorough understanding can
discern speech like this. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Xiangyan, “What is the Way?” Xiangyan
said, “A dragon’s cry in a withered tree.” The monk said, “What is a person in the Way?” Xiangyan said,
“Eyes in a skull.” Later the monk asked Shishuang, “What is a dragon’s cry in a withered tree?”
Shishuang said, “It still carries joy.” “What are eyes in a skull?” Shishuang
said, “It still carries knowing.” The monk then asked Caoshan, “What is a dragon’s cry in a withered tree?”
Caoshan said, “The bloodline is unbroken.” “What are eyes in a skull?” Caoshan said,
“It does not dry out.” “Who can hear it?” Caoshan said, “Across the whole earth there is not a single person
who does not hear it.” The monk said, “I wonder what lines the dragon’s cry is.” Caoshan said, “I do not know what
lines they are; those who hear it all perish.” There is also a verse: “A dragon’s cry in a withered tree truly sees
the Way; in a skull without knowing, the eyes first brighten. When joy and knowing are exhausted, the news is exhausted; how can a person
discern the clear within the turbid?” Xuedou could be said to have great skill—at once he
brought it together and versified it for you. Yet even so, there are no two kinds at all. At the end Xuedou has
a place where he helps people, saying again, “Hard, hard.” Just this “hard, hard”—you must pass through it to get it.
Why? Baizhang said: all language, mountains and rivers and the great earth—each and every one turns back
to oneself. Whenever Xuedou picks up and puts down, in the end it must return to oneself.
Tell me, where is Xuedou’s place of helping people? “Choosing and rejecting, clarity—sir, you
see for yourself.” Since he already spun out a vine-tangle verse, why then does he say, “You see for yourself”? Fine luck—
he lets you see for yourself. Tell me, where does the intent land? Don’t say you people
can’t understand it. Even if this mountain monk came to this point, I too would only be unable to understand it—
unable to understand it.

In his instruction he said: one pivot, one circumstance; one word, one phrase—just to sketch an entry point.
Carving a wound into good flesh—making nests and pits. When great function appears before you, it keeps no fixed rules.
Just try to know there is the matter beyond; it covers heaven and covers earth, yet you cannot grope it out. So,
thus is fine; not thus is fine too—too fussy. Thus is not fine either.
Not thus is not fine either—too lone and perilous. It does not fall into two roads—so what is it, then?
Please try to raise an example and see.

Case 3: When Master Ma was unwell, the head of the monastery asked,This fellow leaks and hints a lot, dragging others along with him.
“Master, how has your honored condition been lately?”The four hundred and four illnesses erupt at once. Not escorting the dead monk after three days is skillful. In the way of benevolence and righteousness,
The great master said, “Sun-Face Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha.”how fresh and deadly! The karma of raising children

Master Ma was unwell. The head of the monastery asked, “Master, how has your honored condition been lately?” The great
master said, “Sun-Face Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha.” If the patriarchs did not meet by way of the essential matter,
how could this Way shine with such radiance? In this public case, if you know where it lands, then you
stride alone in the cinnabar sky. If you do not know where it lands, you often take a wrong road
right in front of Deadwood Cliff. If you are an essential person and reach here, you must have
hands and feet that can drive off a plowman’s ox and snatch away a hungry man’s food—only then do you see what kind of person Master Ma was.
Nowadays many say, “Master Ma was responding to the head of the monastery.” Luckily, that has nothing to do with it.
Many in the assembly misunderstand, glaring and saying, “It’s right here—his left eye is Sun-Face,
his right eye is Moon-Face—what’s the big deal?” They haven’t even dreamed of it in the year of the donkey.
They just keep missing what the ancients were doing. Tell me, when Master Ma spoke like this, where was his intent?
Some say, “Bring a cup of Pingwei Powdered Decoction.” What’s the use of that?
When you get here, how will you go on steadily? Therefore it is said: the road beyond
is not transmitted by a thousand sages. Students wear themselves out like apes grabbing at shadows. This “Sun-Face
Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha” is extremely hard to see. Even Xuedou, coming to this, found it hard to versify—yet
because he saw through it, he used the work of his whole life to point directly at it. Do you all want
to see Xuedou? Look at what follows.

Sun-Face Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha—what are the Five Emperors and Three Sovereigns?
Open your mouth and you see your guts. Like two mirrors facing each other, there is no image in between.
What are they, really? For twenty years I endured bitter hardship; for your sake, how many times I went down into the azure dragon’s cave—humbling, yet worth telling.Too lofty. Do not casually praise it. It can be precious, it can be cheap.It is you yourself who fall
into the weeds. It has nothing to do with this mountain monk. A mute eats bitter gourd.Why bother like that? Do not misapply the mind.
Fine. And do not say there is nothing extraordinary.Sorrow kills people. A sorrowful person should not speak to a sorrowful person.To whom will you speak? Speak to a sorrowful person and sorrow kills people.
Clear-eyed patchrobed monks, do not take it lightly.You must be even more careful. Bah! Retreat three thousand.

When Shenzong was on the throne, he claimed this verse mocked the state, so he would not allow it to be entered into the canon.
Xuedou first picked it up, saying, “Sun-Face Buddha, Moon-Face Buddha.” Once he had picked it up, he then said, “The Five
Emperors and Three Sovereigns—what are they?” Tell me, what was his intent? Just now it has already been said
outright—he went straight in on him. Therefore it is said: he casts a hook across the four seas, fishing only for fierce
dragons. With just this one line, it is finished. After that, Xuedou himself versified what he had devoted his whole life to
investigating: “For twenty years I endured bitter hardship; for your sake, how many times I went down into the azure
dragon’s cave.” What is that like? It is like someone entering the azure dragon’s cave to fetch a pearl—
just so. Later he smashed the lacquer bucket, thinking it was something extraordinary; yet in the end it only took
“What are the Five Emperors and Three Sovereigns?” Tell me—where do Xuedou’s words land?
You must step back and look for yourself; only then do you begin to see where they land. Don’t you see:
Attendant Pou of Xingyang answered Elder Yuanlu’s question: “When Sagara comes out of the sea,
heaven and earth quake. Face-to-face, what is the matter presented?” Pou said, “The Golden-Winged Bird King
rules the universe. In this, who is the one who comes out on top?” Yuan said, “If you suddenly meet someone who comes out on top, then
what then?” Pou said, “Like a falcon seizing a dove. If you don’t believe it, test it in front of a skull—only then
will you know it’s true.” Yuan said, “In that case, bend your pride to your chest and step back three paces.” Pou said,
“You little turtle under Mount Sumeru’s seat—don’t wait until you get your forehead tapped again and sent back.” Therefore,
“The Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors—what are they?” Many people do not see Xuedou’s meaning and just keep
saying it mocked the state. If you understand it like that, it is only emotional opinion. This is what Chan-yue wrote in his verse
on Gongzi Xing: “In bright brocade he holds a hawk in hand; strolling at ease, his air makes many treat him lightly.
He never knows the hardship of farming—what are the Five Emperors and Three Sovereigns?” Xuedou said,
“Humbled, yet worth telling. Clear-eyed patchrobed monks, do not take it lightly.” How many people, down in the azure dragon’s cave,
make their living there. Even if you have eyes on the crown of your head and a talisman behind your elbow—clear-eyed
patchrobed monks who illuminate the four worlds—when you get here, do not take it lightly. You must be
meticulous—only then will it do.

In his instruction he said: under the blue sky and bright sun, do not point east and draw west again. When the time and conditions
call for it, you must also meet illness with medicine. Tell me: is it better to let go, or better to hold fast? Try
to raise an example and see.

Case 4: Deshan arrived at Weishan. Holding his sitting mat, he crossed the Dharma hall from east to west, then from west to east, looking around,A plank-carrying fellow. A wild-fox demon.No harm.
It makes people suspicious. Take in the flaw.Deadly! What use is there in having Zen?
and said, “No, no,” then left. XuedouGive him thirty blows. Deadly! His qi surges to the sky. A true lion's cub, skilled at the lion's roar.
added, “Seen through already.” When Deshan reached the doorway, he thenWrong. Sure enough. A dot.
said, “That won’t do—can’t be careless,”Let go and gather back. Above the head, too lofty; at the end, too low. Knowing a fault, you must change it; how many can do that?
then he assumed proper deportment and went back in to meet Weishan again.Still doing this coming and going as before is already a second layer of flaw. Perilous!
At his seat, Deshan lifted up his sitting cloth and said,Look at this old fellow with a cold eye. To stroke the tiger's whiskers, you must be this kind of person.
“Master—” Weishan moved to take up the whisk; Deshan immediately gave a shout, flicked his sleeves, and went out. Xuedou added, “SeenChanging head and swapping face; raising waves without wind.It must be that fellow. Planning within the tent.
No harm in cutting off the tongues of people under heaven while sitting.Wild-fox-demon views. This one shout also has
provisional, and also real; also illumination, and also function. It is of the same sort as those who grasp clouds and seize mist; within it, extraordinary.
through already.” Deshan turned his back on the Dharma hall, put on his straw sandals, and left.Wrong. Sure enough. A dot.
That evening WeishanThe scenery is lovely. The koan is not yet complete. You win a hat on your neck, but lose the shoes under your feet; you have already lost body and lost life.
asked the head monk, “Where is the newcomer from earlier?” The head monk said, “At the time he turned his back on the Dharma hall and went out wearing straw sandals.”On the east side the joint falls; on the west side the root is pulled. The eyes look to the southeast; the intent
is in the northwest.
Weishan said, “After this, that fellow will head forThe spirit turtle drags its tail. Give him thirty blows. How many should a fellow like this take on the back of the head?
the top of a solitary peak, build a grass hut, and go on scolding Buddha and cursing the patriarchs.” Xuedou added, “Frost on top of snow.”After the thief passes, you draw
the bow. Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out of it.Wrong. Sure enough. A dot.

Beneath Jia Mountain are three dotted characters—do you all understand? Sometimes you take a single blade
of grass and use it as a sixteen-foot golden body. Sometimes you take a sixteen-foot golden body and use it as a single blade
of grass. Deshan was originally a lecturer-monk, lecturing on the Diamond Sutra in Western Shu. Because in the teachings
it says the diamond is a metaphor for samadhi, and later, in the wisdom teachings, it says: for a thousand kalpas you learn the Buddha’s deportment; for ten thousand
kalpas you learn the Buddha’s fine conduct; only then do you become Buddha. Those southern demon fellows then said,
“Mind itself is Buddha.” So he roused himself in anger, shouldered his commentaries and traveled on foot straight to the south to smash
those demon types. Seeing him so incensed, he truly was a fierce fellow. When he first
arrived in Lizhou, on the road he saw an old woman selling fried cakes. He set down his commentaries,
and bought a snack to eat. The woman said, “What are you carrying?” Deshan said, “Diamond
Sutra commentaries.” The woman said, “I have a question. If you can answer it, I will give you
these fried cakes as a gift for your snack. If you can’t answer, buy elsewhere.” Deshan said, “Just
ask.” The woman said, “The Diamond Sutra says: the past mind cannot be obtained; the present mind cannot
be obtained; the future mind cannot be obtained. Senior monk, which mind do you want to ‘refresh’?” Deshan
had nothing to say. The woman then pointed him to go see Longtan. As soon as he crossed the threshold he asked, “I have long admired Long
tan, but now that I have arrived, the pool is not in sight and the dragon does not appear.” The master
Longtan, from behind a screen, leaned out and said, “You have personally arrived at Longtan.” Deshan then bowed in respect
and withdrew. At night he entered the room and stood waiting deep into the night. Longtan said, “Why don’t you go down?”
Deshan said his farewells and lifted the curtain to go out. Seeing it was dark outside, he turned back and said, “Outside the door
it’s dark.” Longtan then lit a paper candle and handed it to him. Just as Deshan received it, Longtan blew it out. Deshan
suddenly awakened and bowed. Longtan said, “What did you see that you bow like this?”
Deshan said, “From now on, I will no longer doubt the tongues of the old masters under heaven.”
The next day Longtan ascended the hall and said, “In here there is a fellow whose teeth are like sword
trees and whose mouth is like a basin of blood. Strike him with a staff and he won’t turn his head. One day, another day, he will go to a solitary
peak top and establish my Way.” Deshan then took his commentaries and, in front of the Dharma hall,
raised a torch and said, “Exhaust all subtle debate—like placing a single hair in vast
emptiness. Drain the world’s pivotal mechanisms—like casting a drop into a great ravine.” Then he burned them. Later he heard that
Weishan’s teaching was flourishing, so he went straight to Weishan and met him like a true adept. Bao did not understand it either.
He went straight up into the Dharma hall, crossed from east to west, then from west to east, looked around and said, “No, no.”
Then he left. Tell me—what was his intent? Was he mad? Many people misunderstand,
using it to prop things up—yet it truly has nothing to do with that. Look at him like this; it is not without its strangeness.
Therefore it is said: to stand out from the crowd you must be a heroic, numinous person; to beat a rival, you need a lion’s cub.
If in selecting Buddhas you lack such eyes, then even after a thousand years what good would it be? When you reach here,
you must be a master of all directions before you can begin to see it. Why? The Buddha Dharma isn’t that complicated.
A matter like this leaves no room for sentimental opinions. Where in his mind could there be
so much fuss? Therefore Master Xuansha said: it is just like the moon’s reflection in an autumn pool, like a bell
sounding in a quiet night—struck without ever being diminished, met by waves and ripples without being scattered. Still, this is
a matter on the shore of birth and death. When you get here, there is no gain or loss, no right or wrong, and no
strange, mysterious marvel. Since there is nothing strange or mysterious, how will you understand him going from east to west, from
west to east? Tell me—what is the point? Old man Guishan doesn’t bother with him
at all. If it weren’t Guishan, he would have been roughed up once. Look at Guishan
that seasoned old hand meeting him—he just sits and watches success and failure. If he didn’t discern the incoming wind deeply,
how could it be like this? Xuedou added a remark: “He has seen through it.” It is like an iron stake
all the same. The assembly calls this an appended remark. Though it stands on both sides, it does not abide on
either side. How do you understand his saying “He has seen through it”? Where is the place that is
seen through? And tell me—seen through Deshan, seen through Guishan. Deshan then went out
to the gate, wanting to pull it up by the roots. He said to himself, “I mustn’t be careless. I want to
have a Dharma battle with Guishan, lay bare his innards—then, after composing myself with proper demeanor, I’ll go back
to meet him again.” Guishan was seated. Deshan lifted up his sitting mat and said, “Master Guishan—”
Guishan reached to take up his whisk; Deshan gave a shout, flicked his sleeves, and walked out. How astonishing! In the
assembly many say, “Guishan was afraid of him—what does that have to do with it?” Guishan wasn’t flustered either. So it is said:
If your wisdom surpasses birds, you catch birds; if it surpasses beasts, you catch beasts; if your wisdom surpasses people,
you catch people. If you investigate this kind of Chan, then across the whole earth, all the myriad forms and
phenomena—heaven and hell, weeds and people and animals—if they all at once give a single shout, he still
doesn’t bother. If they overturn the meditation bench, if they shout and scatter the great assembly, he still doesn’t care. Like the height of heaven,
like the thickness of earth. If Guishan did not have the means to sit and cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven,
it would be very hard to test him. If it weren’t for his fifteen hundred people,
good friends of the Way, even here it would still be hard to sort out. Guishan is one who plans within the tent,
deciding victory a thousand miles away. Deshan turned his back on the Dharma hall, put on his straw sandals, and went out.
Tell me—what was his intent? Do you say Deshan won or lost? Guishan,
in this way—was it winning or losing? Xuedou’s appended remark says: “He has seen through it.” This is because
he applied effort and saw through the ancients’ most knotty, contentious point—only then can it be like this. It certainly
is not hindered from being astonishing. Netang said: Xuedou wrote two “seen through” remarks, making a three-part judgment.
Only then does this gong’an become clear—like a bystander judging between two people. Later this old
fellow, slowly, not until evening, asked the head monk, “Where is the newcomer from earlier
now?” The head monk said, “At the time he turned his back on the Dharma hall and went out in straw sandals.” Gui-
shan said, “In the future this fellow will go to the top of a solitary peak, build a grass hut, and scold the Buddha
and curse the patriarchs.” Tell me—what is his meaning? Old Guishan is not
kindhearted. Later Deshan indeed scolded the Buddha and cursed the patriarchs, storming and raging, yet still he did not
leave his own nest. This old fellow saw through all his life’s tricks. At this point,
do you call it Guishan giving him a prediction? Do you call it “a marsh broad, a storehouse of mountains”? Can principle
subdue a leopard? If it is like that, then happily it has nothing to do with it. Xuedou knew where
this gong’an lands, and dared to make a verdict; he further said, “Adding frost on top of snow,” and again picked it
up to show people. If you can see it, I permit you to join Guishan, Deshan, and Xue-
dou in the same investigation. If you do not see it, strictly avoid giving rise to reckless sentimental interpretations.

lg.l:
One seen through, two seen through—adding frost on snow, he once nearly fell into danger.The words are still in the ear. Passthe double koan.
The three sections are not the same. Where is it?
The Flying Cavalry General entered the enemy court.Perilous! A general of a defeated army needs no second beheading. Losing body and losing life.

How many can again come out intact? Hurry—run past!Gaining life within death.Acting as if no one were there. The thirty-six stratagems, all your spiritual powers.
What use are they?

He will not let him off: sitting in the grass atop a solitary peak.Principle can subdue a leopard, yet it pierces your nostrils.Indeed. Pierced through
the nostrils. That is still not strange. Why then sit in the weeds
Bah!and think you understand? Two blades injure each other. In pairs and threes they walk the old road. Chant and clapping follow along, then strike.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou versified a hundred gong’an; for each one he burned incense and held it up. Therefore
they spread widely in the world. Moreover, he understood literature and penetrated the gong’an, working them over until ripe.
Only then could he put brush to paper. Why? Because it is so: dragons and snakes are easy to tell apart, but a Chan monk is hard to fool.
Xuedou thoroughly investigated this gong’an and at the thorny, contentious point he set down three lines of words.
He gathered them up and brought them out in verse: “Adding frost on snow,” he nearly fell into danger. As for Deshan—
what is he like? He is like Li Guang, naturally skilled at archery; the Son of Heaven appointed him
Flying Cavalry General. He went deep into enemy territory and was captured alive by the chanyu. At the time Guang was wounded and ill; they placed Guang
between two horses, bound and carried lying down. Guang then feigned death. Glancing sideways, he saw beside him a Hu
boy riding a fine horse. Guang sprang up onto the horse, shoved the Hu boy off, and seized his bow and arrows.
He whipped the horse and galloped south, drew his bow and shot back the pursuing riders, and thus escaped. This fellow had
such methods—getting life out of death. Xuedou brought it into his verse to compare it to Deshan
going back in to meet him again; and still he was made to leap out. Look at the ancients:
they saw to it, spoke to it, walked to it, used it—no harm in being spirited. With the ability to kill without
blinking an eye, only then can one become a buddha on the spot. Those who can become a buddha on
the spot naturally kill without blinking an eye—only then is there a share of freedom and ease. People today
who have something of it, when questioned, seem to have a Chan monk’s bearing at first; but press them lightly,
and their waist breaks, their thighs are cut—limbs scattered in all directions, with not the least continuity
anywhere. Therefore the ancients said: continuity is very hard. Look at Deshan and Guishan
like this—how could it be the view of snuffing out and clutching at things? How many can again come out intact?
Hurry—run past! Deshan shouted and then went out. It is like Li Guang after being captured,
devising a plan: with one arrow he killed a barbarian general and escaped the enemy court. When Xue-
dou reached this in his verse, there was great skill. Deshan turned his back on the Dharma hall, put on straw
sandals, and went out—saying it as though he’d gotten the advantage. But he didn’t know: this old fellow still would not let
him come out on top. Xuedou said, “Will not let him off.” Guishan, toward evening, asked the head
monk, “Where is the newcomer from earlier now?” The head monk said, “At the time he turned his back on the Dharma
hall and went out in straw sandals.” Guishan said, “In the future this fellow will go to a solitary
peak’s summit, build a grass hut, and scold the Buddha and curse the patriarchs.” When was that ever letting
him off? No harm in being astonishing. Here, why does Xuedou say, “Sitting in the grass atop a solitary peak,”
and then add another shout? Tell me—where does it land? Investigate
for thirty more years.

The guiding instruction says: in general, to uphold and establish the teaching of the school, one must be a person of heroic spirit, with the
ability to kill without blinking an eye—only then can one become a buddha on the spot. Therefore illumination and function
unfold and contract together, singing in unison. Principle and phenomena are not two; provisional and real proceed side by side. Letting one move
slip by establishes a secondary gate of meaning; one must cut off the creeping vines straightaway. For later students and beginners,
it is hard to approach. Yesterday it was like this, out of necessity; today it is again like this.
The offense reaches the heavens. If you are a clear-eyed person, you cannot be deceived in the slightest. Otherwise,
if not, throwing yourself crosswise into a tiger’s mouth—you can hardly avoid losing body and life. Try bringing it up and see.

[5] Citing: Xuefeng addressed the assembly, saying: Gather up the whole earth and it is likeOne blind man leading a crowd of blind men, nothing extraordinary.
a millet grain—toss it right before your face. You lacquer-bucket who can’t beat the drum, I invite you all: look!What kind of trick is this? This mountain monk has never played at ghost eyes.I only fear you cannot let it go.
What skill do you have?Leaning on power to bully people. Take it yourself and go out. Do not deceive the assembly.
。Blind. Beat the drum for the three armies.

Changqing asked Yunmen, “When Xuefeng speaks like that, is there still a place where one cannot stick one’s head out?”
Yunmen said, “There is.” Changqing said, “How?” Yunmen said, “You must not always make it into wild-
fox-sprite views.” Xuefeng said, “Not enough above, more than enough below. I will still
entangle you with creeping vines.” He held up his staff and said, “Do you still see Xuefeng?” Bah! The royal decree
is somewhat strict: it does not permit snatching and seizing in the marketplace. Dawei Zhe said, “I, for all of you,
will add mud on top of dirt.” He held up his staff and said, “Look, look! Xuefeng in front of everyone’s
faces is taking a dump.” Bah! Why don’t you even know it stinks? Xuefeng addressed the assembly, saying:
Gather up the whole earth and it is like a millet grain. The ancients, in dealing with people and benefiting living beings, had
their astonishing points—yet it is no hindrance to be diligent. Three times to Touzi, nine times to Dongshan.
Carrying a lacquer bucket and wooden ladle, serving as rice cook everywhere—only to break through and be free of this matter.
When he was rice cook at Dongshan, one day Dongshan asked Xuefeng, “What are you doing?”
Xuefeng said, “Washing rice.” Dongshan said, “Wash the sand and remove the rice, or wash the rice and remove the sand?” Xuefeng said,
“Both sand and rice go together.” Dongshan said, “Then what will the assembly eat?” Xuefeng immediately overturned the basin.
Dongshan said, “Your affinity lies with Deshan,” and directed him to go see him. As soon as he arrived he asked, “In the matter of the
school’s succession from above, does this student still have a share or not?” Deshan struck him with a staff and said,
“Speak what?” Because of that he had an awakening. Later, when snowbound on Mount Ao, he said to Yantou,
“Back then, under Deshan’s stick, it was like the bottom dropping out of a bucket.” Yantou shouted,
saying, “Haven’t you heard it said: what enters by the gate is not the family treasure. It must come from your own
chest and flow out, covering heaven and covering earth—only then is there a small measure of accord.” Xuefeng suddenly
had a great awakening, bowed, and said, “Elder brother, only today have you realized the Way on Mount Ao. Nowadays people only go on saying,
‘The ancients deliberately put on a show to teach later people to follow rules.’
If it’s like that, it’s precisely slandering the ancients—called ‘drawing blood from the Buddha’s body.’ The ancients
were not careless like people today. How could they take a word or half a phrase as their whole life?
If one upholds and establishes the teaching, extending the Buddha’s life, then when they spit out a word or half a phrase, it naturally
cuts off the tongues of people everywhere. There is no path for you to set your mind on, no sentimental interpretation to make, no
place to get entangled in principles. Look at this instruction to the assembly—he had once met a true adept.
That’s why he had an adept’s tongs-and-hammer. Whenever he uttered a word or half a phrase, it was not a scheme of the mind,
not consciousness and thinking making a living in a ghost cave. It was truly outstanding, cutting off
past and present, allowing no conjecture. This is how he functioned, entirely. One day
he instructed the assembly, saying, “On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake. All of you must
watch it well.” At that time the Daoshi Ling stepped out from the crowd and said, “If so, then today in this hall
many will lose their bodies and their lives.” He also said, “All of the great earth is a śramaṇa’s
single eye. All of you—where will you piss?” He also said, “Wangzhou Pavilion—
I have met you there. Wushi Ridge—I have met you there. In front of the monks’ hall—
I have met you there.” At that time Baofu asked Ehu, “Set aside ‘in front of the monks’ hall’ for the moment—
what is the place of meeting at Wangzhou Pavilion and Wushi Ridge?” Ehu
strode quickly back to the abbot’s quarters. He often raised words like this to instruct the assembly. But tell me: when it is said,
“Gather the whole great earth and it’s as big as a millet grain,” at that time—tell me—can you measure it with feelings
and consciousness? You must smash the cage; gain and loss, right and wrong—drop them all at once,
free and unencumbered. Then you naturally pass through his trap, and only then see his functioning.
Now tell me: where was Xuefeng’s intent? Many people make a sentimental interpretation and say, “The mind
is the master of all dharmas; the whole great earth is in my hand at once.” Happily, that has
nothing to do with it. Here you must be a true person; just hearing it raised, you penetrate to the bone,
through to the marrow, seeing it through—without falling into feelings, thoughts, or imaginings. If you are a genuine
traveling patch-robed monk, seeing him like this is already being careless with people. Look—
Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Ox-head sinks, horse-head turns back; in Caoxi’s mirror,Like a flash of lightning, and you have already missed it.Like striking flint for sparks.
dust and grime are cut off.Smash the mirror and then I will meet you. You must smash it for it to work.

Beat the drum and look—don’t you see?Poke out your eyes
already. Do not take it lightly. What is hard to see about a lacquer bucket?

When spring comes, for whom do a hundred flowers bloom?The Dharma does not spare each other. One complete mess.
Stick your head out from the vine cave.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou naturally saw through the ancients. He only needed to jab once at the lifeline.
And so he versified it: “Ox-head sinks, horse-head turns back.” Tell me, what is he talking about? One who sees through it
is like eating porridge at dawn and eating rice at mealtime—just ordinary.
Xuedou is compassionate: right on the spot, one hammer-blow shatters it; one line cuts it off. Yet
it remains solitary and steep. Like sparks struck from stone, like a flash of lightning—no blade-edge shown, no
place for you to cling. Tell me, can you grope for it beneath the root of intention? These two lines
say it all at once. Xuedou’s third line opens a thin thread of passage, slightly
showing a bit of style and pattern—already he’s gone down into the weeds. The fourth line, straightaway, is even more down in the weeds.
If you give rise to words on top of words, lines on top of lines, meanings on top of meanings, making explanations and making
understandings, you not only burden this old monk—you also betray Xuedou. Though the ancients’ lines are
like this, their intent is not like this. In the end they do not use principles to bind people. “Caoxi’s
mirror cuts off dust and grime”—how many people say, “A quiet mind is the mirror.” Happily, that has
nothing to do with it. If you just keep calculating principles, what end will there be? This is
proper speech. This mountain monk does not dare depart from what is proper. “Ox-head sinks, horse-head
turns back”—Xuedou has said it clearly. It’s only that people don’t see it. That’s why Xuedou
so carelessly versified: “Beat the drum and look—don’t you see?” Do fools see it or not?
I’ll tell you further: “When spring comes, for whom do a hundred flowers bloom?” One could say he flung open the windows
and doors and unfolded all eight characters for you at once. And when spring comes—to secluded valleys,
wild fields, even places with no one—hundreds of flowers burst into bloom. Tell me—then for whom
do they bloom?

[6] Raised: Yunmen’s “hanging words.” He said, “Before the fifteenth day I won’t ask you; after the fifteenth day, bring me one phrase.” He answered on their behalf: “Every day is a good day.”Half Henan, half Hebei.
Here we do not take old calendar days.You cannot avoid it from morning to dusk. Strictly avoid saying
it. Tomorrow is the sixteenth. Sun and moon flow like a stream.Gather it in. A shrimp cannot jump out of a dipper. Whose house
lacks bright moon and clear wind? Do you know? The sea god knows what is precious but not its price.

When Yunmen first visited Muzhou, Muzhou’s moves were swift as a spinning mechanism, quick as lightning—truly hard to get close to. In his usual way
of receiving people, as soon as someone stepped over the threshold he would grab them and say, “Speak, speak!” If they couldn’t form an answer,
he would shove them out, saying, “The drilling-and-grinding of Qin times.” Yunmen went to see him again and again—by the third
time, as soon as he knocked, Muzhou said, “Who is it?” Yunmen said, “Wenyan.” The moment the door opened, he leapt
inside. Muzhou grabbed him and said, “Speak, speak!” Yunmen hesitated, and was immediately shoved out—one foot
still inside the threshold. Muzhou hurriedly slammed the door, crushing and breaking Yunmen’s foot. Yunmen endured the pain
and cried out—suddenly he had a great awakening. Later, his manner of speaking when guiding people—one touch and they slipped free of Muzhou’s
grasp. Afterward he stayed three years at the residence of the Minister Chen Cao. Muzhou pointed him to go to Xuefeng.
When he arrived there, he stepped out and asked, “What is Buddha?” Xuefeng said, “Don’t speak in your sleep.”
Yunmen bowed. He stayed three years. One day Xuefeng asked, “How is your seeing?”
Yunmen said, “My seeing does not differ from the sages of old by even
a hair’s breadth.” For twenty years Lingshu never invited a head monk. He often said, “My
head monk is being born.” He also said, “My head monk is herding cattle.” Again he said, “My head monk is
traveling on pilgrimage.” Suddenly one day he ordered the bell struck and went to the three gates to receive the head monk. Everyone was
astonished. Yunmen indeed arrived, and was invited into the head monk’s quarters to unpack. Lingshu’s people
called him Chan Master Zhisheng. Past and future matters he foresaw in advance. One day the ruler of Guang,
King Liu, was going to raise troops. He personally entered the monastery and asked the master to decide whether it would be auspicious or not. Lingshu had
already known, and serenely sat and passed away. The ruler of Guang grew angry and said, “When did the master fall ill?” The attendant
replied, “The master was never ill. He just sealed up a small box and told us to wait for the king
to come and present it.” The ruler of Guang opened it and found a note saying: “The eyes of humans and devas, the head monk
in the hall.” The ruler of Guang grasped the point, and thus halted the troops. He invited Yunmen to take up teaching and reside at Lingshu.
Later he came to reside at Yunmen. When the master opened the hall and taught, the palace attendant Ju sent
a question: “Has the fruit of Lingshu ripened yet?” Yunmen said, “In what year did you get news that
the Way is born?” He also cited conditions such as King Liu formerly being an incense-seller. Later King Liu gave the posthumous title
“Lingshu” to Chan Master Zhisheng. Lingshu did not lose his supernatural penetration life after life. Yunmen had
been a king for three lifetimes, and therefore lost it. One day King Liu summoned the master to come inside the palace to spend
the summer retreat. Along with several honored elders, all received inquiries from palace people and gave teachings—only this master
did not speak, and no one drew near him. There was a palace attendant on duty who wrote a verse and posted it
in the Jasper Hall, saying: “Only with great wisdom and practice is it Chan. In the Chan gate, silence is fitting;
noise is not. Of ten thousand clever speeches, how can they match the real? Even so, Yunmen loses out—he never speaks at all.”
Yunmen usually liked to speak “three-character Chan,” such as “Look! Observe! Hey!” He also spoke “one-character Chan.”
A monk asked, “If you kill father and mother, you repent before the Buddha. If you kill the Buddha and kill the patriarchs, in what
place do you repent?” Yunmen said, “Dew.” He also asked, “What is the treasury of the true Dharma eye?” Yunmen
said, “Universal.” He truly allowed no speculation. Yet when he came to level ground, he would still scold people—if
they dropped a single line of talk, it was like an iron stake. Later there emerged the four sages: Dongshan Chu,
Zhimen Kuan, Deshan Mi, and Xianglin Yuan—all became great masters. Xianglin
served as attendant for eighteen years. Whenever Yunmen received him, he only called, “Attendant Yuan.” Yuan would answer,
“Yes.” Yunmen would say, “What is that?” Thus for eighteen years. One day he finally awakened. Yunmen
said, “From now on I won’t call you anymore.” Yunmen’s usual way of receiving people often used Muzhou’s
methods—truly hard to get close to—tongs-and-hammer that pulled nails and yanked wedges.
Xuedou said, “I love Shaoyang’s newly settled device; all my life I have pulled nails and
yanked wedges for people.” He then set out a question to instruct the assembly, saying, “Before the fifteenth day I won’t ask you; after the
fifteenth day, bring one phrase.” He cut off a thousand distinctions, not allowing ordinary or sage to pass through.
He answered on their behalf: “Every day is a good day.” As for “before the fifteenth day”—this line already cut off
Cut off a thousand distinctions. After the fifteenth day, this saying too cuts them off. This is his
not saying, “Tomorrow is the sixteenth.” Later people only keep on producing interpretations from the words. What
does that have to do with Yunmen establishing a school style? There must be a place where he deals with people.
Having given the saying, he then spoke for himself: “Every day is a good day.” This saying runs through ancient
and modern times. From beginning to end, it cuts through all at once. When this mountain monk talks like this, it is also
producing interpretations from words. Better to kill oneself than be killed by him. The moment you make a rationale of it, you fall into a pit, tumble into
a trench. In Yunmen’s one sentence, three sentences are all present. This is simply his household’s guiding principle like
this. When he offers a single phrase, it must return to the source. If it is not so, it is merely
fabrication. This matter has no need for so much theorizing, yet those who have not penetrated it insist on doing so. If
you penetrate it, then you see the intent of the ancients. Watch Xuedou tangle up the vines.

lg.l:
Remove the one, and you lift out the seven above;Seven piercings, eight holes, where do you go? Let one move pass,you cannot lift it out, yet you do not let it pass.
below, in the four directions, none can match it.How could it be? Above is heaven, below is earth; east, south, west, north, and the four corners, what can match it? But the staff
is in my hand.
Walking slowly, you step and break the sound of running water;Do not ask about under your heels. It is hard to investigate in the body. Thrown into the vine cave, gone.

gazing far and wide, you trace out the tracks of flying birds—grassIn the eyes there is also no such news. Wild-fox-demon understanding still remains in the old nest and den.
lush, thick.Pulling the arrow from the back of the head, what news is that? Falling into the ordinary and solid place.

Mist hangs dense; SubhutiNot yet out of this nest and den. Clouds arise under your feet.
by the cliffside—flowers strewn in disorder.Where is it? You useless fellow, it has been seen through.

A snap of the fingers—enough for sorrow: Shunyata
many.The four directions and eight sides are entirely the Dharma realm. Say a line into Shunyata’s nostrils, where is it?

Don’t touch it.Where are the previous words? When you move,
what then?
Touch it, and it’s thirty blows.Take it yourself and go out, then strike.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s verse on the ancients can do precisely this. Right up front he swings the Vajra King’s jeweled sword,
once. Only then does he slightly reveal some of his manner. Even so, in the end there are
not two interpretations. “Remove the one, and you grasp the seven.” Many people take it as arithmetic and say, “Remove
the one” refers to the matter before the fifteenth day. Xuedou suddenly drops two lines of words
that smash that seal, and then he reveals it so people can see. “Remove the one, and you grasp the seven”—absolutely
avoid trying to make a living in the phrases. Why? What juice does a flatbread have? People
mostly fall into consciousness. You must grasp it before words and phrases are born—only then
will you understand. When great function is present, you naturally see it. Thus Shakyamuni, after
awakening, in the land of Magadha, for three times seven days contemplated matters like this.
“The quiescent-extinction aspect of all dharmas cannot be proclaimed with words. I would rather not teach the Dharma, and quickly enter
nirvana.” Here, he could not find a place to open his mouth. Yet by the power of skillful means
he taught the Dharma to the five bhikshus, and down to three hundred sixty assemblies, he taught for a whole lifetime
—all of it merely skillful means. So he took off the precious royal robes and put on tattered, filthy clothes. With no other choice,
he led them into the shallow, accessible parts of the gate of the second meaning, luring and guiding the sons. If
he were to lift it up completely and straight to the top, across the whole great earth there would not be even a half of one person. Tell me:
what is the first phrase? Here Xuedou reveals a bit of his intent to let people see.
You only need to see no buddhas above, no sentient beings below; see no
mountains, rivers, and great earth outside; and within, see no seeing, hearing, sensing, or knowing. Like a person of great death
who nevertheless lives. Long and short, likes and dislikes—hammered into one piece. Pick them up one by one
and there is no differing view. Then in responding and functioning you do not miss what is appropriate—only then do you see what he means by
“Remove the one, and you grasp the seven; above and below, in the four directions, none can match it.” If you penetrate this line,
then truly above and below, in the four directions, there is none equal. The myriad phenomena in their profusion—plants and weeds,
humans and animals—each and every one fully displays your own household style. Therefore it is said: “Amid the myriad appearances,
the body alone is revealed; only when a person approves it for themselves are they intimate with it.” In former years I mistakenly searched along the road;
today, looking back, it is ice in fire. “In heaven above and on earth below, I alone am honored.” People
mostly chase the branches and do not seek the root. Once the root is made correct, naturally the wind moves and the grass bends.
When water arrives, the channel is formed. “Walking slowly, you step and break the sound of running water.” When you move slowly and steadily, the vast
sound of running water should also be stepped through and broken. “Gazing far and wide, you trace out the tracks of flying birds.” Casting your eyes
in a single look—even the tracks of flying birds appear as if written out. Here,
cauldrons of boiling broth and furnaces of coals are blown out; sword-trees and knife-mountains are shattered with a shout. It is not a difficult
thing. When Xuedou reaches this point, out of compassion he fears people will sit down in a realm of nothing-to-do.
So he says again, “Grass lush, mist dense,” thereby covering it up, so that it becomes only “grass lush,
thick, a haze of smoke. Tell me, whose realm is this? Can you call it Every day is
