a good day”? Luckily it has nothing to do with it. It is not even “Walking slowly, you step and break the sound
of running water.” Nor is it “Gazing far and wide, you trace out the tracks of flying birds.” Nor is it “grass lush,”
nor is it “mist dense.” Even if none of it is like that at all, it is precisely
“Subhuti—by the cliffside—flowers strewn in disorder.” You still must turn it over to the other side to make it through.
Have you not seen it? Subhuti sat in meditation in a cliff cave; the gods rained flowers and praised him. The Venerable
one said, “Flowers and praise rain from the sky—who is it?” The deity said, “I am
Indra.” The Venerable one said, “Why do you praise?” Indra said, “I esteem the Venerable one for skillfully teaching
prajnaparamita.” The Venerable one said, “Regarding prajna, I have never spoken a
single word. How can you praise?” Indra said, “The Venerable one has no speaking; therefore I have no hearing.
No speaking and no hearing—this is true prajna.” Again the earth shook and flowers rained. Xuedou also
once had a verse: “After rain, clouds congeal; dawn opens halfway. Several peaks, like a painting, blue and lofty
crags. Subhuti does not understand sitting in the cave; he stirred up heavenly flowers that shook the earth.” Since Indra
shook the earth and rained flowers—at this point, where could it be hidden? Xuedou also says,
“I fear that even if I flee, I cannot flee. Outside the great square it is all filled; bustling and
restless—who knows where it ends? A clear wind from all eight directions brushes the hems of one’s robes.” It comes to be utterly bare and naked,
clean and free, with not the slightest fault or affliction—yet even that is not the ultimate. So in the end,
what is it? Look to the next lines: “A snap of the fingers—enough for sorrow: Shunyata.” “Shunyata” is Sanskrit;
it means “spirit of empty space.” Taking empty space as its substance, it has no body and no tactile awareness.
Only when illuminated by the Buddha’s light does it manifest a body. If you become like the spirit of empty space,
then Xuedou is just right to snap his fingers and lament. He also says, “Don’t touch it.” If you touch it, then what
is it like? In broad daylight under the blue sky, you nod off with your eyes open.

The Preface says: A sentence before sound—no thousand sages transmit it. If you have never personally met it, it is as though separated
by a great chiliocosm. Even if you can discern it before sound and cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven, it
still is not the innate fiery fellow. Therefore it is said: heaven cannot cover it, earth cannot support it; empty
space cannot contain it, sun and moon cannot shine on it. In a place with no Buddha, alone it is called honored—only then is it
somewhat close. If it is not so, then penetrate it on the tip of a single hair and release a great light.
Seven vertical, eight horizontal—free and unimpeded within the Dharma. Whatever you pick up at random, there is nothing that
is not it. But tell me—what do you call it? So extraordinary. Again it says: Great assembly, do you understand?
In the past, sweat-soaked warhorses went unrecognized; they only want to reconsider the achievements that covered an age. As for the matter right now,
set it aside—how does Xuedou’s case go? Look to what follows.

[7] A monk raised a question to the master Fazheng Huichao, asking:Say what? Wearing a cangue and carrying your confession.
“What is the Buddha-Dharma?” Fazheng said, “You are Huichao.”Say what? Eyes bulging out.Slipping out by the pattern. Iron scraps for stuffing. Robbing you on the spot.

Chan Master Fazheng had the timing of pecking from within and from without happening at once, and he possessed the
functioning of that simultaneous pecking. Only then could he answer like this. This is what is called transcending sound and surpassing form, attaining great freedom.
To give or to take, right on the spot; to kill or to let live—it's in me. No harm in being extraordinary. However, this case
is discussed in many places; those who interpret it emotionally are not few. They do not realize
that the ancients, whenever they offered a word or half a phrase, were like striking stone to spark fire, like a flash of lightning.
Straightaway they cleared open a true road. Later people only keep going to the words and phrases, making
interpretations and saying, “Huichao is the Buddha.” That is why Fazheng answered that way. Some
say it is like riding an ox while searching for an ox. Some say the very place of questioning is it. What
does it have to do with anything? If you understand it like that, you not only betray yourself, you also greatly wrong
the ancients. If you want to see the complete functioning, it would take someone who, with one blow, will not turn their head back—
a fellow whose teeth are like sword-trees and whose mouth is like a basin of blood. Only if you know where to return outside the words will you have
a small measure of correspondence. If you interpret each and every part emotionally, then all over the great earth are fellows who wipe out the barbarian seed—
that sort of men. But if the Chan traveler Huichao awakened here, it was also because his everyday
guidance and investigation had been carried on. Thus under a single word, it is like the bottom falling out of a bucket. Only
Now Inspector Ze was in Master Fayan’s assembly. He had never once gone to inquire and enter the room.
One day Fayan asked, “Inspector Ze, why haven’t you come to enter the room?” Ze said, “Master,
don’t you know that I already have an entry point from Qinglin?” Fayan said, “You
try raising it for me to see.” Ze said, “I asked, ‘What is the Buddha?’ Qinglin said, ‘Bing
and Ding, the youths, come seeking fire.’” Fayan said, “Good words. I fear you’ve misunderstood. Could you further
explain it?” Ze said, “Bing and Ding belong to fire; using fire to seek fire. If I myself am Buddha,
yet I go looking for Buddha again.” Fayan said, “Inspector, indeed you have misunderstood.” Ze, not
accepting it, got angry and packed up to cross the river and leave. Fayan said, “If this man turns back, he can be saved. If he
doesn’t turn back, he can’t be saved.” When Ze reached the middle of the road, he reflected, saying, “He is a good spiritual teacher for five hundred
people—how could he be cheating me?” So he returned and again consulted Fayan. Fayan said, “You
just ask me; I’ll answer for you.” Ze then asked, “What is the Buddha?” Fayan
said, “Bing and Ding, the youths, come seeking fire.” At these words Ze had a great awakening. Nowadays there are those
who only keep staring their eyes wide and making interpretations. As the saying goes: since he already has no sore, don’t wound him
again. In a case like this, those who have long practiced—once it’s raised, they immediately know where it lands. Under Fayan,
it’s called “arrow points meeting.” There’s no need for the Five Ranks of ruler and minister, nor the Four Alternatives. Just
speak directly of arrow points meeting—this is his family style. In a single phrase it is seen; right
in the open it penetrates. If you search and think under the phrase, in the end you won’t be able to grasp it. When Fayan came forth to teach
the world, he had an assembly of five hundred. At that time the Buddha-dharma greatly flourished. Then National Teacher Shao had long relied on
Shushan, and claimed he had grasped the meaning. He then gathered Shushan’s lifetime of writings and portrait, and led
the community on pilgrimage. When they arrived at Fayan’s assembly, he also did not go to enter the room, only having
his students follow the group to enter the room. One day Fayan ascended the seat. A monk asked, “What
is ‘a single drop of water from Caoyuan’?” Fayan said, “It is a single drop of water from Caoyuan.” That monk
withdrew in bewilderment. Shao, in the crowd, heard this and suddenly had a great awakening. Later he came forth to teach and inherited
Fayan’s dharma. He presented a verse, saying: “On the summit of Tongxuan Peak, it is not the human world. Outside the mind
there is no dharma; the eyes are full of green mountains.” Fayan certified it, saying, “With just this one verse you can carry on my
lineage. Later you will be respected by kings and nobles; I am not your equal.” Look at those people of old—awakening like
that—what principle is this? You can’t just have this mountain monk talk; you must
yourself, throughout the twelve periods of the day, ready your spirit and take it on like this.
Another day, at the crossroads, letting your hands hang down to serve people—this won’t be a difficult matter. Therefore
when a monk asked Fayan, “What is the Buddha-dharma?” Fayan said, “You are Huichao. Where is
there any place you’ve been wronged?” Haven’t you seen Yunmen say: If you raise it and don’t look back, then there’s deviation. If you
start to calculate and think, in what kalpa will you awaken? Xuedou’s later verse puts it well—boldly and brilliantly. Try raising it
and see.

lg.l:
In the river country, the spring wind cannot rouse it— the partridge criesAcross the whole great earth, where do you get this news? The ornamentation is already revealed.
within deep blossoms.What use is muttering? Blown by the wind into another tune again. How could there be such a thing?

When the third-tier waves run high, the fish transforms into a dragon.
Open this one road. Do not deceive the assembly. Step on the dragon’s head,

A foolish man still bails water from a pond at night.hold the fence and feel the wall, press up to doors and lean by households.
What use is a patch-robed monk? Guarding a stump and waiting for a rabbit.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou was a genuine expert. Where the ancients are hard to bite into, hard to chew, hard to penetrate, and hard to see the joints
and corners and tangled places, he versified it to make people see—nothing wrong with being strange and special. Xuedou recognized
Fayan’s checkpoint key, and also knew where Huichao landed. Moreover he feared later people would, under Fayan’s
words and phrases, make a mistaken interpretation. So he composed a verse about this monk asking like this.
When Fayan answered like this, that is “In the river country, the spring wind cannot rouse it; the partridge cries
within deep blossoms.” These two lines are really just one line. Now tell me—where is Xuedou’s intent
located? In Jiangxi and Jiangnan, many make two kinds of interpretations, saying: “In the river country, the spring
wind cannot rouse it” is used to versify “You are Huichao,” just this bit of news. Even if
even the spring wind in the river country can’t rouse it, “the partridge cries within deep blossoms” is used to versify
the various places debating these words—vastly and noisily—seeming like “the partridge cries within deep blossoms.”
What does that have to do with it? They don’t realize that Xuedou’s two lines are only
one line. To make it seamless and without a gap, he clearly tells you: the words are correct, the speech
is correct—covering heaven and covering earth. He asked, “What is the Buddha?” Fayan said, “You are
Huichao.” Xuedou said, “In the river country, the spring wind cannot rouse it; the partridge cries within deep
blossoms.” If you can take it up right here, you can stride alone into the crimson skies. If you make
sentimental interpretations, for three lives and sixty kalpas. Xuedou’s third and fourth lines are utterly
merciless—he breaks it open for people all at once. Where Master Chao had his great awakening on the spot is like “when the third-
tier waves run high, the fish transforms into a dragon; a foolish man still bails water from a pond at night.” The third-tier waves at Yu Gate—
Mengjin is Dragon Gate. Emperor Yu cut it into three tiers. Now, on the third day of the third month,
when the peach blossoms open, responding to heaven and earth, some fish pass through Dragon Gate: horns grow upon their heads,
their manes and fins and tails rise high; they seize the clouds and depart. Those that cannot leap through get a mark on the forehead and return.
The foolish man bites and chews at the words, like bailing a pond at night to look for fish.
He doesn’t know that the fish has already transformed into a dragon. Old Master Duan had a verse, saying: “One
Wen Daguang coin bought a fried cake; once it’s eaten into the belly, right then you
don’t hear hunger.” This verse is extremely good, but a bit too clumsy. Xuedou’s verse is extremely deft—without
harming the edge or cutting the hand. In old times, Storehouse Master Qing liked to ask people, “What is it for the third-tier
waves to run high and the fish to transform into a dragon?” I don’t have to go into that. I’ll just ask you: once it has transformed into a dragon,
where is it right now?

Instruction says: If you understand, you use it on the road—like a dragon gaining water, like a tiger leaning on the mountain.
If you don’t understand, you spread worldly truths: the ram butting the fence, guarding the stump waiting for a rabbit. Sometimes
one phrase is like a lion crouching on the ground. Sometimes one phrase is like the Vajra King’s jeweled sword.
Sometimes one phrase cuts off the tongues of everyone under heaven. Sometimes one phrase goes along with the waves
and follows the billows. If you use it on the road, meeting a true listener you discern the proper opportunity and know there is no
fault, and together you certify it. If you spread worldly truths, with a single eye you can
sit and cut off the ten directions, standing like a wall a thousand fathoms high. Therefore it is said: when great function appears, there are no
fixed rules. Sometimes you take a single blade of grass and use it as the sixteen-foot golden body. Sometimes you take
the sixteen-foot golden body and use it as a single blade of grass. Now tell me—by what principle is this so?
Do you understand it thoroughly? Try raising it and see.

[8] Case: At the end of the summer retreat, Cuiyan addressed the assembly, saying, “All summer long I have spoken for you brothers—
look—are Cuiyan’s eyebrows still there?” Baofu said, “A thief’s heart is guilty.” Changqing said, “They’ve grown.”Opening your mouth, how do you know it is like this?You only win that your eyes fall to the ground, and your nostrils are lost too. Entering hell
is like an arrow shot.Clearly it is a thief recognizing a thief.
Yunmen said, “Barrier.”Tongue to the ground. Using wrong to go along with wrong. Indeed.Where do you run off to? Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out of it. Ruined.

The ancients had morning attendance and evening requests. When Cuiyan reached the end of the summer retreat, he addressed the assembly like this.
Yet it is fine—solitary and steep; fine—shaking heaven and earth. Now tell me: the whole great canon
of teachings, in five thousand and forty-eight fascicles, cannot avoid speaking of mind and speaking of nature, speaking of sudden and speaking of gradual.
Is there still this message? At such a moment, Cuiyan is indeed
exceptional. Look at him speaking like this—now tell me, where does his intent land?
The ancients let down a hook and never set it in vain; there must be a principle to guide people.
Many people mistakenly understand it, saying, “In broad daylight under the blue sky he speaks words with no proper direction,” making trouble
out of nothing. At the end of summer he first speaks it himself, first checks himself, so others won’t have to check
him. And fortunately it has no connection. This kind of view is called “wiping out the barbarian tribe.” Through
generations, when great masters came forth, if they did not offer instruction to people, there would be no benefit at all. What would they be
aiming at? Only when you see through here do you know the ancients had methods of driving the plowman’s
ox and snatching food from the hungry. Nowadays when people are questioned, they immediately bite and chew
at words and phrases, and set up schemes on the eyebrows. Look at the people in that house—naturally they
know where he walks and stands. A thousand changes, ten thousand transformations; at the joints and corners, in the tangled places, in every move there is a way
out. Then you can respond and exchange like this with him. If these words had nothing exceptional,
why would Yunmen, Baofu, and Changqing, the three of them, smack their lips and respond with him like this?
Baofu said, “A thief’s heart is guilty.” Just because of this one phrase, it has provoked all those earlier
sentimental interpretations. Now tell me—what was Baofu’s intent? Absolutely avoid looking for it in the
phrase and hunting for the ancients there. If you give rise to feelings and thoughts, you’ll have to trade in your eyes. But
you don’t know: Baofu’s turning phrase cuts off Cuiyan’s heels. Changqing said,
“They’ve grown.” Many people say Changqing is turning along with Cuiyan’s heels, so he says “they’ve grown.”
It seems to have no connection. They don’t know Changqing is bringing forth his own understanding, saying “they’ve grown.”
Each has a place where it comes forth. Let me ask you: what place is the place of arising?
It is just like, right before a true adept, the Vajra King’s jeweled sword is used immediately, straightaway.
If you can smash the usual run of views and cut off gain and loss, right and wrong,
then you will see where Changqing and he exchange. Yunmen said, “Barrier.” Unusual enough, but hard to take up.
Master Yunmen often taught people with one-character Zen. Yet within a single character, it must contain
three lines. Look at the ancients: responding on the spot, trading back and forth, naturally far removed from people today.
This is the pattern of the lower line. Though he spoke like this, his meaning is certainly not
there. Since it is not there, tell me: where is it? You must
investigate it carefully for yourself. If you are a clear-eyed person, you have hands
and feet that illumine heaven and earth; at once you are crystal-clear in all directions. For that one word “barrier,” Xuedou
strung it together with the other three and brought it out as a single verse.

lg.l:
Cuiyan instructs his disciples: for a thousand ages, no match.This old thief ruins other people’s sons and daughters.Out of a thousand, ten thousand, there is still one or half of one.
Divide off one section.

With “barrier” they answer each other; losing money, they incurIf you do not believe it, it is not without its strangeness. Only if you are such a person can you speak like this.
guilt.Swallowing breath and gulping down sound, Xuedou is no small thing. Answer in the same voice and strike.

Baofu, down and out.Fellow travelers on the Way still make these comings and goings. Two, three
of them.

Hard to get the downplay and the praise; nagging, nagging—CuiLet go or hold fast, who lives and dies together? Do not slander him. It is fortunate there is no involvement.
yan is plainly a thief.This wild-fox demon should shut his mouth.Saying it is no harm. You have been caught and defeated too.

White jade, without blemish.
Can you still discern it? People under heaven do not know the price.

Who can tell true from false? ChangqingMost of it is only fake. This mountain monk has never had eyes. The blue-eyed barbarian monk
knows it intimately.is an expert recognizing an expert; it has to be him. Not even half is gained.

Eyebrows have grown.Where is it? From the crown of the head down to the
heels, there is not a single blade of grass.

cb:div.p:
If Xuedou were not so compassionate, versing it to let people see, how would he gain the name of a good
spiritual friend? The ancients were like this—each and every case was forced by circumstances, because later
students cling to their words and turn them into emotional interpretations, and so they do not see the ancients’ intent.
If now someone suddenly comes out, flips over the meditation bench, and shouts the assembly apart, you cannot blame him.
Even so, you must truly reach this ground. Xuedou
says, “For a thousand ages, no match.” He only says: Look—are Cuiyan’s eyebrows still there? What
what is so special about it? And yet it becomes for a thousand ages, without a match. You must know the ancients, when they spoke a single word,
or half a phrase, it is not rash; they must have eyes that can settle heaven and earth.
When Xuedou sets down a word or half a phrase, it is like the Vajra King’s jeweled sword, like a lion crouched
on the ground; like striking stone for sparks, like a flash of lightning. If you do not have an eye in the crown of the head,
how could you see where the ancients land? This instruction to the assembly truly deserves “for a thousand ages,
no match,” surpassing Deshan’s staff and Linji’s shout. Tell me: where is Xuedou’s intent in helping people?
Where is it? And how do you understand his saying “for a thousand ages, no match”? “Barrier”
answers “barrier”; losing money, incurring guilt—what is the meaning of this? Even if you have eyes that penetrate the barrier,
when you get here you still must be careful. Tell me: is it Cuiyan
who loses money and incurs guilt? Is it Xuedou? Is it Yunmen?
If you penetrate it, I’ll grant you have eyes. “Baofu, down and out—hard to get the downplay and the praise.”
He puts himself down and lifts up the ancients. Tell me: where does Baofu put down, and
where does he lift up? “Nagging, nagging—Cuiyan is plainly a thief.” Tell me: what
did he steal? Yet Xuedou calls him a thief. On no account follow the drift of his phrasing and get
turned around by it. Here you must have your own grip. “White jade, without blemish”—the verse
praises Cuiyan as very much like flawless white jade, without the slightest speck. Who can tell true from false?
It can be said that few can discern it. Xuedou has great talent, so from beginning to
end he threads it into one string; only at the very end does he say, “Changqing knows it intimately; eyebrows have grown.”
Tell me: where is this “grown”? Fix your eyes and look fast.

The appended instruction says: A bright mirror on the stand—beauty and ugliness distinguish themselves. Moye in hand—killing
and giving life in the moment. Han go, Hu come; Hu come, Han go. In death you gain life; in life
you gain death. Tell me: when it comes to this, how is it? If you do not have the eyes that penetrate the barrier
and the place to turn your body, then when you get here you are plainly helpless. Tell me: what are the eyes that penetrate
the barrier, and where is the place to turn your body? Try to raise it and see.

[9] A monk asked Zhaozhou, “What is Zhaozhou?” Zhaozhou said, “East gate, west gate, south gate, north gate.”Hebei, Henan, you cannot speak of it at all. There are thorns in rotten mud.
It is not in Henan; it is right in Hebei.Open. In trading insults I will allow you
to answer back. In spitting at each other I will allow you to splash water. A ready-made case, do you still see it? Then strike.

In general, to investigate Zen and ask about the Way is to clearly examine yourself. Absolutely avoid picking and choosing phrases.
Why? Do you not see Zhaozhou say, “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes picking and choosing”?
And do you not see Yunmen say, “Nowadays these Zen fellows—three or five gather heads together, muttering
and muttering—and then say, ‘This is a phrase for top talent; that is a phrase struck out right from the body
itself.’” They do not know that in the ancients’ gate of skillful means, for beginners and later learners,
when the mind-ground is not yet clear and the original nature not yet seen, they were forced to set up expedient phrases
and sentences—like “The patriarch came from the West, transmitting only the mind seal; directly pointing to the human mind;
seeing nature, becoming Buddha.” There is no such tangle there. You must cut off language and see the truth beyond it.
If you can break through and cast it off, it is like a dragon gaining water, like a tiger leaning on a mountain. Those who have long studied with former
worthies may have insight but not penetration, penetration but not clarity—this is called requesting instruction. If you have seen through and request instruction,
then you can still circle around with phrases, with no sticking. Long study and
requesting instruction is like handing a ladder to a thief. In truth, this matter is not in phrases at all. Therefore
Yunmen said, “If this matter were in words and phrases, would the Three Vehicles and the twelve divisions of teachings
be without words and phrases? Why would Bodhidharma need to come from the West?” Among Fenyang’s eighteen questions, this
question is called a “testing-the-host question,” also called a “probing-and-pulling question.” This monk posed a question
that is unusual enough. If it were not Zhaozhou, it would be hard to answer him. This
monk asked, “What is Zhaozhou?” Zhaozhou, being an authentic adept, immediately said,
“East gate, west gate, south gate, north gate.” The monk said, “I am not asking about this Zhaozhou.”
Zhaozhou said, “Then which Zhaozhou are you asking about?” Later people call this “nothing-to-do Zen,” cheating
plenty of people. Why is it that he asked about Zhaozhou, and Zhaozhou answered, “East gate, west gate, south
gate, north gate”? Thus he answered only “Zhaozhou” to him. If you understand it like this, you village
bumpkin, you are even more “understanding the Buddha-dharma.” This alone is precisely the destruction of the Buddha-dharma.
It is like comparing a fish eye to a bright pearl: it seems similar, but it is not. This mountain monk
says, “Not in Henan—right in Hebei.” Tell me: is it having affairs, or no affairs?
You must be careful. Master Yuanlu said, “Only the final sentence finally reaches
the solid barrier. The intent of pointing south is not in verbal explanation. One wind every ten days, one rain every five days;
the country is at peace, people enjoy their work, bellies full, singing—this is called a time of great peace; it is called
‘no affairs.’ It is not slapping at the blind and then saying ‘no affairs.’ You must pass through the critical hinge.
You must get out of the thicket of thorns—clean, naked, bare—and still just like an ordinary person.
Then whether you have affairs is fine, whether you have no affairs is fine. Roaming in every direction, you never cling
to “no” or “yes.” Some people say, “Originally there is not a speck of anything; one only
meets tea and drinks tea, meets food and eats food.” This is a great lie—called saying you have attained when you have not attained,
saying you are verified when you are not verified. In truth they have never investigated through. When they see people speak of mind, speak
of nature, speak of the mysterious and the subtle, they say it is all wild talk—originally nothing is the matter. This can be called
one blind man leading a crowd of blind. They do not know that before the patriarch came, where was there calling heaven
earth, calling mountains water? Why then did the patriarch come again from the West? Everywhere
they ascend the hall and enter the room—what are they saying? It is all emotional consciousness and calculation. If it is emotional
consciousness and calculation, only when feelings are exhausted do you see through. If you see through, then still heaven is
heaven, earth is earth, mountains are mountains, water is water. The ancients said, “Mind is
the root; dharmas are dust—these two are like smudges on a mirror.” When you reach this ground, naturally
you are clean, naked, bare. But if you push it to the extreme in doctrine and argument, it is still not a secure
place. When you get here, many people misunderstand and get stuck in the realm of “no affairs”: they do not bow to Buddha,
they do not burn incense. It seems similar, but it is not casting off the body. As soon as they are
questioned, it is only “extremely similar”; as soon as they are pressed, they split into pieces, sitting in
empty-bellied, high-mindedness. When it comes to the thirtieth day of the twelfth month, they switch hands and pound their chests—already
too late. This monk asked like that; Zhaozhou answered like that. Tell me: how are you
to grope for it? Like that won’t do; not like that won’t do either. Ultimately,
how is it? This is the hard part. That is why Xuedou picked it up and, right in your face,
Instructing the assembly. One day as Zhaozhou sat, the attendant reported, “The Great King has arrived.” Zhaozhou—
—startled, said, “Ten thousand blessings to the Great King.” The attendant said, “The master, he has not yet arrived.” Zhaozhou
said, “And yet you said he has arrived.” When you investigate to this point, when you see to this point, it’s no harm if it seems strange
and exceptional. Master Nanquan picked it up and said, “The attendant only knows how to announce a guest; he doesn’t know he is in the imperial
capital. Zhaozhou enters the weeds to look for a man, and without noticing is covered in mud and water.” These real
points—do you all understand them? Look at Xuedou’s verse.

lg.l:
Within a phrase, the pivot is presented—coming straight at you, the vajra eyeSound. When fish move, the water is muddy. Do not slander Zhaozhou.
free of the tiniest speck of dust.Scattering sand and scattering dirt, do not drag Zhaozhou down. Scooping the sky and groping the earth, what are you doing?

East, west, south, north—the gates face
each other.Open. Where are there so many gates? Turning your back on Zhaozhou’s city, where are you going?

Boundless hammers strike, yet cannot break it open.
It is that your hammering wheel cannot reach. Open.

cb:div.p:
When Zhaozhou meets the moment, he is like the jeweled sword of the Vajra King: the instant you form an opinion, he cuts off your
head. Often, right before your face, he even swaps out your eyes. This monk also dared to stroke the tiger’s
whiskers and bring up a question—very much like making trouble out of nothing. But the phrase contains a pivot.
Since he came presenting the pivot, Zhaozhou did not fail his question. So he also
presented the pivot in reply. It wasn’t that he did this on purpose; rather, a person who has seen through to the bottom naturally
fits the track, as if it had been arranged that way. Haven’t you seen the non-Buddhist ascetic who, holding
a sparrow in his hand, came and asked the World-Honored One, “Tell me, the sparrow in my hand—is it dead
or alive?” The World-Honored One then straddled the threshold and said, “Tell me—am I going out, or coming in,
or what?” The ascetic had no words, and then bowed. This story isOne version says: The World-Honored One raised his fist and said: Open and close.
like this case. The ancients’ bloodline was unbroken, so it is said: the question is in the answer,
and the answer is in the question. Xuedou saw through like this, and so he said, “Within a phrase
the pivot is presented—coming straight at you.” The phrase contains a pivot, as if carrying two meanings; it is also like questioning a person,
and also like questioning circumstances. Zhaozhou did not shift a hair’s breadth; he simply told him,
“East gate, west gate, south gate, north gate.” The vajra eye is free of the tiniest speck of dust. This
verse shows Zhaozhou taking away both person and circumstances. In the phrase he presented the pivot and answered him. This is what is called
having both pivot and circumstance. The moment you turn, it shines through and shatters his heart and gall. If it were not like this,
it would be hard to stop his question. “Shuokaluo eye” is Sanskrit; here it means “firm
eye,” also called the “vajra eye.” It illuminates and sees without obstruction—not only seeing a thousand miles, discerning the finest hair,
but also settling what is crooked and deciding what is right, distinguishing gain and loss, sorting the apt moment, recognizing fortune and misfortune. Xue-
dou said, “East, west, south, north—the gates face each other. Boundless hammers strike, yet cannot break it open.” Since
there are boundless hammers, why can it not be broken open? It is simply that Xuedou’s seeing is
like this. And you people—how will you manage to get this gate to open? Please examine it thoroughly
and look.

The instruction says: “So it is, so it is; not so, not so. If we speak of battle,
each stands at the turning point.” Therefore it is said: if you turn upward, you will make even
Shakyamuni and Maitreya, Manjushri and Samantabhadra—thousands of sages, ten-thousands of sages, and the lineage masters under heaven—all
swallow their breath and fall silent. If you turn downward, vinegar flies and gnats, wriggling beings with spirit,
each and every one emits great light; each and every one stands like a sheer wall ten thousand fathoms high. If you neither go up nor
down, then how will you discuss it? If there is a rope, grab the rope; if there is no rope, grab whatever you can. Try raising it
and see.

Case ten. Brought up: Muzhou asked a monk, “Where have you just come from?” The monk gave a shout. Muzhou said, “This old monk has been shouted at by you once.” The monkProbing with a pole, stirring the grass.An adept Zen guest, for now
do not pretend to be clear-headed and still go on like this.A device for trapping a tiger. What can a gibbon-man do?
shouted again. Muzhou said, “After three shouts, four shouts—Look at the horns and crest. It seems like it, but it is not yet it. I only fear a dragon head with a snake tail.
how will it be?” The monk had no words. Muzhou thenWaves against the current. Not a single person has ever gotten his head out. Where do you go in?Truly you cannot find it by groping.
struck him, saying, “You empty-headed fellow!”If Muzhou carried out the command to the full, all the grasses and trees across the great earth would be cut into three pieces.Let one move pass, and it falls
into the second.

In general, to uphold the teaching of the school, there must be a true lineage master’s eye, and
a true lineage master’s functioning. Muzhou’s edge and pivot are like lightning. He loved to test meditation-hall
leaders. In everyday talk, a word or half a phrase from him was like a thicket of thorns—you cannot set down a foot
or a hand. As soon as he saw a monk come, he would say, “A ready-made case!” and let fly thirty
blows. Again, seeing a monk, he would say, “Senior monk!” When the monk turned his head, Muzhou would say, “You plank-headed fellow!”
He also instructed the assembly, saying, “If you don’t have a way in, you must get a way in. Once
you’ve got a way in, don’t fail this old monk.” Muzhou often dealt with people like
this. This monk, too, was good at carving and polishing—yet it became a dragon head with a snake tail. If at that time it had not
been Muzhou, he might have been thrown into confusion by him. Just consider: he asked, “Where have you just come
from?” and the monk immediately shouted. Tell me—what was his intent? This old fellow was not
in a hurry; slowly he said to him, “This old monk has been shouted at by you once.” It seems like he accepted the words
and set them aside; it also seems like he was testing him—leaning aside to watch what he would do. This
monk shouted again. It seems like it, and yet it is not. This old fellow has already pierced his
nostrils. So he asked, “After three shouts, four shouts—how will it be?” This monk indeed
had no words. Muzhou then struck him, saying, “You empty-headed fellow!” The decisive point in testing someone:
as soon as you open your mouth, you know whether it’s in tune. What a pity. Because this monk had no words, it provoked Muzhou to call him
an empty-headed fellow. If it were you people, when Muzhou said, “After three shouts, four shouts—
how will it be?” how should you respond to avoid being called an empty-headed fellow?
Here, if you recognize life and death, distinguish fortune and misfortune, and are a fellow with feet planted on solid ground—who cares
about “after three shouts, four shouts—how will it be”? It was only because this monk had no words that this old
fellow could seize the charge and close the case. Listen to Xuedou’s verse:

lg.l:
Two shouts and three shouts—the author knows the pivot;Thunder roars mightily, yet there is not a drop of rain. From ancient times to now, rarely has anyone been like this.
he can adapt and change.If you are not an expert, how could you verify it? I only fear it is not like this.

If you say it is riding the tiger’s head,Blind fellow. How do you ride a tiger’s head? How many people
understand it like this, and there are also people who make this kind of understanding.

both become blind men—who is the blindTrue words come from the true mouth, not only two of them. Take it yourself and go out.
man? Take it up and show it to the world.Who can be made to discern it? Luckily there is the final line. But it goes so far as to cheat people to death.Seeing, it is not absent. Looking
at it and you go blind. If the venerable fixes his eyes to look, then both hands beat empty space. Raised like this, tell me, which mechanism is it?

cb:div.p:
Xuedou is not without a way of guiding people. If you are not a true hand, it is just random shouting
and chaotic shouting. Therefore the ancients said: sometimes one shout does not function as a shout; sometimes
one shout does function as a shout. Sometimes one shout is like a lion crouching on the ground.
Sometimes one shout is like the jeweled sword of the Vajra King. Xinghua said, “I see you people—
you shout under the east corridor, you shout under the west corridor. Don’t just shout randomly and shout chaotically.
Even if you shouted Xinghua up to the Thirty-Three Heavens, when he fell back down there would not be
a trace of breath left. When I wake up again, I’ll tell you: not yet. Why?
Xinghua has never scattered real pearls inside the purple gauze curtain for you people.”
Why do you keep on shouting randomly and shouting chaotically? Linji said, “I hear you all always
imitate my shout. Let me ask you: a monk comes out from the east hall, a monk comes out from the west hall.
The two together shout. Which one is guest? Which one is host? If you cannot distinguish guest
and host, then from now on do not imitate this old monk.” Therefore Xuedou’s verse says, “The author
knows the pivot and adaptation.” Though this monk was taken in by Muzhou, he still had a place where he recognized the pivot and could adapt.
Tell me—what place was it where this monk recognized the pivot and could adapt? Master Zhichan of Lumen
pointed at this monk and said, “One who knows the Dharma is fearful.” Yantou said, “If we speak of battle,
each stands at the turning point.” Master Huanglong Xin said, “When pressed, then change; when you change,
then it flows through.” This right here is where the ancestral teachers sit and cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven. If you
recognize the pivot and adaptation, once it is raised you immediately know where it lands. There are fellows who say, “Why bother with talk of
three shouts and four shouts? Just keep shouting. What’s this about thirty
or twenty shouts? Shout until Maitreya Buddha descends—this is called riding the tiger’s head.” If
you have this kind of view, not knowing Muzhou is expected. But to see this monk, you are still far
away. If someone rides the tiger’s head, he must have a knife in his hand, and also have the ability to turn and change—only then
will it do. Xuedou said, “If it’s like that, both become blind men.” Xuedou is like a sword that leans against the sky,
a long blade—cold and fierce in its full power. If you grasp Xuedou’s meaning, then naturally in a thousand places, ten thousand
places, all at once you understand. Then you see that Xuedou’s later verse is merely adding footnotes.
Again he says, “Who is the blind man?” Tell me—is the guest side blind, or the host side blind? Or are guest and host
blind at the same time? “Take it up and show it to the world”—this is the living spot.
Xuedou already versified it all at once—so why does he then say, “Take it up and show it to the world”?
Tell me—how do you look? With eyes open, it hits; with eyes closed, it hits. Still—
Can anyone avoid it?

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume One, by the Chan Master Foguo YuanwuEnd.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Jiaoshan Chan Master Wuai’s Memorial on Subduing Demons
cb:div.head: Jiaoshan Chan Master Wuai’s Memorial on Subduing Demons
cb:div.p:
I have heard: the roads of the three vehicles are broad; the dharma-realm is boundless. The sea of wisdom lies calm and clear; the ten directions are at peace,
secure and settled. Yet at times demonic armies rise up, invading and troubling the mind-field. When the six thieves grow strong, the mind-king
is shaken. In the morning a hundred oddities are born; by evening a thousand evils arise. They jolt and confuse suchness, and weary the dharma-
body. The road of bodhi is cut off and blocked. Nirvana is ruined; the Three
Jewels are harmed. The priceless pearls and jades of non-doing are all stolen away; the dharma-treasure of the great storehouse is all plundered
and seized. Dust and toil eclipse the sun; the fire of desire spans the sky. The city of Dharma is tossed about; the holy
realm is set ablaze. Seeing such violence and chaos, I fear the Buddha-Dharma will be hard to preserve. So I conferred with the six
paramitas, and together we moved to cut them down and wipe them out. I dispatched Nature-Emptiness as a secret envoy to spy
on the demon host. Now they are encamped on Five-Aggregates Mountain, with more than eighty-four thousand troops.
Once their strength and posture were known, the plan was settled in an instant. Then I mustered the mighty soldiers of the eighteen realms, and all
raised the banner of Body-Emptiness. Every person had unobstructed power; each one carried courageous strength
and ability. A straight mind was the work of seeing nature; one true thing swept away the turmoil of a hundred evils. We donned sturdy
armor, took up the clang of samadhi, wisdom-arrows and Chan-bows, and the bright sword of insight. We drilled at the gates of the Great Vehicle,
trained within its doorway. We made camp on Quiescent-Extinction Mountain, raised our flags on Three-Lucidities Ridge, and along the Eight
Right Path we arrayed our ranks. We appointed Great-Awakening Nature as the commander for taking prisoners, roaming through the four
directions to seek out the tracks of delusion and to cut off the traces of ignorance. We further had the King of Compassion
smash the stronghold of the three poisons; the commander Forbearance assaulted the city of anger; the army of Diligence
eliminated the monster of arrogance; the soldiers of Joyful Giving captured the bandits of stinginess and greed. Before long the demon
host surged up, murderous energy shooting to the heavens. I then led the maha host, and all at once we charged in together.
At that time, the eyes did not look at forms; the ears did not listen to sounds; the nose did not smell fragrances; the tongue
did not register tastes; the body did not receive touch; the mind did not cling to conditions. With one aim we pressed forward, thought after thought
not retreating. In a flash the demon army was utterly defeated; the six thieves lost completely. The slaughter was without limit.
We swept them away and cleared them out. We captured Delusive Thought alive, seized Ignorance living, and led them to the field of nirvana,
where with the sword of wisdom we cut them into three pieces. The forest of afflictions was crushed on the spot; the mountain of self and other
turned into fine dust. The net of foolish craving was burned by the fire of wisdom; the forest of wrong views was
dried up by the wind of insight. Thus the three clarities shone again; the four wisdoms became whole once more. Within and without, there were no
flaws; vast and pure. The mind-king sat in the hall of joy; suchness ascended the tower of liberation;
self-nature roamed the unobstructed hall; the three bodies took their seat upon the throne of dharma-emptiness. From
here the dharma-realm was tranquil, forever severed from clamor and dust. Together we crossed the river of birth and death, and all arrived at
the shore of bodhi. With the demon host now in retreat, I submit this report in full.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Two, by the Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu

cb:div.p:
Raised for instruction: the great pivotal function of Buddhas and Ancestors returns entirely to the grasp; the lifeline of humans and devas is wholly subject to
a single point and call. A casual phrase, a casual word, startles the crowd and stirs the multitude. One device, one scene—smash
the lock and strike the cangue. To meet the upward-going pivot, to raise the upward-going matter—tell me, what person has ever
come like this? Is there anyone who knows where it lands? Try bringing it up and see.

[11] Raised: Huangbo addressed the assembly, saying, You allStriking water blocked by a basin, swallowing it all in one gulp. Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out.
are nothing but dregs-drinking fellows. Traveling on pilgrimage like this,Speaking it, you wear through straw sandals, overturn heaven and shake earth.
where could you have today? Do you know that in the Great Tang there are noWhat use is today for? It is not a problem to startle the crowd and stir the masses.
Chan masters? At that time a monk came forth and said, If so,This old monk does not understand. Swallowing it all in one gulp is also a Yunju arhat.
what about those in the various regions who guide disciples and lead communities—what are they? HuangboIt would also be good to give one press. Facing the moment, you cannot help but be like this.
said, I do not say there is no Chan; I only say there are no teachers.It ends up impossible to sort close from distant. Tiles collapse, ice melts away. A dragon-head, snake-tail fellow

Huangbo was seven chi tall, with a round jewel on his forehead. By nature he understood Chan. In former times the master traveled
to Tiantai. On the road he met a monk and talked and laughed with him as if they were old acquaintances. Looking closely at him,
his gaze shot into people; he had a rather unusual appearance. So they went along together. When the stream suddenly flooded, he
planted his staff, tossed aside his hat, and stopped. That monk urged the master to cross with him. The master said, Please, cross. He
then hitched up his robe and stepped on the waves as if walking on level ground. Turning back, he called, Cross over, cross over. The master
shouted, You self-satisfied fellow! I knew long ago you were making trouble—I ought to have chopped your shins! That monk
sighed, A true vessel of the Great Vehicle Dharma! When he finished speaking, he vanished. When Huangbo first arrived at Baizhang, Baizhang asked
saying, Majestic and upright—where do you come from? Huangbo said, Majestic and upright, I come from
within the mountains. Baizhang said, And why have you come? Huangbo said, For no other matter. Baizhang
valued him deeply. The next day he took leave of Baizhang. Baizhang said, Where are you going? Huangbo
said, To Jiangxi to pay respects to Master Ma. Baizhang said, Master Ma has already passed away.
Tell me: when Huangbo asked like that, did he ask knowing, or did he ask not knowing?
Then he said, I specifically went to pay respects, but my blessings and conditions were shallow—I did not make it in time for even one
meeting. What were his usual sayings? I wish to hear you raise and show them. Baizhang then brought up again
the circumstances of his second interview with Mazu: The Patriarch saw me come and immediately held up a fly-whisk. I asked
saying, Use it right here, or apart from this use? The Patriarch then hung the fly-whisk on the corner of the Chan bed for a long
while. Then the Patriarch asked me, Later, when you beat the two pieces of hide, how will you teach people? I
took the fly-whisk and held it up. The Patriarch said, Use it right here, or apart from this use? I took the fly-
whisk and hung it on the corner of the Chan bed. The Patriarch shook the assembly with a single shout. At that time, for three days
I was deaf. Huangbo, without realizing it, shuddered and stuck out his tongue. Baizhang said, Later, will you not succeed
Master Ma? Huangbo said, No. Today, because you raised this, I got to see
Master Ma’s great pivot and great function. If I were to succeed Master Ma, in later days I would lose my
children and grandchildren. Baizhang said, So it is, so it is. If your seeing is equal to the master’s, you diminish him by half a virtue.
Only when your wisdom surpasses the master are you fit to receive the transmission. Your present seeing truly has something that goes beyond the master.
Now tell me, everyone: when Huangbo asked like that, did he ask knowing and therefore ask on purpose, or did he
ask because he did not know? You must personally see the conduct and footsteps of that father and son to
get it. One day Huangbo again asked Baizhang, In the lineage of the ancestors from the beginning, how is it pointed out?
Baizhang was silent for a long while. Huangbo said, You must not let later people cut it off. Baizhang said,
I thought you were someone. And then he rose and went into the abbot’s quarters. Huangbo and the Prime Minister Pei
were friends outside the world. When Pei was governor of Wanling, he invited the master to the prefecture and presented a volume of his understanding.
He showed it to the master. The master took it and set it on the seat, not even opening it. After a long while he said, Do you understand?
Pei said, I do not. Huangbo said, If you understood it just like that, it would still be short by a bit.
If it takes form in paper and ink, where is there room for our school? Pei then used a verse
to praise him, saying, Since the Great Being transmitted the mind-seal, a round jewel on the forehead, a body seven chi tall. Carrying
his staff, he dwelt by the waters of Shu for ten years; floating a cup, today he crossed to the banks of Zhang. Eight thousand dragons and elephants
follow his lofty steps; for ten thousand li, incense and flowers form splendid causes. I would like to serve the master as a disciple,
but I do not know to whom the Dharma will be entrusted. The master showed no joy and said, The mind is like the great sea,
without boundary. The mouth spits red lotus to nourish the sick body. I have a pair of hands with nothing to do,
and I have never merely bowed to ordinary people. After Huangbo took up residence, his edge was steep and severe. Linji was in
the assembly; Muzhou was head seat. He asked, You have been here a long time—why don’t you
go ask for a word? Linji said, What should you have me ask? The head seat said, Why
not go ask, What is the true great meaning of the Buddha-Dharma? Linji then went to ask. Three
times he was beaten and driven out. Linji said to the head seat, Thanks to you ordering me to ask three times, I was
beaten and driven out. I fear my conditions are not here; I will go down the mountain for now. The head seat said, If you
go, you must first take leave of the master. The head seat went ahead and reported to Huangbo, saying, That monk who goes to ask
questions is quite something. Master, why not carve and drill him into a single tree
to give later people shade? Huangbo said, I already know. Linji came to take leave. Huangbo
said, You must not go elsewhere. Go straight to the Gao’an riverbank and see Dayu.
Linji reached Dayu and brought up the earlier matter. I do not know where my fault lies.
Dayu said, Huangbo has such an old-woman’s anxious heart—he has gone to the end of hardship for you. What more is there to say
about fault or no fault? Linji suddenly had a great awakening and said, Huangbo’s Buddha-Dharma is not much.
Dayu grabbed him and said, Just now you said there was fault, and now you
say the Buddha-Dharma is not much—how is that? Linji struck three punches under Dayu’s ribs. Dayu pushed him away
and said, Your teacher Huangbo is none of my business. One day Huangbo addressed the assembly, saying, Oxen
Master Tou Rong, talking horizontally and talking vertically, still did not know where the pivotal turning point of the higher way lies.
At that time, under Shitou and Mazu, the Zen fellows in great numbers talked of Zen and talked of the Way.
Why did he speak like this? So he instructed the assembly, saying: All of you people are entirely
dregs-eating drunks. Wandering about like this, you make yourselves a laughingstock. Whenever you see a place with eight hundred
or a thousand people, you head right there. Do not just go looking for excitement. In all cases it is just like
you taking it this easily—where else would there be what is happening today? In the Tang there was one who loved to scold
people, calling them dregs-eating drunks. Many called him Huangbo the scolder. Those with eyes
see for themselves where he lands. In the main he dangled a single hook, fishing for someone to ask. In the crowd there was
a Zen fellow who did not begrudge his body and life; he knew to step out and ask him like this, saying: Just
like the leaders who guide followers in the various quarters—how is it then? A fine squeeze indeed. This old
fellow truly did not hold back in the least; he let it slip and said: It is not that there is no Zen; it is only
that there are no teachers. Now tell me—where is his intent? His fundamental principle has, at times,
capture; at times release; at times kill; at times enliven; at times let go; at times gather in.
I dare ask you all: what counts as a teacher within Zen? This mountain monk speaking like this has already
made a muddle of it. Where are your nostrils? After a long pause
he said: They have been pierced through.

lg.l:
Bracing, solitary wind—he does not boast; seated upright across the seas, he settlesstill does not even know it exists. He too is a Yunju arhat.
dragons and snakes.One must distinguish monk and lay; one must have black and white clearly separated.

The Tianzi of Dazhong once brushed against him lightly;What are you saying about the Son of Heaven of Dazhong bearing
greatness? Even the great must rise from the ground. If you go higher, what can you do about there being heaven?

three times he personally suffered the play of claws and fangs.What is a dead toad doing with so many mouths? It is not yet strange
enough; it is still a small trick. If the great pivot and great function appear right before you, the entire ten-direction world, even mountains, rivers, and the great earth, would all go to Huangbo to beg for their lives.

cb:div.p:
This single verse by Xuedou is like Huangbo’s true portrait. Yet people must not take it as
a literal portrait. Under his lines there is a way out. He plainly says: Bracing,
solitary wind does not boast. When Huangbo instructed the assembly like this, it was not to contend that others owed
him anything, nor to show off and brag. If you understand this news, then let it be seven releases and eight sideways moves as you please.
Sometimes standing alone on the peak of a solitary mountain; sometimes lying crosswise in the noisy marketplace. How could one narrowly
guard one corner? The more you discard, the less you stop; the more you seek, the less you find; the more you shoulder and carry, the more
you drown. The ancients said: wingless, it flies through the world; having a name, it is passed on in the world. Completely
cast off Buddha-Dharma doctrines, the abstruse, the marvelous, the strange—set it all down at once, and it is better by a bit
then. Naturally, wherever you touch it, it is already there. Xuedou said: seated upright across the seas, he settles dragons and snakes.
Dragon or snake—when it enters the gate, test it at once. This is called the eyes that settle dragons and snakes, and the knack that seizes
tigers and rhinoceroses. Xuedou also said: settling dragons and snakes—how straight those eyes; seizing tigers and rhinoceroses—
the mechanism is not complete. He also said: the Tianzi of Dazhong once brushed against him lightly; three times he personally suffered
the play of claws and fangs. Huangbo was no crude hack like people today—he has always been like this. As for the
Tianzi of Dazhong, the Continuation of the Xiantong Record relates that Tang Xianzong had two sons. One
was called Muzong; one was called Xuanzong. Xuanzong was Dazhong. At thirteen years old, as a youth
he was quick and clever, and often loved to sit cross-legged. When Muzong was on the throne, after the morning court had ended,
Dazhong playfully climbed onto the dragon throne and made gestures of greeting to the assembled ministers. The great ministers saw it and called
it a madness of the heart, and reported it to Muzong. Muzong saw it and sighed with admiration, saying: My younger brother is
the outstanding scion of our line. In the fourth year of Changqing, Muzong passed away. He had three sons,
called Jingzong, Wenzong, and Wuzong. Jingzong succeeded his father; within two years palace eunuchs plotted
to replace him. Wenzong succeeded and reigned fourteen years. Wuzong then ascended the throne and often called
Dazhong a foolish slave. One day Wuzong resented that Dazhong had once playfully climbed his father’s throne,
and had him beaten to death and thrown into the rear garden. They poured filth over him, and he revived again. He then slipped away in secret
and was at the assembly of the master Xiangyan Xian. Later he shaved his head and became a novice, not yet having received full
precepts. Later, traveling with Zhixian, he arrived at Mount Lu. Because Zhixian inscribed a waterfall poem
that said: Piercing clouds and passing through stone, it does not refuse the toil; only at a far distance do you know its source is high. Xian recited
these two lines and pondered them for a long time, wanting to fish out his thread of speech and see how it went. Dazhong
continued: How could the stream be kept and held? In the end it returns to the great sea to become waves. Xian
then knew he was no ordinary person, and silently recognized him. Later, arriving at Yanguan’s assembly,
they invited Dazhong to serve as secretary. Huangbo was there as head seat. One day Huangbo
was bowing to the Buddha, and Dazhong saw him and asked: Not clinging to Buddha to seek, not clinging to Dharma to seek,
not clinging to the assembly to seek—when you bow, what are you seeking? Huangbo said: Not clinging to Buddha to seek, not
clinging to Dharma to seek, not clinging to the assembly to seek—always bow just like this. Dazhong said: What use is bowing
for? Huangbo then slapped him. Dazhong said: Too rough! Huangbo said: What sort of place is this,
to talk of rough or fine? Huangbo slapped him again. Later Dazhong succeeded to the throne and bestowed on Huang-
bo the title Rough-Practice Monk. When Minister Pei was at court, he later memorialized that he be granted the Chan title Duanji,
Master Duanji. Xuedou knew the source of his lineage and used it skillfully. Nowadays is there still anyone who will
play with claws and fangs? Then strike.

The instruction says: The knife that kills and the sword that gives life are the styles of antiquity, and also
the pivot of today. If we speak of killing, it does not harm a hair; if we speak of giving life, it loses
body and life. Therefore it is said: the single road upward is not transmitted by a thousand sages. Students toil
their bodies like monkeys grabbing at shadows. Now tell me—since it is not transmitted, why then are there
so many vine-entangled public cases? Those with eyes, try speaking.

Case twelve: A monk asked Dongshan, What is Buddha? Dongshan said: ThreeAn iron caltrop—patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out of it.
pounds of hemp.Blazing clear. A broken straw sandal. Point at the locust tree and curse the willow tree—taken as a weight on the scale.

How many people misunderstand this public case—truly hard to chew, with nowhere for you to get your teeth into it.
Why is it bland and tasteless? The ancients gave many answers about Buddha—some
said: the one in the hall; some said: the thirty-two marks; some said: the bamboo-staff whip from beneath Zhanglei Mountain—
and so on. But when it comes to Dongshan, he says: three pounds of hemp. It is enough to cut off the tongues of the
ancients. Many people take it as mere talk and say: at that time Dongshan was in the storeroom, weighing
hemp, and a monk asked, so he answered like this. Some say: Dongshan was asked east and answered
west. Some say: you are Buddha—why ask Buddha? So Dongshan answered by a roundabout
route. Dead men even have another kind of saying: this very three pounds of hemp is
Buddha. That at least is irrelevant. If you go hunting under Dongshan’s words like this,
even if you practice until Maitreya Buddha descends, you still will not have dreamed of it. Why? Words
are only vessels that carry the Way. People do not know the ancients’ intent and only keep seeking in the phrase
itself—what real nose or face is that? Have you not seen the ancients say: the Way is originally without words; because of words
the Way is revealed. When you see the Way, you forget the words. If you reach here, then return my first move to me
and only then is it right. This three pounds of hemp is like the single main road in Chang’an:
lifting a foot, setting a foot down—none of it is not it. This saying is the same kind as Yunmen’s rice-cake saying;
it is not out of place that it is hard to understand. The former master Wuzu composed a verse, saying: He cheap-sells the carrying-board
fellow; he posts a price, three pounds of hemp. For a thousand hundred years the goods have sat unsold; there is nowhere to put your whole body. You
just need to pack up the dust of feelings and the thoughts of mind, and the calculating of gain and loss, right and wrong, and in one moment make it all
clean—then naturally you will understand.

lg.l: The golden crow is swift, the jade rabbit is quick.The left eye weighs half a jin. Even a swift hawk cannot catch up. Throwing oneself sideways within flames.The right eye weighs eight liang. Making a nest in Chang’e’s palace.

Shanying—when was there ever a light brushing? Unfolding the matter and meeting the moment, he sees Dong-Like a bell when it is struck; like a valley receiving an echo.
shan.Mistaking the fixed star as certain—of course it is the acarya who sees it this way.

A lame softshell turtle and a blind tortoise enter an empty valley.You lead it out yourself; it is no different from being in the same pit.
of dirt. Who struck your hawk dead?

Flowers in clusters, brocade in clusters—southA double-layer case—take it over in one stroke, and it is still the same.
land bamboo, north land wood.There is also triple-layer. A quadruple-layer case—putting a head on top of a head.

Because he thought of Changqing and Lu
the Grand Master.A lazy child dragging companions. This mountain monk is like this too; Xuedou is like this too.

He understood and said: it fits to laugh, it does not fit to weep.Ha ha. Blue heaven.
At midnight, adding further grievance and bitterness.
Yi!Hah, what is this? Then he hits.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou saw through it, so right off he said: the golden crow is swift, the jade rabbit is quick.
It is no different from Dongshan’s answer, three pounds of hemp. Sunrise, moonset—day after day
it is thus. People mostly interpret it emotionally and keep saying: the golden crow is the left eye, the jade rabbit is
the right eye. As soon as they are questioned, they stare and say: what does that have to do with anything here?
If you understand it like this, Bodhidharma’s lineage is swept away entirely. Therefore it is said: dangling the hook
across the four seas, it only fishes for fierce dragons. The extraordinary mysterious mechanism is for seeking a true confidant. Xuedou
If one is a person who has stepped beyond the shadowy realms, how could one form views like this? Xuedou lightly
taps the gate and strikes the beat, revealing just a little for you to see. Then he adds a footnote
saying: Good responding has never been a light touch. Dongshan did not lightly answer this monk, like a bell
being struck; like a valley receiving an echo. Great or small, it responds accordingly, not daring to touch lightly. Xuedou, at once,
pulls out heart, liver, and the five viscera and presents it to you people. Xuedou has
a verse on stillness and good responding: Presented face to face, not in many complications. Dragons and snakes are easy
to tell apart; a patched-robed monk is hard to deceive. The golden hammer’s shadow moves; the jeweled sword’s gleam is cold. Come straight down
right here—quick, open your eyes and look. When Dongshan first visited Yunmen, Yunmen asked, Recently you left what
place? Dongshan said, Zha Crossing. Yunmen asked, Where did you spend the summer? Dongshan said, Baozi in Hunan,
Baoci. Yunmen asked, When did you leave there? Dongshan said, The twenty-fifth day of the eighth month.
Yunmen said, I’ll let you off with three rounds of blows—go to the hall. That evening the master entered the room; in close attendance,
he asked, Where exactly was my fault? Yunmen said, You rice-bag! From Jiangxi to Hu-
nan and you just go like that. At these words, Dongshan suddenly had a great awakening. Then he said, Someday I
will, in a place where there are no people, set up a small hut. I will not store a single grain of rice.
I will not plant a single stalk of vegetables. I will always receive the great good friends of the ten directions who come and go, and I will
pull out their nails, knock out their wedges, snatch off their grease-smeared caps, and strip off their rancid
cloth shirts, letting each one be clean and unencumbered, and go off as a person with nothing to do. Yunmen
said, With a body as big as a coconut, you open such a huge mouth! Dongshan then took his leave. At that time his
place of awakening was straight and outstanding—how could it be the same as a shallow view, later on, when he appeared in the world and responded
to circumstances with the saying, Three pounds of hemp. Teachers everywhere only take it as an answer about Buddha, as in What is Buddha?
A bamboo-whip under Zhanglin Mountain; the lads of Fire come seeking fire. They only go on
making reasoning around Buddha. Xuedou said, If you handle it like this, taking it as a display of skill and a meeting with the situation,
it’s exactly like a lame turtle and a blind tortoise entering an empty valley—what year and month will they ever find a way out?
Flower-clusters, brocade-clusters. This was when a monk asked Master Zhimen: What is the meaning of Dong-
shan’s saying, Three pounds of hemp? Zhimen said, Flower-clusters, brocade-clus-
ters. Understand? The monk said, I don’t. Zhimen said, Bamboo in the south, timber in the
north. The monk went back and told Dongshan. Dongshan said, I won’t explain it for you; I’ll explain it for the great
assembly. So he ascended the hall and said: Words do not display the matter; phrases do not fit the situation. Those who take up words
are lost; those who get stuck in phrases are deluded. Xuedou breaks people’s emotional views, intentionally drawing out a
string of verses. Later people instead turn around and give rise to emotional views, saying hemp is mourning clothes, bamboo is
the mourning staff. Therefore they say, Bamboo in the south, timber in the north; flower-clusters, brocade-clus-
ters—these are painted flowers and grasses by the head of the coffin. Do you still know shame? Yet they do not
know: Bamboo in the south, timber in the north, and three pounds of hemp are just like dad and
father—similar. When the ancients answered with a turning word, their meaning certainly was not like that. It is just
like Xuedou saying, The golden crow is urgent, the jade rabbit swift—by nature it is broadly open. It’s only
that brass and gold are hard to tell apart; “fish” and “Lu” get mixed up. Xuedou, with an old-woman’s anxious heart, wants to smash
your knot of doubt, and so he brings up another dead fellow. He recalls Minister Lu of Changqing, who could say
one should laugh, not cry. If we speak of his verse, it’s just the first three lines—at once it is versified
and done. Let me ask you: all in all it’s only three pounds of hemp, yet Xuedou has
so many entangling vines—he is simply far too compassionate, and so it is like this. Official Lu
Geng served as the Surveillance Commissioner of Xuanzhou and visited Nanquan. When Nanquan passed away, Lu heard the news and went into
the monastery to offer rites, but he laughed loudly. The rector said, The late master and you, sir, had
the bond of teacher and student—why don’t you cry? The official said, If you can speak it, I’ll cry. The rector had no
reply. Lu then wailed loudly, Blue heaven, blue heaven—my late master has been gone for so long! Later
Changqing heard and said, The official should laugh, not cry. Xuedou borrows this meaning and in broad
outline says: if you interpret it with this kind of sentiment, then it’s exactly right to laugh and not cry. “It is” is “it is.”
At the very end there is one word, not without its harshness—he even adds, Eh? Can Xuedou still wash
himself clean of it?

The pointer says: Clouds congeal over the great plain—throughout the whole realm there is nowhere to hide. Snow covers the reed-flowers—hard to distinguish
traces and tracks. Where it is cold, it is cold like ice and snow; where it is fine, it is fine like rice flour. In the deepest places,
even the Buddha-eye cannot peer; in the most hidden places, demons and outsiders cannot fathom. To raise one and illuminate three—set that aside
for now. Sit and cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven—how will you speak? Tell me: whose affair is this,
after all? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 13: A monk asked Baling, What is the tradition of Deva-A white horse enters reed-flowers—what are you saying about marks and
Baling said, Snow contained in a silver bowl.plugging up and choking your throat? Seven blossoms, eight splits.

In this gong’an, many people misunderstand and say, This is the teaching of outsiders—what
does it have to do with us? The fifteenth patriarch, Venerable Deva, was also once counted among the outsiders.
Because he met the fourteenth patriarch, Venerable Nagarjuna, and threw a needle into a bowl—Nagarjuna deeply
recognized his capacity, transmitted the Buddha-mind tradition, and he became the fifteenth patriarch. The Lankavatara Sutra says: The Buddha
takes mind as the principle; no-gate is the Dharma gate. Mazu said, Whatever has words and phrases is
Deva’s tradition. Take only this as the main point. You all are guests under the patched-robed monks’
gate—have you ever investigated Deva’s tradition? If you have investigated it, the ninety-six kinds of outside teachings in the West
are all subdued by you at once. If you have not investigated it, you won’t avoid
having to put on a robe backwards and go off. But tell me—how is it? If you say words and
phrases are it, that has nothing to do with it. If you say words and phrases are not it, that also has nothing to do with it. So
tell me: where was Master Ma’s meaning? Later Yunmen said, Master Ma
had fine words—only no one asked. A monk then asked, What is Deva’s
tradition? Yunmen said, Of the ninety-six kinds, you are the very lowest kind. Long ago a monk took leave of
Great Sui. Sui said, Where are you going? The monk said, I’m going to pay respects to Samantabhadra. Great
Sui raised his whisk and said, Manjushri and Samantabhadra are all right here. The monk drew a
circle-sign and held it up to present to the master, then tossed it behind his back. Sui said, Attendant, bring
a packet of tea and send this monk off. Yunmen also said, In the West they chop heads and cut
arms; here you take yourself out. He also said, The red banner is in my hands. In the West,
in debate the winner holds the red banner; the defeated puts on the robe backwards and enters and leaves by a side
gate. In the West, to debate one must receive a royal edict, in a great monastery.
They ring bells and beat drums, and then they debate. Thus the outsiders, in the monks’ monastery, sealed off
and forbade bells and drums, suppressing them. At that time Venerable Kanadeva knew the Buddha Dharma
was in trouble, so he used spiritual powers, climbed the tower and struck the bell, wishing to expel the outsiders. The outsiders then
asked, Who is the one sounding the bell up there? Deva said, Heaven. The outsiders said, Who is
Heaven? Deva said, Me. The outsiders said, Who am I? Deva said, I am you. The out-
siders said, Who are you? Deva said, You are a dog. The outsiders said, Who is the dog?
Deva said, The dog is you. Like this for seven exchanges, the outsiders themselves knew they were defeated, bowed in
submission, and then opened the gate themselves. Deva then came down from the tower holding the red banner. The out-
siders said, Why don’t you go behind? Deva said, Why don’t you go ahead? The outsiders said, You
are a low person. Deva said, You are a fine person. In this way they exchanged questions and answers back and forth. Deva
refuted them with unobstructed eloquence, and thus they submitted. At that time Venerable Deva held
the red banner; those who fell in meaning stood beneath the banner. The outsiders all offered their heads to apologize. Then De-
va stopped them, and instead transformed them, having them shave their heads and enter the Way. Thus Deva’s tradition greatly flourished.
Later Xuedou used this incident and versified it. Among Baling’s community he was called “Mirror-
Mouth.” He often sewed sitting-cloths and traveled on foot, deeply grasping Yunmen’s great matter beneath his heel.
That is why it is extraordinary. Later, when he appeared in the world, he was Dharma heir to Yunmen. First he resided at Baling in Yuezhou.
He did not even make a Dharma-heir letter; he only took three turning phrases up to Yunmen: What is
the Way? A clear-eyed person falls into a well. What is the hair-blowing sword? Coral branches, branch after branch, prop up
the moon. What is Deva’s tradition? Snow contained in a silver bowl. Yunmen said,
On the day of my memorial, just raise these three turning phrases—repaying kindness is enough. From
then on he indeed did not hold memorial feasts; following Yunmen’s instruction, he only raised these three
turning phrases. Yet teachers everywhere, answering these words, mostly answer on the level of things. Only Baling spoke like
this—utterly solitary and steep; it is not amiss that it is hard to understand, and it does not reveal the slightest edge or point.
Attacked from eight sides, each move has a way out; each move has a chance to trap a tiger. It strips away
human emotional views. If we speak of matters on the side of the one color, when you get here it must be personally penetrated
It is shed; only on meeting the right person can it be so. Thus it is said: Daowu danced his baton, meeting with his peers;
Shigong bent his bow, and those who act know it well. If this principle lacks a master’s seal of transmission,
what Dharma-words could you use for abstruse talk? Xuedou then lifted it up to guide people, and so he composed a verse
and brought it forth.

lg.l:
Old Xinkai, truly distinct.A thousand soldiers are easy to get; one general is hard to find. A talkative master—what is the real point? One move on the crown of the head—you have not even dreamed
it.

Understanding says: snow contained in a silver bowl—ninetyA shrimp cannot jump out of the measuring bucket. A double-layer case—how many people lose body and life
six should know of themselves.with their whole selves inside. Do you know, acarya? Buried in one pit,

If you do not know, then ask the sky’s-edge
moon.far, far away. You lead it out yourself and make an appeal to empty space.

The Devadatta school, the Devadatta school.What are you saying? This mountain monk is right here,
with a mouth full of frost.

Beneath the red banner a pure breeze arises.A hundred fragments. He strikes and says: You have already been hit. Now go and bring me beheading and severed arms.
I will tell you one line.

cb:div.p:
Old Xinkai—Xinkai is the name of a monastery. Truly distinct. Xuedou’s praise has
its share; but tell me, where is the distinct place? All language is Buddhadharma.
If this mountain monk speaks like this, what sense does it make? Xuedou slightly revealed a bit
of his intent, saying: it is simply truly distinct. Later he opens it up, saying: understanding says, a silver bowl
holding snow. I will add a footnote for you: ninety-six should know of themselves.
Only then can you bear the burden and fall into it. If you do not know, ask the sky’s-edge moon. The ancients once answered
this saying: ask the sky’s-edge moon. Xuedou then versified it; at the end there must be a living
road, with a line of the lion’s turning throw. He raises it again to tell you: the Devadatta school,
the Devadatta school—beneath the red banner a pure breeze arises. Baling said: snow contained in a silver bowl.
Why then does Xuedou say, beneath the red banner a pure breeze arises? Do you know
that Xuedou kills without using a knife?

[14] A monk asked Yunmen: What is the teaching of an entire lifetime? Yunmen said: Speaking in response to the One.To this very day it is not finished. The lecturer does not
understand. In the vine-tangle nest,a holeless iron hammer—seven blossoms, eight splits. An old rat bites raw ginger.

In the Zen school, if you wish to know the meaning of buddha-nature, you should observe time and conditions; this is called the teaching
of a separate transmission outside the scriptures: solely transmitting the mind-seal, directly pointing to the human mind, seeing nature and becoming Buddha. Shakyamuni,
the old fellow, lived in the world for forty-nine years, held three hundred sixty assemblies, and opened talks on sudden and gradual,
provisional and real—this is called the teaching of an entire lifetime. This monk picked it up and asked: what
is the teaching of an entire lifetime? Why didn’t Yunmen explain it to him in detail, but instead
said to him: Speaking in response to the One? In Yunmen’s usual single sentence, there must be three sentences:
the sentence that encloses heaven and earth; the sentence that follows the waves and chases the currents; the sentence that cuts off all streams.
Letting go and drawing in, it is naturally striking—like cutting nails and slicing iron—so that people’s interpretive understanding
cannot reckon it. The entire Tripitaka needs only three characters. In all directions,
on all sides, there is no place for your picking and drilling. Many people misunderstand it, saying it is a matter of responding to the One as a timely expedient,
and so it is spoken thus. Others say: the myriad phenomena and all appearances are all stamped by the One Dharma,
and this is called speaking in response to the One. Still others say: it is simply speaking of that One Dharma;
what does it have to do with anything? Not only do they not understand, they fall into hell like an arrow—utterly
unaware that the ancients’ intent was not like this. Hence it is said: grinding bones to powder and shattering the body is not enough to repay it;
one phrase, clearly understood, surpasses a hundred billion. It is indeed striking. What is the teaching of an entire lifetime?
You need only say: Speaking in response to the One. If you can realize it on the spot, you can go home
and sit securely. If you cannot realize it, then bow and listen for your assignment.

lg.l:
Speaking in response to the One is utterly solitary and cut off.Lively and quick. The words are still in the ear; not without solitary sharpness.Bystanders have a share. Not only like a sheer wall a thousand fathoms high—how could there be
such a thing?
