A holeless iron hammer drives the wedge hard.It is a mistaken understanding of fine words. Old Yunmen too is washing a lump of dirt in mud; Xuedou too
is decoration.
Beneath the Jambu tree he laughs, ha ha.Across four districts and eight counties, none can see such a fellow. Only those on the same road know—how many can know?

Last night the black dragon had its horn wrenched and snapped—distinct, distinct.Not only the black dragon is wrenched and broken—who has seen it? Is there any verification? Mute
praise has its share; it must be Xuedou. Where is there any other place

The old man of Shaoyang got a single peg.—in what place? Still
there is one stake—who will it be entrusted to? Even Deshan and Linji must retreat three thousand li. And that one stake—how is it? Then he hits.

cb:div.p:
Speaking in response to the One—utterly solitary and cut off. Xuedou cannot finish praising it. This saying alone escapes the solitary
and perilous, outshining the past and cutting off the future—like a ten-thousand-foot cliff, and also like a million-soldier
battle array, with no place for you to stand. It is just too solitary and perilous. The ancients said: if you want intimacy,
do not take a question to ask. The question is in the answer; the answer is at the tip of the question. It is utterly solitary
and steep. Tell me, where is this solitary steepness? People in the world cannot do anything about it.
This monk too was a capable one, and so he asked like this. Yunmen answered like that;
it is very much like a holeless iron hammer driving the wedge hard. Xuedou used literary phrasing:
he used it quite skillfully. Beneath the Jambu tree he laughs, ha ha. In the Sutra on the Arising of the World it says there must be
a vaidurya tree on the southern side of Mount Sumeru, casting its reflection so that all within Jambudvipa appears blue. This continent
is named after the great tree, and is called Jambudvipa. The tree extends seven thousand yojanas in length and breadth.
Below it is a mass of Jambu gold, twenty yojanas high; because the gold is produced from beneath the tree,
it is called the Jambu tree. Thus Xuedou speaks of himself: he is beneath the Jambu
tree laughing, ha ha. Tell me, what is he laughing at? He laughs that last night the black dragon
had its horn wrenched and snapped. One can only gaze and look up; praising Yunmen has its share. Yunmen
said: speaking in response to the One is like what? It is like wrenching and snapping one horn of a black dragon.
If there were not such a matter here, how could one speak like this? Xuedou, in one
go, finished the verse; at the end he says: distinct, distinct. The old man of Shaoyang got a single peg. Why
not say he got it all? Why does he only get a single peg? Tell me, where is that single peg?
It directly pierces through the second person.

The instruction says: the knife that kills and the sword that gives life are the style and standard of antiquity, and are now
the pivotal key. Tell me, which one today is the knife that kills and the sword that gives life?
Try bringing it up and see.

[15] A monk asked Yunmen: When it is not the present opportunity, and also not the present matter,
what then? Yunmen said: Inverting the One, speaking.What are you jumping for? Retreat three thousand li.Brought out flatly. Drawing out a convict’s mouth by confession—still you must not
let it pass. Lying crosswise in wild grass.

This monk was indeed a capable one, able to ask like this. On the surface it is called requesting
benefit; it is a question that presents understanding, also called a question that hides the edge. If it were not Yunmen,
one could not do anything with him. Yunmen had such hands and feet; since he brought the question,
Yunmen responded as he must. Why? A capable lineage master is like a bright mirror facing the stand:
when a barbarian comes, a barbarian appears; when a Han comes, a Han appears. The ancients said: if you want intimacy, do not take
a question to ask. Why? The question is in the answer; the answer is in the question. From the sages above,
when was there ever a single Dharma given to people? Where is there any Zen Way brought to you? If you
do not create the karma of hell, you naturally will not invite the fruit of hell. If you do not create
the causes of heaven, you naturally will not receive the fruit of heaven. All karmic conditions are self-
made and self-received. The ancients plainly told you: if we speak of this matter, it is not in
words and phrases. If it were in words and phrases, would the three vehicles and the twelve divisions of the teaching be without
words and phrases? Then what need was there for the Patriarch’s coming from the West? Earlier he said, speaking in response to the One; here
he says, inverting the One, speaking. It differs by only one character—why then are there a thousand divergences and ten thousand
distinctions? Tell me, where is the awkwardness? Thus it is said: Dharma follows Dharma in practice;
the Dharma banner is raised wherever one is. When it is not the present opportunity, and also not the present matter,
what then? It only takes a single point right at the head. If you are a clear-eyed person, even one point cannot fool
you. Since the place of questioning is awkward, the place of answering must be like this. In truth, Yunmen
rode a thief’s horse to chase a thief. Some misunderstand, saying it was originally the host’s words, but
become the guest’s words. Therefore Yunmen said, inverting the One, speaking. What deadly urgency is there?
This monk asked well: when it is not the present opportunity, and also not the present matter,
what then? Why didn’t Yunmen answer with other words, but only said to him, inverting the One,
speaking? Yunmen shattered him at once. Yet here, saying inverting the One, speaking, is also
like gouging a sore in good flesh. Why? When verbal traces arise, white clouds stretch ten thousand miles; the ways by which divergent
paths are born. Suppose for a time there were no words and no phrases—just pillar and lamp,
when were there words and phrases? Do you understand? If you do not understand, when you reach here you still must
turn it around before you know where it lands.

lg.l:
Inverting the One, speaking—cut into a single section.You cannot put it down. Seven blossoms, eight splits. South of Sumeru—rolling up all five thousand and forty-eighton your side and on my side.
Half Henan, half Hebei—hand in hand, walking together.

Together in death, together in life—this is the secret for you.Washing a lump of dirt in mud—what reason is there? I will not
let you go.

Eighty-four thousand are not phoenix down; threeLike feathers—far too much diminishing people’s awesomeness. A lacquer bucket, like hemp, like millet.
thirteen people enter the tiger’s den.Only I can know. One general is hard to find. A whole troop of wild-fox spirits.

Distinct, distinct.Where is there any other place?
Stop showing off. Jump as you like.
In restless hurry, the moon in the water.Blue sky, bright day—losing your head and taking shadows for real. What are you rushing for?

cb:div.p:
Xuedou was no less a true adept; with a single line he at once cuts off a section, clearly
letting one move pass, taking you by the hand to walk together. He has always had ways of letting go,
daring to enter mud and water with you, to die together and live together. That is why Xue-
dou composed this verse. In truth it is nothing else: he only wants to free you from sticking and binding, to pull
nails and pry out wedges. Yet now, because of words and phrases, you instead give rise to sentimental interpretations. Just like Yantou
said, Xuefeng may share the same branch of life with me, but he does not share the same branch of death. If one is not
a person whose whole functioning breaks through, attaining great freedom, how could he die together with you and live together with
you? Why? Because he has no place where gain and loss, right and wrong, can seep in. Thus Dong-
shan said: if you want to discern the genuine from the false among people of the higher way, there are three kinds of seepage:
emotional seepage, perceptual seepage, and verbal seepage. With perceptual seepage, the functioning does not leave its position
and falls into a poisonous sea. With emotional seepage, wisdom is always for or against; the place you see from is one-sided and withered.
With verbal seepage, the subtle essence loses its source; functioning is darkened from start to finish. These three seepages you should already
know. And there are also three mysteries: mystery within the body, mystery within the phrase, and mystery within mystery. The ancients
who reached this realm had whole functioning and great activity: meeting life, they lived together with you; meeting death,
they died together with you. They lay themselves crosswise in the tiger’s mouth, letting limbs go free; for a thousand miles, ten thousand
miles, you may carry them off in your jaws. Why do we still grant him this move? Only then does it work. The eighty-
four thousand who are not phoenix feathers: the eighty-four thousand holy assembly at Vulture Peak are not phoenix
feathers either. The History of the South says: in the Song, Xie Chaozong, a man of Yangxia in Chen Commandery, the son of Xie
Feng, broadly learned and outstanding in literary talent, unmatched at court; in his day he was regarded as
without peer. Skilled in writing, he served as a Regular Attendant in a princely household. When the king’s mother, Yin Shuyi, died,
Chaozong wrote the funeral eulogy and presented it. Emperor Wu read it and praised it greatly, saying, Chao-
zong truly has phoenix feathers. An old poem says: when court is dismissed, fragrant smoke fills your sleeves; when a poem is finished,
pearls and jade are in the sweep of the brush. If you want to know the beauty of one who holds the threads of edicts in hand, by the pond today there is
phoenix feather. Long ago at the assembly on Vulture Peak, the fourfold community gathered like clouds; the World-Honored One held up a flower—only
Kashyapa alone broke into a slight smile; the rest did not know what the message was. Thus Xuedou
said: the eighty-four thousand are not phoenix feathers; thirty-three people entered the tiger’s den.
Ananda asked Kashyapa: besides transmitting the gold-brocade robe, what other teaching did the World-Honored One transmit separately?
Kashyapa called out, Ananda! Ananda answered, Yes! Kashyapa said: knock down the flagpole
in front of the gate. Ananda thereupon awakened. Thereafter, patriarch after patriarch transmitted it, from the Western Heaven to this land.
These thirty-three people had the hands and feet to enter the tiger’s den. The ancients said: if you do not enter the tiger’s
den, how can you get the tiger cub? Yunmen was such a person, well able to die together and live together.
A lineage master guiding others must come to this: sitting on the wooden seat with its curved backrest, he dares to let
you smash it, allows you to stroke the tiger’s whiskers. You too must reach such ground
before it works. With seven things carried on the body, one can live together and die together. Those who are high he presses down;
those who are low he lifts up. Those who lack he supplies. Those on a solitary peak he rescues and makes enter
the wild grasses; those who have fallen into the wild grasses he rescues and makes dwell on a solitary peak. If you enter the cauldron of boiling soup, the furnace
of coals, I too enter the cauldron of boiling soup, the furnace of coals. In truth it is nothing else: it is only to free you from
sticking and binding, to pull nails and pry wedges, to remove the halter, to unload the horn-burden. The master Pingtian
has a verse that is best: numinous light is not obscured; for ten thousand ages, the splendid model. Enter this
gate and do not keep intellectual understanding. Separate, separate. In restless hurry, the moon in the water—there is still no harm
in there being a road to step out, and also a pivot for bringing people to life. Xuedou picked this up to teach people
to go and clearly awaken to the living pivot themselves—do not follow his words and phrases. If you follow him, that is exactly
restless hurry, the moon in the water. So how do you now go in calm steadiness? Let
one move pass.

The instruction says: the Way has no sideways shortcut; to stand is solitary and perilous. The Dharma is not in seeing and hearing; words and thought are
far beyond. If you can pass through the thicket of thorns, untie the bonds of Buddha and patriarchs, and gain a stable,
tight ground, the gods offering flowers will have no road, and outsiders peering in will have no gate. All day
you walk yet have never walked; all day you speak yet have never spoken. Then you can be free
and at ease, unfold the peck-and-chirp pivot, and wield the sword of killing and bringing to life. Even if it is so, you must further
know that within the gate of teaching there is one hand lifting and one hand grasping—still a bit short of it.
But as for the original matter itself, it has nothing to do with it. What is the original
matter? Try bringing it up and see.

Case sixteen: A monk asked Jingqing, I chirp; please, Master, peck. Jingqing said, Can you still get to live or not? The monk said, If I do not live, people will laugh at me. JingqingRaising waves with no wind—what for? You use so
many views—what for?A jab—buying a physiognomy reading. Taking mistake upon mistake—you must not always be like
this.Dragging each other down. Propping up heaven and bracing earth—an eaves-plank fellow.
said, You are also a fellow in the grass.Indeed. You lead it out yourself. Letting it pass is not allowed.

Jingqing was a successor of Xuefeng, a contemporary of Benren, Xuansha, Shushan, Taiyuan Fu, and the like.
When he first met Xuefeng and obtained the point, he often used the peck-and-chirp pivot to instruct
later students. He was skilled at responding to circumstances to speak the Dharma. He addressed the assembly, saying: in general, a traveling practitioner must
have the eye of chirping and pecking at the same time, and the function of chirping and pecking at the same time—only then can he be called a patch-robed monk. For
when the mother wants to peck, the chick cannot but chirp; when the chick wants to chirp, the mother cannot but
peck. Then a monk came forward and asked: mother pecks and chick chirps—on the master’s side, what sort of matter does this accomplish?
Jingqing said, A fine piece of news. The monk said: chick chirps and mother
pecks—on the student’s side, what sort of matter does this accomplish? Jingqing said, It reveals a
face. Therefore, in Jingqing’s gate there is the peck-and-chirp pivot. This monk too was
a guest in his gate, who understood the affairs of the house, and so asked like this: I chirp,
please, Master, peck. In the Dongshan school this question is called borrowing an affair to illuminate functioning. It is not like that here.
When the chick chirps and the mother pecks, naturally it is exactly simultaneous. Jingqing too was fine; it can
be said that fist and kick correspond, mind and eye illuminate each other. So he answered: Can you still get to live or not?
That monk too was fine; he also knew adaptive turns. Within a single line there is guest and host, illumination
and function, killing and bringing to life. The monk said: If I do not live, people will laugh at me. Jingqing
said: You are also a fellow in the grass. All the same, it is entering mud and water; Jingqing is not to be faulted for
a rough hand. Since this monk could ask like that, why then did he say, You are also
a fellow in the grass? Thus the eyes of a true adept must be like this: like striking stone to make
fire, like a flash of lightning—whether you can set it up or cannot set it up, you cannot avoid losing body and life. If it is like
this, then you see why Jingqing said fellow in the grass. Therefore Nanyuan addressed the assembly, saying: the various
quarters only have the eye of chirping and pecking at the same time, but do not have the function of chirping and pecking at the same time. A monk
came forward and asked: what is the function of chirping and pecking at the same time? Nanyuan said: a true adept does not chirp
or peck; chirping and pecking at the same time is missed. The monk said: this is still where the student doubts. Nanyuan
said: what is it that you doubt? The monk said: missed. Nanyuan then struck him. The
monk would not accept it, so Nanyuan drove him out. Later the monk went to Yunmen’s assembly and brought up the earlier exchange.
Another monk said: Nanyuan’s staff broke there. That monk suddenly had an awakening. Tell me,
where is the intent? That monk went back to see Nanyuan, but Nanyuan had just passed away.
He then went to see Fengxue; as soon as he bowed, Fengxue said: are you not the monk who at that time asked the former master about chirping
and pecking at the same time? The monk said: yes. Fengxue said: how did you understand it at the time?
The monk said: at first, it was like walking within lamplight and shadows. Fengxue
said: you understood it. Tell me, what principle is this? All along this monk only
said: at first, it was like walking within lamplight and shadows. Why then did Feng-
xue at once say to him, you understood it? Later Cuiyan picked it up, saying: although Nanyuan
planned within the tent, what can be done that the land was vast and people were few, and those who truly understood were rare? Fengxue
picked it up, saying: if at that time Nanyuan had waited for him to open his mouth and then struck him across the back, see what he would
do. If you see this gong’an, then you see where this monk met Jingqing.
Everyone, how will you manage it so that he cannot call you a fellow in the grass? That is why Xuedou loved
his saying fellow in the grass, and so he versified it:

lg.l:
An ancient Buddha has the household style; raising it in response brings demotion.The words are still in the ear—an example for a thousand ages. Do not slander old Shakyamuni’s fine
Stripped.nostrils—why are they in this mountain monk’s hands? Eight blows to thirteen—what will you do? Let one move pass, and then he hits.

Child and mother do not
know each other.Since you do not know each other, why is there pecking from within and without? Naturally,

Who is it that chirps and pecks together?a hundred fragments. An old woman’s anxious heart—do not mistakenly
take it.

Pecking, he finds he is still in the shell; once more he is knocked down.What are you saying? It falls into the second head.Why not come out and show your head?Wrong. Then he hits. Two-
layer cases—three-layer, four-layer, finished too.

Patch-robed monks under heaven are merely famous in name, and far off in truth.Let it pass too; no need to raise it. Is there anyone who can clearly name it
or not? If you can name it clearly, you are still a fellow in the weeds. For a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, it is pitch-black—filling ditches and blocking ravines, with no one who understands.

cb:div.p:
The ancient buddha has a household style; with one line Xuedou has already versified it. Whenever anything sticks its head out,
you absolutely cannot draw near. If you do draw near, you are ten thousand miles away at Yazhou. The moment it
pokes its head out, it has already fallen into the weeds. Even if it runs seven ways and eight ways, it takes no more than a pinch. Xue-
dou said, The ancient buddha has a household style; it is not like people do it nowadays. Old Shakyamuni,
when he had just been born, with one hand he pointed to heaven and with one hand he pointed to earth, and looking around in the four directions
he said, In heaven above and on earth below, I alone am honored. Yunmen said, If I had been there at the time
and saw it, I would have killed him with one blow and fed him to a dog, just to keep the world at peace. In this way
one would finally repay it properly. Therefore the timing of pecking and chirping is all the household style of the ancient buddha.
If you reach this Way, you can knock down Yellow Crane Tower with a single punch, and with one kick
topple Parrot Isle. It is like a great mass of fire: draw near and it scorches your face. It is like
the Tai'a sword: try to match it and you lose your body and your life. Only one who has broken through and shed
everything into great liberation can be like this. If you are confused about the source and stuck on phrases, you definitely cannot muster
talk like this. In raising and responding you meet dismissal and stripping away: that is one guest and one host.
One question, one answer; right within question and answer there is stripping away. This is called raising and responding meeting dismissal and stripping away.
Xuedou deeply knew this matter, so he only versified it within two lines.
At the end it is still falling into the weeds, breaking it open with notes for your sake. The child and the mother do not know each other. Who is it that
pecks and chirps together? Though the mother pecks, she cannot bring about the child's chirping; though the child chirps, it cannot
bring about the mother's pecking. Each does not know the other. At the moment of chirping and pecking, who is it that chirps and pecks
together? If you understand it like this, you still cannot get out from within Xuedou's final line. Why?
Do you not see Xiangyan saying: The child chirps, the mother pecks; the child awakens to having no shell; child and mother both
are forgotten. Responding to conditions is not mistaken; those on the same path sing in harmony; wondrous mystery, one leg. Xuedou does not
mind falling into the weeds, spinning vines with the word peck. This one word is used to versify Jingqing's answer to the question, Is it still alive or not?
Awake. He versifies this monk's saying: If it is not alive, you will be mocked and laughed at. Why
then does Xuedou immediately say, Still in the shell? Xuedou, in the flash of sparks struck from stone,
distinguishes black-robed from white; in the instant of lightning he discerns the beginnings. Jingqing said, He too is a fellow in the weeds.
Xuedou said, He gets struck down again; that is the harder point. Since Jingqing said he too is a fellow in the weeds,
can you call that Jingqing changing people's eyes? Is this line not Still in the shell? For now, let it go without concern; how could it be like that?
If you can understand it, you can wander the world on pilgrimage and have your share of repaying kindness. This mountain monk talking like this is also
a fellow in the weeds. The world’s patched-robed monks are named Miaoyue; who is not named Miaoyue?
When you come to this point, Xuedou himself cannot escape the name Miaoyue, and instead further burdens all the monks in the world.
Tell me: how is Jingqing acting for this monk? The monks of the world cannot jump out of it.
Tell me, how was Jingqing acting on behalf of this monk? Monks everywhere leap about
They cannot jump out.

Instruction says: Only by cutting nails and slicing iron can you be a true master of your own portion. If you dodge arrows and skulk from blades,
how could you be a broad-minded author? Where needle and awl cannot enter, set it aside for now. When white
waves billow up to the sky, what then? Try raising it and see.

Case 17. Raised: A monk asked Xianglin, What is the intent of the Patriarch's coming from the West? Xianglin said, Sitting long becomes toil.Plenty of people doubt it; still
there is this bit of news here.Fish move and the water grows muddy; birds fly and feathers fall. Better to shut the dog’s mouth. A master hand’s eyes—sawing
and splitting a scale-weight.

Xianglin said, Sitting long becomes toil. Do you understand? If you understand, atop the tips of a hundred grasses
you can lay down weapons. If you do not understand, bow and listen for your sentence. When the ancients went on pilgrimage,
they made friends and chose companions as fellow travelers on the Way, parting the grass and watching the winds. At that time Yun-
men’s flourishing teaching was in Guangnan. Xianglin came out of Shu and, together with Ehu Jingqing,
first visited Baozi in Hunan, and only afterward arrived at Yunmen’s assembly, serving as an attendant
for eighteen years. At Yunmen’s place he personally received and personally heard. Though his awakening came late, it still
does not hinder that he was a great vessel. Staying at Yunmen’s side for eighteen years, Yunmen often only
called, Attendant Yuan. As soon as he responded, Yunmen would say, What is it? At that time Xianglin also
offered phrases, presenting views and toying with cleverness, but in the end he never matched. One day he suddenly said, I
understand! Yunmen said, Why not bring it forth in an upward way? He stayed another three years. In Yunmen’s
room he set forth great pivots and discernment, mostly for that Attendant Yuan, letting him enter and work in at every place.
Whatever single word or phrase Yunmen had, all of it was gathered into the attendant Yuan. Xianglin
later returned to Shu, first residing at the Crystal Palace of Daojiang, and later at Xianglin in Qingcheng. The master Zhimen
Zuo, a native of Zhejiang, greatly hearing of Xianglin’s Way and teaching, specially came into Shu
to pay respects and inquire. Zuo was Xuedou’s teacher. Though Yunmen guided countless people, among those of the time
who truly walked the Way, only Xianglin’s line was the most flourishing. Returning to Sichuan, he stayed as abbot for forty years.
Only at eighty did he pass away. He once said, Only after forty years did I finally hammer it into one piece.
Whenever he instructed the assembly he said: In general, when you go on pilgrimage and seek out teachers, you must travel with eyes; you must
distinguish black-robed from white, and only then see what is shallow and what is deep. First you must set your resolve. And old Shakyamuni,
when he was at the stage of causes, whenever he uttered a word or formed a thought, it was all setting resolve. Later a monk asked,
What is a lamp inside the room? Xianglin said, Three people confirm a turtle becomes a soft-shelled turtle. Again he asked,
What is the matter beneath the patched robe? Xianglin said, In the twelfth lunar month, fire burns the mountain. Since ancient times
there have been many answers about the Patriarch’s intent, but only this one case of Xianglin severs the tongues of everyone under heaven,
leaving you no place to calculate and make up principle. A monk asked, What is the intent of the Patriarch’s coming
from the West? Xianglin said, Sitting long becomes toil. It can be said: words without flavor, phrases without
flavor. Flavorless talk blocks mouths and shuts them off, leaving you nowhere to breathe out. If you want to see, then
see. If you do not see, absolutely do not make interpretations. Xianglin had once met an adept, therefore
he had Yunmen’s methods. There are three-phrase modes and tones. Many people mistakenly understand it as: the Patriarch came from
the West and faced the wall for nine years, so is that not sitting long becomes toil? What
is the point? They do not see where the ancients gained great freedom. He was feet planted on solid
ground, without so much Buddhist doctrine, views, and reasoning. He used it as needed in the moment, what is called: the dharma
follows the dharma; the dharma-banner is raised wherever you go. Xuedou, because the wind blows the fire, points
to one or half of one.

lg.l:
One, two, ten thousand, a million—cast off the cageWhy not follow it and act on it? Like hemp, like millet—why form into groups and make into squads?
head and remove the horn-load.From today onward you should be open and unbound—can you rest or not yet?

Turn left, turn right, and follow along behind.
Still you cannot put it down—shadows and echoes. Then he hits.

Zihu wants to strike Liu the Iron Grinder.This mountain monk will snap the staff and no longer carry out this
command. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—then he hits. Risky,

cb:div.p:
Xuedou straightaway is like sparks struck from stone, like a flash of lightning, pressing it out and letting you
see. You must at least, hearing it raised, immediately understand. It would not hurt if you were his household child
or grandchild; only then can you speak like this. If you can straightaway understand it like this, it is no hindrance
to be extraordinary. One, two, ten thousand, a million—cast off the cage head and remove the horn-load; free and
open, unrestrained. Not tainted by birth and death, not bound by emotional interpretations of saint and ordinary.
Above, no reaching and yearning; below, cut off self-concern—just like Xianglin’s snow-bursting.
Not only ten thousand, a million—so that all the people across the whole earth are all like this.
Buddhas before and buddhas after are also all like this. If you make interpretations within words and phrases,
it is like Zihu wanting to strike Liu the Iron Grinder. In truth, as soon as it is raised, with
one voice he strikes. Zihu studied under Nanquan, practicing alongside Zhaozhou and Cen the big tiger. At that time
Liu the Iron Grinder had set up a hut below Weishan, and the various places could do nothing with her.
One day Zihu went straight there to visit and said, Are you Liu the Iron Grinder or not? The Grinder said,
I would not dare. Zihu said, Turn left, turn right. The Grinder said, Venerable, do not turn things upside down. Zihu
with one voice struck her. Xianglin answered this monk’s question, What is the intent of the Patriarch’s coming
from the West, and said, Sitting long becomes toil. If you can understand it like this, turning left and turning right, follow
along behind. Tell me: with Xuedou versifying it like this, what is his intent? No business
is good. Please try raising it and see.

Case 18. Raised: Emperor Suzong asked National Teacher Zhong, After a hundred years, whatoriginally this was Emperor Daizong’s error.
will be needed? The National Teacher said, Make forScratching in advance, waiting for the itch—sure enough you raise patterns and shapes. Old and great, making these moves—do not point east and make it west.
this old monk a seamless stupa. The emperor said, Please show me the stupa’s model.You cannot hold it.Good—give one jab.
The National Teacher was silent for a long time and said, Do you understand?Keeping convicts makes wisdom grow—ending up pointing east and drawing west, taking south as north, until the mouth is like a flat eave.
The emperor said, I do not understand. The National Teacher said,Luckily he did not understand. At that time he should have been given another squeeze, making his mouth full of frost—then it would have been somewhat better.
I have a dharma-heir disciple, Danyuan, who understands this matter. Please issue an edict and ask him.
After the National Teacher passed away, the emperor summoned Danyuan by edict and asked what this meant. Danyuan said, South of Xiang, north of Tan. Xuedou added a remark: One hand does not make a loud sound; within it there is gold, filling a whole country.
Luckily he did not flip over the meditation bench. Why not give him his proper fodder? Do not smear the barbarian’s beard as good. Let one move pass—that will do. A pity.
Indeed he mistakenly took the fixed star as certain.The son carries on the father’s trade—falling into the
second head, the third head.Still you cannot hold it. Why go two by two, three by three? Half open,
half closed.One blind leads a c
Why chase the crooked and do evil?Above is heaven, below is earth. With no such news—whose affair is it, then?
Xuedou added a remark: A mountain-shaped staff has no shadow.It has been wrenched and snapped; it is still just a made-up model.
Under the tree they join together in the same boat. Xuedou added a remark: The sea is calm,The Patriarch is lost as well. Venerable, what do you say?
When the river runs clear, in the lapis hall there is no “knower”; XueVast surging waves, white breakers flooding the sky—still not quite enough.Bah!
Dou added a remark, saying: “Picked up and done.”Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—his words are still in your ears.

Suzong and Daizong were both descendants of Xuanzong. When he was crown prince, he often loved to go and
study Zen. Because the realm had a great bandit threat, Xuanzong then fled to Shu. Tang’s original capital was Chang’an; it was
usurped and occupied by An Lushan. Later the capital was at Luoyang, and Suzong served as regent. At that time the National Teacher Zhongguo
was living in a hermitage on Baiya Mountain in Dengzhou, where Xiangyan’s practice site is today. For more than
forty years he did not come down from the mountain. His conduct and practice became known in the imperial capital. In the second year of Shangyuan, an edict
a palace messenger, summoning him to enter within. He was received with the courtesies due a teacher, and was deeply respected. He once expounded with the emperor
the unsurpassed Way with the emperor. When the master withdrew from court, the emperor himself held onto the carriage and escorted him. All the court officials showed
displeasure and wanted to memorialize that it was improper. The National Teacher had knowledge of others’ minds, and foresaw
the imperial memorial in advance, saying: “Before Indra, lord of gods, I see a petty human emperor like a flash of lightning,
like lightning’s light.” The emperor respected him even more. When Daizong ascended the throne, he again invited him to reside at Zhiguang
Monastery for sixteen years, teaching according to conditions. In the tenth year of Dali, he passed on
and transformed. The master of Qingcuo Mountain in Shannan Prefecture had once traveled with the National Teacher. The National Teacher
once petitioned the emperor to issue an edict for him. Three summons came and he did not rise. He often cursed the National Teacher for craving fame and loving
profit, clinging to the human world. The National Teacher served as national teacher through three reigns for that father and son.
That father and son, for a time, studied Zen. According to what is examined in the Transmission of the Lamp Record, this is
a question posed by Daizong. But if the question is “National Teacher, what is the tenfold body that tames and guides,” this
was instead asked by Suzong. When the National Teacher’s karmic conditions ended and he was about to enter nirvana, he took leave of Dai-
zong. Daizong asked: “After the National Teacher’s hundred years, what will be needed?” It is just
an ordinary line of questioning. This old fellow raised waves without wind, and said: “Build for this old
monk a seamless stupa.” Tell me, under bright day and clear sky, what is this for?
Just making a stupa would do. Why did he instead say, make a seamless stupa? Daizong
was no slouch either. He pressed him with one shove: “Please, Master, the stupa pattern.” The National Teacher, for a long
while, said: “Do you understand?” Strange, this kind of thing is hardest to work on. The great National
Teacher, pressed by that one shove, was left with a mouth like a flat eave. Even so, if it were not
for this old fellow, he would almost have toppled it. How many people say: “The National Teacher’s not speaking
is the stupa pattern.” If you understand it like that, you grind the whole lineage to dust and sweep it all away.
If you say the long pause itself is it, then even a mute would be able to understand Zen. Don’t you see the outsider
who asked the Buddha, not asking for words, not asking for no words—World-Honored One remained silent for a long while. The outsider bowed in
homage and praised: “World-Honored One, with great kindness and great compassion you have parted my cloud of confusion, allowing me to
enter.” After the outsider left, Ananda asked the Buddha: “What realization did the outsider attain, that
he says he has entered?” The World-Honored One said: “Like a fine horse in the world that moves upon seeing the shadow of the whip.” Many people
try to understand at the long pause—what’s the point? Master Wuzu picked it up and said: “In front
are pearls and agate; behind are agate and pearls. On the left are Avalokiteshvara and Mahasthamaprapta; on the right
are Manjushri and Samantabhadra. In the middle there is a banner.”
The wind blows it and it goes hu lu, hu lu. The National Teacher said: “Do you understand?” The emperor said:
“I don’t understand.” That’s a bit better. Tell me: is this “don’t understand” the same as Emperor Wu’s “I don’t recognize,”
or different? Though it resembles it, it is not actually it. The National Teacher said: “I
have a Dharma-heir disciple, Danyuan, who understands this matter. Please issue an edict and ask him.” Xue
Dou picked it up and said: “One hand does not make the waves roar.” Set aside Daizong’s not understanding for now—does Danyuan
understand or not? Just by saying “Please, Master, the stupa pattern,” people across the whole earth cannot do
anything about it. Master Wuzu picked it up and said: “You are the teacher of a nation—why
don’t you speak, instead pushing it off onto your disciple?” After the National Teacher passed on, the emperor summoned Danyuan and asked
what this meant. Danyuan then came to serve as National Teacher, babbling in mixed talk, explaining principles.
Naturally he understood the National Teacher’s words. It only took one verse: “South of the Xiang,When the Ancestors’ Garden Anthology was first compiled,
north of the Tan: within it is gold filling a whole nation. Beneath the shadowless tree, they share
a boat. In the lapis hall there is no ‘knower.’” Danyuan’s name was Yingzhen. At the National Teacher’s place
he served as an attendant. Later he resided at Danyuan Monastery in Jizhou. At that time Yangshan came to study with Danyuan.
Danyuan said: “Zhongxing is harsh-natured, fierce, and must not be offended—you can’t stay.” Yangshan first went to study with Master Xing-
kong. A monk asked Xingkong: “What is the Patriarch’s meaning in coming from the West?” Xingkong
said: “It’s like someone in a thousand-foot well. Without using even an inch of rope, get the person out—then
I will answer you about the meaning of coming from the West.” The monk said: “Recently in Hunan, Master Chang also guides people by
talking east and speaking west.” Xingkong then called a novice: “Drag this corpse out and throw it on the mountain!”the novice Yangshan,
Later he raised it and asked Danyuan: “How do you get the person out of the well?” Danyuan said: “Tch! You foolish
fellow—who is in the well?” Yangshan did not accord with it. Later he asked Guishan. Guishan then called:
“Huiji!” Yangshan answered. Guishan said: “He’s out.” Because of this, Yangshan had a great awakening.
He said: “With Danyuan I got the substance; with Guishan I got the function.” It is just
this one verse that leads many into perverse interpretations. Many people mistakenly understand it, saying: “Xiang
means meeting face to face; Tan means discussing.” In the middle there is a seamless stupa—so they say.
“Within it is gold filling a whole nation” means the exchange between the emperor and the National Teacher. That is the shared boat beneath
the shadowless tree. The emperor did not understand, so it says: “In the lapis hall there is no ‘knower.’”
Others say Xiang is south of Xiangzhou, and Tan is north of Tanzhou. Within
it is gold filling a whole nation. They chant that the ruler blinked and glanced around, saying: “This is
the seamless stupa.” If you understand it like that, you never get beyond emotional views. But as for Xue Dou’s four turning
phrases below, how will you understand them? People today really do not know what the ancients meant. Tell me:
“South of the Xiang, north of the Tan”—how do you understand it? “Within it is gold filling a
whole nation”—how do you understand it? “Beneath the shadowless tree they share a boat”—how do you
understand it? “In the lapis hall there is no ‘knower’”—how do you understand it? If you can see like that,
you may rejoice for your whole life. “South of the Xiang, north of the Tan”—Xue Dou said: “One
hand does not make the waves roar.” With no choice, I’ll tell you: “Within it is gold filling a whole nation.”
Xue Dou said: “Mountain-shaped staff.” The ancients said: “If you recognize the staff,
your lifelong study is finished.” “Beneath the shadowless tree they share a boat”—Xue Dou said: “The sea is calm,
the river runs clear.” In one moment the doors and windows are flung open—crystal clear in all eight directions. “In the lapis hall
there is no ‘knower’”—Xue Dou said: “Picked up and done.” In one stroke I’ve told it to you. It’s
not that it’s impossible to see; seeing it is fine too. It’s just that there are places where you misrecognize—following words,
you give rise to interpretations. At the very end he says “picked up and done,” which is a bit better. Xue Dou clearly
delivered his remarks all at once. After that, he gives a separate verse just on the seamless stupa.

lg.l:
Seeing the seamless stupa is still hard; the clear pool does not allowthis seam—how huge it is! What are you saying?Not something the eye can see. Blind!
an azure dragon to coil.Do you see? The vast waves surge—where does the azure dragon coil? Here you simply cannot grope your way to it.

Tier upon tier, standing clear;Do not let your eyes go blurry.
What is blurred vision for?

shadows gathered round, a thousandYour whole body is eyes. Falling seven, falling eight; in twos and threes you walk the old road—turn left, turn right, and follow behind.
ages, ten thousand ages, for people to see.Do you see? How does a blind fellow see? Venerable, can you make it out?

cb:div.p:
Xue Dou begins by saying: “Seeing the seamless stupa is still hard.” Though it alone stands revealed, without selfishness,
when you want to see it, it is still hard. Xue Dou is overly compassionate, and further tells you:
“The clear pool does not allow an azure dragon to coil.” Master Wuzu said: “Of Xue Dou’s verse on the ancients,
I only love his one line, ‘The clear pool does not allow an azure dragon to coil.’ Still, it’s a bit
better.” How many people go to the National Teacher’s long pause and make a living off it. If you understand it like that,
you miss it all at once. Don’t you see it said: “A coiled dragon does not reflect in still water.” Where there is no
place for moon-waves to be clear, there are places where waves rise without wind. It also says: “The coiled dragon long fears the emerald
pool’s clarity.” If it is this fellow, even if vast floods surge and white waves reach the sky,
he still will not coil there. When Xue Dou gets here, he has finished the verse; after that he adds some
“eyes,” chiseling out a seamless stupa, and then says: “Tier upon tier, shadows
gathered round—through a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, for people to see.” How do you see it? Right now,
where is it? Even if you see it clearly, do not misrecognize the fixed
pole star.

The pointer says: When one mote is raised, the whole earth is gathered; when one flower opens, a world arises. But
before the mote is raised, before the flower opens, how do you set your eye? Thus it is said: like cutting a single
strand of silk—one cut, and all is cut; like dyeing a single strand of silk—one dyeing, and all is dyed.
Just now, cut off the tangled vines at once, and bring forth your own family treasure, high and low
Universally responsive, with no discrepancy from beginning to end; each is complete in itself. If it is not yet so,
look at what follows.

[19] Case. Master Juzhi: whenever he was asked anything, he would simply raiseWhat news is there? Dull-rooted teacher!
one finger.This old fellow also wants to cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven. When it is hot, all of heaven and earth are hot; when it is cold, all of heaven and earth are cold. He swaps out everyone’s tongues.

If you understand it on the fingertip, you betray Juzhi. If you do not understand it on the fingertip,
it is like being cast from raw iron. Understanding goes like this; not understanding also goes
like this. High goes like this; low goes like this. Right goes like this.
Wrong goes like this. Therefore it is said: when a single mote of dust arises, the whole earth is gathered in. When
a single flower is about to open, the world comes forth. On one hair-tip a lion; on a hundred billion hair-tips it appears.
Yuanming said: when it is cold, all heaven and all earth are cold; when it is hot, all heaven and all earth are hot.
Mountains, rivers, and the great earth below penetrate to the Yellow Springs; the myriad forms in their profusion above reach the vault of heaven. Tell me,
what thing is it that is so strange? If you recognize it, there is no need for a single
pinch. If you do not recognize it, it blocks you to death. Master Juzhi was a man of Jinhua
in Wuzhou. When he first lived in a hermitage, a nun named Shiji came; she walked straight in.
Without removing her hat, holding her staff, she circled the meditation seat three times and said, Speak and I will remove my hat.
In this way she asked three times. Juzhi had no reply, so the nun left. Juzhi said, It is getting rather
late; stay the night. The nun said, Speak and I will stay. Juzhi again had no reply, so the nun
went on. Juzhi sighed, Though I have a man's form, I have no man's spirit.
So he roused himself, determined to clarify this matter. He planned to abandon the hermitage and travel to various places to seek instruction, packing
for pilgrimage. That night the mountain spirit told him, You need not leave here. Tomorrow a bodhisattva in the flesh
will come to teach the Dharma for the master. Do not go. Sure enough, the next day, Master Tianlong
came to the hermitage. Juzhi greeted him with bows and fully related what had happened before. Tianlong simply raised
one finger to show him. Juzhi suddenly had great awakening. It was because at that time he was earnest
and wholly focused that the bottom of the bucket was easy to drop out. After that, whenever he was asked anything, he only raised
one finger. Changqing said, Fine food does not satisfy a person who eats it wrongly. Xuansha said, If I had seen it then,
I would have twisted off the finger. Xuanjue said, When Xuansha speaks like that, what is his intent?
Yunju Xi said, Just consider Xuansha speaking like that: does he approve of him, or does he not?
If he approves, why say he would twist off the finger? If he does not approve, where is
Juzhi at fault? Formerly Caoshan said, Juzhi's taking up was crude; he only
recognized one device and one scene. It is just like clapping and rubbing your hands, seeing his strange spectacle in the western
garden. Xuanjue also said, Tell me, did Juzhi awaken or not? Why
is his taking up crude? If he did not awaken, yet it is said that all his life he used only
the one-finger Zen without exhausting it. Tell me, where is Caoshan's meaning? At that time Juzhi
truly did not understand. Yet once he had awakened, whenever he was asked anything, he only raised
one finger. Why? A thousand people, ten thousand people cannot net it; they cannot
break it down. If you use it as understanding the finger, you will definitely not see the ancients' intent. This sort
of Zen is easy to enter, but hard to understand. Nowadays as soon as people are asked, they raise a finger, raise
a fist, merely playing with spirit. You must penetrate to the bones and marrow; only then can you see through.
In Juzhi's hermitage there was a boy. Outside, someone questioned him, What method does the master
usually use to instruct people? The boy raised a finger. Returning, he told the master.
Juzhi cut off his finger with a knife. The boy cried out and ran away. Juzhi called out
once; the boy turned his head. Juzhi then raised a finger. The boy suddenly
understood. Tell me, what principle did he see? When he was about to pass away, he said to the assembly, I
obtained Tianlong's one-finger Zen; all my life I have used it without exhausting it. Want to understand? Raise
a finger and it slips away. Later, Mingzhao the one-eyed dragon asked his grand-uncle, Master Guotai Shen,
The ancients say Juzhi merely recited a three-line spell and thereby gained a name surpassing all
people. How would you take away those three lines of spell? Shen also raised one
finger. Zhao said, If not for today, how would one recognize this Guazhou traveler? Tell me,
what is the meaning? Pimo all his life used only a fork; Master Dadi, whenever
he was asked anything, only struck the ground once. Later someone hid his staff; when asked
What is Buddha? he only opened his mouth. This too was something used all one's life without exhausting. Wuye
said, The patriarchs observed that this land has roots for the Great Vehicle, so they transmitted only the mind-seal, singly. They
point out the deluded path. Those who obtain it are not chosen by foolishness or wisdom, by ordinary or sage. Moreover,
better a little truth than much emptiness. You great man, right now, directly, rest and let go.
At once stop the myriad entanglements; transcend the stream of birth and death; stand far outside the usual pattern. Even if you have family
and adornments, without seeking you attain it of yourself. All his life, whenever Wuye was asked anything, he only said, Do not
indulge in deluded thinking. Therefore it is said: break through in one place and a thousand places, ten thousand places break through at once. When one
device is clear, a thousand devices, ten thousand devices are clear at once. People today are not like this; they only
indulge their own emotional interpretations, not understanding the ancients' essential point. Is it not that there is no
pivoting and turning? Why use only one finger? You must know that Juzhi
at this point had a deep and secret way of benefiting people. Want to understand the labor-saving way? Return to
Yuanming's saying: when it is cold, all heaven and all earth are cold; when it is hot, all heaven and all earth are hot.
Mountains, rivers, and the great earth connect upward to solitary heights; the myriad forms in their profusion penetrate downward to perilous depths. From what
place could a one-finger Zen be obtained?

lg.l:
Raising it up in praise, he deeply loves old Juzhi; though the universe is empty,Leprous children tugging companions—only those on the same path know. It still cannot avoid being one device, one scene.
who else is there?Two or three—and there is yet another; that one too must be killed.

He once set a floating log down upon the vast sea;
a log.It is entirely this. Yes, yes—yet it is too solitary and steep. What use is a torn straw sandal?

In night waves, together it meets and receives the blind turtle.
Fishing up heaven, groping earth—when will it ever end? Even if you catch it, what can you use it for? Act according to the command and drive on into a world with no Buddha. Having caught it, Venerable—one blind fellow.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou was skilled in parallel prose, thoroughly adept in every direction. Whenever there was a bizarre, twisted,
unusual gong'an, he especially loved to compose a verse on it. Raising it up in praise, he deeply loves old Juzhi; though the universe is empty,
who else is there? Students today either praise or disparage the ancients, sometimes as guest, sometimes as host, question
and answer, holding it up face to face. There is such a way of benefiting people. Therefore it is said: raising it up
in praise, he deeply loves old Juzhi. Tell me, what does Xuedou love about him? Since heaven and earth
were opened up, who else has there been? Only old Juzhi alone. If it were
someone else, there would have to be mixture and admixture. Only old Juzhi used only one finger, straight
until old age and death. People of the time often had a perverse understanding, saying that mountains, rivers, and the great earth are empty; people too
are empty; Dharma too is empty. Even if the universe were empty all at once, still old Juzhi
alone would have nothing to do with it. He once set a floating log down upon the vast sea; now it is called
the sea of birth and death. Sentient beings are in the sea of karma, heads emerging, heads sinking, not understanding
themselves, with no time of escape. Old Juzhi let down compassion and received beings; within the sea of birth and death,
he used one finger to receive people, like a floating log receiving a blind turtle, enabling
all beings to reach the other shore. In night waves, together it meets and receives the blind turtle. The Lotus Sutra
says: like a one-eyed turtle meeting the hole in a floating log, there is no danger of drowning. A great good
friend who receives someone like a dragon, like a tiger, teaches him: in a world with Buddhas,
they mutually act as guest and host; in a world without Buddhas, they sit and cut off the vital pass. If you receive
a blind turtle, what use is it?

The instruction says: piling up mountains, stacking peaks, crashing into walls, banging against barriers, standing and thinking to stop the mechanism, a whole
bitter oppression. Perhaps there is a fellow who comes forth, overturns the great sea, kicks down Sumeru, shouts
apart the white clouds, smashes open empty space, and directly, in one device and one scene, sits and cuts off
the tongues of everyone under heaven. There is no place for you to come near. Tell me, from above until now, who
has ever been like this? Try bringing it up.

[20] Case. Longya asked Cuiwei, What is the meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West? Cuiwei said, Hand me the Chan board. Longya handedOld talk from all quarters.
It too must be examined through.What are a Chan board and cushion for? You ought to let it pass. Yum!
the Chan board to Cuiwei. Cuiwei took itStill you cannot hold it. Harnessed to the azure dragon yet you cannot ride—what a pity. Face to face you do not accept it.
and struck Longya, saying, You may strike,Hit! What does beating a dead fellow accomplish? You have also fallen into the second head.
but there is, after all, no meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West. LongyaThis fellow’s words are in the second head—drawing the bow after the thief has passed.
again asked Linji, What is the meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West? Linji said, Hand me the sitting cushion. Longya took the cushion and handed it to Linji. Linji took it and struck Longya, saying, You may strike,
Old public cases from all quarters—ask them again, and they are not
worth half a coin.The waves of Caoxi are like this; countless ordinary people are submerged on dry land. In one batch they are herded
through, and buried in a single pit.As before, you cannot grasp it; as before, you are not nimble—vaguely like the state of Yue.
Faintly like Yangzhou.Strike! What a pity to beat such a dead fellow—cast from one mold.
but there is, after all, no meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West.Plainly you make a living in a ghost cave, thinking you have gotten a bargain.
Drawing the bow after the thief has passed.

Master Cuiyan Zhi said, It was like that then. But the patched-robed monks of today, is there still
blood under your skin? Weishan Zhe said, Cuiwei and Linji can be called masters true to their station.
Longya and his like were clearing the grass and watching the wind; they can serve as a mirror for later people.
After he became abbot, a monk asked, Master, back then did you approve of those two venerables? Longya
said, Approve, yes I approve, but there is no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. Longya looked ahead and behind,
prescribing medicine to match the illness. But Dawei was not like that. If he were asked, Master, back then
did you approve of those two venerables? clear or not clear, he would strike him across the back at once. Not only did he uphold
Cuiwei and Linji, he also did not fail the one who came to ask. Shimen Cong said, If no one presses Longya,
it is still acceptable; but once that patched-robed fellow crowds him, he loses one eye. Xuedou
said, Linji and Cuiwei only know how to seize and hold, not how to let go and open. If I had been there,
acting as Longya, when he asked for the sitting mat or the meditation board, I would have snatched it up and straightaway
hurled it in his face. Wuzu Jie said, The master has gotten such a long face. Someone said, The Patriarchs and old worthies
are bearing down on him. Huanglong Xin said, Longya drives off the plowman’s ox and snatches away the hungry man’s
food. If it is clear, then it is clear; so why is there still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West?
Do you understand? The staff has eyes, bright as the sun; to know true gold, look for it in the fire.
In general, to rouse and raise the essential, to lift up and proclaim the tradition, you must understand it at the first pivot.
Then you can sit and cut off the tongues of everyone under heaven. If you hesitate, you fall into the second.
These two old fellows, though they strike like wind and rain, shaking heaven and earth, still
have not yet struck a clear-eyed man. How hard the ancients labored in investigating Chan. They set
the resolve of a great man, traversed mountains and rivers, and went to visit venerable elders. Longya first
visited Cuiwei and Linji, and later visited Deshan. Then he asked, When a student brandishes the Mo-ye sword
and intends to take the master’s head, what then? Deshan stretched out his neck and said... Longya said, The master’s head
falls. Deshan gave a faint smile and let it rest. Next he went to Dongshan. Dongshan asked, Where have you just come from?
Longya said, From Deshan. Dongshan said, What words does Deshan have?
Longya then raised the previous exchange. Dongshan said, What did he say? Longya said, He had no
words. Dongshan said, Do not say no words. Try presenting to this old monk the head that Deshan let fall,
and let me see. Longya had an awakening here. He then burned incense, bowed toward Deshan from afar,
and repented. Deshan heard of it and said, That old fellow Dongshan does not know good from bad. This fellow
has been dead for so long; what use is it to save him? Let him carry this old monk’s
head around the world. Longya’s natural endowment was keen; he carried a bellyful of Chan and traveled on foot,
heading straight to Cuiwei in Chang’an, and asked, What is the meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West?
Cuiwei said, Hand me the meditation board. Longya took the meditation board and gave it to Cuiwei. As soon as Cuiwei received it
he struck him. Longya said, Strike if you like, but there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West.
Then he asked Linji, What is the meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West? Linji said, Hand me
the sitting mat. Longya took the sitting mat and gave it to Linji. As soon as Linji received it he struck him. Longya said,
Strike if you like, but there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. He set up this line of questioning;
it is no harm if he wanted to see those old fellows on their bentwood and plank beds, and also to clarify for himself
a great matter. It can be said his words were not empty, his triggers were not fired at random; it came from doing the work.
Do you not see Wuxie visiting Shitou? He agreed beforehand: if one word
matched, he would stay; otherwise he would leave. Shitou sat on his seat; Wuxie flicked his sleeves and
went out. Shitou knew he was a vessel of Dharma, so he offered instruction. Wuxie did not take up its meaning, and
took his leave and went to the gate. Shitou called out, Venerable. Wuxie turned his head. Shitou
said, From birth to death, it is only this. Do not turn your head and swivel your brain looking for something else.
Wuxie had a great awakening upon these words. Also, Magu carried his staff to Zhangjing, circled the meditation platform
three times, shook the staff once, and stood upright. Zhangjing said, Yes, yes. Then he
went to Nanquan. As before, he circled the platform, shook the staff, and stood. Nanquan said, It is not that it is not
so, but this is turned by the force of wind; in the end it becomes ruin and decay. Magu said, Zhangjing said
yes; why does the master say not? Nanquan said, Zhangjing is indeed yes; but yes
is not you. The ancients were not averse to lifting and sustaining this one matter until it was thoroughly released.
Nowadays, as soon as people are questioned, there is not the slightest place where they have applied effort. Today it is
only like this; tomorrow it is only like this. If you are only like this all the way
to the end of the future, you still will not have a finishing day. You must shake yourself awake and rouse your spirit; only then will you have
a small measure of accord. Look at Longya raising a single question: What is the meaning of the Patriarch
coming from the West? Cuiwei said, Hand me the meditation board. Longya handed it over to Cuiwei. Cuiwei
received it and struck him. When Longya took up the meditation board at that time, could he not have known Cuiwei meant to
strike him? Yet he could not simply say Cuiwei did not understand. Why then did he hand over the meditation board
to him? Tell me, if he could have taken responsibility right on the spot, how should it have been? He did not
apply it in living water, but went into dead water to make a living scheme, always making himself the master.
So he said, Strike if you like, but there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. Then he ran off
to Hebei to visit Linji, and asked in the same way. Linji said, Hand me the sitting mat
. Longya handed it to Linji. Linji received it and struck him. Longya said, Strike if you like.
But there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. Tell me, those two venerables were not of the same Dharma
lineage; why were their replies alike, and their use the same? You must know the ancients: one
word, one phrase, they did not act at random. Later, when he was abbot, a monk asked, Master,
back then when you met those two venerables, did you approve of them or not? Longya said, Approve, then approve,
but there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. There are thorns in rotten mud; letting it pass on to
others, he has already fallen into the second. This old fellow held it firmly, but only managed to be an elder of the Dongshan
line. If he were under Deshan or Linji, you must know there would be another way of life. But if
it were this mountain monk, it would not be so: I would only tell him, Approve, and yet not approve; but there is still no
meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West. Do you not see a monk asking Damei, What is the meaning of the Patriarch
coming from the West? Damei said, Coming from the West has no meaning. Yanguan heard and said, One coffin.
Two dead fellows. Xuansha heard and said, Yanguan is a genuine hand. Xuedou said, There are three
of them too. If, when this monk asked about the meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West, you instead told him
Coming from the West has no meaning, and you understood it like that, you would fall into the realm of nothing-to-do. Therefore
it is said: you must investigate the living phrase, do not investigate the dead phrase. If you realize it under the living phrase, through endless kalpas you will not
forget. If you realize it under the dead phrase, you cannot even save yourself. Longya speaking like this is not without
excellence. The ancients said continuity is very difficult. Those ancients, one word and one phrase,
did not act at random. Before and after illuminated each other; there was provisional and there was real; there was illumination and there was function; guest
and host were distinct; they exchanged and crossed freely. If you want to discern what is truly intimate, Longya, though he did not
obscure the tradition, still fell into the second. At that time those two venerables demanded the meditation board and the
sitting mat; Longya could not but know their intent. They wanted to use what was in his own chest
and bosom. Even so, their use was not without being too steep. Longya asked like this,
the two elders answered like that; why then was there still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West? Here
you must know there is another special point. Xuedou picked it up and showed it to people.

lg.l:
In Longya Mountain, the dragon has no eyes, in dead water
Blind! You can fool others, sure. Washing a clod in mud—everyone under heaven knows it.
how could it ever stir the ancient wind?If it suddenly comes alive, there is nothing to be done; it drags everyone under heaven down so they cannot raise their heads.

The meditation board and sitting mat cannot
be used.Who can be made to say it? What do you want a Chan board and cushion for? Is this a charge to you, Venerable?

It should only be handed over to Elder Lu
.Even then it cannot be assigned. Lacquer bucket, do not make such an understanding.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou stated the charge and concluded the case. Though he composed the verse like this, tell me, where is his intent?
Where is the lack of eyes? Where is the dead water? Here you must
have adaptability for it to work. Therefore it is said, A clear pool does not allow a blue dragon to coil; dead
water has never had a fierce dragon. Do you not see the saying: dead water does not hide a dragon. If it is a living
dragon, it must go where the vast floods surge and the white waves billow to the sky. This means Longya
ran into dead water. He was struck by others, yet he said, Strike if you like.
But there is still no meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West, which drew Xuedou to say, how could dead water ever
stir the ancient wind? Even so, tell me: is Xuedou supporting him, or diminishing
his authority and radiance? Many people misunderstand, saying, Why should it only be handed over to Elder Lu?
They do not realize it is Longya who is handing it over to others. In general, in inquiry and visitation, you must
discern it on the pivot; only then do you see where the ancients met. The meditation board and sitting mat
It can’t be used at all. Cuiwei said, “Pass me the chan board.” Ya handed it to him.
Isn’t this making a living scheme in dead water? Plainly it’s being given a blue dragon to ride—only
he doesn’t know how to ride it, so he can’t use it. It should simply be handed over to Lord Lu.
It’s wrong to keep calling him the Sixth Patriarch. He never handed it over to anyone. If you say he
did hand it over to someone, you’d have to use it to beat people—what would that turn into? Long ago Xuedou
called himself Lord Lu. He inscribed “Hiding his tracks, he brings it on himself,” saying: “In paintings back then I loved Dong-
ting Lake; in the heart of the waves the seventy-two peaks are green. Now I lie high at ease, thinking of past matters—adding
Lord Lu leaning on a stone screen.” Xuedou wanted to walk on Longya’s head, yet feared people
would misunderstand, so he made another verse to cut off people’s doubtful interpretations. Xuedou then again brought it up, saying:

“This old fellow still hasn’t been utterly finished off.” He then made another verse: “Though Lord Lu handed it over, what is there to rely on?”Clearly so—how many can know it? On your own
knowing is somewhat better. Luckily there is the final line.Across the whole earth it is hard to find such a person—who can be made to take up the words?
“Whether sitting or leaning, don’t take up the Patriarchs’ lamp to pass on; it can face the eveningA grass-side fellow—strike him down to sit beneath Black Mountain; he falls into a ghost cave.
clouds returning before they fully gather—distant mountains, endless blue, layerOne and a half of one—raise it and it is wrong; indeed you cannot get out.
upon layer.”Plug up your eyes, plug up your ears; drown in a deep pit—study another thirty years.

“Though Lord Lu handed it over, what is there to rely on?” What evidence is there to rely on? You must understand it right here like this—
and don’t cling to the stump waiting for a rabbit. Smash through it once right in front of the skull; with
not a speck of anything in your chest, let it be free and unencumbered—then what need is there
to rely on anything? Whether sitting or leaning, there’s no need to make Buddhist doctrines out of it. That’s why it says: sitting or leaning,
don’t take up the Patriarchs’ lamp. Xuedou brought it up in one stroke. He has a place to turn around—
and at the very end he reveals a bit of news himself. There’s a little something good in it, saying: it can face the evening
clouds returning before they fully gather. Tell me—where is Xuedou’s intent? When the evening clouds return, about to
gather yet not gathered, what do you say then? Distant mountains, endless blue, layer upon layer.
Still you crash right into the ghost cave. When you reach here, gain and loss, right and wrong—all at once
are cut off, free and unencumbered—only then is it somewhat close. Distant mountains, endless blue, layer upon layer.
Tell me, is this Manjushri’s realm? Samantabhadra’s realm? Avalokiteshvara’s
realm? When you get to this point, tell me—whose business is it?

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Two, by Chan Master Foguo YuanwuEnd.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Three, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu

cb:div.p:
Pointer: Raising the banner of Dharma and establishing the guiding principle is like adding flowers atop brocade. Taking off the cage’s head and un-
harnessing the horn-burdened pack. In times of peace, if you can discern a phrase beyond the pattern, raise one and illuminate three.
If not, then as before you must bow and await judgment.

Case 21: A monk asked Zhimen, “What about when the lotus has not yet emerged from the water?” Zhimen said, “Lotus.” The monkHooked in the place of no-doubt,
in that ground. Washing a clod in mud—where would you get this news?One, two, three, four, five, six, seven—doubt kills everyone under heaven.
said, “After it emerges from the water, what then?” Zhimen said, “Lotus leaf.”Do not do your living in a ghost cave. Off you go again like this.Youzhou.
That is still acceptable; worst is Jiangnan—two ends and three faces, laughing everyone under heaven to death.

If Zhimen were merely responding to conditions and meeting people, it would still be somewhat close. But if it’s cutting off the
whole current, it’s a thousand miles, ten thousand miles away. Tell me—this lotus, emerged from the water and not yet emerged from the water—are they
one or two? If you can see it like that, I’ll grant you a way in. Even so,
if you say it is one, that’s muddled buddha-nature and vague true suchness. If you say it is two,
mind and object aren’t forgotten—you fall onto the road of explanation and run along it; when will there ever be rest? Tell me,
what was the ancients’ intent? In truth there aren’t so many things. That’s why Touzi said:
“Just don’t cling to famous phrases. If you understand all matters, naturally you won’t cling, and then
there aren’t so many different ranks and positions. You encompass all dharmas; all dharmas encompass
you—yet cannot take hold of you. Originally there is no gain or loss; dreams and illusions—so many labels—you must not force
names onto them. Can that kind of deception scare you people?” You people ask—
therefore there are words. If you didn’t ask, what could I possibly tell you?
Everything is something you bring in yourselves; it has nothing to do with me. The ancients
said, “If you want to know the meaning of buddha-nature, you should observe the seasons and conditions.” Haven’t you seen Yunmen cite:
A monk asked Lingyun, “What about before the Buddha appeared in the world?” Lingyun held up his whisk.
The monk said, “After he appeared in the world, what then?” Lingyun also held up his whisk. Yunmen said, “At the
front you hit it; at the back you can’t hit it.” He also said, “Don’t speak of appearing or not appearing—where
is there any ‘when’ for him to ask about?” The ancients’ one question, one answer—responding in time, responding in season—
there aren’t so many matters. If you chase words and run after phrases, it has nothing to do with it. If you can
penetrate words within words, intent within intent, pivot within pivot—then let it be
easy and unhurried; only then will you see where Zhimen’s answer lies. Asking “Before the Buddha appeared in the world, what
about?” “Before Niutou met the Fourth Patriarch, what about?” “Before chaos within the mottled stone was
differentiated, what about?” “Before your parents were born, what about?” Yunmen said, “From ancient times to the present,
it’s just one stretch of affair—no right, no wrong; no gain, no loss; no born and
not-yet-born. When the ancients got here, they let out a thread, saying there is exit and entry. If you’re someone who
hasn’t understood, you prop yourself on fences and grope walls, clinging to grass and leaning on trees. Maybe someone tells you to put it down, and then
you’re knocked into a vast, boundless, desolate place. If you’re someone who has gotten it, in the twenty-four
hours you don’t rely on a single thing. Though you don’t rely on a single thing, if you reveal a single pivot
and a single scene, how will you grope for it? This monk asked, “When the lotus has not yet emerged from the water,
what about?” Zhimen said, “Lotus.” It was just a single question, a single answer—no harm in being striking.
Everywhere people call it inverted speech—how could that be? Haven’t you seen Yantou
say, “It’s always prized before the mouth opens; that’s still somewhat close.” Where the ancients reveal their pivot,
they’re already leaking and giving it away. Students nowadays don’t understand the ancients’ intent—they just
go on theorizing about emerging from the water and not yet emerging—what does that have to do with it? Haven’t you seen a monk
ask Zhimen, “What is the substance of prajna?” Zhimen said, “A clam holds the bright moon.” The monk asked,
“What is the function of prajna?” Zhimen said, “A rabbit is with child.” Look at answers like this—
no one under heaven can find his verbal thread. Or suppose someone asked Jiashan,
“When the lotus has not yet emerged from the water, what about?” You could just answer, “Dewy pillars and lanterns.” Now tell me,
are they the same as the lotus or different? After it emerges from the water, what about? Answer him, “On the staff-tip,
the sun and moon are carried aloft; underfoot the mud is too deep.” Tell me—is it so or not? For now don’t
mistake the fixed star for your reference point. Xuedou is outrageously compassionate—he smashes human-sentiment interpretations, so
he brings forth the verse:

lg.l:
Lotus and lotus leaf report it to you: how is it when it emerges from the water,An old-womanly urgent heart; a ready-made public case—its ornamented phrasing is already displayed.
and when it has not yet emerged?Washing a clod in mud—separating it is fine; you must not go along in a muddle.

North of the river, south of the river—ask Old Wang.
Where is the main person? What are you doing asking Teacher Wang? You yourself wear out your straw sandals.

One doubt after another doubt.Bury it in one pit. Your own
doubt is your doubt—you cannot avoid that the doubt-feeling has not ceased. He struck and said: Do you understand?

cb:div.p:
Zhimen was originally from Zhejiang. He deliberately entered Sichuan to visit Xianglin. After he broke through, he returned
and lived at Zhimen in Suizhou. Xuedou was his disciple. He saw it well, plumbing the mysterious to the utmost,
reaching the wondrous. He went straight to: “Lotus and lotus leaf report it to you—how is it when it emerges from the water, and when it has not yet emerged?”
Here it requires that people understand immediately. This mountain monk says: when it has not yet emerged from the water, what about?
Dewy pillars and lanterns. After it emerges from the water, what about? On the staff-tip, the sun and moon are carried aloft; underfoot the
mud is too deep. For now don’t mistake the fixed star for your reference point. Nowadays people bite and chew on other people’s phrases—
what limit is there? Tell me: when it emerges from the water, what season is that? When it has not yet
emerged from the water, what season is that? If you can see it here, I’ll grant you personally
meeting Zhimen. Xuedou said: if you don’t see it, north of the river and south of the river, ask Old Wang.
Xuedou’s meaning is: you just go north of the river and south of the river, asking venerable elders about emerging from the water
and not yet emerging. South of the river you add two lines; north of the river you add two lines—one
layer adds another layer, and doubt arises again and again. Tell me, when will you be rid of doubt? Like
a wild fox, full of suspicion, walking on ice ridges, listening for the sound of water—only if it doesn’t sound can it
cross the river. If a practitioner has one doubt after another doubt, when will they ever get steady
and secure?

Pointer: The great square has no outside; the small is as fine as adjacent emptiness. Capturing and releasing are not up to another—rolling up and
unfurling are in me. If you truly want to undo sticking and remove binding, you must erase your tracks and swallow your voice—everyone sits
cutting off the vital pass; each and every one stands like a sheer wall a thousand fathoms high. Tell me, whose realm is this?
Try bringing it up and see.

Case 22: Xuefeng addressed the assembly, saying, “In the Southern Mountain there is a soft-shelled-turtle-nosed snake. You people must look carefully.”If you see the strange and do not take it as strange, then
the strange will break of itself. What a huge strange affair—no harm in making people doubt it.A whole bout of leaking and letting slip.
Changqing said, “Today in the hall there will be many who lose their bodies and lose their lives.” A monk related this to Xuansha. XuanshaA person of Pu Prefecture escorts
a thief—taking one of your own side’s peopleinto the same pit, with no difference of soil. A servant sees a maid being diligent—same illness, mutual pity.
said, “It would take Brother Leng to do it.” Even so, I am not like that.”
The monk said, “Then how would the master do it?” Xuansha said, “What use is the Southern Mountain?” Yunmen, with his staff, jabbed it toward Xuefeng’s face, making aInevitably it turns into a wild-fox-spirit understanding. What sort of news is this? Poisonous vapors injure people.It is also good to press hard on
this old fellow.Third Brother Xie on a fishing boat—this very wild-fox spirit is still
a little better. Losing body and losing life, and not even knowing it.
threatening gesture.Why fear it? If you get it firsthand, still it is the same as playing with spirit and soul. Everyone, try to discern it.

If you lay it out flat, then let it be flat; if you smash through, then let it be smashed through. Xue-
Feng traveled together with Yantou and Qinshan. In all, they went three times to Touzi, and nine times up to Dongshan.
Later he visited Deshan, and only then smashed the lacquer bucket. One day he led Yantou to call on Qinshan.
They were snowed in at the Ao Mountain inn. Yantou spent every day just sleeping. Xuefeng
sat in meditation the whole time. Yantou shouted, “Go on, sleep and eat!” On the bed every day, just like
the village earth god of seven hamlets. One of these days, you’ll bewitch the men and women of people’s homes
and lead them off. Xuefeng pointed to his own chest and said, “In here I’m not yet steady; I dare not
deceive myself.” Yantou said, “I thought that later you’d go to the top of a solitary peak and knot together a grass
hut, spreading the great teaching—yet you’re still talking like this.” Xuefeng said, “I truly am not
steady.” Yantou said, “If it’s really like that, then from your point of view, bring it all out,
