one by one. Where it’s right, I’ll confirm it for you; where it’s not, I’ll cut it off for you.” So Xuefeng
raised the case of Yanguan in the hall expounding the meaning of form and emptiness, and found an entry point. Yantou said, “From this
for thirty years—on no account bring it up.” Xuefeng brought up another: Dongshan’s verse on crossing the stream.
He found an entry point. Yantou said, “If it’s like that, you can’t save yourself.” Later he went to Deshan.
He asked about the matter of the ancestral school: “Does a student still have a share in it or not?” Deshan struck
him with his staff. “Say what?” At that moment I felt as if the bottom dropped out of a bucket. Yantou then shouted,
“Haven’t you heard: what comes in through the gate isn’t the family treasure.” Xuefeng said, “Afterwards
what then is it?” Yantou said, “If someday you want to spread the great teaching, let it all come
flowing out from your own chest. For me, cover heaven and cover earth with it.” Xuefeng, on
hearing this, had a great awakening. He bowed in homage. Rising, he cried out again and again, “Only today is it
that I’ve realized the Way at Ao Mountain! Only today is it that I’ve realized the Way at Ao Mountain!” Later he returned to Fujian and lived at
Xianggu Mountain. He composed a verse for himself, saying: “A human life flashes by, only a brief moment;
in the floating world, how could one stay long? Leaving the passes, I had just turned thirty-two;
entering Fujian, I was already over forty. It’s not that he doesn’t use it—he keeps raising it again and again;
but once it’s passed, it should be removed at once. I report to all the court’s crimson-and-purple nobles:
King Yama isn’t afraid of your gold fish pendants.” Whenever he ascended the hall to instruct the assembly, he said: “Each and every one covers heaven and covers earth. I won’t talk of the abstruse or the wondrous; nor will I
talk of mind or nature. Suddenly it stands alone, revealed—like a great mass of fire: come near and it scorches
your face. Like the Tai’e sword: if you so much as aim at it, you lose body and life. If you pause to think
and halt your workings, then there’s no connection at all. Take Baizhang asking Huangbo, “Where are you coming and going?”
Huangbo said, “Coming and going to pick mushrooms below Mount Daxiong.” Baizhang said, “Did you see a great beast?”
Huangbo made a tiger’s roar. Baizhang lifted an axe and made a chopping gesture. Huangbo then slapped Baizhang
once. Baizhang, humming and laughing, returned and mounted the seat, telling the assembly, “On Mount Daxiong
there is a great beast. All of you must watch carefully. Today this old monk personally
ran into it.” Zhaozhou, whenever he saw a monk, would ask, “Have you ever been here?” If they said, “I have,”
or if they said, “I haven’t,” Zhaozhou would always say, “Go drink tea.” The prior said, “Master,
you regularly ask monks whether they’ve been here or not, and you always say ‘Go drink tea.’ What’s the point?”
Zhaozhou said, “Prior.” The prior answered. Zhaozhou said, “Go drink tea.” Zihu
set up a sign at his gate. On the sign it read: “Zihu has a dog.
It takes people’s heads above, their waists in the middle, their feet below. If you deliberate, you lose body and life.” Or
when a newcomer had just met him, the master would shout, “Watch the dog!” As soon as the monk turned his head, the master would
return to the abbot’s quarters. Just like Xuefeng saying, “On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake. You
people must watch carefully.” Right at such a time, how would you respond?
Don’t step in the tracks of those before. Try saying it. Here you must understand a phrase beyond the standards
to make it. As for all the language of public cases, once you bring it up, you immediately know where it falls.
Look at how he instructed the assembly like this: I won’t talk to you about practice or explanation. Can you still
measure it with feelings and intellect? If you are his descendants, naturally you can say it just right.
Therefore the ancients said: take up words and you must understand the aim; don’t set up your own rules. Speech must
be beyond the standards; phrases must pass through the barrier. If your words don’t leave the ruts and holes, you fall into
a sea of poison. Xuefeng instructing the assembly like this can be called tasteless talk—blocking
people’s mouths. Changqing and Xuansha were both people of his own household; only then could they understand him
speaking like this. Just take Xuefeng’s saying, “On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake.”
Do you people know where it lands? Here you must possess the eye of one who comprehends in all directions
to manage it. Isn’t there Zhenjing’s verse: “Beating drums, playing the pipa—two who understand meet;
Yunmen can sing in response; Changqing can go along with it. The ancient tune has no melody;
the turtle-nosed snake of South Mountain—who knows this meaning? Truly it is Xuansha.” Just as for Chang-
qing responding like that—tell me, what does it mean? Here it is like striking stone for
sparks, like a flash of lightning—only then can you approach it. If even the slightest trace doesn’t go away, then
you can’t reach it. What a pity—many people give rise to emotional interpretations under Changqing’s words,
saying that as soon as there is something heard in the hall, it’s already losing body and life. Some say,
“There wasn’t a single thing to begin with—speaking like this out of the blue just makes people doubt.” When people hear
him say “On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake,” you immediately doubt. If you understand it like that,
you’ll at least have no connection at all—only making a living calculation on his words. Since you don’t
understand it that way, then how do you understand it? Later a monk brought it up to Xuansha. Xuan-
sha said, “It must be Brother Leng.” Even so, I’m not like that.
The monk said, “Then how are you?” Xuansha said, “What use is South Mountain? Just
look—within Xuansha’s words there’s a place to emerge.” He then said, “What use is South Mountain?
If it weren’t Xuansha, it would be very hard to answer. Just take him saying like that—
“On South Mountain there is a turtle-nosed snake”—tell me, where is it? Here
it must be someone of the upward way who can understand such talk. The ancients said, “On the fishing boat
you thank Third-Lord Xie; you don’t love the turtle-nosed snake of South Mountain.” Then he went to Yunmen and
jabbed his staff toward Xuefeng’s face, making a frightening gesture. Yunmen had the hands and feet of a snake-handler,
not offending the sharp edge. He strikes the bright head and strikes the dark head. In his usual guidance of people,
it’s like dancing the Tai’e sword. Sometimes it flies toward a person’s eyebrows
and eyelashes; sometimes it flies three thousand miles away to take someone’s head. Yunmen jabbed
his staff to make a frightening gesture—yet it’s not a trick with spirits. Wouldn’t that also be losing body
and life? A true master of the school never makes his living on a single word or phrase.
As for the scheme: Xuedou only because he loved that Yunmen had matched and confirmed Xuefeng’s intent, therefore he composed this verse.
It comes out:

lg.l:
Elephant-bone crags are lofty; people do not reach there—whoever reaches there must beA thousand or ten thousand cannot grope it out—it is not your realm.
a snake-handling expert.When it is spirit, recognize spirit; when it is thief, recognize thief. Why form packs and teams? You must be of the same fire.

Master Ling, Master Bei,
could do nothing about it.Herded through in one batch—let one move pass.

How many have lost their bodies and lives?The crime is not heavily punished, yet it
implicates ordinary people.

Shaoyang, knowing what matters, parts the grass.Still not quite enough. This old fellow has only one eye; he cannot avoid pulling tricks.Falling into the weeds,
what use is such a fellow? If he truly is somewhere, then strike.
North, south, east, west—there is nowhere to seek it.Is there? Is there? Venerable, your eyes are blind.

Suddenly it bursts forth from the tip of the staff, tossed right at Xuefeng—thrown wide open.Look up and fix your eyes high—then strike.
mouth.You do it yourself and you suffer it yourself. What does swallowing a thousand or ten thousand accomplish? Everyone under heaven cannot grope it out.

Opening wide the mouth—together with a flash
of lightning.A double-layer public case—indeed. Luckily there is the final line.

Lift up your eyebrows, and still you do not see it.You have missed it. Five
lakes and four seas—hard to find such a person. Where is he now?

Now it is hidden in front of Rufen Peak.Where has he gone? Big
or small, even Xuedou makes this coming and going. Today this mountain monk too suffers one bite

Those who come—each and every one, look for the skillful means.of blindness. Do not look beneath your own heel;
look—look beneath the senior seat’s heel. An arrow has struck.

The master shouted loudly, “Watch your feet!”Drawing the bow after the thief has passed.
Second head, third head—repeated words are not fit to eat.

cb:div.p:
Xianggu Cliff is high; people do not reach it. Those who reach it must be snake-handlers. At Xuefeng Mountain
below there is Xianggu Cliff. Xuefeng’s pivot-words are lofty and steep; few people reach that place.
Xuedou was one of his own household, like the same feathers and down: same sound responds, same energy
seeks its match. It still requires those of broad understanding to confirm each other. As for this turtle-nosed
snake, it’s not that it can’t be hard to handle—you must know how to handle it. If you don’t know how, instead
you’ll be injured by the snake. The former master Wuzu said, “This turtle-nosed snake must have not-
This is the move that damages hands and feet: grab him right at the seven-inch point, pinch and hold fast, and then
the old monk takes you by the hand and walks with you. Changqing and Xuansha had this kind of skill. Xuedou
said, Master Leng and Master Bei could do nothing about it. People mostly say Changqing and Xuansha could do nothing
about it, so Xuedou alone praises Yunmen, making it seem unrelated. But they do not know
that among the three, the pivot has no winning or losing; there is only closeness and distance. Tell me, everyone—what
place is it where Master Leng and Master Bei could do nothing? How many lose their bodies and forfeit their lives
because of it? In this verse, Changqing said, Today in the hall, many people lose their bodies and forfeit their lives.
At this point you must have the hands of a snake-handler; only careful attention will do. Xuedou brings out his
Yunmen, so he sweeps the rest aside for the moment and leaves only Yunmen standing, saying Shaoyang knows.
He stirs the grass again, because Yunmen knew that Xuefeng said, On South Mountain there is a
snouted-snake that falls there—so he stirs the grass again. When Xuedou’s verse reaches this point,
there is something even subtler: It cannot be sought in north, south, east, or west. Tell me, where
is it? Suddenly it pops out at the tip of the staff. It turns out it is only right here. You must not
then go make a living scheme on the tip of the staff. Yunmen jabbed with his staff
in front of Xuefeng’s face to put on a frightening display. Yunmen then used the staff as that snouted-
snake. Sometimes he would say, The staff transforms into a dragon and swallows heaven and earth.
Where do mountains, rivers, and the great earth come from? It is just one staff. Sometimes it is a
dragon, sometimes it is a snake. Why is it like this? Only when you reach here do you know.
The ancients said, The mind turns along with the ten thousand conditions; where it turns, it truly can be subtle. The verse says: He threw it down
before Xuefeng, and Xuefeng opened his mouth wide. Opening the mouth wide is like lightning. Xuedou had talent to spare.
He picked up Yunmen’s venomous snake and said: this opening the mouth wide, like lightning
is just like it. If you deliberate, you will lose your body and forfeit your life. Even if you lift your eyebrows, you still
do not see—where will you go? After Xuedou finished the verse, you must go to the living place to help people.
Taking Xuefeng’s snake, he himself picks it up and plays with it; he can kill or bring to life as the moment demands. Want to see?
He said, Now it is hidden in front of Ru Peak. Ru Peak is the name of Xuedou’s mountain. Xue-
dou has a verse saying: The rocks look around on all sides and the blue sea is cramped; vast and lonely, it does not allow white clouds
to be white. Changqing, Xuansha, and Yunmen—though they can handle it, it still cannot be seen. Yet he says, now
it is hidden in front of Ru Peak; those who come should each look for themselves by skillful means. Xuedou is still entangled in fine points,
not speaking and just using it. Then he shouted loudly, Watch your feet! From above until now,
how many people have picked it up and played with it? Tell me—has it ever injured anyone or not? If it has not injured
anyone, the master strikes.

Instruction: Jade is tested by fire; gold is tested by stone; a sword is tested by hair; water is tested by
a staff. As for the school of patch-robed monks: a word, a phrase; one pivot, one situation; one
going out, one coming in; one press, one shove—if you want to see depth and shallowness, want to see facing and turning away, tell me,
what will you use to test? Please bring it up.

Case 23: Baofu and Changqing were out roaming the mountains. Baofu pointed with his hand
These two weed-fallen fellows,
and said, Right here is the summit of Miao Peak. Changqingraise a pile of bones on level ground. Absolutely avoid speaking it—dig the earth and bury it deep.
said, It is, it is—what a pity.If not for iron eyes and bronze pupils, you would almost be deluded. Same illness, mutual pity—bury the two in one pit.
Xuedou added a remark: Today, roaming the mountains with this fellow—what are we aiming for?
And again: A hundred or a thousand years later, it is not that there will be none,No harm in cutting down people’s weight—still not quite enough. A bystander grips his sword.
it is only that there will be few. Later it was brought up to Jingqing. Jingqing said, IfShow off less. It is also a Yunju arhat.There is good and there is bad.
it were not Lord Sun, you would see skulls covering the wilds.Only those on the same path know. The great earth is boundless and kills people with sorrow. A servant sees
a maid being diligent—same illness, mutual pity. Even if Linji and Deshan came out, they too would have to take the stick.

Baofu, Changqing, and Jingqing all inherited from Xuefeng. The three of them attained together and
realized together; saw together and heard together; picked up together and used together. One going out, one coming in—they pressed
and shoved one another, because they were people from the same line. The moment it was raised, they knew where it landed. In
Xuefeng’s community, the everyday questions and answers were just these three. The ancients, in walking, standing,
sitting, and lying down, kept this Way in mind. Therefore, when it is raised, they know where it lands. One
day while roaming the mountains, Baofu pointed with his hand and said, Right here is the summit of Miao Peak.
Nowadays, if a Zen fellow is asked like this, his mouth just hangs open like a signboard eave. Luckily it was
Changqing who was asked. Tell me: when Baofu spoke like that, what was he aiming for? The an-
cients did this to test whether one has eyes or not. If you are one of his own people, you naturally
know where it lands, and so you answer him: It is, it is—what a pity. Tell me,
what was Changqing’s meaning in saying that? You must not just go on like this.
Similar, indeed similar—rarely is there anything so casual with not the slightest matter. Fortunately Changqing saw through
him. Xuedou added a remark: Today, roaming the mountains with this fellow—what are we aiming for?
Tell me, where does it land? And again: A hundred or a thousand years later, it is not that there will be none; it is
only that there will be few. Xuedou’s way of pointing to the chest is just like Huangbo saying, It is not that there is no Chan, it is
only that there are no teachers. For Xuedou to speak like this is indeed steep and perilous. If they were not
matching in the same voice, how could it be so solitary, precarious, and strange? This is called adding a remark: it falls
on both sides. Though it falls on both sides, it does not stay on either side. Later it was brought up to Jing-
qing. Jingqing said, If it were not Lord Sun, you would see skulls covering the wilds. Lord Sun was
Changqing’s lay surname. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Zhaozhou, What is the solitary summit of Miao Peak?
Zhaozhou said, This old monk will not answer you that. The monk said, Why won’t you answer
that? Zhaozhou said, If I answered you, I fear it would fall onto flat ground. In the teachings they speak of
the solitary summit of Miao Peak: Bhikshu Deyun never goes down the mountain. Sudhana went to visit him.
For seven days he did not meet him; one day they met on another peak. And once they met, he
spoke to him of one thought and the three times, all Buddhas, the light of wisdom, universally seeing
the Dharma gate. If Deyun does not go down the mountain, why did they meet on another peak?
If you say he went down the mountain, the teachings say Bhikshu Deyun never once went down
the mountain, always on the solitary summit of Miao Peak. Up to here—where exactly
were Deyun and Sudhana? Later, Layman Li tangled up the vines, and did it well, saying the solitary summit of Miao Peak
is a single-flavored Dharma gate of equality: each and every one is true, each and every one is complete.
It uniquely reveals itself in the place of no gain and no loss, no right and no wrong. Therefore Sudhana did not
see it. When you reach the place that accords with nature, it is like the eye not seeing itself, the ear not hearing itself, the finger not
touching itself; like a knife not cutting itself, fire not burning itself, water not washing itself. When you reach
here, the teachings have a lot of old-woman-like helping. So they let out one thread, saying: in
the gate of the second meaning, they set up guest and host, set up pivot and situation, set up questions and answers. Therefore they say,
the Buddhas do not come forth into the world, and there is no nirvana; as expedient means to ferry beings, they therefore manifest
such matters. Tell me, ultimately how can you avoid Jingqing and Xuedou speaking like this?
Back then they could not match each other in crisp claps, so for all the people across the earth
skulls cover the wilds. Jingqing realized it like this; how did those two use it
like this? Xuedou’s later verse brings it out even more clearly. The verse says:

lg.l:
On the solitary summit of Miao Peak, the grasses are thick; having grasped it clearly, to whom will you hand it over?Sink away with your whole body—beneath your feet it is already several fathoms deep.
To whom will you give it?What is it used for? No one on the great earth knows. What use is a dry shit-stick? When you pinch the nostrils, you lose the mouth.

If it were not Lord Sun, who could discern
what is truly so?You misread the arrow; the thief has already struck, and you do not even know it.

When skulls hit the ground, how many people know?There is no second chance for life—like hemp and millet.
The acharya grabs the nostrils and loses the mouth.

cb:div.p:
On the solitary summit of Miao Peak, the grasses are thick. Rolling in the grass—what end is there? Having grasped it
clearly, to whom will you hand it over? What place is the clear place? The verse is on Baofu saying, Right
here is the summit of Miao Peak. If it were not Lord Sun discerning what is truly so—what did Lord Sun see
as principle, that he then said, It is, it is—what a pity? Just as for skulls hitting
the ground, how many people know? Do you all know or not? Blind!

Instruction: Stand on the lofty peak top—demons and outsiders cannot know. Walk on the deep sea bottom—
even the Buddha-eye cannot spy it. Even if your eyes are like shooting stars and your pivot is like a flash of lightning, you still
cannot avoid the spirit turtle dragging its tail. When you reach here, what will you do? Try bringing it up.

Case 24: Liu Tiemuo arrived at Weishan. Weishan said, Old cow,It is fine that it is hard to come together. This old woman does not keep to her proper place.
you have come. Tiemuo said, Tomorrow at Taishan there is a greatA touch: probing with a pole, watching the grass and shadows—where will you see any clumsy error?
assembly feast. Will the master go too? Wei-The arrow never misses. Great Tang beats the drum, Silla dances. Letting it go is too fast; drawing it back is too late.
shan stretched out and lay down. Tiemuo then went outHit the mark. Where are you going to see Guishan? Who would know that far-off mist and waves hold other fine reflections?
and left.It passes. Act when you see the moment.

Liu Tiemo is like striking sparks from flint, like the flash of lightning. If you try to deliberate, you lose your bodyA nun, too.
and your life. When the Way of Chan reaches the crucial point, there are not so many affairs. They
who are adepts meet like this: see horns over a wall and you know it is an ox; see smoke beyond a mountain
and you know it is fire. Press it and it moves; hold it down and it turns. Guishan said, this old monk
after a hundred years will go to a lay donor’s home below the mountain and become a water buffalo. Under the left flank
there will be written five characters saying: “Monk so-and-so of Guishan.” Now right at that moment, if you call it
a Guishan monk, that is calling it a water buffalo; that is it. When people today are questioned
about it, you can be sure they cannot sort it out. Liu Tiemo has long practiced, her edge sharp and steep; people
nicknamed her Liu the Iron Grinder. Ten li from Guishan she set up a hermitage. One day she went to visit Guishan;
when Guishan saw her coming he said, “Old cow, you’ve come.” Tiemo said, “Tomorrow at Mount Tai there is a great assembly feast.
Will the master go?” Guishan let his body go and lay down. Tiemo
then went right out. Look at them—just like ordinary talk. Yet it is neither Chan nor
the Way. Can you call this “understanding as nothing to do”? Guishan went to Mount Tai—separated by
several thousand li. Why did Liu Tiemo make Guishan go to the feast? Just say:
what is the meaning? This old woman understood Guishan’s words—thread coming, line going,
one release, one gather—answering each other back and forth like two mirrors reflecting one another, with no image
to be seen. Move matching move, phrase fitting phrase. People today can be prompted three times and still won’t turn their heads.
This old woman could not hide a thing from him. But this is not worldly truth,
not emotional views. It is like a bright mirror on its stand, a shining pearl in the palm: when a barbarian comes, a barbarian appears; when a Han comes,
a Han appears. It is because they know there is a matter above, so it is like this. People today only keep on
trying to make it into an “idle understanding.” Master Si-zu Yan said, “Do not take what has affairs and make it no-affairs;
often affairs arise from no-affairs.” If you penetrate your investigation, you’ll see them speaking just
like ordinary people. Most get blocked by words, and therefore
do not understand. Only those who truly “know the tune” understand it. Consider Qianfeng addressing the assembly, saying,
“Raise one and you cannot raise two; let one pass and you land on the second.” Yunmen stepped out
from the crowd and said, “Yesterday there was a monk who came from Tiantai and went on to Nanyue.” Qianfeng
said, “Today the cook may not do the general labor.” Look at those two: release and both release;
gather and both gather. In the Guiyang line this is called “the state of affairs.” Wind, dust, the stirring of grass—all
are thoroughly traced to their source. It is also called “a phrase separated by a body”: the intent is clear but the words are blocked. When you reach here,
you must deflect left and turn right—only then are you an adept.

lg.l:
Once he rode an iron horse into the fortified city; the imperial decree went out, the news spreadA veteran fighter, a true hand; a frontier general, with the seven necessities on his person.
the six states are pacified.A dog holds an amnesty edict; the Son of Heaven within the realm—yet what can be done when seas are calm and rivers run clear?

Still gripping a golden whip, he asks the returning traveler,What
news is this? One staff, supported by two people—calling each other to go together and to return together.

deep into the night, who will walk the imperial street together?You head for Xiaoxiang; I head for Qin.
Tell me: what are you going for?

cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s verse: the various places take it as the utmost standard. Among the hundred verses, this one most
fully contains the reasoning, and within it is supremely subtle, close to the body and plainly expressed. “Once he rode an iron
horse into the fortified city” praises Liu Tiemo coming like that. “The decree went out, the news spread, the six states
are pacified” praises Guishan asking like that. “Still gripping a golden whip, he asks the returning traveler” praises Tiemo saying,
“Tomorrow at Mount Tai there is a great assembly feast—will the master go?” “Deep into the night, who will walk the imperial
street together?” praises Guishan letting his body go and lying down, and Tiemo going right out. Xuedou has this kind of
talent: at urgent points he verses urgently; at slow points he verses slowly.
Fengxue also once took this up, in the same spirit as Xuedou. Everywhere they praise this verse.
“Stand on the highest peak—demons and outsiders cannot know. Walk on the ocean’s deepest floor—the Buddha eye
cannot see.” Look at them: one lets his body go and lies down; one goes right out. If you add
any fussing around, at once you cannot find the path. Xuedou’s intent in the verse is best: it is
“Once he rode an iron horse into the fortified city.” If they were not of the same attainment and the same verification, how could it be
like this? Tell me, what is the meaning? Have you not seen a monk ask Fengxue: “Guishan said,
‘Old cow, you’ve come.’ What is the meaning?” Fengxue said, “In the depths of white clouds a golden
dragon leaps.” The monk said, “Then as for Liu Tiemo saying, ‘Tomorrow at Mount Tai there is a great assembly feast.
Will the master go?’ What is the meaning?” Fengxue said, “In the heart of blue waves, the jade rabbit
is startled.” The monk said, “Guishan then made the lying-down posture. What is the meaning?” Fengxue said, “The old
fellow—slack and lazy on a day with nothing to do—idly sleeps and lies high, facing the green mountains.” This meaning too
matches Xuedou’s.

The prefatory instruction says: If the pivot does not leave its place, you fall into the poisonous sea. If words do not startle the crowd, you sink into
common vulgarity. Suddenly—within the sparks from striking flint—distinguish black from white; within the flash of lightning—discern killing
and bringing to life. You can sit and cut off the ten directions, stand like a cliff a thousand fathoms high. Do you know there is such a
moment? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 25 raised: The hermitage master of Lotus Peak picked up his staff and showed it to the assembly, saying, “When the ancients reached here, why were they unwilling to stay?” The assembly was silent, so he answered for them, saying,Look: right atop the head, there is a single eye.
It is also the den of people today.You must not drive a stake into empty space.
Provisionally set up a conjured city.A thousand, ten thousand—like hemp and millet. Yet they still fall short by a bit—what a pity. A whole perch of fine falcons.
“Because their road and path lacked power.” Again he said, “Ultimately, how is it?” and againIf you try to sort it out on the road, you are still short by half a month’s journey. Even if you gained strength, what could you do? How could there be not even a
single one?A thousand people, ten thousand people—just sit right here. Among a thousand or ten thousand, one or two might understand,
answered for them, saying, “With a shoulder-pole across the arms, not looking back at people, he goes straight into a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks
and off he goes.”and they still deserve thirty blows—because they carry a plank, and you can see their cheeks from behind. Do not associate with them.

Can you all discern the hermitage master of Lotus Peak? His heels have not even touched
the ground. In the early days of the state he set up a hermitage on Lotus Peak at Tiantai. After the ancients attained
the Way, in that thatched hut and stone chamber, in a three-legged pot, they boiled wild vegetable roots
and ate to pass the days. They did not seek fame or profit, but were broad and free, going along with conditions. They offered a turning word, and
wished to repay the kindness of Buddhas and patriarchs, transmitting the Buddha’s mind-seal. As soon as they saw a monk come, they would pick up the staff
and say, “When the ancients reached here, why were they unwilling to stay?” For more than twenty
years, in the end not one person could answer. Just this one question contains both provisional and real, both
illumination and function. If you know his boundaries and sketch, you do not need even a pinch. Tell me, why
for twenty years did he ask like this? Since it is what a lineage master does, why did he only
guard a single stake? If you see within this, naturally you will not run after the dust of feelings.
Over those twenty years, how many people laid out flat words for him, presenting their views
and understanding, exhausting every trick. Even if someone did speak, it still would not reach his utmost.
Besides, though this matter is not in words and phrases, without words and phrases you still cannot discern it.
Have you not seen it said: the Way is originally without words; through words the Way is revealed. Therefore, where you test someone’s genuineness,
the moment they open their mouth you know whether they know the tune. When ancients offered a word or half a phrase, it was for nothing else—only wanting
to see whether you know there is or do not know there is. Seeing people did not understand, he answered for them, saying,
“Because their road and path lacked power.” See how he said it—naturally it accords with principle and matches the occasion.
How could it ever miss the main point? The ancients said, “Receiving words, you must understand the source; do not set up
your own rules.” People today just crash straight in—that’s all. If they get it, they get it; but
how can they help being dull and muddled? If you come before an adept, he uses the three essential phrases to stamp emptiness,
stamp mud, stamp water, to test you—then you see a square peg shoved into a round hole, with nowhere to land.
When you reach here and seek someone of the same attainment and the same verification, where can you look at the moment?
If you are someone who knows there is, open your heart and get the message through—what is impossible? If
you do not meet the right person, then roll it up and keep it in your breast. Let me ask you all: a staff is
what a patch-robed monk uses every day—why then say the road and path lack power? The
ancients, upon reaching here, were unwilling to stay. In truth, though gold dust is precious, if it gets into the eye it becomes a film.
Master Shandao of Stone Chamber, in his time, suffered dismissal and suppression. He often used his staff to show the assembly, saying,
“The Buddhas of the past are also like this; the Buddhas of the future are also like this; the Buddhas of the present are also
like this.” One day Xuefeng, in front of the monks’ hall, picked up his staff and showed it to the assembly, saying,
“This is only for people of middling and lesser capacities.” At the time a monk came forward and asked, “If one suddenly meets a
superior person, what then?” Xuefeng picked up the staff and left. Yunmen said, “I am not
like Xuefeng, smashing things into a mess.” The monk asked, “Then how is it with you, master?” Yunmen
struck him. In general, inquiry and investigation are not so many affairs: because outwardly you see there are
mountains, rivers, and the great earth; inwardly you see there are seeing, hearing, awareness, and knowing; above you see there are Buddhas to
seek; below you see there are beings to save. You must spit it all out at once. Then, through the twelve
double-hours—walking, standing, sitting, lying down—forge it into one piece. Though it is on the tip of a single hair,
it is vast as a great thousand-world of sands. Though you dwell in boiling cauldrons and burning coals, it is like being in the Land of Ease.
Though you dwell among the seven treasures and eight jewels, it is like being under thatch and weeds. These
kinds of matters—if you are a broad and complete adept, when you reach the ancients’ real place, naturally there is no need to expend
power. Seeing that no one could get a grip on him, he again questioned himself, saying: Ultimately, how is it?
Yet it still can’t be helped. He said to himself: Carry the staff crosswise on the shoulder, paying no mind to anyone, and go straight into
a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks. What then is the meaning of this? Tell me, what place does it point to
as the home ground? It’s fine if within the line there is an eye, and beyond the words there is intent. Rising by itself, falling by itself.
Letting go by itself, gathering in by itself. Haven’t you seen Venerable Yanyang? On the road he met a monk and lifted up
his staff, saying: What is this? The monk said: I don’t recognize it. Yanyang said: You don’t even recognize a single staff,
either. Yanyang again jabbed the staff into the ground once and said: Do you recognize it now? The monk
said: I don’t recognize it. Yanyang said: You cave-dweller, you don’t recognize it either. Yanyang again shouldered the staff
and said: Do you understand? The monk said: I don’t understand. Yanyang said: Carry the staff crosswise on the shoulder, pay no mind to anyone, and go straight
into a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks. When the ancients reached this point, why were they unwilling to stay?
Xuedou has a verse, saying: Who can raise it right on the spot without being cheated? Even that is rare. Ravaging the sheer
cliffs, it melts away the dark subtlety. The double barrier once opened wide; the doers have not returned together. The jade rabbit
suddenly full, suddenly lacking; the golden crow seems to fly and yet not fly. Old Lu, no one knows where he went.
White clouds and flowing water, both lingering on. Why does this mountain monk say: If you can see the cheeks behind the head,
do not associate with him. The moment you start calculating and comparing, you’re making your living in a ghost cave on Black Mountain.
If you see through it and can truly trust it, then among a thousand people, ten thousand people, you naturally cannot be netted in
and held. It still can’t be helped. Once you touch it, once you’re pressed up against it, there is naturally killing and there is living. Xuedou
understood his meaning in the words, go straight into a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks, and only then made this verse. If you want to know
where it lands, look at Xuedou’s verse:

lg.l:
Dust and sand in the eyes, dirt in the ears, a thousand peaks,Dull and muddled under three hundred eaves; blundering and crashing—what limit is there? There are still fellows like this.
ten thousand peaks, unwilling to stay.Where are you going? Tell me, what news is this?

Falling blossoms, flowing water, so vast
and boundless.Fine news indeed: the moment of a lightning flash. Waiting and thinking is wasted effort—look left, a thousand lives; look right, ten thousand kalpas.

Lift the eyebrows—where
do you go?Under the heels, add another pair of eyes. It turns out it is only here. Can you still cut off the hermit’s heel? Even so, you must reach this ground for it to work. He struck and said:
Why is it only here?

cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s verse is extremely good. There is a place to turn the body, not keeping to one corner. So he says: Dust and sand in the eyes,
dirt in the ears. This one line is a verse about the Hermit of Lotus Peak. When a patch-robed monk reaches this point,
above there is nothing to cling to, below he cuts off himself. At all times,
he is like a fool, like a blockhead. Don’t you see how Nanquan said: People who study the Way—those who are like fools and dullards
are hard to come by. Chan-yue’s poem says: I often recall Nanquan’s fine words; such fools
and dullards are still rare. Fadeng said: Who understands this meaning, making me recall Nanquan?
Nanquan also said: Seven hundred eminent monks all understand the Buddha-dharma; only
Layman Lu does not understand the Buddha-dharma—he only understands the Way. Therefore he received the robe and bowl. Now tell me:
how far apart are the Buddha-dharma and the Way? Xuedou raised it, saying: You can’t put sand in the eyes,
It won’t do. You can’t get water into your ears. If there is some fellow who can truly believe it and take hold of it,
hold fast and not be deceived by others. What are the words and teachings of patriarchs and buddhas but the clatter of a hot bowl?
Then please hang up your bowl-bag high, snap your staff in two, and I guarantee you’ll be one carefree person of the Way.
He also said: Put Mount Sumeru into the eyes; put the water of the great sea into the ears.
There is another kind of fellow who accepts others’ judgments: the words and teachings of patriarchs and buddhas are like a dragon getting water, like
a tiger leaning on a mountain. Then he should pick up the bowl-bag, shoulder the staff crosswise—he too is a carefree person of the Way.
He added: Like this won’t do; not like this won’t do either.
Only then is there no involvement. Among these three carefree people of the Way, you must choose one
to be your teacher. It must be exactly this sort of man cast from raw iron. Why? Whether he encounters a bad
circumstance or an extraordinary circumstance, before him it is all just like a dream.
He does not know of the six faculties; he does not know of morning or evening. Even if you reach such
a state, you must strictly avoid guarding cold ashes and dead fire and plunging into a pitch-black place.
You must have a single road for turning the body. Haven’t you seen the ancients say: Do not guard cold
cliffs where strange grasses grow green; sitting out the White Cloud lineage is not good. Therefore the Hermit of Lotus Peak
said: Because his road lacked strength, he had to go straight into a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks
for it to work. Now tell me: what do you call a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks? Xuedou only loved his
saying: Carry the staff crosswise on the shoulder, pay no mind to anyone, go straight into a thousand peaks, ten thousand peaks. Therefore
he composed the verse. Now tell me: where do you go? Is there anyone who knows the place you go to?
Falling blossoms, flowing water—so vast. Blossoms falling in profusion, flowing water boundless. In the instant of a lightning flash,
what is right before your eyes? Lift the eyebrows—where do you go? Why does Xuedou
not know his whereabouts? Just as this mountain monk just now raised the whisk,
tell me: where is it right now? If you people can see it, then together with
the Hermit of Lotus Peak, you can study together. If not, then under the three rafters, before the seven-foot
bed, go and investigate it.

Case twenty-six is raised: A monk asked Baizhang: What is an extraordinary matter? Baizhang said: Sitting alone on Great Hero Peak.Sound within the words; the pivot shown within the phrase—shocking
people. Having eyes, yet never seeing.A grim, bristling might across four hundred prefectures; sitting and standing alike are both defeated and flawed.
The monk bowed; Baizhang immediately struck him.Quick-witted patch-robed monks—there are such people, too—who want to see such a matter.A true master of masters—why do his words not come in plenty?
The command does not go forth in vain.

Having eyes in a critical moment, not caring about danger or death—therefore it is said: If you don’t enter the tiger’s den, how will you get
the tiger cub? Baizhang was ordinarily like a tiger with wings. This monk also did not avoid
life and death; he dared to stroke the tiger’s whiskers and asked: What is an extraordinary matter? This monk too
had eyes. Baizhang then shouldered it for him, saying: Sitting alone on Great Hero Peak. The monk then
bowed. For a patch-robed monk, it must be distinct from the intent before the question was asked. This
monk’s bowing was not like the usual. It too requires eyes. Don’t
pour out your lifelong heart and guts to others; even acquaintances should be as if not acquainted. Just this:
the monk asked: What is an extraordinary matter? Baizhang said: Sitting alone on Great Hero Peak. The monk
bowed; Baizhang struck him. See: when he lets go, all of it at once is so; when he gathers it in, he
sweeps away traces and erases tracks. Now tell me: what was the intent of his bowing? If you say it was
good, then why did Baizhang strike him? If you say it was not good, then
what is wrong with his bowing? When you reach here, you must know fortune and misfortune and distinguish
black robes and white. Only then can you stand atop a thousand peaks. This monk bowed, like stroking
the tiger’s whiskers. It only lacked the place to turn the body. Fortunately, Baizhang had eyes on the crown of his head,
and a talisman behind his elbow—illuminating and breaking open the four worlds, keenly discerning the wind that comes. Therefore he struck.
If it were someone else, there’d be no way to deal with him. This monk used the moment to meet the moment, used intent to send off
intent. That is why he bowed. As Nanquan said: Manjushri and Samantabhadra, last night at the third watch,
raised a view of Buddha and a view of Dharma—each gets twenty blows, banished to the two Iron Encircling Mountains
they go. Then Zhaozhou stepped forward and said: Master, who should eat these blows? Nanquan said: Old Wang
the teacher—what fault does he have? Zhaozhou bowed. In the house of a lineage master, you do not casually see where he
makes use of it; once you reach the point where he takes it up right on the spot, it is naturally lively and leaping. The Fifth
Patriarch formerly often said: It is like grappling in front of a horse. You must always have seeing,
hearing, sounds and forms all at once cut off. Grasp it firmly and be the master of it; only then do you see Baizhang.
Now tell me: when he lets it pass, how is it? Look at Xuedou’s verse:

lg.l:
In the patriarchs’ domain they crisscross—celestial colts; in the teaching gate,One appears in five hundred years; among a thousand or ten thousand there is one, or half of one—son inheriting the father’s work.
unfurling and rolling up, not the same road.It is already before the words. He gets his freedom—grant him the methods of a true hand.

Lightning flash, flint spark—
the pivot of change is kept alive.It comes straight at your face. Turn left, turn right—do you still see where Baizhang acted for people, or not?

Laughable are those who come to stroke the tiger’s
whiskers.Give him thirty blows. Where heavy rewards are offered, brave men will appear—yet one cannot avoid losing body and life. Let the acharya off by one move.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou saw it through, and only then composed the verse. A celestial colt travels a thousand miles in a day, running across
and running straight, galloping as if flying—only then is it called a celestial colt. Xuedou’s verse praises Baizhang
within the patriarchs’ domain: running east toward west, running west toward east—coming and going,
seven vertical, eight horizontal, with not the slightest obstruction—like a celestial colt, skilled at crisscrossing
and racing. Only then do you see the place of freedom. This is precisely having obtained Mazu’s great pivot and great
functioning. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Mazu: What is the great meaning of the Buddha-dharma? Mazu immediately struck him,
saying: If I don’t strike you, people everywhere will laugh at me. Another asked: What
is the meaning of the Patriarch’s coming from the West? Mazu said: Come closer and I’ll tell you. The monk came closer.
Mazu slapped him across the face and said: Six ears do not share the same scheme. See how he thus attains great
freedom. Within the gate of establishing and transforming, sometimes he rolls up, sometimes he unfurls; sometimes unfurling is not within rolling up,
place. Sometimes the rolling up is not in the unrolling; sometimes the rolling up and unrolling are both not there. Therefore,
they say: same road, different tracks. In this verse, Baizhang has such a knack. Xuedou
says: Like lightning, like sparks from striking stone, he preserves the pivot of adaptation. He praises this monk as sparks from struck stone, like flashing
lightning light. It all lies in that bit of adaptive pivot. Yantou said: Rejecting things is above; chasing
things is below. If you speak of the struggle, each one stands at the turning point. Xuedou said: If the mechanism’s
wheel has not yet turned, once it turns it must run to both ends. If it cannot turn, what use
is there? A true man must also recognize a bit of adaptive pivot. Nowadays
people only keep supplying him with provisions; the other party bores through their nostrils. What end will there be?
This monk, amid lightning and sparks from stone, could preserve the adaptive pivot, and so he bowed. Xuedou
said: It’s laughable, someone comes to stroke the tiger’s whiskers. Baizhang is like a great beast.
It’s laughable that this monk went to stroke the tiger’s whiskers.

Pointer says: Ask one, answer ten. Raise one and illuminate three; see the rabbit and loose the hawk. Use the wind to
blow the fire. Not sparing your eyebrows, set that aside for now—when entering the tiger’s den, how is it?
Try to bring it up and see.

Case 27. A monk asked Yunmen: When the trees wither and the leaves fall, how is it? Yunmen said: The body reveals the golden wind.What season is this? The family is broken,
the people are gone. People gone, family broken.Propping up heaven and bracing earth; cutting nails and slicing iron—clean and naked, bare and bright.
Walking level into the blue sky.

If you can realize it right here, only then do you see where Yunmen meets people. If not,
it is still just calling a deer a horse—blind eyes, deaf ears. Who reaches this
realm? Tell me, was Yunmen answering his words, or was he trading
lines with him? If you say he answered him, you mistake the fixed star. If you say he was harmonizing with him,
you at least avoid irrelevance. Since it’s not like that, what is it, after all? If you
see through it, a patch-robed monk’s nostrils need not be pinched even once. If not, still
you get driven into a ghost cave. In general, to uphold the school’s vehicle, you must with your whole body
shoulder the burden, not sparing your eyebrows, throw yourself across the tiger’s mouth, letting it drag you sideways or pull you down.
If not like this, how could you work for people? This monk raised this question-point, and it is not
without steepness. If you look at it as an ordinary matter, he seems like a monk who minds other people’s business.
But judged within the gate of patch-robed monks, looking into the lifeline, there is no harm in saying there is something subtle
there. Now tell me: when trees wither and leaves fall, whose realm is that? Among the eighteen questions,
this is called a discerning-the-host question; it is also called a borrowing-the-occasion question. Yunmen did not shift
a hair’s breadth; he only said to him: The body reveals the golden wind. The reply is exceedingly fine, and it also does not
dare fail the point of the question. For his asking-place has eyes, and the answering-place is also straight
and true. The ancients said: If you want it intimate, do not use a question to ask a question. If it is one who knows the tune,
once it is raised, he immediately knows where it lands. If you go fishing in Yunmen’s verbal pulse, then you
have already missed it. It’s just that in Yunmen’s phrases he often likes to provoke sentimental interpretations. If you take it as
a sentimental interpretation, you won’t avoid losing my sons and grandsons. Yunmen likes to ride the thief’s horse
to chase the thief. Didn’t you see a monk ask: What is the place beyond thinking? Yunmen said: Discriminating feelings
are hard to measure. This monk asked: When trees wither and leaves fall, how is it? Yunmen said: The body reveals the golden
wind. In this phrase he does not fail to block the vital pass, allowing no traffic for ordinary or holy. You must understand his raising
one to illuminate three, raising three to illuminate one. If you go seeking in his three phrases, then an arrow
is pulled from the back of your head. Within his one phrase, the three phrases must be present: the phrase that encloses heaven and earth; the phrase that follows
waves and chases currents; the phrase that cuts off the multitude of streams. Naturally it fits perfectly. Among Yunmen’s three phrases,
tell me, which phrase does he use to meet people? Try to discern it. Verse says:

lg.l:
The question has its lineage; the answer matches it too.Deeply discerning the coming wind, the arrow never misses.How could there be two kinds? Like a bell waiting to be struck. The effort does not
get squandered.

With three phrases you can discern it—one arrowhead flying far in open space.High, middle, low—what line is it now? You must take it up outside the three lines for it to work.
Middle—passes. Hit and clack, hit and clack; the arrow passes beyond Silla.

Over the wide plain, cool gusts rustle and sweep.Under all heaven, over the whole earth—do you still feel your bones and hairs stand on end?
So? Let it pass on.

Across the long sky, sparse rain is misty and dim. YouWind vast and mighty; water boundless. Boundless above the head, boundless beneath the feet.
don’t see the long-sitting guest of Shaolin who has not yet returned,There is also a fellow who cannot say a word, dragging others to their deaths. From the headwaters of the Yellow River, it pours right
through.

quietly leaning on Bear’s Ears, cluster after cluster.With eyes open asleep, it hits; with eyes closed, it hits. Making a living in a ghost den—blind eyes, deaf ears—who can reach
this realm? You cannot avoid having your flat teeth smashed.

cb:div.p:
The ancients said: To receive words you must understand the lineage; do not set up rules on your own. The ancients’ words were not
empty contrivance. Therefore it is said: whenever you ask about a matter, you must recognize a bit of like and dislike.
If you don’t recognize high and low, advance and retreat, don’t recognize purity and contact, and you babble whatever comes to mouth, what
benefit is there? Whenever you speak and breathe out words, it must be like pincers, like shears—having hooks and
locks; it must be continuous and unbroken. This monk’s asking-place has a guiding principle; Yunmen’s
answering-place is the same. Yunmen commonly meets people with three phrases; this is the ultimate
standard. Xuedou’s verse on this case is similar to his verse on the Dalong case. With three
phrases it can be discerned; within one phrase, three phrases are contained. If you can discern it, you penetrate beyond the three phrases
altogether. One arrowhead in far-off space—an arrowhead is the metal point of an arrow. It shoots too far; you must quickly
fix your eyes on it to see. If you see it clearly, then beneath a single phrase
you can unfold the great thousand worlds like grains of sand. Here, having finished the verse, Xuedou still has talent to spare; therefore
he opens it up and chants: Over the wide plain, cool gusts rustle and sweep; across the long sky, sparse rain
is misty and dim. Tell me—is it mind or is it scene, is it dark mystery or is it subtle wonder? The ancients said: Each and every dharma
does not hide; in ancient and modern times it is always revealed. He asked: when trees wither and leaves fall, how is it?
Yunmen said: the body reveals the golden wind. Xuedou’s intent is just to make it one scene. Right before your eyes
now, the wind blows and brushes—if it is not a southeast wind, it is a northwest wind. You must straightaway
understand it just like that. If you further make a Zen-and-Dao kind of understanding, it has nothing to do with it.
Don’t you see—the long-sitting guest of Shaolin who has not yet returned: when Bodhidharma had not yet returned to the Western Heaven,
he faced the wall for nine years, quietly and still. Tell me—is that trees withering and leaves falling, or tell me
is that the body revealing the golden wind? If you go from here, all ordinary and holy of past and present, the vast
heaven and earth are hammered into one piece—only then do you see Yunmen and Xuedou’s true way of meeting people. Quietly
leaning on Bear’s Ears, cluster after cluster. Bear’s Ears is Shaolin on Mount Song near the western capital. The front
mountain has thousands of clusters, and the back mountain also has thousands of clusters. Where do you all
see from? Do you still see where Xuedou meets people? It is also like the numinous tortoise dragging its tail.

Case 28. Nanquan visited Baizhang, the Nirvana master. Baizhang asked: Of all the sages of old,
is there still a dharma that is not spoken for people?The master surely knows: a wall standing ten thousand fathoms—do you still feel your teeth falling out?
Nanquan said: There is. Baizhang said: What is the dharma that is not
Fallen into the weeds already. What is Meng Balan doing, that there is such a matter?
spoken for people? Nanquan said: NotWatch how he does it; watch him flustered, hands busy, feet in chaos—turning wrong into wrong. Just try asking and see.
mind, not Buddha, not a thing. Baizhang said: Spoken.Sure enough he accepts a defeat; sure enough he leaks and lets out quite a lot.
Nanquan said: I’m just like this.Don’t tell it to him outright; let him be wrong for a whole lifetime. You should not have said it to him like that.
Master, how are you? Baizhang said: IFortunately there is a place to turn around. Given long, it is long; given short, it is short. When long appears, then
am also not a great good-knowing advisor; how would I know whether there is speaking or not speaking? Nanquan said: I do not understand. Baizhang said: I have really spoken for you too muchwatch him flustered, hands busy, feet in chaos—hiding the body yet revealing
the shadow. Ten parts headed for death. Thorns in rotten mud—so it is. You tricked meinto it—how could it be like this? Fortunately
it is that he does not understand; if he understood, he would smash your head. Fortunately this fellow is only like this.
already.Frost added on top of snow—what is the use of a dragon head and a snake tail?

At this point, there is no need for mind-is or mind-is-not; no need for not-mind or not-not-mind.
Straight down from head to toe, not even one eyebrow hair remains—still it is a bit short.
Mind-is and mind-is-not—Master Shou called these affirming interpretation and negating interpretation. This is the Nirvana
master, Chan Master Fazheng. In former times at Baizhang he served as West Hall; he opened
the fields and spoke the great meaning. At that time Nanquan had already seen Mazu, but he was just going about
to various places to decide and test. Baizhang posed this one question—it is very hard to answer—saying: of those from
the sages of old, is there still a dharma not spoken for people? If it were this mountain monk, I would cover
my ears and leave, watching this old fellow’s whole muddle. If it were a real adept, seeing him
ask like this, he would immediately see through him. Nanquan simply went by what he saw and said
there is. He too is like Meng Baluo. Baizhang then took the mistake and followed it, and next said: what
is the dharma not spoken for people? Nanquan said: Not mind, not Buddha.
Not a thing. This fellow greedily watches the moon in the sky and loses the pearl in his palm. Baizhang said:
Spoken. What a pity—he pierced it for him with a note. At the time, he should have simply struck him across the back with a staff,
to make him know pain and itch. Even so, tell me: at what point is the speaking
done? According to Nanquan’s view: not mind, not Buddha, not a thing—he never
spoke anything. Now I ask you all: why then say, Spoken? His
words leave no trace either. If you say he did not speak, why then did Baizhang
say it like that? Nanquan is one who can adapt; immediately he pressed forward and said: I
am just like this—Master, how are you? If it were someone else, they would inevitably be unable to avoid
coming off clumsy and out of step. But Baizhang is an adept; his answering place is not without its peculiar wonder, and so he
saying, “I’m not some great spiritual friend either. How would I know whether there is speaking or not speaking?” Nanquan
then said, “I don’t understand.” Did he truly understand and say “I don’t understand,” or was he really
not understanding? Baizhang said, “I’ve already explained it for you too much.” Tell me, what
place is the place of speaking? If they were mud-ball fellows, the two would just muddle together.
If both were adepts, it is like a bright mirror set on a stand. In truth, the first two
were both adepts; the latter two both let it pass. If you are a man with eyes, clearly test
it. But tell me, how do you test him? Look at Xuedou’s verse, where he sang:

lg.l: Patriarchs and Buddhas have never been for people.Each keeps to his own boundary; if there is a branch, cling to the branch. Remember the original footing in your heart—entering hell is like an arrow.

Patch-robed monks, past and present, race with lowered heads; a bright mirror stands on the platform.Wear out straw sandals, snap the staff, and hang the bowl bag high.
The images arrayed are each distinct.Fall, and break. Break the mirror and meet me face to face.

Each one faces south and looks at the Northern Dipper.Do you still see this old monk
riding the Buddha hall out through the mountain gate? In Silla it once ascended the hall; in Great Tang the drum has not yet been struck.
The dipper handle hangs down.You do not even know where it lands—where is it?

There’s nowhere to seek it; when you pick up the nostrils, you lose the mouth.Blind—what a pity. A bowl falls to the ground; a plate shatters into seven or eight pieces.
Where did you get this news? If it really is so, then strike.

cb:div.p:
Old Shakyamuni appeared in the world; for forty-nine years he never spoke a single word. Beginning from
the land of Radiant Splendor, ending at the Bati River—between those two, he never once spoke
a single word. Said like this—tell me, is it speaking or not speaking? Now the Dragon Palace
fills the ocean treasury; then how is it not speaking? Haven’t you seen what Master Xiushan said:
“If all Buddhas had not appeared in the world, there would be the forty-nine years of teaching. If Bodhidharma had not come from the West, Shaolin
would have its subtle secret.” He also said, “The Buddhas have never appeared in the world, and there is not a single dharma given to people.
They only observe the minds of living beings, respond to the illness according to the occasion, give medicine and prescribe methods; thus there are the three
vehicles and the twelvefold teachings. In truth, patriarchs and Buddhas, from ancient times to the present, have never for people
spoken. This very not-for-people is exactly worth investigating. This mountain monk often says: if you add
a single sentence, sweet as honey—look closely, it is precisely poison. If you split
the spine with a staff at once, slap the mouth on the spot, and shove him out—only then is it intimate, for the sake of people.
Patch-robed monks, past and present, race with lowered heads. Everywhere they ask “is it?” and also ask “isn’t it?” They ask
the Buddha, ask the patriarchs, ask upward, ask downward. Even so, if you haven’t reached this
ground, you can’t avoid it. It is like a bright mirror on its stand, with images all distinct. All it takes is one
sentence to distinguish it clearly. The ancients said, “The ten thousand forms and all phenomena are what a single dharma
seals.” And they also said, “All phenomena and the ten thousand forms are wholly rounded within this.” Master Shenxiu
said, “The body is the bodhi tree; the mind is like a bright mirror-stand. Constantly polish it diligently; do not
let it gather dust.” Daman said, “He’s only outside the gate.” Xuedou said it like this:
tell me, is it inside the gate or outside the gate? You people each have an ancient
mirror. All phenomena and the ten thousand forms—long and short, square and round—each appears within it. If you
go to understand it in terms of long and short, in the end you won’t be able to find it. Therefore Xuedou said: a bright mirror
stands on its platform, the images arrayed are distinct—yet it must be that each one faces south and looks at the Northern Dipper. Since it is
facing south, why does it look at the Northern Dipper? If you can understand it like this, then you see
the place where Baizhang and Nanquan met. These two lines of verse praise Baizhang’s pressing. Baizhang
said, “I’m not some great spiritual friend either; how would I know whether there is speaking or not speaking?” Xuedou
when he came to this, if he versified it he would fall into dead water. Fearing people would misunderstand, he then lifted it up himself
and said: “Right now, before your eyes, the dipper handle hangs down—where else will you go to seek it? As soon as you
grab the nostrils, you lose the mouth; if you grab the mouth, you lose the nostrils as well.”

The instruction says: When fish move, the water turns muddy; when birds fly, feathers fall. Clearly distinguish host and guest; thoroughly divide
black-robed and plain-clothed. It is just like a bright mirror on its stand, a luminous pearl in the palm. When a Han appears, a Hu comes; sound
is displayed and form is revealed. Tell me, why is it like this? Try raising an example and see.

Case Twenty-Nine: A monk asked Dasui, “When the kalpa fire rages and the great thousand worlds are all destroyed, I wonder
does this get destroyed or not?” Dasui said, “Destroyed.”What is this thing? This one line cannot be grasped by patch-robed monks under heaven—scratching in advance, waiting for the itch.
The monk said, “In that case, it goes along with it.”A holeless iron hammer is hurled right at your face, wiping out the nostrils. Before you open your mouth, it has already been seen through.
Dasui said, “It goes along with it.”Turning within the word-thread of an immeasurable great person—sure enough, you misrecognize it.The first arrow is still light; the later arrow is deep. Just this—how many
people cannot find it. As the water rises, the boat rises; the more mud, the bigger the Buddha. If you say follow him, where is it? If you say do not follow him, then how will you be? Then strike.

Dasui Zhenru, the master, was successor to Master Da’an. He was from Yanting
County in Dongchuan. He visited more than sixty spiritual friends. In former times, at Guishan’s assembly
he served as the cook. One day Guishan asked, “You’ve been here for several years, yet you still don’t
know how to bring a question and show it—how about that?” Dasui said, “What should you have me ask
to make it?” Guishan said, “You just don’t know to ask, ‘What is Buddha?’” Dasui used his hand to
cover Guishan’s mouth. Guishan said, “After this, you won’t even find someone to sweep the ground.” Later
he returned to Sichuan. First, by the roadside at Pengkou Mountain, he boiled tea to receive travelers, for three
years. Only afterward did he come forth in the world, founding a monastery and residing at Dasui. A monk asked, “When the kalpa fire rages
and the great thousand worlds are all destroyed, I wonder: is this destroyed or not?” This monk was only relying on doctrinal
meaning to ask. In the teachings it says: formation, abiding, decay, emptiness; when the three disasters arise in a kalpa, the destruction reaches to the third
dhyana heaven. This monk, from the start, didn’t know where the critical point of the saying lands. Tell me, what kind of
person is this? Many make an interpretive explanation, saying: “This is the original nature of sentient beings.” Dasui
said, “Destroyed.” The monk said, “In that case, it goes along with it.” Dasui said, “It goes along with it.”
Just this—how many people make interpretive explanations and cannot find it. If you say “it goes along with it,”
where is it? If you say “it doesn’t go along with it,” then how is it? Haven’t you seen it said:
“If you want it to be intimate, don’t use a question to ask a question.” Later a monk asked Master Xiushan,
“When the kalpa fire rages and the great thousand worlds are all destroyed, I wonder: is this destroyed or not?” Master Xiushan
said, “Not destroyed.” The monk said, “Why is it not destroyed?” The master said, “Because it is the same as the great
thousand.” “Destroyed” also blocks and kills people; “not destroyed” also blocks and kills people. Since that monk
didn’t understand Dasui’s words, it’s no wonder he kept this matter on his mind. So he
held onto this question and went straight to Touzi Mountain in Shuzhou. Touzi asked, “Where have you come from lately?”
The monk said, “Dasui in Western Shu.” Touzi said, “What sayings does Dasui have?” The monk then raised
the previous exchange. Touzi burned incense and bowed, saying, “In Western Shu an ancient Buddha has appeared in the world. You
should quickly return.” The monk returned again to Dasui, but Dasui had already passed away. The monk was
utterly frustrated. Later the Tang monk Jingzun inscribed on Dasui: “Clearly there is no other
dharma—who says only Yinnan can? One sentence, ‘it goes along with it,’ and patch-robed monks run over a thousand mountains. Crickets
chirp cold among the courtyard leaves; ghosts at night bow to the shrine-lamp. After chanting, outside the lonely window, he paces with regret
that he cannot bear.” Therefore Xuedou later brings out these two lines in his verse. Now, too,
you must not take it as “destroyed,” and you must not take it as “not destroyed.” In the end, how
do you understand it? Look closely, right now.

lg.l:
In the blaze of the kalpa fire he sets up the point of questioning; the patch-robed monk still sticks in two layersWhat are you saying? You are already wrong.
of barriers.Cut this person off while sitting—how can he be saved? A hundred turns, a thousand layers—yet there is still toe and sole.

Pitiful—one sentence, “it goes along with it,”
Patch-robed monks under heaven make calculations like this—ten thousand lines are still not enough. What is so hard about cutting off where his heel stands?

ten thousand miles, anxiously alone,
going and returning.Karmic consciousness is vast and dim; he misses it and does not know. It is just that he has worn through his straw sandals.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou, seizing the moment, sang it out; within the lines there is a place to emerge. “In the blaze of the kalpa fire he sets up
the point of questioning; the patch-robed monk still sticks in two layers of barriers.” Where this monk asked, he first carried “destroyed” and
“not destroyed”—that is the two layers of barriers. If you are someone who has attained it, saying “destroyed” also has a place to emerge,
and saying “not destroyed” also has a place to emerge. Pitiful—one sentence, “it goes along with it,” ten
thousand miles, anxiously alone, going and returning. This verse is about the monk holding this question to Touzi, and then returning again
to Dasui—indeed, “ten thousand miles, anxiously alone.”

Case Thirty: A monk asked Zhaozhou, “I have heard the master personally met Nanquan—is that so?”
Zhaozhou said, “Zhenzhou produces big radish heads.”Hearing a thousand times is not as good as seeing once—squeezing the brows into an eight-shaped mark.Propping up heaven and bracing earth; cutting
nails and slicing iron. The arrow passes beyond Silla; cheeks seen from behind—do not associate.

This monk too was someone long engaged in practice; in his question there is no harm in having eyes. But Zhaozhou
was an adept, so he answered him: “Zhenzhou produces big radish heads.” It can be called
tasteless talk, blocking and cutting off people’s mouths. This old fellow is very much like a thief who steals in plain sight—
the moment you open your mouth, he swaps out your eyes. If you are an outstanding, heroic
and numinous fellow, then right in the spark from striking stone, in the flash of lightning, as soon as you hear it raised,
you lift it and go. If you so much as pause to think or halt the mechanism, you won’t avoid losing your body and your life.
Chengsan Shengpan of Jiangxi called this “asking in the east and answering in the west,” and named it “not answering.”
Don’t step into his trap. If you understand it like that, how could you get it? Master Yuanlu said, “This is
a sidelong remark,” and stored it among the nine bands. If you understand it like that, you haven’t even dreamed the dream.
It’s there. And it drags Zhaozhou along with it. Some say Zhenzhou has always produced huge
radishes; everyone under heaven knows it. And everyone under heaven knows Zhaozhou always paid visits to Nanquan.
Yet this monk went on to ask, I have heard the master personally met Nanquan,
is that so? So Zhaozhou said to him, Zhenzhou produces huge radishes. For the moment,
it has nothing to do with it. You do not understand it like that at all. So how do you finally understand it? His own house
has a road that reaches through the night. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Jiufeng, I have heard the master personally met Yanshou,
is that so? Jiufeng said, Has the wheat in front of the mountain ripened yet? That directly matches Zhaozhou’s
reply to this monk. It’s just like two iron hammers with no holes. Old man Zhaozhou is
a man with nothing to do. If you casually ask him, he’ll swap out your eyes. If
you are someone who knows there is something here, chew it carefully and swallow. If you are someone who doesn’t know there is something here, it’s
like swallowing a jujube whole.

lg.l:
Zhenzhou produces huge radishes; monks everywhere under heavenEveryone under heaven knows it—strictly avoid speaking it. Each time it is raised, it is each time renewed.
take it as the standard,Yet if not like this, who needs these idle words and long talk?

knowing only from ancient times to the present,Half open, half shut—like
hemp and millet. From ancient times it was not like this; even now it is not like this.

quibbling over swan white and crow black.The whole pivot is keen and free. The long are long of themselves.
The short are short of themselves. One who recognizes it is precious; there is no need to discriminate.

Thief, thief, the monk’s nostrilsBah—nothing else. It is just carrying the cangue and submitting the confession.
have ever been pinched.Passing through it already—twisting and tearing,

cb:div.p:
Zhenzhou produces huge radishes. If you take that as the ultimate standard, you are already wrong.
Even the ancients, taking someone by the hand up a high mountain, could not avoid the bystanders’ laughter. Everyone
knows this is a phrase of ultimate standard, yet in the end they do not know where the ultimate standard lies. Therefore
Xuedou said, monks everywhere under heaven take it as the standard, knowing only from ancient times to the present, and arguing
over swan white and crow black. Though they know people today answer like this, and people of old also answered like this, how
have they ever distinguished black and white? Xuedou said, you must go into it
in the spark struck from stone, in the flash of lightning, and there distinguish swan white and crow black. The gong’an reaches here
and the verse is done. Xuedou brings out his own intent, and from the living, lively place he says to you again:
Thief, thief, the monk’s nostrils have ever been pinched. The buddhas of the three times are also thieves. The patriarchs
through the generations are also thieves. Skilled at being thieves, they swap out people’s eyes without laying a hand
or foot on them. Only Zhaozhou is granted approval. Tell me, where is it that Zhaozhou is good at being a thief?
Zhenzhou produces huge radishes.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Three, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwuend.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Four, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu

cb:div.p:
The pointer says: Move and the shadow appears; awaken and ice forms. If you neither move nor awaken,
you cannot avoid falling into the wild fox den. If you penetrate through and can fully trust it, there is not the slightest obstruction
or obscuring. Like a dragon gaining water, like a tiger leaning on a mountain. Let go, and even tiles and stones give off light; hold fast,
and even true gold loses its color. The ancients’ gong’an cannot avoid roundaboutness. Tell me, what side of the matter
are you going to judge and discuss? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 31. Magu, carrying his staff, came to Zhangjing. He circled the meditation seat three times, shook his staff once
and stood upright. Zhangjing said, Yes, yes. Xuedou’s remark says, Wrong. Magu then went to Nanquan, circled the meditation seat three times, shook his staff onceThe look of Caoxi is copied and stripped out in one mold, enough to startle heaven and shake earth.In the mud,
washing a clod of dirt—tricking a whole boatload of people to death. What kind of talk is this? A donkey-tethering stake.You must not let it pass; it is still short by
one move.
and stood upright. Nanquan said, Not.
Still washing clods in the mud, then hauling them back again. A shrimp cannot jump out of the dipper.
Not. Xuedou’s remark says, Wrong. Ma-Why not take responsibility? Killing without blinking—what kind of talk is this?You must not let it pass.
gu said at the time, Zhangjing said yes; master, why do you say not?
Nanquan said, Zhangjing is indeed yes; youWhere is the true person? This fellow has always been taking people's tongues—he has let it slip too.
are not. This is turned by the power of wind.Fine. To kill you must see blood; to help people you must carry it through. How many people has he fooled?
It will in the end decay and collapse.Sure enough, he was covered and trapped by him. But what can you do about yourself?

The ancients on pilgrimage traveled through monasteries everywhere, keeping only this matter in mind: they wanted to discern whether those old masters on the curved-record wooden bed
had eyes or lacked eyes. If one word matched, they stayed; if one word did not match, they left.
Look at Magu going to Zhangjing:
he circled the meditation seat three times, shook his staff once, and stood upright. Zhangjing said, Yes,
yes. The killing blade, the life-giving sword. It must be a person of the house. Xuedou said,
Wrong. It falls into two sides. If you understand it by going to the two sides, you won’t see Xuedou’s intent. He
stood upright; tell me, what is it about? Why did Xuedou instead
say wrong? Where is his wrong? Zhangjing said yes; where is the yes?
Xuedou is like sitting and reading a verdict. Magu carried off that one character yes and then went
to see Nanquan. Just the same, he circled the meditation seat three times, shook his staff once, and stood
upright. Nanquan said, Not, not. The killing blade, the life-giving sword. It must be a true
master of the house. Xuedou said, Wrong. Zhangjing said yes, yes; Nanquan said not,
not. Are they the same or different? The first said yes; why is that also
wrong? The latter said not; why is that also wrong? If you can realize it under Zhangjing’s phrase,
you cannot even save yourself. If you can realize it under Nanquan’s phrase, you can take the patriarchs and buddhas
as your teachers. Even so, a monk must affirm it for himself before it counts.
Don’t just seize on people’s eloquence. Since his question was the same, why does one
say yes and one say not? If you are a person of broad capacity who has gained great liberation,
you must have another way of living. If you do not forget the mechanism and the situation, you will certainly
get stuck on these two ends. If you want to clearly distinguish ancient and modern and sit cutting off the tongues
of everyone under heaven, you must clearly take up these two wrongs. When it comes to Xuedou’s later verse,
he also only versifies these two wrongs. Xuedou wants to lift up the lively, living place, and so it is
like this. If you are a man with blood under the skin, naturally you won’t make interpretations inside words and phrases.
You won’t make principles on the donkey-tethering stake. Some say Xuedou made these two wrongs on behalf of
Magu; what does it have to do with anything? They do not know that the ancients’ remarks
lock off the vital checkpoint. This side is also it, that side is also it, yet in the end it is not
on these two ends. Qing the storehouse master said, Carrying the staff and circling the meditation seat, both yes and not are
wrong. In truth it also isn’t here. Haven’t you seen Yongjia go to Caoxi to see the Sixth
Patriarch: he circled the meditation seat three times, shook his staff once, and stood upright. The patriarch said,
A monk possesses three thousand dignified forms and eighty thousand subtle practices. Great Virtue, from what
direction have you come, giving rise to great arrogance? Why did the Sixth Patriarch say he gave rise to great
arrogance? Here it is not said to be yes, and not said to be not. Yes and not
are both donkey-tethering stakes. Only Xuedou’s placing of two wrongs is somewhat better. Ma-
gu said, Zhangjing said yes; master, why do you say not? This old fellow
does not spare his eyebrows, and he lets out quite a lot. Nanquan said, Zhangjing is indeed yes; you
are not. Nanquan can be called seeing the rabbit and releasing the hawk. Qing the storehouse master said, Nanquan is
far too sloppy. Not, then stop. Instead he gave him a further path: This is turned by the power of wind,
and in the end it will decay and collapse. The Perfect Enlightenment Sutra says, My present body is a combining of the four elements:
hair and body hair, nails and teeth; skin, flesh, tendons, bones; marrow and brain, grime and color, all return
to earth. Spit and snot, pus and blood all return to water. Warmth returns to fire. Movement and turning return
to wind. When the four elements each separate from this illusory body, where will it be? Magu
carrying his staff and circling the meditation seat, since it is turned by the power of wind, will in the end decay and collapse. Tell me,
in the end, where is the matter that illuminates the mind’s source? When it comes to here, you also must
be a man cast from raw iron. Haven’t you seen the scholar Zhang Zhuo?
He called on Chan Master Zang of Xitang and asked, Are mountains, rivers, and the great earth existent or nonexistent? The
buddhas of the three times, are they existent or nonexistent? Zang said, Existent. Scholar Zhang Zhuo said, Wrong. Zang
said, What person have you previously called on? Zhang said, I called on the master of Jingshan.
Whatever I asked about, Jingshan always said nonexistent. Zang said, What family do you have?
Zhang said, I have a mountain wife and two foolish, stubborn ones. Then he asked,
