What family does Jingshan have? Zhang said, Jingshan is an ancient buddha; master, don’t slander him.
Zang said, When you reach the level of Jingshan, then you can say nonexistent to everything.
Zhang Zhuo lowered his head and that was all. In general, a true master wants to help people loosen what clings
and remove bonds, pull nails and extract wedges. You cannot merely hold to one side, pushing left then turning right, pushing
right then turning left. Just look at Yangshan going to Zhongyi to thank Xiejie. Zhongyi saw him coming and
clapped his hands on the meditation seat and said, Master. Yangshan then stood on the east side, then on the west side,
then stood in the center. After that he thanked Xiejie, then stepped back and stood behind. Zhongyi
He said, “Where did you get this samadhi from?” Yangshan said, “From the seal of Caoxi,
I took it off there and brought it out.” Zhongyi said, “Tell me—using this samadhi, whom does Caoxi receive?
Yangshan said, “He receives the awakened in a single night.” Then Yangshan again asked Zhongyi, “Master,
where did you get this samadhi from?” Zhongyi said, “I got this samadhi from Mazu.
Speaking like this—wouldn’t that be to raise one and make three clear? Those who see the root
and chase the branches—fellows like that. Longya addressed the assembly, saying, “Those who study must break through the patriarchs
and buddhas before it will do. Master Xinfeng said, ‘When you see the words and teachings of the patriarchs and buddhas, treat them like a mortal enemy.
Only then do you have a share in study. If you cannot break through, you will be deceived by the patriarchs and buddhas.’ At that time there was
a monk who asked, ‘Do the patriarchs and buddhas still have any intention to deceive people?’ Longya said, ‘Tell me—do rivers and lakes
have any intention to obstruct people?’ And he also said, ‘Though rivers and lakes have no intention to obstruct people,
it is simply that people of the time cannot pass through. Therefore rivers and lakes instead become an obstruction to people.
You must not say that rivers and lakes do not obstruct people. Though the patriarchs and buddhas have no intention to deceive people,
it is simply that people of the time cannot penetrate through. Therefore the patriarchs and buddhas instead become deceivers of people.
You must not say that the patriarchs and buddhas do not deceive people. If you can break through the patriarchs and buddhas,
then you pass beyond the patriarchs and buddhas. Yet you must embody the intent of the patriarchs and buddhas—only then is it in accord with what is above. The ancients were the same.
If you have not broken through, and you just study buddhas and study patriarchs, then for ten thousand kalpas there will be no time of attainment. Again he asked,
‘How can one avoid being deceived by the patriarchs and buddhas?’ Longya said, ‘You must awaken by yourself.
When you get here, it must be like this to succeed.’ Why? ‘To help people, you must help them through. To kill,
you must see blood.’ Nanquan and Xuedou were people like this—only then did they dare to take it up and handle it. The verse says:
It says:

lg.l:
This wrong, that wrong—strictly avoid picking it up and discarding it.Spare your eyebrows. Act according to the command: In heaven and on earth, I alone am honored.Two holeless
iron hammers. Even Thousand-Hand Great Compassion could not lift them. If you pick it up, the acarya takes thirty blows.

When the waves of the four seas are level,No one under heaven dares to touch it. East and west,
north and south, all the same family style. Lately there has been much rain.
the tides of the hundred streams recede.Cleanly naked, bare and bright—at least you yourself are at peace, until seas are calm and rivers clear.

The ancient goad—its wind is high over the twelve gates; gate after gateHow does it compare to this? The tip of the staff has no eyes—strictly avoid making a living scheme on the staff.
has a road, empty and desolate.Not a single thing—cheating you your whole life. Look at it and you go blind.

Not desolate—Indeed. Luckily there is a place to turn around.
Already blind—then strike.

the adept is good at seeking medicine when there is no illness.Once dead, never alive again. In the twelve hours, why do you doze
and sleep? What are you doing, groping heaven and grabbing earth?

cb:div.p:
This one verse is like Deshan’s case of seeing Guishan. First he takes the case,
adds two turning words, threads them into a single strand, and then brings out the verse. “This wrong, that
wrong—strictly avoid picking it up and discarding it.” Xuedou’s meaning is: wrong here, wrong there—
strictly avoid picking it up and discarding it. If you pick it up and discard it, you go astray. It must be like this: establish these two
wrongs, and straightaway the waves of the four seas are level and the tides of the hundred streams recede—how delightful, pure wind and bright moon.
If you can understand within these two wrongs, there isn’t the slightest thing left. Mountains are mountains,
waters are waters. The long are long, the short are short. Five days a wind, ten days
a rain. Therefore it is said: the waves of the four seas are level, the tides of the hundred streams recede. After that he versifies Magu holding up his staff:
“The ancient goad—its wind is high over the twelve gates.” The ancients used a whip as a goad.
In the house of patch-robed monks, the staff is the goad. At the Queen Mother of the West’s Jasper PoolIn the Matters of the Patriarchal Courtyard, the old record cites the Sutra of Raising the Staff.
there are twelve vermilion gates. The ancient goad is precisely the staff. The clear wind above it is higher
than the twelve vermilion gates. The places where the Son of Heaven and Indra dwell likewise each have
twelve vermilion gates. If you understand these two wrongs, light is born on the head of the staff—
you won’t even need the ancient goad. The ancients said, “Recognize the staff, and the work of a lifetime of study is finished.”
They also said, “It is not a matter of empty forms and false matters; this is the Tathagata’s precious staff itself,
a true trace.” Things of this kind. When you arrive here, tumbling head over heels, at all times
you attain great freedom. “Gate after gate has a road, empty and desolate.” Though there is a road, it is only
empty and desolate. When Xuedou gets here, he feels he has let something slip, so he breaks it open for you again. Yet
even so, there is also a place that is not desolate. Even an adept—when there is no illness—still
must first go ask for some medicine to take.

The pointer says: Sitting cut off in the ten directions, a thousand eyes suddenly open. One phrase cuts the stream; ten thousand impulses
are laid to rest and shaved away. Is there anyone who dies together and lives together? The ready-made case cannot be bundled up
and set down. The tangled vines of the ancients—try to bring them up and see.

[Thirty-two] Raised: Senior Seat Ding asked Linji, “What is the great meaning of the Buddha Dharma?” Linji came down from the meditation bench, grabbed him, and gave him a slap, thenHow many people arrive
here bewildered—yet this is still here. Why act so sloppy?
shoved him away. Ding stood there. A monk beside him said, “Why don’t you bow, Senior Seat Ding?” Ding then bowed, and suddenly had a great awakening.Today he is caught and exposed. Old-woman mind is urgent. Patch-robed monks of the world cannot jump out of it.Already fallen into a ghost cave—missed it. Still
you cannot avoid losing your nostrils.In the cold ground someone sees through it, and wholly obtains
his power. The east neighbor dies, the west neighbor helps mourn.Use diligence to make up for clumsiness.Like the dark getting a lamp; like
the poor getting treasure. Take error as error. Tell me, what did Seat Ding see that he immediately bowed?

Look at him like this—straight out, straight in; straight there, straight back—this is Linji’s true
school, with such function. If you can break through, you can turn heaven and earth over, and
gain your own use. Senior Seat Ding was such a fellow: struck by Linji’s slap, when he rose from bowing
he immediately knew where he had landed. He was a man from the north, most plain and direct. After he got it,
he never again went out into the world. Later he used Linji’s whole device—no harm in his sharpness and freedom.
One day on the road he met three men—Yantou, Xuefeng, and Qinshan. Yantou then asked,
“Where have you come from?” Ding said, “Linji.” Yantou said, “Ten thousand blessings to the master.” Ding said, “He has already
passed on.” Yantou said, “The three of us went especially to pay our respects, but our good fortune is shallow
and meager, and we met his return to stillness. We wonder: while the master was alive, what sayings did he have? Please, Senior Seat,
raise one or two for us to see.” Ding then raised Linji’s words from one day when he addressed the assembly, saying: “On the red
flesh lump there is a true person of no rank, constantly coming in and out through the face-gate of you people.
Those who have not yet proven it, look, look!” At that time a monk stepped out and asked, “What is the true person of no rank?”
Linji immediately grabbed him and said, “Speak, speak!” The monk tried to consider it; Linji immediately shoved him away
and said, “True person of no rank—what dried shit-stick is that?” Then he returned to the abbot’s quarters. Yantou,
without noticing, stuck out his tongue. Qinshan said, “Why not say ‘not the true person of no rank’?” Ding grabbed him
and said, “The true person of no rank and the not-true person of no rank—how far apart are they?
Quick, speak, quick!” Qinshan had no words—his face turned sallow, then green. Yantou and Xuefeng
came forward and bowed, saying, “This newly ordained one doesn’t know good from bad and has offended the Senior Seat. We beg
for compassion—please let him go.” Ding said, “If not for these two old fellows, I would kill
this bed-wetting brat.” Again, returning from a meal offering in Zhenzhou, he rested on a bridge and met three
lecture-masters. One asked, “What is the deep place of the Chan river, where one must reach the very bottom?” Ding
grabbed him and was about to throw him off the bridge. The other two lecture-masters hurriedly pleaded, “Stop, stop!
He is the one who offended the Senior Seat—please show compassion.” Ding said, “If not for you two lecture-masters,
I’d let him reach the bottom.” Look at such methods—this is entirely Linji’s
function. Now look again at Xuedou’s verse:

lg.l:
At the boundary’s end, the whole device continues the later track—when you bring it forth, why must itThe Yellow River is muddy from its source. The son inherits the father's trade.
be in leisurely ease?Where is it? Yet there are people like this—can someone with no hands or feet still get him or not?

When the giant spirit lifts his hand, there is not
much to it—Scaring people to death. Stop showing off. One whisk strike—and he will not examine again.

he splits Mount Hua through ten thousand layers.Heaven and earth
and the great earth are revealed all at once. Fallen.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s verse: “At the boundary’s end, the whole device continues the later track—when you bring it forth, why must it be in leisurely ease?”
Huangbo’s great opportunity and great function—only Linji alone continued his track. When you take it up and bring it forth,
it allows no deliberation. If you hesitate, you fall into the shadowy realm. The Shurangama Sutra says,
“When I snap my fingers, the sea-seal shines; when you so much as raise a thought, dusty toil arises first.” The giant spirit
lifts his hand—there is not much to it; he splits Mount Hua through ten thousand layers. The giant spirit has great
divine power: with his hands he cleaves Great Hua and lets the waters flow into the Yellow River. Senior Seat Ding’s
doubt-mass was like mountains heaped and peaks piled—struck by Linji’s slap, it directly shattered
and melted away like ice.

The pointer says: Not distinguishing east from west, not dividing south from north—from morning to dusk, from dusk
to morning—do you still say he is dozing? Sometimes his eyes are like shooting stars—do you still say he is
awake? Sometimes he calls south “north.” Tell me—is it with mind, or without mind?
Is he a person of the Way, or an ordinary person? If you can penetrate here, only then do you know where it lands; only then
do you know the ancients’ “like this” and “not like this.” Tell me—what season is it? Try to raise it
and see.

[Thirty-three] Raised: Minister Chen Cao went to see Zifu. As soon as Zifu saw him coming, he drew a circle.
Chen Cao said, “When a disciple comes like this,The keen recognize the keen; thieves recognize thieves. If you are not contained and grounded, how will you recognize this fellow? Do you still see the diamond ring?
it already does not hit the mark—how much more so drawing a circle?” Zifu then shut the door to the abbot’s quarters. XuedouToday he ran into a dozing fellow.
This old thief—thieves do not rob the poor. Already he has entered his snare.
said, “Chen Cao only has one eye.”Xuedou has eyes in the crown of his head—tell me, where is his intent? It is also good to give it a round mark.
Clearly a dragon head and snake tail. Back then it would have been good to give him one squeeze, so that advancing has no gate and retreating has no road. Tell me, what further squeeze would you give him?

Minister Chen Cao, a Shangshu, lived in the same time as Pei Xiu and Li Ao. Whenever he saw a monk come, he would first
invite him to a meal, and give three hundred coins as support. But he insisted on testing them. One day Yunmen came to pay a visit,
and he asked, I will not ask about the Confucian books. As for the three vehicles and the twelvefold teachings, there are lecturers
for that. What is the matter of a patch-robed monk's travels? Yunmen said, Shangshu, you once
asked how many people have come. Chen said, I am asking you right now, Venerable. Yunmen said, For now, set that aside.
What is the meaning of the teachings? Chen said, Yellow scrolls with red rollers. Yunmen said, This
is words and language. What is the meaning of the teachings? Chen said, The mouth wants to speak,
but the phrasing is lost; the mind wants to grasp, but thought ceases. Yunmen said, The mouth wants to speak, but the phrasing is lost
is in response to there being words. The mind wants to grasp, but thought ceases is in response to deluded thoughts. What, then,
is the meaning of the teachings? Chen had no reply. Yunmen said, I hear Shangshu reads the Lotus Sutra, is that
so? Chen said yes. Yunmen said, The sutra says: All managing of livelihood and production
does not go against true reality. Tell me, in the Heaven of Neither-Perception-nor-Non-Perception, right now how many
people have stepped down from their rank? Chen again had no reply. Yunmen said, Shangshu, do not be hasty. Masters among monks
cast aside the Three Scriptures and Five Treatises and enter the monasteries. After ten or twenty years, they still
cannot do anything about it. How could Shangshu possibly understand? Chen bowed and said, I have faults and
offenses. Another day he climbed a tower with other officials. They saw several monks coming. One official
said, Those who are coming are all Chan monks. Chen said, They are not. The official said, How do you know
they are not? Chen said, Wait until they come closer and I will test them for you. When the monks reached the front of the tower, Chen suddenly
called out, Venerable. A monk raised his head. The Shangshu said to the officials, If you do not believe me, only
Yunmen is the one. I cannot test him. He went to pay respects to Muzhou, and one day
he went to visit Zifu. When Zifu saw him come, he immediately drew a circular sign. Zifu was a senior disciple in the line of Weishan
and Yangshan, and he usually liked to receive people by presenting a scene. When he met Minister Chen Cao,
he drew a circular sign. But Chen was a capable man and would not be fooled.
He examined himself and said, For a disciple to come like this already misses the mark; how could I
go and draw a circular sign as well? Zifu shut the gate. A case like this is called discerning the point within words,
and hiding the mechanism within a phrase. Xuedou said, Chen Cao has only one eye. Xuedou
can be said to have an eye on the very top of his head. Tell me, where is the intent? It would also be fine to give
a circular sign. If it is all like this, how can patch-robed monks guide people? I will ask you:
if, at that time, you all were in Chen Cao's place, what words could you put down
to avoid Xuedou saying he has only one eye? Therefore Xuedou
overturned it and made this verse:

lg.l:
Round and round, pearls encircle jade; clinking, clattering, the horseA three-foot staff stirs the Yellow River—only a blue-eyed barbarian monk can manage it. Cast from raw iron.
carries donkeys and camels onto an iron boat.What use is so much? What limit is there? Let me show you.

Hand it over to sea and mountains,
to the carefree traveler.Some people do not want it. Even an idle guest cannot use it—it must be one who has nothing going on.

When fishing for the great turtle, cast down
a looped snare.Coming like this, going like this—you cannot get out for the time being. If it is just shrimp, what can be done? How will shrimp, clams, snails, and mussels manage? You must fish for the giant turtle.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou again said, Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out ofAnd the body is inside; bury it in one pit—can you still jump
out or not?

Round and round, pearls encircle jade; clinking, clattering. The horse carries donkeys and camels onto an iron boat. Xuedou, on the spot,
versified it. He only versified that circular sign. If you can understand it, it is like a tiger wearing horns.
This bit of it requires the bottom of the bucket to drop out, the mechanisms exhausted; gain and loss, right and wrong
all released at once. Do not take it as reasoning, and do not take it as something mysterious.
In the end, how do you understand it? You must look at it as the horse carrying donkeys and camels onto an iron
boat. Only here can you see it. Elsewhere it cannot be handed over. You must take it and
hand it over to the carefree traveler of sea and mountains. If you have even a little something in your belly, then
you cannot bear it. Here you must be one for whom having matters and having no matters, going against feelings and going along with circumstances, if
Buddha or Patriarch cannot do anything about you, only then can you bear it. If there is Chan
to be practiced, if there are measures of ordinary and holy feelings, you definitely cannot bear it. If you can bear it,
then how do you understand it? He said, When fishing for the great turtle, cast down a looped snare. To fish for the great turtle
you must use a looped snare. Therefore Fengxue said, Accustomed to fishing whales and sea-monsters, clear the vast
waters, yet you sigh at frog-steps trampling mud and sand. He also said, Let not the great turtle carry the Three Mountains
away; I want to walk atop Penglai. Xuedou again said, Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump
out of it. If you are a great turtle, you will never make a patch-robed monk's understanding. If you are a patch-robed monk,
you will never make a great turtle's understanding.

[Case 34] Raised: Yangshan asked a monk, Where have you recently come from? The monk said, Mount Lu. Yangshan said, Have you visited Five Old Peaks? The monk said, I have not been there. YangshanEveryone under heaven is the same—you must also ask through it. Wind fans the fire; you must not
fail to follow the usual course.A straightforward person is hard to find.Because of conduct,
it is fine to swing your arms. When have you ever missed it?Move a single step. A red face is not as good as straight speech. It is also like forgetting the former and losing the later.
said, Venerable, you have not toured the mountains. YunmenToo many affairs arise. Better spare your eyebrows. Why is this old fellow in such deadly haste?
said, These words are all for the sake of compassion; they are talk that falls into the weeds.A killing knife, a living
sword. Two or three. To know the mountain road, you must be someone who has gone and returned.

The decisive point in testing a person is that as soon as they open their mouth you know whether they are in tune. The ancients said, A boundless great person
turns things around within the vein of words. If you have an eye on the top of your head, as soon as it is raised you know where it lands.
Look at this one question and one answer: clearly distinct. Why then did Yunmen
say, These words are all for the sake of compassion; they are talk that falls into the weeds? When the ancients came
here, it was like a bright mirror on a stand, a shining pearl in the palm: when a barbarian comes, a barbarian appears; when a Han comes,
a Han appears. Even a single fly could not pass beyond their discernment. Tell me, how
is it that for the sake of compassion there is talk that falls into the weeds? It is fine if it is steep and perilous. When you reach
this ground, you still must be a true fellow to be able to pick it up. Yunmen raised it and said, This monk
personally came from Mount Lu. Why then did Yangshan say, Venerable, you have not toured the mountains? Weishan
one day asked Yangshan, If a monk comes from the various places, what will you use to
test him? Yangshan said, I have a place to test from. Weishan said, Try raising it
for me. Yangshan said, When I usually see a monk come, I only raise my fly-whisk toward him and say,
Do the various places still have this? When he has something to say, I only say to him, This
we will set aside for now; how about that one? Weishan said, This is the fangs and claws of a person of the beyond.
Have you not seen how Mazu asked Baizhang, Where have you come from? Baizhang said, From the mountain
below. Mazu said, Did you meet anyone on the road? Baizhang said, I did not.
Mazu said, Why did you not meet anyone? Baizhang said, If I had met someone, I would have brought it up to
the master. Mazu said, Where did you get this news? Baizhang said, I have faults and
offenses. Mazu said, It is actually this old monk who has faults and offenses. Yangshan's question to the monk is just like this.
At that time, when he asked, Have you visited Five Old Peaks, if the monk were a true fellow,
he would only say, Trouble. Instead he said he had not been there. Since this monk was not capable, why did Yangshan
not act according to the command and avoid all the entangling vines that follow? Instead he said, Venerable,
you have not toured the mountains. Therefore Yunmen said, These words are all for the sake of compassion;
they are talk that falls into the weeds. If it were talk that comes out of the weeds, it would not be like this.

lg.l:
Coming out of the weeds, going into the weeds who can traceVast above the head, vast beneath the feet—half open, half closed. He is like this, and I am like this.
it out?An eye in the crown of the head—yet you do not know how to search it out.

White clouds in layers, the red sunA thousand layers, a hundred coils—putting a head atop the head.
shines bright.Broken. Blind. Raise your eyes and you are wrong.
Glancing left, no flaw;Blind fellow—still nothing is the matter. Why do you make so many tricks?

looking right, already old. Do you not see? Hanshan set out too early,One thought passes ten thousand years.A leper drags companions.
Not early at all.

for ten years he could not return, forgetting the road he came by.Right now, where is it? Clearlyhe gets his own
freedom. Let one move pass, then strike. Do not be like this—forgetting the former and losing the later.

cb:div.p:
Coming out of the weeds, going into the weeds who can trace it out? Xuedou truly knew where it landed. When you reach
here, one hand lifts, one hand grips. White clouds in layers, the red sun shines bright. It is greatly like
grass thick and soft, mist dense and veiling. Here there is not the slightest bit that belongs to the ordinary, not the slightest bit
that belongs to the holy. Across the whole world nothing has been hidden; not one thing can be covered over. This is what is called
the realm of no-mind: in cold you do not hear cold, in heat you do not hear heat. Altogether it is a great
gate of liberation. Glancing left, no flaw; looking right, already old. Master Lanzan lived in seclusion
in a stone chamber on Mount Heng. Tang Dezong heard of his name and sent an envoy to summon him. The envoy
arrived at his chamber and proclaimed, The Son of Heaven has issued a decree; the venerable one should rise and give thanks for the favor. Zan
was stirring a cow-dung fire, then took roasted taro to eat. In the cold his tears hung down to his chin, yet he never answered.
The envoy laughed and said, Let me urge the venerable one to wipe away your tears. Zan said, How could I have the leisure
to wipe tears for worldly people? In the end he did not rise. The envoy returned and reported. Dezong greatly
admired and praised him. Like this, pure and clear, plainly white, not taking orders from others.
Truly it is held fast and steady, as if cast from raw iron. Take, for example, Shandao the master:
after being expelled, he never again became a monk; people called him the Stone Chamber ascetic.
Whenever he stepped onto the pestle, he forgot to move his feet. A monk asked Linji, "The Stone Chamber ascetic forgets to move his feet—
what is the meaning of this?" Linji said, "Drowning in a deep pit." Fayen’s Verse on Perfecting True Nature says:
When principle reaches the utmost, feelings are forgotten—how could any analogy match it? In the end, the frosty night moon,
carried along by circumstance, falls into the stream before us. Fruit ripens and the monkeys grow heavy; the mountains stretch long, as if the road were lost. Raise your head—
the lingering light is there; it turns out he lives to the west. Xuedou said, "Haven’t you seen? Hanshan’s walking is
too early—ten years and he cannot return, forgetting the road he came by." Hanshan’s poem says:
If you would find a place to settle your body, Hanshan can be kept for long. A faint breeze stirs the secluded pines; listen closely,
the sound only gets better. Below there are grey-haired men, babbling as they read Huang-Lao; ten years and they cannot return,
forgetting the road they came by. Yongjia also said: Mind is the root, dharmas are dust; the two
are like marks on a mirror. When the marks and grime are gone, the light first appears; when mind and dharmas are both forgotten,
nature is the real. When you reach here—like a fool, like a stump—only then do you see this koan. If you do not
reach this ground, you only run about in words—what understanding is there?

Pointer: Settle dragons and snakes, distinguish jade from stone; separate black from white, decide what wavers. If you are not
one with eyes on the crown of your head and a tally beneath your elbow, you will often miss it right in front of you.
Right now seeing and hearing are not obscured; sounds and forms are utterly real. Tell me: is it black or is it white?
Is it crooked or straight? When you get here, how do you discern?

Case Thirty-Five: Wenshu asked Wuzhuo, "Where have you just come from?"
You cannot but ask by the way—there is also this bit of news.
Wuzhuo said, "The South." WenshuStick your head out from the grass nest—why carry it up onto your eyebrows? The great square has no outside; why then is there a south?
said, "How is the Buddhadharma maintained in the South?" WuzhuoIf you ask someone else, trouble is born. It is still hanging on lips and teeth.
said, "Bhikshus of the latter age seldom uphold the precepts." Wenshu said, "How manyA straightforward person is hard to find.
monks?" Wuzhuo said, "Perhaps three hundred, perhaps five hundred." Wuzhuo asked Wenshu, "How is it maintained here?" Wenshu said, "Ordinary and sage dwell together; dragons and snakes are mixed." WuzhuoAt that time he should have given one shout; with one squeeze he would have been squeezed down.All of them are wild-fox spirits.
Indeed, it leaked.Squeezed, he then turned the spearhead around
and came back.There are plenty of flaws—so much that feet get busy and hands get chaotic.
said, "How many monks?" Wenshu said, "Three-three in front, three-three behind."Give me back my topic! You must not let it pass.
。Upside-down words, inverted speech—tell me, how many is it? Thousand-Hand Great Compassion cannot count enough.

Wuzhuo traveled to Wutai. Midway, in a desolate place, Wenshu conjured up a monastery to receive
him for the night. Then he asked, "Where have you just come from?" Wuzhuo said, "The South." Wenshu said, "In the South,
how is the Buddhadharma maintained?" Wuzhuo said, "Bhikshus of the latter age seldom uphold the precepts."
Wenshu said, "How many monks?" Wuzhuo said, "Perhaps three hundred, perhaps five hundred." Wuzhuo then asked
Wenshu, "How is it maintained here?" Wenshu said, "Ordinary and sage dwell together; dragons and snakes are
mixed." Wuzhuo said, "How many monks?" Wenshu said, "Three-three in front, three-three behind." Then they drank
tea. Wenshu lifted up a glass cup and said, "Does the South still have this?"
Wuzhuo said, "No." Wenshu said, "Then what do you usually use to drink tea?" Wuzhuo had no words, and so took his leave
and departed. Wenshu told the youth Junti to see him to the gate. Wuzhuo asked the youth,
"Just now you said three-three in front, three-three behind—how many is that?" The youth said, "Venerable
sir!" Wuzhuo answered. The youth said, "How many is it?" Wuzhuo again asked, "What monastery is this?" The youth
pointed behind Vajra. Wuzhuo turned his head; the conjured monastery and the youth had all vanished, not to be seen.
It was only an empty valley. Later, that place was called Vajra Cave. Later a monk asked
Fengxue, "What is the lord within Clear and Cool Mountain?" Fengxue said, "Not even a single phrase
can spare you." Wuzhuo asked; to this day he is still taken for a wandering monk. If you want to penetrate it plainly and truly,
with your feet planted on solid ground, realize it right beneath Wuzhuo’s words—then even if you dwell in a cauldron of boiling soup
or among furnace coals, you will not hear heat; if you dwell upon cold ice, you will not hear cold. If you want
to penetrate it so it is solitary, perilous, steep, and austere—like the Vajra King’s jeweled sword—realize it beneath Wenshu’s
words; then water cannot splash you, wind cannot enter you. Haven’t you seen Dizang of Zhangzhou ask a monk,
"Where have you just come from?" The monk said, "The South." Dizang said, "How is the Buddhadharma there?"
The monk said, "They discuss it in great bustle." Dizang said, "How does that compare to my place here—
farming the fields to earn food to eat?" Tell me: compared with Wenshu’s reply, is it the same or different?
Some say Wuzhuo’s reply is not so, while Wenshu’s reply has dragons and has snakes, has ordinary and has sage—what does it have to do with anything?
Can you clearly discern "three-three in front, three-three behind" or not?
The earlier arrow is still light; the later arrow goes deep. Tell me: how many is it? If
you break through here, a thousand phrases, ten thousand phrases are just one phrase. If beneath this one
phrase you can cut it off and hold it steady, then in due course you arrive at this realm.

lg.l:
A thousand peaks coil and bend, their color like indigo—who says Wenshu was holding a dialogue?Do you still see Manjushri?
Even if Samantabhadra were here, he would not look back. Missed it.

Ridiculous—how many monks at Clear and Cool Mountain?Now tell me, what are you laughing at? It is already before the words.
Three-three in front and three-three behind.Try to discern it under your feet. In rotten mud there are thorns. A bowl falls to the ground; a plate becomes seven pieces.

cb:div.p:
A thousand peaks coil and bend, their color like indigo. Who says Wenshu was holding a dialogue? Some say
Xuedou merely picked it up again and did not truly verse it. But consider a monk who asked Fayen,
"What is a single drop of water from Caoxi?" Fayen said, "It is a single drop of water from Caoxi."
And a monk asked the master Langya Jue, "The original nature is pure—how did mountains,
rivers, and the great earth suddenly arise?" Jue said, "The original nature is pure—how did mountains, rivers, and the great earth suddenly arise?"
You cannot call that merely picking it up again. Mingzhao the One-Eyed Dragon also versified its meaning:
"A device that covers heaven and earth: it spreads through the world of sands, surpassing monasteries.
Everywhere you look, Wenshu is holding a dialogue; within the words you do not know to open the Buddha-eye—turn your head and you only see
the green mountain crags." "Spreads through the world of sands, surpassing monasteries" points to the conjured monastery in the grass hut; it
is what is called a device that works with both provisional and real. "Everywhere you look, Wenshu is holding a dialogue"; within the words
you do not know to open the Buddha-eye; turn your head and you only see the green mountain crags. At just such a time,
can you call it the realm of Wenshu, Samantabhadra, and Avalokiteshvara? In any case, it is not
that principle. Xuedou merely altered Mingzhao’s use, yet there are stitches and seams. "A thousand peaks
coil and bend, their color like indigo" does not harm the edge nor cut the hand. Within the line there is provisional and real,
principle and fact. "Who says Wenshu was holding a dialogue?" One night of dialogue, yet not knowing
it was Wenshu. Later Wuzhuo served as kitchen steward on Wutai Mountain; Wenshu
would appear over the porridge pot. Wuzhuo would grab the porridge-stirring paddle and strike him. Even so,
it was still like drawing the bow after the thief has passed. Back then, when he asked, "How is the Buddhadharma in the South maintained?"
he should have brought the staff down across his back at once—still that would have been closer. Ridiculous—how many monks
at Clear and Cool Mountain! In Xuedou’s laugh there is a knife; if you understand where this laugh is, then you will see
his saying "three-three in front and three-three behind."

Case Thirty-Six: Changsha one day went roaming in the mountains. When he returned to the gate, the head monk asked, "Master, where have you been?" Changsha said, "Roaming the mountains." The head monk said,Today all day long, you just keep falling into the weeds. Ahead,
it is also falling into the weeds; behind, it is also falling into the weeds.It must also be examined through.
This old fellow—his head has passed Silla.You must not fall into the weeds. The flaws are many. A weeds-dweller
"What places did you go to?" Changsha said, "At first I followed the fragrantsqueezed—if you arrive somewhere, you still cannot avoid falling into the weeds, dragging each other into the fire pit.
grasses away; then I chased the falling blossoms back." The head monk said, "It greatlyPlenty has leaked. From the start he only sat in a bramble thicket.
resembles springtime." Changsha said, "It even surpasses autumn dew drippingFollowing along. Take error as error: one hand lifts, one hand grips.
on lotus." Xuedou added a remark: "Thanks for the replyAdding mud atop earth. The earlier arrow is still light, the later arrow goes deep—what end is there to it?
words."One fire—this mud-ball fellow. The three take it in one bundle and carry it off.

Changsha, Great Master Zhaoxian of Luyuan, was a Dharma heir of Nanquan, a contemporary of Zhaozhou, Zihu,
and the like. His adaptive function was swift. If someone asked for instruction, he gave instruction; if they wanted a verse,
he gave a verse. If you wanted a meeting of adepts, he would give you a meeting of adepts.
Yangshan’s ordinary functioning was foremost. One day he and Changsha were enjoying the moon
when Yangshan pointed to the moon and said, "Everyone has this, but they cannot use it."
Changsha said, "Exactly so. Then I’ll ask you to use it." Yangshan said, "You try using it and see." Changsha
took one step and knocked Yangshan down. Yangshan got up and said, "Uncle Master, you’re like a great beast!" Later
people called him Cen the Great Beast. One day, when he returned from roaming the mountains, the head monk—also someone from his assembly—
asked, "Master, where have you been?" Changsha said, "Roaming the mountains."
The head monk said, "What places did you go to?" Changsha said, "At first I followed the fragrant grasses away; then I chased
the falling blossoms back." Only someone who sits cutting off the ten directions can do this. The ancients, in their coming and going,
never failed to keep this matter in mind. Look at how guest and host trade places; meeting the occasion, they cut straight
through—neither yields an inch. Since it was roaming the mountains, why ask, "To what
places did you go?" If it were today’s Zen fellows, they would say, "I went to Jiasan Pavilion
and came back." Look at the ancients: not the slightest calculating over principles, and no place to cling.
Therefore he said, "At first I followed the fragrant grasses away; then I chased the falling blossoms back." The head monk then
followed his intent and said, "It greatly resembles springtime." Changsha said, "It even surpasses autumn dew dripping
on lotus." Xuedou said, "Thanks for the reply words." It stands in for the final line, and yet it too falls to both
sides. Ultimately it is not on these two sides. In the past there was Scholar Zhangzhuo, who saw a thousand Buddhas
a named scripture—then he asked about the hundreds and thousands of buddhas: we only hear their names; we do not know what country
and land they dwell in—do they still transform beings or not? Sha said: after Cui Hao inscribed his poem on Yellow Crane Tower, did any scholar
ever inscribe one there again? Zhuo said: no one ever inscribed one. Sha said: when you have leisure, inscribe a piece yourself—
that would be fine. Cen Dachong, in his dealings all his life, was straight to the point—pearls turning, jade revolving—he wanted
people to understand right in their faces. The verse says:

lg.l:
When the great earth is free of the slightest dust, whose eyes do notWho is it that throws open the windows and sits facing the eaves? You cannot be lacking this at all—then the world is at peace.
open?You must release great brightness from the crown of your head. Why scatter dirt and sand?

First, follow the fragrant grasses and go;Plenty has leaked—not just a single time of falling
into the weeds. Luckily it was already said earlier:

then chase the fallen blossoms and return. A gaunt craneEverywhere is wholly true. Glad you have returned—under your feet the mud is three feet deep.
perches on a cold tree;Left then right. Add one sentence and there are many more idle matters.

a wild ape howls on an ancient terrace.Yet it is because of newly applied effort.
You cannot add a single sentence, and you cannot subtract a single sentence.

Changsha’s meaning is boundless.Then he strikes. What does the final line say? Buried in one pit.
Fallen into a ghost cave. Tsk! A fellow in the weeds. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—do not let it slip by.

cb:div.p:
Now tell me about this gong’an: it is like Yangshan asking a monk, “Where have you just come from?” The monk said, “Mount Lu.”
Yangshan said, “Have you been to Five Elders Peak?” The monk said, “I haven’t been there.” Yangshan said,
“Acarya, you have not yet toured the mountains.” Distinguish black and white and see whether it is the same or different—when you get here,
you must have all contrivances exhausted and all consciousness forgotten: mountains, rivers, the great earth; grass and weeds; people and beasts—without
the slightest seepage. If it is not so, the ancients called it still being in a superb and wondrous
realm. Have you not seen Yunmen say: even if mountains, rivers, and the great earth have not the tiniest fault,
you are still being turned by things. Not seeing a single form is only half a lifting up; you must further know
there is a time of full lifting up—one upward opening—then you can sit securely. If you penetrate it,
as of old, mountain is mountain and water is water; each abides in its own place, each matches its own essence—like
clapping hard for a blind person. Zhaozhou said: at the rooster’s crow, in the chou hour, sorrowfully I rise
and still there are leaks. I have not even a skirt or short shirt; the shape of a kasaya I have only a little,
yet my underpants have no crotch and my trousers have no opening. On my head, three to five pecks of blue-gray ash—originally it was
for cultivation and for benefiting people; who would have known it would instead turn into muttering. If you truly reach
this realm, whose eyes do not open? Let it be seven topsy-turvies and eight tumbles—every place
is this realm; all of it is this time. In the ten directions there are no walls; on all sides
there is likewise no gate. Therefore it says: first follow the fragrant grasses and go, then chase the fallen blossoms and return. Xue-
dou is quite clever: he merely sticks one line on the left side, and one line on the right side—
just like a poem. A gaunt crane perches on a cold tree; a wild ape howls on an ancient terrace.
Xuedou leads it up to here, then feels his own leakage; suddenly he says, “Changsha’s meaning is boundless.”
Bah! Like waking up from a dream. Though Xuedou gives a shout, it does not yet cut off
completely. If it were this mountain monk, it would not be so: “Changsha’s meaning is boundless”—dig the ground deeper
and bury it.

The instruction says: in the moment of a lightning flash, it is futile to stand and think; a thunderclap in midair—covering your ears
won’t help. A red banner is planted on your forehead; twin swords whirl behind your ears—if you are not
sharp of eye and sure of hand, how could you manage it? There are those who lower their heads and wait in thought, calculating
under the mind-root—unaware that before their skulls they see countless ghosts. Tell me: not falling
into the mind-root, not clutching gain and loss—if someone suddenly raises it like this and becomes aware, how will you
respond? Try raising it and see.

Case thirty-seven: Panshan offered a phrase, saying, “In the three realms there is no dharma—where can one seek the mind?”Once the arrow leaves the bowstring, it has no turning back. Moonlight clearly shows the night-
traveler. It hits. Knowing the Dharma, the elder fears; at a good harmonious sound, he strikes.Do not deceive people, all right. No need to raise it again—check it yourself. Then he strikes, saying:
What is this?

To the north in Youzhou, the master Baoji of Panshan was an honored elder under Mazu. Later
there appeared one named Puhua. As the master was nearing death, he said to the assembly, “Is there anyone who can depict
my true likeness?” The assembly all drew portraits and presented them; the master scolded them all. Puhua came forward and said,
“I can depict it.” The master said, “Why not present it to this old monk?” Puhua immediately did a somersault
and left. The master said, “From now on this fellow will receive people like a mad wind.” One
day he addressed the assembly, saying, “In the three realms there is no dharma—where can one seek the mind? The four great elements are originally empty; the Buddha
relies on what to abide? The pivot does not move; still quiescence leaves no trace. Presented face to face, there is nothing more—
no remaining matter. Xuedou picked up two lines to make a verse—truly unalloyed gold and uncut jade. Have you not
seen it said: to cure an illness, one need not donkey-camel medicine. Why does this mountain monk say, “With a harmonious voice
I strike”? Only because he bears the cangue and passes through the accusation. The ancients said: when you hear the phrase beyond the sound,
do not seek it in your thoughts. Now tell me, what is his intent like? It is like rushing streams crossing
a blade-edge; lightning turns, stars fly. If you speculate and ponder, even if a thousand buddhas appear in the world, you still will not be able to grope
it out. If you go deep into the innermost, penetrating to bone and marrow, seeing through to the bottom,
Panshan is a complete defeat. If you take his words and “understand the essential,” turning left and right, Panshan
will only get a single stake. If you drag mud and carry water, turning in the heap of sounds and forms, you have not
even dreamed of Panshan. The ancestral master Wuzu said: only by passing through to the other side do you have a share of
freedom. Have you not seen the Third Patriarch say: if you cling to it, losing measure, you surely enter a deviant path; if you let it go,
the essential nature naturally has no coming or going. If here you say “no Buddha, no dharma,” you again fall
into a ghost cave. The ancients called it the deep pit of liberation. It was originally a good cause
yet it draws an evil result. Therefore it is said: even a person with nothing to do and nothing going on still runs into the hardship of golden locks.
You must exhaust it to the very end. If in the place of no words you can speak, and in the place where you cannot act you can act,
this is called the turning point. In the three realms there is no dharma—where can one seek the mind?
If you make it into an emotional interpretation, you die right under his words. Xuedou’s insight has seven
piercings and eight holes; therefore he composed this verse:

lg.l:
In the three realms there is no dharma—where can one seek the mind? WhiteThe words are still in the ear.No need to raise it again—check it yourself. He strikes, saying: What is this?
clouds as a canopy,Setting a head atop a head—thousands upon thousands of layers.
running springs as a zither.Hear it? It follows along. The moment you hear it, it is hard not to grieve.

One tune, two tunes—no one understands;It does not fall into gong and shang, and it is not a matter of jiao and zhi. Borrowing the road to pass through—the five tones and six pitches are all distinct. Take it up and go out yourself.
If you listen, you go deaf.
after the rain, in the night pond, autumn waters are deep.A thunderclap is too fast to cover your ears. It drags mud and hauls water—where is it? Then he strikes.

cb:div.p:
“In the three realms there is no dharma—where can one seek the mind?” Xuedou’s verse is like a Huayan realm.
Some say Xuedou sings it out of nothing; if you are someone whose eyelids have split open, in the end
you will not understand it that way. Xuedou steps to the side and sticks on two lines, saying: “White clouds
as a canopy; running springs as a zither.” Su Neihan, seeing illumination and awakening, had a verse saying: the sound of the stream is precisely
the broad, long tongue; are not the mountain colors the pure body? Last night, eighty-four thousand
gathas—how will you raise them to someone another day? Xuedou borrows the running springs to make a single piece of long
tongue; therefore it says, “One tune, two tunes—no one understands.” Have you not seen Jiufeng Qian
the master say: do you recognize life? Running springs are life; clear stillness is the body. A thousand
waves competing to rise is Manjushri’s family style; a single stretch of clear sky is Samantabhadra’s realm.
Running springs as a zither—one tune, two tunes—no one understands. A melody like this
requires a true connoisseur. If it is not the right person, it is futile to cock your ear. The ancients said:
even a deaf person sings a barbarian tune; likes and dislikes, high and low, he never hears at all. Yunmen said:
raise it without regard and it is already off; if you try to think about it, in what kalpa will you awaken? Raising is essence; regarding is
function. If before it is raised, before the first signs have split, you can see it, you sit cutting off the crucial pass.
If as soon as the signs split you can see it, there is illumination and function. If after the signs split you see it,
it falls into the mind-root. Xuedou is far too compassionate; he tells you further: it is like
“after the rain, in the night pond, autumn waters are deep.” People have once weighed and discussed this single verse, praising Xue-
dou for having the talent of a Hanlin. “After the rain, in the night pond, autumn waters are deep”—you must look quickly and sharply;
if you hesitate, you will not find it.

In his instruction he said: Speaking of gradual, return to the ordinary and accord with the Way, seven vertical and eight
horizontal, right in the bustling marketplace. Speaking of sudden, leaving no trace; even a thousand sages cannot grope it out. If
you do not set up sudden or gradual, then how will it be? A sharp person, one word; a swift horse, one whip.
At just such a time, who is the one acting? Try to raise it and see.

Case 38. Raised: Fengxue, at the prefectural office in Yingzhou, ascended the hall and said: The Patriarchs’Leaning on the duke to talk Zen—what are you saying?
mind-seal is like the mechanism of an iron ox: go and it stamps and holds; stay and it stamps and breaks. But if neither going nor staying,A thousand, ten thousand people cannot shake it. Where are the distortions and rough edges? The seal of the three essentials
opens—without offending the blade’s edge.When the true command is in force—wrong!A second offense is not tolerated. Watch when the command is carried out.
He presses in—then strikes.Look—there is nowhere to set it down. How many distortions!
stamping is not-stamping, and not-stamping is stamping. At that time Elder LupeiAll under heaven, heads popping out of heads—no distinction. The brilliance is already revealed; just topple the meditation bench and shout to scatter the crowd.
stepped forth and asked: I have the mechanism of the iron ox; I ask the masterIf you fish up one who truly understands, it is not out of the ordinary.
not to apply the stamp. Fengxue said: Long used to fishing whales and sea-dragons in the clear vastA fine topic of inquiry—yet how can one avoid distortions?
deep, yet I sigh at a frog’s steps trampling mud and sand. Lupei paused,Like a falcon seizing a dove. A jeweled net fills the sky. A spirit steed runs a thousand miles.
thinking. Fengxue shouted: Elder, why not speak? As Lupei was about to consider, Fengxue struck with the whisk. Fengxue said: Do you still remember the topic? Try to raise it.What a pity. Yet there is a way out. What a pity to let it pass.Seizing the banner,
snatching the drum—raising a ruckus.Dead three times. A double-layered koan.A good beating—this one.
The command must be like this; only then can people act.
As Lupei was about to open his mouth, Fengxue struck again with the whisk.Why bother? Frost on top of snow.Once dead, it will not live again. This fellow is so dull he kills—falling into his vicious hands.
The prefect said: The Buddha-Dharma and the king’s law are the same. Fengxue said: What principle do you see? The prefect said:Clearly—yet he is seen through by bystanders.
Exposed.It is good to give him one squeeze—then the spearpoint turns back.
When you should cut, if you do not cut, you turn around and invite disorder. Fengxue then descended from the seat.It seems so, yet it is not so. You must know the bystander has eyes. When the man of the east house dies, the west house
helps with mourning.Take wrong and make it serve wrong; see the moment and adapt. For now, the matter of study is concluded.

Fengxue was a venerable elder in the Linji line. Linji, back when he was in Huangbo’s assembly,
was planting pines. Huangbo said: In the deep mountains, what are you doing planting so many pines? Linji said:
First, to make scenery for the monastery gate; second, to serve as signposts for later people. Having said that, he
hoed the ground once. Huangbo said: Even so, you have already taken twenty blows.
Linji struck the ground once more and said: Hush, hush. Huangbo said: Our school will greatly flourish in the world through you.
Weishan Zhe said: Linji like this is like taking a fall on flat ground.
Even so, when facing danger he does not change—only then is he called a true man. Huangbo said:
Our school will greatly flourish in the world through you—like doting on a child and not noticing its ugliness. Later,
Weishan asked Yangshan: At that time Huangbo entrusted it only to Linji. Was there anyone else?
Yangshan said: There was, but the era is far removed; I do not wish to bring it up before
you, master. Weishan said: Even so, I want to know—just raise it and let me see.
Yangshan said: One man points south—Wu and Yue carry out the command; when a great wind is met, it stops. This
was a prophecy of Fengxue. At first Fengxue studied under Xuefeng for five years. Because he asked for instruction, Linji
entered the hall; the head monks of the two halls together gave a shout. A monk asked Linji: Are there still guest
and host or not? Linji said: Guest and host are distinct. Fengxue said: I do not yet understand the meaning—how
is it? Xuefeng said: Long ago I went with Yantou and Qinshan to see Linji, but on the road
we heard he had already passed away. If you want to understand his talk of guest and host, you must study elders in his lineage.
Later Fengxue saw Ruiyan, who always called to his own “true person,” saying to himself,
Yes. Then he said: Stay awake; later do not let people fool you. Fengxue said: Picking it up yourself and
playing with it yourself—what’s difficult about that? Later, at Lumen in Xiangzhou, he spent the summer with Attendant Kuo,
who pointed him to go study with Nanyuan. Fengxue said: Entering the gate, you must discern the host; I earnestly
ask the master to differentiate. One day he went to see Nanyuan and brought up the earlier words, saying: I
have come specially to pay my respects in person. Nanyuan said: Xuefeng is an old Buddha. One day he met Jingqing. Jingqing
asked: Where have you just come from? Fengxue said: I came from the east. Jingqing said: Did you cross Little River?
Fengxue said: A great vessel drifts alone in empty space; Little River is not something to be crossed. Jingqing said: Mirror
waters and painted mountains—birds can fly but do not cross. Don’t steal-hear leftover words. Fengxue said: Even the blue sea
still fears the force of armored ships; set sails across the ranks of rivers and cross the Five Lakes. Jingqing raised the whisk
and said: What about this? Fengxue said: What is this? Jingqing said: As expected,
you do not recognize it. Fengxue said: Appearing and vanishing, rolling up and unfurling—we use it together. Jingqing said: Divining
by ladle and tortoise, listening to empty sounds; deep sleep is full of babble. Fengxue said: Broad marshes hide mountains; in principle
you can subdue leopards. Jingqing said: Forgive crimes and release faults—quickly get out. Fengxue said: If I go out,
I lose it. So he went out to the Dharma hall and said to himself: A great man—if a public case
is unresolved, how could I just stop? He turned back and re-entered the abbot’s quarters. Jingqing was sitting; Fengxue then
asked: Just now I rashly presented a foolish view and presumed upon your honored presence. I have received the master’s
compassion and have not been blamed. Jingqing said: Just now you came from the east—wasn’t it from Cuiyan?
Fengxue said: Xuedou personally perches east of the jeweled canopy. Jingqing said: Not chasing the lost sheep,
the mad explanations cease—yet you come here reciting poems. Fengxue said: When you meet a swordsman on the road, you must
show your sword; if he is not a poet, do not offer poems. Jingqing said: Quickly hide the poem; lend me
the sword’s edge for a moment. Fengxue said: The man at the first steamer carries the sword away. Jingqing said: Not only
do you touch off a response to conditions, you also reveal your own dullness. Fengxue said: If I do not touch off a response,
how would the old Buddha’s mind be made clear? Jingqing said: What is called the old Buddha’s mind? Fengxue again said: Grant me
another allowance—what do you have now, master? Jingqing said: A patched-robed fellow from the east cannot even tell beans from barley. Fengxue said: I have only heard
of taking what is not taken—how could you suppress taking what is taken? Jingqing said: Great waves surge a thousand fathoms; the clear
ripples do not leave the water. Fengxue said: One phrase cuts off the current; the ten thousand activities are laid to rest and pared away. Fengxue then bowed
in homage. Jingqing tapped three times with the whisk and said: Splendid! Sit and have tea. When Fengxue
first arrived at Nanyuan, he entered without bowing. Nanyuan said: Entering the gate, you must discern the host.
Fengxue said: I earnestly ask the master to differentiate. Nanyuan slapped his left knee once with his left hand; Fengxue then
shouted. Nanyuan slapped his right knee once with his right hand; Fengxue also shouted. Nanyuan raised his left hand and said: This
comes from you, practitioner. Then he raised his right hand and said: And what about this one? Fengxue
said: Blind! Nanyuan then picked up his staff. Fengxue said: What are you doing? I will seize the staff
and strike the master with it—don’t say I didn’t warn you. Nanyuan immediately threw down the staff and said: Today
I’ve been dulled once by this yellow-faced fellow from Zhejiang. Fengxue said: The master is very much like
someone unable to hold a bowl, yet pretending not to be hungry. Nanyuan said: Practitioner, haven’t you ever come here before?
Fengxue said: What kind of words are those? Nanyuan said: Ask well, ask well. Fengxue said: I
won’t let it pass either. Nanyuan said: Sit and have tea. You see—this fine fellow naturally has sharp
and steep edge. Even Nanyuan had not yet discerned him. By the next day, Nanyuan simply
asked in the ordinary way: Where were you this summer? Fengxue said: At Lumen, spending the summer with Attendant Kuo.
Nanyuan said: So you really have personally met a master. And he asked: What did he
say to you? Fengxue said: From start to finish he only taught me to always be the host.
Nanyuan then struck him and drove him out of the abbot’s room, saying: A fellow like this, taking in defeat and defects—what
use is he? From then on Fengxue submitted in his heart and served in Nanyuan’s assembly as the garden head. One
day Nanyuan came to the garden and asked: How do they discuss “a single blow” in the south? Fengxue
said: They discuss it in an extraordinary way. Fengxue asked: Master, how do you discuss it here?
Nanyuan raised his staff and said: Under the staff, the patience of the unborn; meeting the moment, not yielding even to the master. Fengxue
suddenly had great awakening. At that time, during the upheavals of the Five Dynasties, the prefect of Yingzhou invited the master
to spend the summer. In those days the Linji school was flourishing greatly. In all his question-and-answer instructions,
his phrases were sharp and fresh, gathered like flowers and brocades; every word had its landing. One
day the prefect invited the master to ascend the hall. He instructed the assembly, saying: The Patriarchs’ mind-seal is like the
mechanism of an iron ox: go and it stamps and holds; stay and it stamps and breaks. But if neither going nor staying,
stamping is not-stamping, and not-stamping is stamping. Why? It is not like the mechanism of a stone man or wooden horse.
Straight on, it is like the mechanism of an iron ox—there is nowhere you can shake it. The moment you go, it stamps
and holds; the moment you stay, it stamps and breaks. It shatters you into a hundred fragments. But if neither going nor
staying, stamping is not-stamping and not-stamping is stamping. Seeing him instruct like this, it can be said there is bait
on the hook. At that time among the seats was Elder Lupei, also a venerable elder in the Linji line,
who dared step forward to meet him on the spot. He then turned the topic and posed
a question-point—indeed extraordinary—saying: I have the mechanism of the iron ox; I ask the master
not to apply the stamp. But Fengxue was a master; he answered: Long used to fishing whales and sea-dragons
in the clear vast deep, yet I sigh at a frog’s steps trampling mud and sand. His words had an echo within them.
Yunmen said: Casting the hook into the four seas, he only fishes ferocious dragons; the extraordinary profound mechanism is to seek
a true one who knows. The vast deep is the twelve-headed water buffalo used as hook and bait, yet he only fished up
a single frog. These words have no mystique, and there is no principle to weigh and calculate.
The ancients said: If you look at it in things, it is easy; if you divine and measure beneath the root of intention,
it has nothing to do with it. Lupei paused in thought—seeing it and not taking it is hard to meet in a thousand years. What a
pity. Thus it is said: Even if you can expound a thousand sutras and treatises, once you face the moment,
it is hard to speak. In truth, Lupei wanted to seek a pleasing phrase to answer him, not wishing to carry out the command; but
Fengxue consistently used the edge that seizes the banner and snatches the drum, pressing him onward without pause.
With no choice but to submit. As the common saying goes: when the battle is lost, you cannot forbid even a rush broom from sweeping. At the outset
you still wanted to go fetch spear techniques to fight him. By the time you fetch them, your head will already be on the ground.
The Prefect Mu had long studied under Fengxue, and understood that the Buddha Dharma and the king’s law are the same. Fengxue
said, What do you see? The Prefect Mu said, If what should be cut off is not cut off, it only invites further disorder.
Fengxue was wholly a single mass of spirit, like a gourd floating on water. Press it
and it turns; hold it down and it moves. He could speak the Dharma according to the occasion; if he did not accord with the occasion, it would turn
into false speech. Fengxue then descended from the seat. Now Linji has the talk of four guest and host. As for
those who study the Way, you must be very careful. When guest and host meet, there are words discussing the guest
and host coming and going; or responding to things as forms appear, functioning with the whole body; or wielding devices and expedients in joy
and anger; or manifesting half the body; or riding a lion; or riding an elephant king. If there is a true
student, he gives a shout. First he produces a lacquer basin. If a good spiritual friend cannot discern
it as a circumstance, he climbs onto that circumstance, putting on airs and posturing. Then the student shouts again.
The one in front refuses to put it down. This is an illness lodged in the vitals, beyond treatment. This is called
the guest watching the host. Or there is a good spiritual friend who does not bring out any thing, but whatever the student
asks, he immediately snatches away. The student, once snatched from, will not let go even to the death. This is the host watching the guest.
Or there is a student who presents a pure, clean circumstance before a good spiritual friend. The friend
discerns it as a circumstance and flings it into a pit. The student says, What a fine good spiritual friend!
The friend at once says, Tsk! You do not know good from bad. The student bows. This is called
the host watching the host. Or there is a student wearing a cangue and chains who comes before a good spiritual friend.
The friend adds another layer of cangue and chains. The student is delighted. Neither side
can discern it. This is called the guest watching the guest. What the monks of Great Deshan cited is all about distinguishing demons and selecting
what differs, knowing what is crooked and what is straight. Have you not seen a monk ask Ciming: With one shout you separate guest and host; when illumination
and function operate at the same time, what then? Ciming immediately shouted. And Master Yunju Hongjue
instructed the assembly, For example, when a lion seizes an elephant, it uses all its strength; when it seizes a rabbit, it also
uses all its strength. At that time a monk asked, What strength is it that is used in full? Yunju said, The strength
that does not deceive. Look at Xuedou’s verse as it comes forth.Hard as iron, sharp through and through—like an owl, severing heads and hanging them upside down.

lg.l:
Capture Lu Po, stride the iron ox, the three mysteries;Among thousands upon thousands, one must still display skill. A defeated general is not beheaded again.
spear and armor are not repaid lightly.Those inside the game are confused. Receive disaster as if receiving blessing; accept surrender as if accepting an enemy.

By the walls of the King of Chu’s city, the waters
flow toward the sea in homage;What talk of waters returning to the sea? Vast and flooding, it fills heaven and earth—though it be the four seas, they too must flow backward.

under a shout, it once was made
to flow back.If not for this one shout cutting off your tongue—tsk! It startles the iron ox of Shan Prefecture into flight, and scares the great elephant of Jiazhou to death.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou knew that Fengxue had such a house style, so he versified: Capture Lu Po
and stride the iron ox. The three mysteries’ spear and armor are not repaid lightly. Under Linji there are the three mysteries and the three
essentials. In every single phrase, the three mysteries must be present; within one mystery, the three essentials must be present. A monk
asked Linji, What is the first phrase? Linji said, When the seal of the three essentials is opened, the vermilion
dot is narrow; before deliberation is allowed, host and guest are distinguished. What is the second phrase? Linji
said, Subtle discernment does not allow Wuzhu’s question; Ouhe does not fail the device that cuts off the stream. What
is the third phrase? Linji said, Just watch the puppets played atop the shed; pulling and hauling
wholly relies on the person inside. In one phrase Fengxue already embodies the three mysteries’ spear and armor. The seven
affairs attend the body; he does not repay him lightly. If it were not like this, what could he do about Lu Po? Later,
Xuedou wants to bring out the edge of Linji’s devices. Do not say Lu Po is a fake; even if
by the walls of the King of Chu’s city the vast flood surges and white waves billow to the sky, all going to pay homage to the sea—still
it takes only one shout, and it must be made to flow backward.

Instruction said: What is used on the road is like a tiger leaning on a mountain. What spreads through worldly truth
is like a monkey in a cage. If you want to know the meaning of Buddha-nature, you must observe time, season, and conditions. If you want to forge
refined gold a hundred times over, it must be an expert’s furnace and bellows. Tell me: when great function appears before you,
with what will you test it?

Case 39. A monk asked Yunmen, What is the pure Dharma-body? Yunmen said, A flower-and-medicine railing. The monkOn a heap of trash, you see a sixteen-foot golden body.
Mottled and speckled—what is it?The question is not true where it is asked; the answer comes crude and reckless. It bumps and hits—crookedness cannot hide straightness.
said, If one goes on like that, what then? Yunmen said, A golden-hairedA whole lump swallowing a jujube—why play the fool?
lion.Both praise and blame. Two colors, one throw of the dice. Taking wrong to serve wrong—what kind of mind-practice is this?

Do you all know where the monk’s question is and where Yunmen’s answer is? If you know it,
two mouths share not a single tongue. If you do not know it, you cannot avoid dull confusion. A monk asked Xuansha,
What is the pure Dharma-body? Xuansha said, Dripping with pus. Possessing vajra eyes.
Try to discern it and see. Yunmen was not like other people. Sometimes he pins things down, standing like a wall ten thousand
fathoms high, leaving you nowhere to approach. Sometimes he opens for you a single thread of a path, dying together, living together.
Yunmen’s three inches were very tight. Some say he answered offhand with random colors. If
you understand it that way, then tell me: where does Yunmen end up? This is business inside the house;
do not divine it outwardly. Therefore Baizhang said, The dense array of all phenomena, and all
words and language, all turn back to oneself. Make them turn rolling and rolling; then, in the lively, quick
place, speak. If you speculate and think it over, you fall into the second phrase. Yongjia
said, When the Dharma-body is realized, there is not a single thing; the original source and self-nature is the naturally true Buddha. Yunmen
tested this monk. That monk too was someone inside his house; he had long studied and knew the matters
inside the house. He pressed on, If one goes on like that, what then? Yunmen said, A golden-haired
lion. Tell me, was he approving him or not approving him? Was he praising him or blaming him? Yantou
said, Speaking of battle, each one stands at the turning point. And he also said, He studies living phrases,
not dead phrases. If one realizes under a living phrase, one never forgets through endless ages. If one realizes under a dead phrase,
one cannot even save oneself. Again a monk asked Yunmen, Is the Buddha Dharma like the moon in water
or not? Yunmen said, In clear waves there is no path through. The monk pressed on, Master, from where
do you get it? Yunmen said, Ask again: where do you come from? The monk said, When it is exactly like this, what then?
Yunmen said, Layer upon layer of barrier-mountain roads. You must know that this matter is not in words and phrases.
It is like striking stone and sparks, like a flash of lightning. Whether you contrive it or cannot contrive it, you cannot avoid losing
body and life. Xuedou was someone of that kind, so he immediately brought forth a verse on the spot.

lg.l:
Flower-and-medicine railing—do not be muddle-headed. The star is in the scale,The words are still in the ear.Like hemp, like millet—there is also a bit you must take up and go out with yourself.
not in the pan.Too much tangled vine. Each of you turn back and look beneath your robe—yet you cannot avoid talking principle.

Just like that—tooA whole lump swallowing a jujube.
groundless.Take it up and go out yourself. Clearly—do not wrongly blame Yunmen.

A golden-haired lion—everyone, look!Let one out;
even half of one is still a dog. Yunmen too is a man of Puzhou, sending off the thief.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou plays command after command at the mat, plucking the string to make a different tune, judging it phrase by phrase as he goes.
This one verse is no different from the style of citing the old. Flower-and-medicine railing—he immediately says, Do not be muddle-headed.
Everyone says Yunmen answered casually with random colors, and they all understand him by emotional interpretation.
Therefore Xuedou sets down the fundamental and says, Do not speculate—so he says, Do not be muddle-headed. For Yunmen’s
intent is not at the flower-and-medicine railing. Therefore Xuedou says, The star is in the scale,
not in the pan. This line leaks and reveals too much. In water there was never any moon; the moon is in the blue
sky. Like the star in the scale, not in the pan. Tell me, which is the scale? If
you can discern it clearly, you will not disappoint Xuedou. When the ancients got here, it was still no hindrance
to be compassionate: plainly telling you, It is not here; go to the other side. Tell me,
what place is that other side? That is the first line at the head of this verse. In what follows he versifies
the monk’s saying, If one goes on like that, what then? Xuedou says, This monk is also too
groundless. Tell me, does it fit the bright side or the dark side? If you understand, you speak like this; if you do not understand,
you still speak like this. A golden-haired lion—everyone, look! Do you see the golden-haired lion
or not? Blind.

Instruction said: Let it go, let it rest—an iron tree blooms. Is there, is there, a sly one
who slips through? Even if you go seven vertical and eight horizontal, you still cannot avoid having your nose pierced. Tell me,
where is the error and confusion? Try to raise it and see.

Case 40. Minister Lu Gen was conversing with Nanquan. Lu said, Master Sengzhao
said, Heaven and earth and I share the same root; the ten thousand things and I are one body. That is quite
strange. Nanquan pointed to the flower in front of the courtyard and called the minister, saying, People of today see this one flower as if in a dream.Making a living in a ghost cave. A painted cake cannot satisfy hunger. It is also bargaining in the weeds.Saying it like that.
Tsk! Sutras have sutra teachers, treatises have treatise teachers—this has nothing to do with this mountain monk. Tsk! A true man then could utter one turning phrase: not only cutting off Nanquan, but also giving all patched-robed monks under heaven
a chance to breathe.
。The mandarin ducks are embroidered—look all you like. Do not pass the golden needle on to others. Do not talk in your sleep; it will lure the orioles down from the willow branches.

Minister Lu Gen had long studied with Nanquan, and ordinarily kept his attention on principle and nature, roaming
through Sengzhao’s Treatise. One day while sitting, he raised these two lines, taking them to be extraordinary, and asked
He said, Master Zhaolun declared: Heaven and earth and I share the same root; the ten thousand things and I are one body.
It is indeed strange. Master Zhaolun was a great monk of the Jin era, together with Sengzhao, Sengrong, and Sengrui, sharing
studies under Kumarajiva; they were called the Four Sages. In his youth he loved reading Zhuangzi and Laozi; later, because
he copied the old Vimalakirti Sutra and had an awakening, he then realized that Zhuangzi and Laozi were still not fully adequate. Therefore
he synthesized the sutras and composed the Four Treatises. The intent of Zhuangzi and Laozi is that heaven and earth are vast in form;
my form is likewise, and we are born together within emptiness. Zhuangzi’s main point only
argues the leveling of things. Lord Zhao’s main intent discusses nature as all returning to oneself; he does not take up their doctrine
of the Middle Way. As for the utmost person: empty and open, without appearance, and the myriad things are none other than what I make. To gather
the myriad things as oneself—only a sage can do that. Though there are spirits, there are humans, there are
worthies and there are sages—distinct, yet all share one nature, one body. The ancients said: throughout the whole
heavens and earth, it is just one single self. When cold, all heaven and all earth are cold. When hot,
all heaven and all earth are hot. When there is, all heaven and all earth are. When there is none, all heaven
and all earth are none. When it is, all heaven and all earth are it. When it is not, all heaven and all earth are not.
Fayan said: You, you, you; I, I, I. North, south, east, and west are all acceptable; not
acceptable, acceptable. Yet only I have nothing that is not acceptable. Therefore it is said: in heaven above and earth below, only I
alone am honored. Shitou, because he read Zhao’s Treatise, came to this point of taking the myriad things as oneself.
He suddenly had a great awakening. Later he wrote a version of the Cantongqi, which also does not depart from this meaning. Look at
how he asks: tell me, the same as what root, the same as which body? When you get to
here, it is not without its strangeness. How could it be like ordinary people who do not know the height of heaven or the thickness of
the earth? How could there be such a thing? Minister Lu Geng asked in this way—strange indeed,
very strange—yet it still does not go beyond the meaning of the teachings. If you say the meaning of the teachings is the ultimate standard, then why did the World-Honored One
pick up the flower again? Why did the patriarch come from the West? Where Nanquan answered, he used the monks’
noses to point out the sore spot, to smash his nest and burrow. Then he pointed to the flowers in the courtyard
and called to the minister. People today see this single flowering plant as if in a dream, as if
leading someone to a ten-thousand-zhang cliff and giving a shove, cutting off his life. If you
knock down Maitreya’s descent on level ground, you still will not understand life being cut off. It is also
like someone in a dream: about to wake yet not waking, when someone calls him awake. If Nanquan
had crooked eyes, he would certainly have been smeared over and led away by him. Watch him speak
like this—it is not without being hard to grasp. If one’s eyes are steady and lively, the living hear it.
It is like the finest flavor of ghee. If the dead hear it, it turns into poison. The ancients
said: if you see it in terms of events, you fall into ordinary feelings. If you divine it from the root of thought,
in the end you cannot find it. Yantou said: this is the livelihood of one who goes upward; it only reveals
a little right before the eyes, like a lightning flash. Nanquan’s main intent is like this: he has
the hands and feet to seize tigers and rhinoceroses, to settle dragons and snakes. When you get here, you still must understand for yourself
before it works. Haven’t you heard: the one road upward is not transmitted by a thousand sages. Learners toil their bodies like
an ape snatching at shadows. Look at Xuedou’s verse.

lg.l:
Hearing, seeing, sensing, and knowing are not one-by-one.Among the myriad forms and phenomena, there is not a single dharma. Split into seven flowers, eight pieces. Eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind—at once are a holeless
iron hammer.

Mountains and rivers are not viewed within a mirror.Here, there is no such news. The long are long, the short are short. Blue is blue, yellow is yellow.
From where do you look?
In a frosty sky the moon sets; the night is nearing midnight.I have led you into the weeds already. It has never been hidden throughout the whole realm. On no account sit in a ghost cave.

Who will, together with the clear deep pool, shine on the cold reflections?Is there? Is there? If you do not sleep in the same bed, how would you know the quilt is torn? A worried person should not speak to a worried person—speak
to a worried person, and worry will kill.

cb:div.p:
Nanquan spoke in a light doze; Xuedou spoke in a deep sleep. Though it is dreaming, he still dreamed
a fine dream. Earlier he spoke of one body; here he speaks of not the same. Hearing, seeing, sensing, and knowing
are not one-by-one. Mountains and rivers are not viewed within a mirror. If you say they are viewed within a mirror, then
only afterward do you understand—but then you do not depart from the mirror. Mountains and rivers, the great earth, grasses and woods, dense forests—
do not take up the mirror as a model. If you take up the mirror as a model, it becomes two sections. Just let mountains be
mountains and water be water. Each dharma abides in its dharma position; the marks of the world ever abide. Mountains and rivers are not
viewed within a mirror—tell me, from what place do you view them? Do you understand? When you get to
here—at the frosty sky, moon setting, night nearing midnight—on this side I settle it all with you.
On that side you measure it yourself. Do you know that Xuedou uses the fundamental matter to work for people?
Who, together with the clear deep pool, shines on the cold reflections? Is it shining on itself, or shining together with others?
You must cut off devices and cut off interpretations before you reach this realm. Right now there is no need to clarify
