pool, and no need to wait for the frosty sky and the moon setting. Right now, what is it?

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Four, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.End.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Five, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.

cb:div.p:
The pointer said: where right and wrong knot together, even a sage cannot know. When favorable and adverse interweave in every direction,
even a Buddha cannot distinguish. To be a person who cuts through the world and surpasses the common, to reveal the ability of
a great being who rises above the crowd—walk upon ice spikes, run upon a sword’s edge, straight on like a qilin’s
head and horns, like a lotus within fire; you clearly see what surpasses all directions. Only then do you know the fellow Way—who
is the skilled hand? Try raising it and see.

Case 41. Zhaozhou asked Touzi: When someone who has died the great death comes back to life, how is it? Touzi said: Do not permit night-travel; cast yourself into the lightThere is such
a thing: thieves do not rob the poor man’s house. Only one long used to being a guest truly pities a guest.
and you must arrive.Watching a tower, striking a tower—thieves recognize thieves. If you do not lie in the same bed, how would you know the quilt is torn?

Zhaozhou asked Touzi: When someone who has died the great death comes back to life, how is it? Touzi replied
to him: Do not permit night-travel; cast yourself into the light and you must arrive. Tell me, what kind of moment
is this? A holeless flute strikes a felt clapper-board—this is called a question that tests the host; it is also called
a question of the mind’s conduct. Throughout the various places, both Touzi and Zhaozhou were praised for attaining the eloquence
that surpasses the crowd. Though the two elders inherited different lines, see how their sharp points meet as if in one boat. Touzi
one day, for Zhaozhou’s sake, set out a tea seat to receive him. He personally passed steamed cakes to Zhaozhou;
Zhaozhou did not take them. Touzi had an attendant pass over sesame cakes to Zhaozhou; Zhaozhou paid respect
to the attendant with three bows. Tell me, what was his meaning? See how it is all aimed at the root
itself, lifting up this fundamental matter to work for people. A monk asked: What is the Way? He answered,
Way. What is Buddha? He answered, Buddha. Again asked: When the golden lock has not yet been opened,
how is it? He answered: Open. When the golden rooster has not yet crowed, how is it? He answered: There is no
such sound. After it crows, how is it? He answered: Each knows in their own time. Touzi’s whole life,
his questions and answers were always like this. Look at Zhaozhou’s question: When someone who has died the great death comes back to life, how
is it? He immediately said: Do not permit night-travel; cast yourself into the light and you must arrive. Straight down, it is like striking flint
for fire, like a flash of lightning. Only one who goes upward is fit for it. A person who has died the great death has
no Buddha-dharma, no principles, no mysteries, no gain or loss, right or wrong, long or short. When you reach here, you only
rest like this. The ancients called it: countless dead people on level ground. To pass through
the thorn forest is the skilled hand. You must also penetrate through to the other side before it works. Even so,
for people today to reach this kind of field is already hard to come by. If there is
reliance or interpretive understanding, then it has nothing to do with it. Master Zhe called this “seeing not
pure and clean.” The former teacher, Fifth Patriarch, called it “the life-root not cut off.” You must die the great death once,
and only then can you live again. Master Yongguang of Zhe said: if the edge of words is off, it misses by
ten thousand li. You must let go at the cliff’s edge, and willingly take responsibility yourself; after utter cutting off, revive again—
you cannot deceive anyone. It is an extraordinary meaning—how could a person hide it? Zhaozhou’s question
has this intent. Touzi is a master hand and did not fail what was asked. It is just that
he cuts off feeling and cuts off traces; it is not without being hard to grasp. He only reveals a bit right in front. Therefore the ancients
said: if you want intimacy, do not use a question to question. The question is in the answer; the answer is in
the question. If it were not Touzi, faced with Zhaozhou’s one question, it would be very hard to respond.
Only because he is a master hand—raise it and he immediately knows where it lands. The verse says:
。What is kept is instead concealed.

lg.l:
With eyes within the living, it is still like a death-drug.Neither knows the other. Over and over again. If you are not restrained and contained, how can you discern this fellow’s black and white?
What taboo is there—why must you mirror a master hand?If you have not tested it, how can you discern what is exact? When you meet it, try giving it one look in the mirror—what harm is there? You must also ask it through.

Ancient
Buddhas still say the meeting has not arrived.Good that there are companions. A thousand sages do not transmit it; this mountain monk also does not know.

I do not know who can let go and scatter
dust and sand.Even now it is not little. With eyes open you land on it; with eyes closed you land on it. You, Acarya, raise it like this—where does it land?

cb:div.p:
With eyes within the living, it is still like death. Xuedou is someone who truly knows, and so he dares
to compose this verse. The ancients said: he investigates the living phrase, not the dead phrase. Xuedou says: within the living
there are eyes, and it is just like a dead fellow—how could it be dead-dead? Within it there are eyes
like a living person. The ancients said: only after killing off all the dead people do you then see the living person. When the living are fully alive,
Only a dead man can recognize a dead man. Zhaozhou was a living man, so he deliberately posed a dead question to test
Touzi, like taking what a medicine contraindicates and using it to try out and verify.
Therefore Xuedou said, Why consult an expert about a drug taboo? This verse praises where Zhaozhou asked,
and afterward praises Touzi. Even the ancient Buddha said he had never reached it; only this great
dead man is alive there. The ancient Buddha also never reached it. The old masters under heaven
have never reached it either. Even if it were old Shakyamuni himself, that blue-eyed barbarian monk would still have to
investigate again to manage it. Hence it is said: only allow the old barbarian to know; do not allow the old barbarian to understand.
Xuedou said: I do not know who can scatter dust and sand. Do you not see a monk asking Changqing:
What is the eye of a good spiritual friend? Changqing said: If there is a vow, do not scatter sand. Baofu said: One should not
scatter it even more. The old masters under heaven sit on bent wooden meditation benches, wielding the staff,
shouting, raising the whisk, knocking the bench, displaying miracles and acting as lords and masters, all of it is scattering sand. Now
tell me, how can it be avoided?

Pointer: Lifting it up alone, handling it solo, hauling water and dragging mud, striking and chanting both at once. A silver mountain,
an iron wall. If you speculate, you see ghosts before a skull; if you ponder, you sit in meditation beneath Black Mountain.
The bright sun shines in the sky; the fresh wind sweeps the earth. Tell me, did the ancients
still have any mistaken points? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 42. A layman Pang took leave of Yaoshan. Yaoshan ordered ten Zen guestsThis old fellow is acting strange.
to see him off to the gate. The layman pointedDo not take him lightly. What kind of realm is this? It must be a patched-robed monk who can recognize the signs.
at the snow in the air and said: Fine snow, flake after flake, does not fall anywhere else. At that time the Zen guest Quan said: Where does it fall? The layman slapped him once. Quan said: The layman, you too must not be carelessRaising waves without wind with a fingertip—he has eyes. This old fellow’s words
have an echo within them.It hits. It follows along. Indeed.
Taking the hook—caught. Indeed. Hooking the thief and wrecking the house.
The layman said: You call yourself a Zen guest like this? King Yama has not yet letStaring wide-eyed in the coffin.
you off. Quan said: Layman, howThe second ladle of foul water is splashed—why only Yama? This mountain monk here will not let it pass either.
is that so? The layman slapped him again and said: Eyes seeing like the blind; mouth speaking like the mute.The mind does not change—again he wants to eat the stick. This monk, from start to finish, finds no convenience.Indeed. Frost on
snow. After eating the stick, he makes his confession.There are also cutting and harmonizing lines; again he is read the verdict.
Xuedou added: At the first question, just grab a snowball and strike.It is so, it is so—drawing the bow after the thief has passed.
He also lets quite a bit slip out. Even so, you must see arrowpoints meeting—yet it ends up fallen into a ghost cave.

Layman Pang studied under both Mazu and Shitou, and has verses from both places. When he first met Shitou, he
asked: Not keeping company with the ten thousand dharmas, what person is that? Before the words were finished, Shitou
covered his mouth. Having gained an understanding, he composed a verse saying: In daily use there is nothing different, only
I myself am naturally in accord. In everything there is no choosing or rejecting; everywhere there is no obstruction. Who
assigns titles of red and purple? Green mountains are utterly free of specks of dust. Spiritual powers and wondrous function are: carrying water and hauling firewood.
Later he studied with Mazu and again asked: Not keeping company with the ten thousand dharmas, what person is that? The Patriarch
said: Wait until you swallow the waters of the West River in one gulp, then I will tell you. The layman suddenly
had great awakening and made a verse: The ten directions gather together; each one studies non-doing. This is
a place for selecting Buddhas; with mind empty, one graduates and returns. Because he was an adept, later the monasteries
looked to him; wherever he went, people vied to praise him. Having stayed long at Yaoshan, he then took leave of Yaoshan. Yaoshan
valued him greatly and ordered ten Zen guests to see him off. At that time it happened to be snowing. The layman
pointed at the snow and said: Fine snow, flake after flake, does not fall anywhere else. The Zen guest Quan said: Where
does it fall? The layman then slapped him. Since the Zen guest Quan could not carry out the command, the layman
carried the command halfway. Though the command did function, the Zen guest Quan responded like that; it is not that
he did not know where it falls. Each has his own sharp point; their opening and closing differ. Yet there was something he did not reach
at the layman's place, so he fell under the layman's frame and could hardly escape his range. After the layman struck him,
he further explained the principle, saying: Eyes seeing like the blind, mouth speaking like the mute. Xuedou
added to the earlier words: At the first question, just grab a snowball and strike. Xuedou spoke like this
in order not to fail the thrust of the question, but the timing was slow. Storekeeper Qing said: The layman's
timing is like lightning. If you wait to grab a snowball, until when will that be? Respond in the same voice,
strike in the same voice, only then is it completely cut off. Xuedou himself versified the place of the strike, saying: Trap
。Gu-dou-qie: drawing a bow and crossbow—bow fully drawn.

lg.l:
Snowball strike, snowball strike, old Pang,Yet it falls into the second move. No need to pick it out—above is vast, below is vast.
his mechanisms have nothing to grab.Often people do not know—only fearing it is not like this.

In heaven above and among humans he does not himself
know.What news is this? Does Xuedou still know?

In eyes and in ears, utterly crisp and clean.Arrowpoints meet—eyes see as if blind; the mouth speaks
as if mute.

Crisp and clean to the utmost, even the blue-eyed barbarian monk finds it hard to tell apart.How is it? Where is it? Seeing Old Pang and Xuedou
the difference.bring Bodhidharma out—what would they say to you? He strikes, saying: What are you saying? Buried in one pit.

cb:div.p:
Snowball strike, snowball strike. Old Pang's mechanisms have nothing to grab. Xuedou wanted to
act above the layman's head. The ancients used snow to illuminate the matter of the one-color edge. Xuedou's intent
was to say: If at that time one had grabbed a snowball and struck, then even if the layman had whatever mechanisms,
it would still be hard for them to take hold. Xuedou praised his own striking point, not realizing there was a snag
there. In heaven above and among humans he does not himself know. In eyes and in ears, utterly crisp and clean. In the eyes
there is also snow; in the ears there is also snow. He is lodged right at the one-color edge; it is also called
Samantabhadra's realm, the matter of the one-color edge; it is also called striking it into one piece. Yunmen
said: Even if the whole universe and great earth were without the slightest fault, it would still be a turning phrase.
Not seeing the one color is only half raising it. If you want to raise it fully, you must know there is an upward
road. When you reach here, great functioning must appear right before you; not even a needle can be inserted.
You do not listen to others' arrangements. Hence it is said: He investigates living phrases, not dead phrases.
The ancients said: One fitting opening phrase is a stake that ties a donkey for ten thousand kalpas. What
use is that? Xuedou, upon reaching this, versified it to death, then turned the mechanism and said: Only this crisp-and-clean
to the utmost, even the blue-eyed barbarian monk would find it hard to distinguish. If even the blue-eyed barbarian monk
finds it hard to distinguish, what more could this mountain monk say?

Pointer: A phrase that settles heaven and earth is followed together for ten thousand generations. A device for seizing tigers and rhinoceroses is something a thousand sages
cannot discern. Right here there is not the slightest haze; the whole mechanism manifests everywhere equally. To clarify the upward
tongs-and-hammer, one must have the expert's forge and bellows. Tell me, from above on down, is there still such
a family style or not? Try raising it and see.

Case 43. A monk asked Dongshan: When cold and heat arrive, how do you avoid them? Dongshan said: Why not go to the place with no cold and heat? The monk said: What is the place with no cold and heat? Dongshan said: When it is cold, cold kills the venerable; when it is hot, heat kills the venerableThis is not the time. Split!
Where is it, right in front, splitting your head?No one under heaven can find it. Hidden
body, it shows its shadow. Xiao He sold off a fake silver cityand duped a whole boatload of people to death. Let it
turn; one cast of the hook and up it comes.
one.The true does not cover the false; the crooked does not hide the straight. At the cliff’s edge, you watch tigers and rhinoceroses—an especially staged bout of sorrow. He flips the great sea and kicks over Sumeru. Tell me, where is Dongshan?

The new master Huanglong raised it and said: Dongshan strikes the collar with his sleeve cuff, and tucks the lapel under his armpit.
Yet this monk would not accept it. If someone now came out and asked Huanglong, tell me
how would he dispatch it? After a long pause he said: To settle in Zen you need not have mountains and waters; extinguish the fire in your
mind and it naturally becomes cool. Everyone, tell me, where does Dongshan's circling and painting fall?
If you can clearly discern it, then you will know that under Dongshan the Five Positions interchange, using upright and inclined to meet people.
It is not without its strangeness. Only upon reaching this upward realm can it be like this. There is no need to arrange
anything; it is naturally just right. Hence it is said: The inclined within the upright: at the first night of the third watch the moon is bright
before you. Do not blame meeting without recognition; faintly there still lingers the old days' dislike. The upright within the inclined
is lost: a morning old woman meets an ancient mirror; clearly face to face, yet there is no truth. Do not
further lose your head and take shadows for the real. Coming from within the upright: within the empty there is a road that leaves the dust, only
do not touch the present taboo, and you surpass the previous dynasty's tongue-cut talent. Reaching within the inclined: two
blades cross in combat and there is no need to avoid; a good hand is like a lotus in fire, naturally possessing
a soaring-to-heaven energy. Arriving in the mutual: not falling into being or nonbeing, who dares to respond? Everyone entirely
wants to leave the common stream; but when it is folded together, you return to sit in the coals. The record of Fushan Yuanlu says:
This koan is the pattern of the Five Positions. If you understand one of them, the rest naturally
are easy to understand. Yantou said: It is like a gourd on the water; press it and it turns.
It does not take the slightest effort. Once a monk asked Dongshan: When Manjushri and Samantabhadra
come to study, what then? Dongshan said: Drive them into the herd of water buffalo. The monk said:
The master enters hell like an arrow. Dongshan said: It is entirely due to his power. Dongshan said: Why
not go to the place with no cold and heat? This is the upright within the inclined. The monk said: What is
the place with no cold and heat? Dongshan said: When it is cold, cold kills the venerable; when it is hot, heat kills the venerable
one. This is the inclined within the upright. Though upright, it is inclined; though inclined, it is complete. In the Caodong Record
it is recorded in detail. If it were under Linji, there would not be so many complications; such a case
would be understood straightaway. Some say: Great, no cold and heat. What is there to
grasp? The ancients said: If you walk on the blade of a sword, it is quick; if you look through feelings and consciousness,
it is slow. Do you not see a monk asking Cuiwei: What is the meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West?
Cuiwei said: Wait until no one is here, then I will tell you. Then he went walking into the garden. The monk said:
Here there is no one. Please speak, master. Cuiwei pointed to the bamboo and said: This single stalk of bamboo
Thus long—how did that single bamboo pole become thus short? At this the monk suddenly awakened.
Again Caoshan asked a monk, “It’s so hot—where will you go to avoid it?” The monk said, “In a cauldron
of boiling soup, in a brazier of coals to avoid it.” Caoshan said, “How do you avoid it in boiling soup and furnace coals?”
The monk said, “All sufferings cannot reach there.” Look at the people of his own household—naturally they understand their own
household’s way of speaking. Xuedou used this “household matter” and brought it out in verse.

lg.l: Letting down a hand is still like a ten-thousand-fathom cliff.If you’re not a genuine hand, who can tell it apart? Where is it not perfectly interfused? Once the king’s edict goes forth, the feudal lords clear the road.

Upright and inclined—why must it be set up and arranged?If it were mere arrangement, how could there be today? How do you avoid involving either end? When wind moves, grass bends; when water arrives,
the channel is formed.

The bright moon shines on the ancient crystal hall; unable to hold back a grin, the Han houndRound and complete—on no account recognize the shadow. For now, don’t face it head-on.
runs up the steps in vain.If it’s not this time, you’ve already missed it. What are you chasing bits and pieces for? He struck and said: You and this monk practiced together
The hound.at Longdu—“qie-tong” all read as “lu.”

cb:div.p:
In the Caodong line there is “entering the world” and “not entering the world”; there is “letting down a hand” and “not letting down a hand.” If one does not
enter the world, one’s eyes gaze into the clouds and sky. If one enters the world, one is ash-headed and dirt-faced. Gazing into the clouds and
sky is the peak-top of ten thousand fathoms; ash-headed and dirt-faced is the business of letting down a hand.
Sometimes ash-headed and dirt-faced is right on the ten-thousand-fathom peak; sometimes the ten-thousand-fathom peak
is precisely ash-headed and dirt-faced. In truth, entering the marketplace with lowered hands is the same as standing alone on a solitary peak.
Returning to the source and realizing one’s nature is no different from discriminating wisdom. Absolutely avoid making two
stakes of it. Therefore it is said: letting down a hand is still like a ten-thousand-fathom cliff—truly there is nowhere for you to cozy up
and dock. Upright and inclined—why must it be arranged? When it comes time to use it, it is naturally
like this; it is not a matter of arrangement. This verse praises the place where Dongshan answered. Later it says: the ancient crystal
hall shines in the bright moon; unable to hold back a grin, the Han hound runs up the steps in vain. This directly praises this monk
chasing after words. In the Dongshan school there are things like the stone woman, the wooden horse, the bottomless basket, the night-shining
pearl, the dead snake, and eighteen such kinds. In general they only clarify the proper position, like the moon shining on the crystal
hall—seeming to have a round reflection. Dongshan answered, “Why not go to the place without cold
or heat?” That monk was just like the Han hound chasing a clod, hurrying up the steps to grab
the moon’s reflection. He asked again, “What is the place without cold or heat?” Dongshan said, “When it’s cold,
cold kills the monk; when it’s hot, heat kills the monk.” Like the Han hound chasing a clod, he ran up
the steps, yet again did not see the moon’s reflection. The “Han hound” comes from the Strategies of the Warring States: it says the Han
clan’s hound is a swift dog; Zhongshan’s rabbit is a crafty rabbit; only that hound
can track that rabbit. Xuedou cites this as a metaphor for this monk. Now as for all of you—
do you recognize where Dongshan meets people? After a long pause he said, “What rabbit are you hunting for?”

[44] Raised: Heshan’s incidental words said, “Practiced learning is called hearing; cutting off learning is called
being near. To pass beyond these two is called true passing.” A monk came forward and asked, “What is true passing?”Patchrobed monks throughout the land can’t jump out of it. A holeless iron hammer—an iron stake.At the crown of the head,
why have a single eye there?Say what? With one stroke it’s all crossed off—there is an iron stake.
Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” He asked again, “What is true truth?” Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” He asked again,Iron stake, iron caltrop—firm, firm,say what
thing? A double-layered case; again there is an iron stake.Iron stake, iron caltrop—firm, firm,
“Mind itself is Buddha I won’t ask about. What is neither mind nor Buddha?” Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” He asked again,say what? This mound
of trash—three sections not the same. Again, an iron caltrop.Iron stake, iron caltrop—firm, firm,
“When a person of the upward way comes, how do you receive him?”
say what? You’ve met his fourth ladle of foul water. Again there is an iron stake.
Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.”Iron stake, iron caltrop—firm, firm. Tell me, where does it land? In the morning you reach the Western Heaven; by evening you return to the Eastern Land.

Heshan’s incidental instruction said, “Practiced learning is called hearing; cutting off learning is called being near. To pass beyond
these two is called true passing.” This saying comes from the Treatise on the Treasury. Learning reaches no
learning—this is called cutting off learning. Therefore it is said, “Shallow hearing, deep awakening; deep hearing, no awakening.”
This is called cutting off learning. In one night he awakened to the Way: “In my early years I accumulated learning, and also
sought commentaries and searched sutras and treatises. When practiced learning is exhausted, it is called cutting off learning—an idle person of the Way without
contrivance. Only upon reaching cutting off learning does one begin to be close to the Way, truly passing beyond these two learnings.
This is called true passing. That monk was quite sharp, so he took up these words and asked Heshan.
Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” This is what is meant by “words have no flavor, phrases have no flavor.” If you want to understand this
koan, you must be a person of the upward way; only then can you see that these words do not touch principle or nature,
and there is no place for argument. Understand it directly, like the bottom falling out of a bucket—only then
is it the secure place for a patch-robed monk. Then you first accord with the meaning of the Patriarch’s coming from the West. Therefore Yunmen
said, “Xuefeng rolls a ball; Heshan beats a drum; the National Teacher’s water bowl; Zhaozhou drinks
tea”—all of it is taking up and presenting the upward matter. He asked again, “What is true truth?” Heshan said,
“Able to beat the drum.” True truth does not establish a single dharma. If it is conventional truth, all things are
fully present. True and conventional are not two—this is the first principle of the holy truths. He asked again, “Mind itself is Buddha
I won’t ask about. What is neither mind nor Buddha?” Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” Mind itself
is Buddha is easy to seek; but when it comes to neither mind nor Buddha, it is difficult—few people reach it.
He asked again, “When a person of the upward way comes, how do you receive him?” Heshan said, “Able to beat the drum.” A person of the upward
way is one who is thoroughly free and unbound. These four lines are taken by the monasteries everywhere as
the essential point, and are called Heshan’s “four drum-beatings.” For example, a monk asked Jingqing, “At the start of the New Year
is there still Buddhadharma or not?” Jingqing said, “There is.” The monk said, “What is the Buddhadharma at the start of the New Year?”
Jingqing said, “At the first of the year the mandate opens; all things are wholly renewed.” The monk said, “Thank you, master,
for answering.” Jingqing said, “Today this old monk has lost profit.” With answers like this there are eighteen kinds of “lost profit.”
Again, a monk asked Master Jingguo, “When a crane stands on a lone pine, how is it?”
Jingguo said, “Under your feet—one ‘mo.’” He asked again, “When snow covers a thousand mountains, how is it?”
Jingguo said, “After the sun rises—one ‘mo.’” He asked again, “During the Huichang suppression
of Buddhism, where did the Dharma-protecting spirits go?” Jingguo said, “Two fellows outside the three gates—
one ‘mo.’” The monasteries everywhere call these the “three mo’s.” Again, Baofu asked a monk, “In the hall
what Buddha is it?” The monk said, “The master will surely see.” Baofu said, “Old Shakyamuni.”
The monk said, “Don’t go fooling people.” Baofu said, “It’s you who are fooling me.” Again he asked
the monk, “What’s your name?” The monk said, “Xianze.” Baofu said, “If you encounter drying up
what then?” The monk said, “Who is the one dried up?” Baofu said, “Me.” The monk said, “Master,
don’t go fooling people.” Baofu said, “It’s you who are fooling me.” Again he asked the monk, “What work do you do
to eat so much and get so big?” The monk said, “The master isn’t small either.” Baofu took
a squatting posture. The monk said, “Master, don’t go fooling people.” Baofu said, “It’s you who are fooling
me.” Again he asked the bath manager, “How wide is the bath cauldron?” The manager said, “Please, master, measure
it.” Baofu made a measuring gesture. The manager said, “Master, don’t go fooling people.” Baofu said, “It’s
you who are fooling me.” The monasteries everywhere call these Baofu’s “four foolings.” And then there’s Xuefeng’s
“four lacquer buckets.” All of these are from the ancient masters, each bringing forth profound and subtle intent to meet
people according to the occasion. After this, Xuedou adds a single tightening pull; following Yunmen’s instruction to the assembly, he versifies
this koan.

lg.l:
One tug at a stone—two kinds of earth.An imperial edict to the whole realm. A leper leads his companions. People of the Way above come like this.A frontier general’s command. The two
take it together in one receipt—those with the same illness pity each other.

To release the trigger must be a thousand-jun crossbow.If it were a thousand-jun weight, you still couldn’t pierce through. Don’t answer lightly—how
could it be for a dead shrimp?
The elephant-boned old master once rolled a ball,Someone has indeed come like this. There is a holeless iron hammer—who doesn’t know it?

but how could it compare to Heshan who can beat the drum—let me tell you.An iron stake—only this old fellow can truly return it. One point personally grasped,
even Xuedou never dreamed of it. Adding frost on top of snow—do you know it or not?

Don’t be reckless: sweet is sweet,There’s a bit of it. Dull and muddled,
and bitter is bitter.you thank him for the reply—wrongly adding a footnote. You deserve thirty blows. Can you take the blows or not? He strikes—still pitch-black as ever.

cb:div.p:
One day Guizong called everyone out to tug a stone. Guizong asked the rector, “Where are you going?” The rector
said, “To tug the stone.” Guizong said, “Tug the stone as you like, but you must not disturb
the central tree.” Mujing, whenever a newcomer arrived, would first have him carry three rounds of earth.
Mujing had a verse and told the assembly, “East Mountain’s road is narrow, West Mountain is low; newcomer, don’t
refuse the three rounds of mud. Alas, you’ve been on the road for days on end—clearly you don’t understand, yet you
end up confused.” Later a monk asked, “Inside the three rounds I won’t ask about; outside the three rounds,
how is it?” Mujing said, “The Iron-Wheel King’s edict throughout the realm.” The monk had no words, and Mujing
struck him. Therefore it is said: one tug at a stone, two kinds of earth; to release the trigger must be a thousand-jun
crossbow. Xuedou uses the thousand-jun crossbow as a metaphor for these words—if you want to see where he meets people.
Thirty jin make one jun; one thousand jun is thirty thousand jin. If it’s a savage dragon,
tigers, wolves, and fierce beasts, only then do you use this crossbow. If it’s some tiny little thing like a wren, you surely
must not release it lightly. Thus a thousand-jun crossbow is not triggered for a field mouse.
“The elephant-boned old master once rolled a ball” refers to when Xuefeng one day saw Xuansha coming and rolled
three wooden balls at once. Xuansha immediately made a chopping-the-plaque gesture; Xuefeng deeply approved
of it. Although all of it is the full functioning of great use, none of it compares to Heshan who can beat
the drum—so direct and cutting through. It’s just hard to understand. Therefore Xuedou said, “How could it compare to He-
shan who can beat the drum.” Yet he feared people would only stay on the phrase-head, making a living off it, not knowing
the reason for it. Blundering and crude. Therefore it is said: I tell you, do not be reckless and crude. It too
must truly reach this stage. If you want to not be crude, sweet is
sweet, bitter is bitter. Though Xuedou thus toys with it, in the end he still cannot jump
out of it.

Instruction said: If you want to speak, then speak; unrivaled in the whole world. If you should act, then act; the whole functioning
yields to none. Like sparks struck from stone, like a flash of lightning. Swift flame outruns the wind; a rushing stream crosses
a blade. Raise the upward pincers and hammer, and you cannot avoid losing the edge and being tongue-tied. Let loose a single thread of the Way.
Try bringing it up and see.

Case 45: A monk asked Zhaozhou, All dharmas return to the one; where does the one return? Zhaozhou said, In Qingzhou I made a cloth robe.Pressing this old fellow.
Piling up mountains, heaping up peaks—on no account make a living in a ghost cave.
It weighed seven jin.Sure enough, seven vertical and eight horizontal; he yanks down the net that fills the sky. Do you still see Zhaozhou? A patchrobed monk once pinched his nostrils—do you know where Zhaozhou lands?
If you see it here, then it’s “above heaven and below heaven, I alone am honored.” When water arrives, the channel forms; when wind moves, grass lies down. If not, this old monk is right under your heels.

If you understand it where one blow immediately goes through, then all the old masters under heaven will have their nostrils
pierced through at once, and there is nothing they can do to you. Naturally, when water arrives, the channel is formed. But if you hesitate,
this old monk is right beneath your heels. The essentials of Buddhadharma are not in many words; speech
is not in being elaborate. Just consider this monk asking Zhaozhou: All dharmas return to the one; where does the one
return? He instead answered: In Qingzhou I made a cloth robe; it weighed seven jin.
If you discriminate in the words and phrases, you mistake the fixed pole star. If you do not discriminate in the words and phrases,
then how will you deal with his speaking like this? This koan: though hard to see, it is easy to understand; though
easy to understand, it is hard to see. Hard means silver mountains and iron cliffs; easy means directly clear and awake.
There is no place for your calculating right and wrong. These words are just like Puhua saying, Tomorrow at the Great Compassion
Monastery there will be a meal; there is nothing else. One day a monk asked Zhaozhou: What
is the meaning of the Patriarch coming from the West? Zhaozhou said, The cypress in the courtyard. The monk said, Master,
do not use a thing as a scene to point it out to people. Zhaozhou said, This old monk has never used a scene to point it out to people. See how he
thus can turn right at the utmost, where turning is impossible; naturally it covers heaven and covers earth.
If you cannot turn, then wherever you go you get stuck. Tell me: does he have any Buddhadharma to discuss or
not? If you say he has Buddhadharma, when did he ever speak of mind or speak of nature, speak of the mysterious
or speak of the subtle? If you say he has no purport of Buddhadharma, he never let down your
question. Haven't you seen: a monk asked Master Muping, What is the great
meaning of Buddhadharma? Muping said, This winter melon is this big. Another monk asked an ancient worthy: In the deep mountains,
on sheer cliffs, in a remote place with no people, is there still Buddhadharma or not? The ancient worthy said, There is.
The monk said, What is Buddhadharma in the deep mountains? The ancient worthy said, Stones: the big are
big, the small are small. Look at such koans: where is the distortion? Xuedou
knew where it lands, so he opened up the road of meaning and gave you a verse.

lg.l:
Bianbi once pressed the old ancient awl; a seven-jin robe, heavy.Why must you press this old fellow? Where are you trying to shove and press him to?
How many people know?Come again and you’re not worth half a cent. Even if your mouth were as straight as a shoulder pole, you’d still let him win another round.

Now toss it
into West Lake.Only then can you return Xuedou’s hands and feet. This mountain monk also doesn’t want

Below, the clear wind: to whom will it be entrusted?from ancient times
to the present. Tell me, Xuedou traded verses with him, and added a footnote for him. One point personally grasped,

cb:div.p:
Among the eighteen questions, this is called the Bianbi question. Xuedou said: Bianbi once pressed
the old ancient awl. Bianbi means to marshal the ten thousand dharmas and make them return to one. This monk wanted to press
Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou too was a seasoned hand: where turning is impossible, he has a road of coming forth.
He dared to open his big mouth and say: In Qingzhou I made a cloth robe; it weighed
seven jin. Xuedou said: This seven-jin cloth robe, how many people can know it? Now
toss it into West Lake. All dharmas return to one, and even the one is not wanted. The seven-jin cloth
robe is not wanted either. In one stroke toss it into West Lake. Xuedou lived at Dongting, on Cuifeng;
he too has a West Lake. Below, the clear wind: to whom will it be entrusted? This is Zhaozhou instructing
the assembly: If you come from the north, I will give you the upper load; if you come from the south, I will give you
the lower load. If you come from Xuefeng or Yunju, you are also a plank-carrying fellow. Xuedou
said: Such a clear wind, to whom is it fit to entrust? The upper load means I speak to you of mind
and speak of nature, speak of the mysterious and the subtle, all kinds of expedients. If it is the lower load, there is no such
mass of abstruse principle and mystery. Some carry a load of Zen; when they arrive at Zhaozhou, they cannot use even
the slightest bit. All at once he packs it up for them, making it open and loose, with not a single
speck of business. This is called: after awakening, still the same as before awakening. People nowadays all take it as
meaning there is nothing to do. Some say: no delusion, no awakening; do not seek further. But just consider: before the Buddha
appeared in the world, before Bodhidharma came to this land, you could not help but be like this. What use is it
for the Buddha to appear in the world? What use for the Patriarch to come from the West? If it is all like this,
what does it matter? Yet you must have great penetration and great awakening; then as before, mountains are
mountains and waters are waters, and all the ten thousand dharmas entirely appear. Only then do you begin to be
a person of no affairs. Haven't you seen Longya say: Studying the Way, first you must have a cause for awakening.
It is like you once raced dragon boats: though it is an old pavilion and idle ground, you must win once
before you can rest. Take Zhaozhou's phrase about the seven-jin cloth robe: see
how the ancients spoke like this, like gold, like jade. This mountain monk speaks like this; you all
listen like this: it is all the upper load. Tell me then, what is the lower load? Under three
rafters, look and see.

Instruction said: One hammerblow and you transcend the ordinary and surpass the holy. A single phrase can break it, removing bonds
and loosening sticking. Like walking on icicles, running on a sword edge. Sit amid heaps of sound and form; walk upon
the heads of sound and form. Set aside the wondrous functioning of freedom; when in an instant you must go, how is it
then? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 46: Jingqing asked a monk, What sound is outside the gate?casually he lets down a single hook. Not fearing deafness—what are you asking?
The monk said, The sound of raindrops. Jingqing said, Sentient beings are inverted, deludedIt can be downright real. That’s also good news.
about themselves and chase after things. The monk said, Master, then howTrouble arises. Used to taking advantage, he hooks and lassoes with a jingling hook—return his proper hands and feet.
should it be? Jingqing said, Then do not be deluded about yourself. The monk said, If one is not deluded about oneself, what is the meaning? Jingqing said, Coming forth is still easy; speaking the Way after shedding the body is hard indeed.Sure enough he takes in the flaw and turns the spear around. It’s hard to handle—and then he reverses the spearhead to stab people.Tsk! So straight you can separate
but can’t get any distance.Pressing this old fellow, he forces people to death. The first arrow
is still light; the later arrow strikes deep.The affinity of raising a child—although
it is so, where did Deshan and Linji go? If you don’t call it the sound of raindrops, what do you call it? So straight you can’t separate or keep apart.It is enormous—right up close and immediate.

Right here is also a good place to take it up. The ancients set forth one device and one situation in order to receive
people. One day Jingqing asked a monk, What sound is outside the gate? The monk said, The sound of raindrops.
Jingqing said, Sentient beings are inverted, deluded about themselves and chase after things. He asked again, What sound is outside the gate?
The monk said, The sound of a turtledove. Jingqing said, If you want not to invite karmic obstruction, do not
slander the Tathagata's true Dharma wheel. He asked again, What sound is outside the gate? The monk said, The sound of a snake biting
a shrimp. Jingqing said, You think sentient beings suffer, yet there are sentient beings who suffer even more. These words
are no different from the previous koan. If a patch-robed monk breaks through here,
then amid heaps of sound and form he can be free. If he cannot break through, he is bound by sound
and form. Koans like this are called in all quarters forging phrases. If you forge
and refine, you only make mental activity. You do not see where the ancients worked for people. It is also called passing through
sound and form: first, clarify the eye of the Way; second, clarify sound and form; third, clarify the mind school; fourth, clarify forgetting
feelings; fifth, clarify unfolding and displaying. Still, you can examine it closely. Yet there are grooves and ruts in it. Jingqing
asked like this, What sound is outside the gate? The monk said, The sound of raindrops. Then he said,
Sentient beings are inverted, deluded about themselves and chase after things. Everyone misunderstands it, calling it deliberately turning
people. That has nothing to do with it. They do not know that Jingqing had hands and feet for working for others.
Bold, he is not constrained by one device or one situation; he is utterly careless of his eyebrows. How could Jingqing
not know it was the sound of raindrops? Why ask again? You must know the ancients used a probing pole
to stir the grass and startle the snakes, to test this monk. This monk too was good at pressing; he then said, Master, then
how does it go straight? Jingqing entered mud and entered water and said to him, Then do not be deluded
about yourself. If that monk is deluded about himself and chases after things, then why is Jingqing also deluded
about himself? You must know: within the testing phrase there is already a place to come forth. This monk was too muddled;
wanting to cut it off, he asked further: Just this, not being deluded about oneself, what is the meaning
of it? If it were Deshan or Linji, the stick and shout would already have been applied. Jingqing let one
thread of the Way run through, letting him spin vines; then he said to him, Coming forth is still easy; shedding
the body and speaking the Way should be hard. Even so, the ancients said continuity is also very hard. Jingqing
with just one sentence made clear for this monk the great matter beneath his heels. Xuedou
versified, saying:

lg.l:
In the empty hall, the sound of raindrops; the author is hard to answer.Never once has it been interrupted. Everyone is hereand truly does not know. This mountain
monk has never been a “maker.” There is authority and reality, releasing and gathering; killing and reviving, capturing and letting go.

If you say you have once entered the stream,A thorny head plunged into a glue basin. If you don’t call it
the sound of rain filling, what do you call it?
Still you do not understand.When have I ever asked you? You lacquer bucket—give me back my holeless iron hammer!

You never understood the downpour swirling over South Mountain and North Mountain.Both ends are cut off while sitting; the two places are not divided. It is not on either side,above the head
or below the feet. If you call it rain sound, you’re blind. If you don’t call it rain sound, what do you call it? Here you must have your feet on solid ground.

cb:div.p:
In the empty hall, the sound of raindrops. Even a seasoned hand finds it hard to respond. If you call it the sound of rain, then it is
losing yourself and chasing after things. If you do not call it the sound of rain, then how do you turn things around? When it comes to this,
even a seasoned hand finds it hard to respond. Therefore the ancients said: if your view equals the teacher's,
you diminish the teacher by half a virtue. Only when your view surpasses the teacher are you fit to receive the transmission. And Nanyuan said: under the staff,
there is the forbearance of the unborn. Facing the moment, do not yield to the teacher. If you say you have entered the stream, still
you do not understand. In the teachings it says: first, within hearing, enter the stream and forget what is heard; once what is entered
is quiet, the two appearances of motion and stillness clearly do not arise. If you say it is the sound of raindrops, that also is not it.
If you say it is not the sound of raindrops, that also is not it. The earlier verse about two shouts and
three shouts, where a seasoned hand knows the shifts of the moment, is exactly like this verse. If you say it is entering the stream of sound and form,
that also is not it. If you call it sound and form, still you do not understand his meaning. It is like
using a finger to point at the moon: the moon is not the finger. Whether you understand or not, South Mountain and North Mountain
are still swirling with rain.

The instruction says: What does heaven say? Yet the four seasons proceed. What does earth say? Yet the myriad things
are born. In the place where the four seasons proceed, you can see essence; in the place where the myriad things arise, you can
see function. Now tell me: where can you see a patch-robed monk? Leaving aside words,
actions, walking, standing, sitting, lying down—seal up throat, lips, and mouth—can you still discern it?

Case 47 is cited: A monk asked Yunmen, "What is the dharma body?" Yunmen said, "The six do not take in."How many people are stuck in doubt. Even a thousand sages can’t jump out. Leaking and dribbling
is not a little.Cut nails and slice iron—an eight-sided millstone runs in empty space. If you realize it when the numinous tortoise drags its tail and the signs are not yet divided, that is already the
second head. If you realize it after the signs have arisen, you fall into a third turn. If you go on distinguishing in words, well then—gladly, it has nothing to do with it.

Yunmen said, "The six do not take in"—it is truly hard to put together. If you try to put it together before the faint signs have even divided,
that is already a second head. If you realize it after the faint signs have already arisen, then you
fall into a third. If you try to sort it out in phrases, in the end you cannot lay a hand on it. So then,
ultimately, what do you take as the dharma body? If you are a seasoned hand, as soon as you hear it raised, you
lift it up and move at once. If you linger in thought and stop the workings, bowing to await orders, Taiyuan Fu, the senior seat,
was originally a lecturer. One day he mounted the seat and, in the course of lecturing, spoke of the dharma body, saying: vertically it exhausts
the three times; horizontally it spans the ten directions. A Chan traveler in the audience heard this and burst out laughing.
Fu came down from the seat and said, "Where was I lacking just now? I ask the Chan traveler to explain."
The Chan traveler said, "Seat master, you have only spoken of the edges and measures of the dharma body—you have not seen the dharma
body." Fu said, "So what, ultimately, is it?" The Chan traveler said, "You may suspend your lectures for a while and in
a quiet room sit; you will certainly see for yourself." Fu did as he said. One night, sitting in silence, he suddenly
heard the fifth-watch bell, and suddenly had great awakening. Then he knocked on the Chan traveler's door and said, "I understand
!" The Chan traveler said, "Try saying it." Fu said, "From today on, I
will no longer twist and pinch the nostrils my parents gave me." And in the teachings it also says: the Buddha's true
dharma body is like empty space; responding to things, it manifests forms, like the moon in water. And a monk asked
Jiashan, "What is the dharma body?" Jiashan said, "The dharma body has no appearance." "What is
the dharma eye?" Jiashan said, "The dharma eye is without flaw." Yunmen said, "The six do not take in." In this case,
some say it is simply the six sense faculties, six sense objects, and six consciousnesses. These six all arise from the dharma
body; the six faculties cannot take it in. If you make that kind of emotional interpretation, fine—at least it has nothing to do with it,
but it drags Yunmen down with it. If you want to see, then see—there is no place for your forced contrivances. If you do not see,
the teachings say: this dharma is not something that thinking and discriminating can understand. His replies
often provoke people's emotional interpretations. Therefore within one phrase there must be three phrases, so as not to
fail your question. Responding to time and season, one word, one phrase, one dot, one stroke—does not
prevent there being a way out. Therefore it is said: if one phrase penetrates, a thousand phrases and ten thousand phrases penetrate at once.
Now tell me: is it the dharma body, or is it the patriarch? I grant you thirty blows. Xuedou's verse
says:

lg.l:
One, two, three, four, five, six—the blue-eyed barbarianRound and round, returning again—drip water, drip ice. Why waste so much effort?
monk's count is not enough.Three lives, sixty kalpas—when did Bodhidharma ever dream of this? Venerable, why do you knowingly commit it on purpose?

Shaolin vainly says
it was handed to Shenguang.One person passes on the empty, ten thousand pass on the solid—right from the start it has already been wrong.

Rolling up his robe, he again says he returned to India.
Duping a whole boatload of people to death—no small matter.

Vast and boundless India—there is nowhere to search.Where is it that peace begins? Now it is in what
place?

Last night he instead lodged facing Rufu Peak.To stab out your eyes is also raising waves when there is no wind. Tell me— is it the dharma body or the Buddha body?
I’ll let you off with thirty blows.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou was good at bringing forth an eye from a place with no seam or crack, versing it out to make people see.
Yunmen said, "The six do not take in." Why then does Xuedou say, "One, two, three, four
five, six"? It is that even the blue-eyed barbarian monk's count is not enough. Therefore it is said: only
allow the old barbarian to know; do not allow the old barbarian to understand. You must return it to the children and grandchildren in his own house,
and only then will it do. Just now it was said: one word, one phrase, responding to time and season—if you can pass through,
only then will you know it is not in phrases. If not, you cannot avoid making an emotional interpretation.
The master Fifth Patriarch said: Shakyamuni Buddha, a lowly guest acting as a child; the cypress tree in the courtyard;
one, two, three, four, five. If under Yunmen's words you can clearly and rightly
see it, then in due course you reach this realm. Shaolin vainly says it was handed to Shenguang—the Second Patriarch
was first named Shenguang. And later they again say he returned to India. Bodhidharma was buried beneath
Mount Xiong'er. At that time Song Yun was sent as envoy to the west; on the western ridge he saw Bodhi-
dharma carrying a single sandal, returning to the western heaven. When the envoy returned he reported to the throne; they opened the tomb and only saw
a single sandal left behind. Xuedou said: in truth, how is this matter to be handed down?
Since there is no handing down, he rolls up his robe and again says he returned to India—now tell me, why? In this land,
there are two or three versions, passed along in this way. Here it is not a problem if there are errors and distortions—
but you still must be able to put it together before you can enter and work. Vast and boundless India—there is nowhere to search. Last
night he instead lodged facing Rufu Peak. Now tell me, where is he right now? The master then struck
and said, "Blind!"

Case 48 is cited: When Grand Preceptor Wang entered Zhaoqing, Senior Seat Lang was brewing tea, and Mingzhao was holding the kettle. Lang overturned the teapot. The Grand Preceptor saw it and asked the senior seat, "Under the tea brazierWhen genuine hands gather, there must be something singular. Casually, with nothing going on, everyone puts on one
eye—stirring up trouble.A fiery mud-ball fellow—can’t even brew tea—dragging down
other people.Trouble arises. Sure enough,
what is it?" Lang said, "The brazier-bearing spirit." The Grandsure enough—it’s a calamity.Sure enough, you’ve been hit by his arrow. It’s not a problem—singular indeed.
Preceptor said, "If it is the brazier-bearing spirit, why did you overturn the teapot?" Lang said, "A thousand days as an official, lost in a single morning." The Grand Preceptor flicked his sleeves and left. Ming-Why not give him
his proper fodder? Trouble arises.What is this mistaken pointing and annotation?
Talk. Fabricated “Zen peace” as thick as hemp and millet.Clearly a genuine hand—I grant him possession of one eye.
zhao said, "Senior Seat Lang has eaten Zhaoqing's rice, and then goes outside the river to beat
wild stumps." Lang said, "Master,And give him thirty more blows. This one-eyed thing—dragons only have one eye—still needs a clear-eyed person to point it out and break it open.
what about it?" Mingzhao said, "A non-person gets hisPress him—fine, give him a pressing. In the end, don’t make this kind of dead, slack interpretation.
advantage." Xuedou said, "At that time, if he had only kicked overSure enough, he has only one eye—he says only half. One hand lifts, one hand grips.
the tea brazier and stump."But what can you do—drawing the bow after the thief has passed. Even so, it still doesn’t qualify as a guest under Deshan’s gate. All the same, he’s a brazen rogue; among them, singular indeed.Stumps all
cut from dried tree roots.

If you want to know the meaning of buddha-nature, you should observe the causes and conditions of time and season. Grand Preceptor Wang governed Quanzhou.
He had long studied with Zhaoqing. One day he entered the monastery; at that time Senior Seat Lang was brewing tea and overturned
the teapot. The Grand Preceptor too was a seasoned hand; as soon as he saw him overturn the teapot,
he asked the senior seat, "Under the tea brazier what is it?" Lang said, "The brazier-bearing spirit." Not bad—
there is an echo in the words. But alas, beginning and end contradict, losing the essential point, dulling the edge and injuring the hand.
It not only betrays himself, it also offends others. Although this is
a matter without gain or loss, if you pick it up, there are still nearness and distance, black and white.
If you discuss this matter, it is not in words; yet you must use words to discern
a living place. Therefore it is said: he studies living phrases, not dead phrases. As for Senior Seat Lang
speaking like that, it is like a mad dog chasing a clod. The Grand Preceptor flicked his sleeves and left, as if unwilling
to accept him. Mingzhao said, "Senior Seat Lang has eaten Zhaoqing's rice, and then goes outside the river
to beat wild stumps." A wild stump is a charred wooden stake out in the wilderness, burned by fire; it is called
a wild stump. This shows that Senior Seat Lang does not proceed in the proper place, but instead goes off to the outside.
Lang pressed him, saying, "Master, then what about it?" Mingzhao said, "A non-person gets his advantage." Ming-
zhao naturally had a way out, and did not fail the question. Therefore it is said:
A keen dog bites without showing its teeth. Master Weishan Zhe said: Grand Preceptor Wang greatly
resembles Xiangru seizing the jade—he went so far his whiskers and beard bristled and his cap was askew. It was that Mingzhao could not
restrain his amusement and could scarcely find such an opening. If Great Weishan were in Senior Seat Lang's place, seeing the Grand Preceptor flick
his sleeves and go, he would put down the teapot and laugh aloud. Why? Seeing it and not taking it up—
hard to meet in a thousand years. Do you not see how Baoshou asked Hu Dingjiao, saying, "I have long heard of Hu
Dingjiao—are you the one?" Hu said, "I am." Baoshou said, "Can you nail empty space
too?" Hu said, "Please, master, break it open and bring it." Baoshou struck. Hu would not accept it. Baoshou
He said, One day there will be some loudmouthed master who will spell it out for you.
Later Hu went to see Zhaozhou and brought up the earlier story. Zhaozhou said, Why did he beat you?
Hu said, I do not know where the fault was. Zhaozhou said, With just this one crack, you still cannot manage?
How could you deal with it? Would you have him smash open empty space too? Hu stopped and left. Zhaozhou spoke on his behalf:
Just nail down this one crack. Hu then had an awakening. In Jingzhao, Master Miqi went traveling on pilgrimage,
and on his return an old veteran asked, On a moonlit night, a broken well-rope—everyone calls it a snake.
When Master Miqi sees the Buddha, what does he call it? Master Miqi said, If there is anything seen,
then it is the same as ordinary beings. The old veteran said, That too is a thousand-year peach pit. National Teacher Zhong asked
Court Attendant Zilin, I have heard you can explain and annotate the Siyi Sutra—is it so? The attendant said,
Yes. The teacher said, Whoever annotates a sutra must first understand the Buddha’s intent. The attendant said, If I did not
understand the intent, how would I dare speak of annotating sutras? The teacher then had an attendant bring a bowl of water, seven
grains of rice, and a single chopstick laid across the bowl, and present it to the attendant. He asked, What
does it mean? The attendant said, I do not understand. The teacher said, If you still do not understand the teacher’s intent, what
are you talking about Buddha’s intent? Grand Tutor Wang and Senior Seat Lang did not agree in their understanding of such talk. Xuedou
in the end said instead, Back then one should simply have kicked over the tea brazier. Though Mingzhao was like
that, in the end he was not as good as Xuedou. Xuefeng, in Dongshan’s community, served as head cook. One
day while washing rice, Dongshan asked, What are you doing? Xuefeng said, Washing rice. Dongshan said, Washing
rice to remove sand, or washing sand to remove rice? Xuefeng said, Sand and rice removed all at once. Dongshan said,
Then what will the assembly eat? Xuefeng immediately overturned the basin. Dongshan said, Your conditions are
not here. Even so, how could it compare to Xuedou saying, Back then one should simply have kicked over the tea
brazier. It is the same kind of moment; when it reaches its use, it naturally soars in the present and shines through
the past—there is a living, unbound place. Verse says:

lg.l:
He comes to question—if it makes a wind, responding to the moment is not skillful.The arrow never misses; by chance it becomes patterned text. It’s fine to want the subtle.That mud-ball fellow—
what limit is there? A square peg pokes at a round hole—still can bump into a genuine hand.
Pitiful, the one-eyed dragon.Having only one pair of eyes, he gets only one stake.

It has never yet displayed its fangs and claws—when fangs and claws open,He has no fangs or claws to display. What fangs and claws are you talking about? You also can’t fool him.Do you see it now?
Xuedou still falls a bit short. If one had such hands and feet, he would kick over the tea brazier.

clouds and thunder are born.Everyone under heaven would take a beating at once; patch-robed monks throughout the land would have nowhere to plant themselves.
A thunderclap on a clear, dry day.
Against-the-current waves—how many times have they passed?Seventy-two blows turn into one hundred fifty.

cb:div.p:
He comes to question—if it makes a wind, responding to the moment is not skillful. Where the Grand Tutor questioned resembles wielding the ax
so that a wind is made. This comes from Zhuangzi: a man of Ying smeared mud on a wall, leaving a small hole, then rolled mud
and threw it to fill it. Some mud fell on the tip of his nose. A carpenter nearby said, Sir,
you fill holes very skillfully. I will wield my ax and remove the mud from the tip of your nose. The mud on the tip
was like a fly’s wing. He had the carpenter cut it away. The carpenter swung the ax, making a wind, and cut
it off, removing all the mud without injuring the nose. The man of Ying stood there without losing his composure. This is what is meant
by both being wonderfully skillful. Although Senior Seat Lang responded to the situation, his words lacked finesse, therefore
Xuedou said, He comes to question—if it makes a wind, responding to the moment is not skillful. Pitiful, the one-eyed
dragon has never yet displayed its fangs and claws. Mingzhao spoke very marvelously, but
he had not yet the fangs and claws that seize clouds and snatch mist. Xuedou would not go along; he could not help but break into a grin.
Speaking to vent for him, Xuedou secretly went along with his intent, and himself versified that kicking over the tea
brazier phrase: When fangs and claws open, clouds and thunder are born; against-the-current waves, how many times have they passed? Yunmen
said, I do not hope you have against-the-current waves; it is enough if you only have an along-the-current intent.
Therefore it is said: if you can realize it under a living phrase, through endless kalpas it will not be false. Senior Seat Lang and Ming
zhao’s phrases seem dead; if you want to see the living place, just look at Xuedou kicking over the tea
brazier.

Raising a pointer, it says: Seven piercings, eight holes; snatching the drum, seizing the flag; a hundred turns, a thousand layers—looking ahead
and behind; sitting on the tiger’s head, grabbing the tiger’s tail—still not a true adept. The ox head sinks, the horse head
turns back; that too is not remarkable. Tell me, when someone beyond measure comes, what then? Try
to bring it up and see.

Case forty-nine is raised: San Sheng asked Xuefeng, The golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net—what does it take as
food? Xuefeng said, Wait until you come out of the net,It is fine to move freely in all directions. This question is too lofty; you should know for yourself—why ask again?
and then I will tell you. San Sheng said, A thousand five hundredIt cuts down a man's reputation by a great deal. A true adept, a lineage master, is naturally at ease.
people, good teachers—yet they do not even recognize the topic of conversation. Xuefeng said,Sudden thunder and lightning—enough to startle the crowd. Jump all you like.
This old monk’s abbot affairs are many.It is not about victory or defeat. Letting one move pass—these words are the most poisonous.

Xuefeng and San Sheng—though there is one coming out, one going in, one pushing, one squeezing—still victory and
defeat are not yet decided. Tell me, what kind of eyes do these two venerable elders have? San Sheng received confirmation from Linji
and traveled everywhere; all treated him as an honored guest. Look at how he sets up this
question—how many people cannot even grope their way to it. It does not touch principle, nature, or Buddha-dharma; instead
he asks: the golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net—what does it take as food? Tell me, what is his intent?
Since the golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net normally does not eat fragrant bait, one does not know what
it takes as food. Xuefeng is an adept, as if at leisure; he answers him with just a bit.
He says to him, Wait until you come out of the net, and then I will tell you. Fenyang called this
a question that presents an understanding; in the Dongshan line it is called borrowing an affair to ask. One must transcend the ordinary and surpass categories to gain
great use; only with an eye in the crown of the head can one be called a golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net. Yet Xue
feng is an adept and does not mind cutting down a man’s reputation; he says, Wait until you come out of the net
and then I will tell you. Look at these two households: they hold the border firmly; they stand like a sheer wall ten thousand fathoms high.
If it were not San Sheng, with just this one sentence he could not move on. Yet San Sheng
is also an adept; only then can he say to him, A thousand five hundred people, good teachers—
yet they do not even recognize the topic of conversation. Xuefeng then says, This old monk’s abbot affairs are many. These words
are that stubborn and insolent. When adepts meet, one seizes, one releases; meeting the strong, one becomes
weak; encountering the lowly, one becomes noble. If you make it into a contest of winning and losing, you have not even dreamed of Xuefeng.
Look at these two men: at first solitary, perilous, steep and severe; at the end both are slack and careless.
Tell me, is there still gain and loss, victory and defeat? Their exchange between adepts surely does not know
such things. When San Sheng was head of the monastery under Linji, Linji at his passing gave this instruction: After I am gone, do not
extinguish my treasury of the true dharma eye. San Sheng stepped forward and said, How would I dare extinguish
the master’s treasury of the true dharma eye? Linji said, Later, if someone asks you, what
will you do? San Sheng gave a shout. Linji said, Who would have known my treasury of the true dharma eye would be extinguished by this blind
donkey! San Sheng then bowed. He was Linji’s true son, and only then dared
to exchange like that. In the end, Xuedou only versified the golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net, revealing where adepts
meet. Verse says:

lg.l:
The golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net—do not say it lingers in shallow water.A thousand soldiers are easy to get; one general is hard to find. What is he like, this fellow? Even a thousand sages cannot do anything about him.Toward him
he stands beyond the clouds, vividly alive—do not be dull and sluggish.
It shakes heaven and churns earth.An adept, an adept—but that is not yet his strange, exceptional place. Let it out—what harm is there?

It bristles its mane and swings its tail; a thousand-foot whale spouts,Who would dare distinguish its beginnings and ends? If you can pull off a trick, show it off—no harm in startling the crowd.
and great waves fly.Turn it over to that side—no harm if it is extraordinary. Swallow everyone under heaven in a single bite.

One thunderclap shakes—clear gusts
arise.Having eyes and ears, yet like the deaf and the blind—who would not shudder?

When clear gusts arise, among heaven and humankind, whoWhere is it? Bah!
knows?Xuefeng firmly grips the head of the formation; San Sheng firmly grips the feet. What is the point of throwing dirt and sand? He strikes and says: Where are you?

cb:div.p:
The golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net—do not say it lingers in shallow water. Wuzu said, This one line already versifies it.
Since it is the golden-scaled fish that has passed through the net, how could it dwell in stagnant water? It must be in vast floodwaves,
where white breakers surge to the sky. Tell me, throughout the twelve hours of the day, what does it take as food? All of you,
under the three rafters and before the seven-foot bench, try to settle it and see what Xuedou says.
This matter is handled as fits the share. Like that golden-scaled kind—when it bristles its mane and swings its tail, it
makes heaven and earth tremble. A thousand-foot whale spouts and great waves fly. This versifies San Sheng saying,
A thousand five hundred people, good teachers—yet they do not even recognize the topic of conversation, like a whale spouting great
waves. One thunderclap shakes, clear gusts arise—this versifies Xuefeng saying, This old monk’s abbot
affairs are many, like a thunderclap shaking as clear gusts arise. In general, it versifies
both of them as adepts. Clear gusts arise—among heaven and humankind, who knows? Tell me,
where does this one line land? A gust is wind. When clear gusts arise,
in heaven and among humankind, how many can know?

Raising a pointer, it says: Passing beyond ranks and transcending expedients, meeting moment after moment, phrase after phrase
matching—if one does not enter the gate of great liberation and attain the function of great liberation, how could one wield provisional
Weighing buddhas and patriarchs, a tortoise-mirror for the school’s vehicle—tell me, when meeting the moment it is straight and direct, turning freely with adverse and favorable.
How will you speak a phrase that steps out? Try presenting it.

Case 50. A monk asked Yunmen, What is the samadhi gate in each and every speck of dust? Yunmen said, Rice in the bowl, water in the bucket.Patch-robed monks throughout the land all make their nests here,
their caves. Their mouths are full of frost—what is the point of throwing sand and dirt?A cloth bag holding an awl. Golden sand
all mixed together—take a mistake and make it serve. In the Hanyuan Hall, you do not ask about Chang'an.

Can you settle it decisively? If you can settle it, Yunmen’s nostrils are in everyone’s hands;
if you can’t settle it, everyone’s nostrils are in Yunmen’s hands. Yunmen
has a phrase that cuts nails and severs iron. Within this one phrase are three phrases. Some, when asked,
immediately say, Rice in the bowl—each grain is perfectly round; water in the bucket—each drop is plainly manifest. If
you understand it like that, you still haven’t seen where Yunmen truly helps people. The verse says:

lg.l:
Rice in the bowl, water in the bucket—this talkative masterIt even shows. What is the point of throwing sand and dirt? Rinse your mouth for three years before you get it.
is hard to get a word in with.Pull your tongue back in. Those who know the Dharma are afraid—why bring it up like this?

The Northern Dipper and the Southern Star—positions not
different.Why call east "west"? Sitting and standing are solemn; the tall have a tall Dharma body, the short a short Dharma body.

White waves flood the sky—on level ground
they rise.Under your feet it is several fathoms deep. Guest and host switch places; suddenly it is over your head—what will you do then? Strike!

Think or don’t think,Blue sky, blue sky—bah!
stop or don’t stop,What are you saying? You only add more resentment and suffering.
each one is a rich man’s son with no trousers.There is no lack of clatter; onlookers snicker.

cb:div.p:
In the preceding verse, Xuedou praised Yunmen’s speaking to the One: speaking to the One is utterly
solitary. A holeless iron hammer drives the wedge down hard. After that he again praised Mazu’s cutting off the four propositions
and severing the hundred negations: The river’s head is white, the sea’s head is black. Even a clear-eyed patchrobed monk won’t
be able to make sense of it. If you penetrate this case, then you’ll see this verse. Right at the start Xuedou
says, Rice in the bowl, water in the bucket. In the words there is an echo; in the phrase the pivot is revealed. This
talkative master is hard to answer. Right after that he gives you a footnote. If you
seek subtle marvels and doctrines here, the more you calculate the harder it is to speak. Xuedou
is fine stopping right here. He likes to pin it down at the outset, fearing that among the assembly there may be those with eyes who would see through it.
When he reaches the later part he must let one move go through, bending down
for beginners, opening up the verse so people can see: the Northern Dipper is still in the north; the Southern
Star is still only in the south. Hence he says, The Northern Dipper and the Southern Star—positions not different. White
waves flood the sky—on level ground they rise. Suddenly, on flat ground, waves surge—what then?
If you look in the realm of events, it’s easy; if you search beneath the root of thought,
in the end you can’t find it. This is like an iron stake—you can’t budge it, you can’t get a word in,
you can’t interrupt. If you speculate, wanting to understand yet not understanding; stopping yet not stopping—confusedly presenting your muddled
bag—that’s exactly each one a rich man’s son with no trousers. Hanshan’s poem says: The six directions constantly
nurse suffering; the nine dimensions are discussed in vain. With talent you’re cast aside in the wilds; without influence you shut the brushwood door.
The sun rises yet the cliffs are still dark; the smoke clears yet the valleys remain dim. Among them, these rich men’s sons—each
one has no trousers at all.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Five, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.End.

cb:div.p:
After Dahui burned it in a single blaze, this collection again suffered the ravages of war; in the world
there are few good printings. Now we have obtained a Shu edition; the collation is largely complete, yet I still fear that within it
there may be one or two boar-for-pig or Lu-for-fish errors. I ask clear-eyed eminent people from all quarters to
correct it as they copy it; if instructed, I will revise it again, so it may become as fine as gold and jade. For the Chan school,
this would be most fortunate.
Written by the recluse Shuyin of Yuzhong.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Six, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.

cb:div.p:
Instruction: The moment there is right and wrong, you lose your mind in a flurry. Not falling into stages, yet with nothing
to grope for—tell me, is letting go right, or is holding fast right? When you come here, if there is
the slightest route of explanation, you’re still stuck in words, still bound by circumstances and objects—all of it is clinging to grass
and attaching to trees. Even if you reach the place of solitary release, you still can’t avoid gazing toward your home pass from ten thousand miles away. Can you
connect with it? If you haven’t connected with it, just understand this case made from a principle. Try
presenting it.

Case 51. When Xuefeng was living in a hut, two monks came to pay respects.What for? Take one charge and carry it through.
Seeing them come, Xuefeng braced the hut door with his hand, stuck his body out, and said, What is this?
The monks also said, What is this?Ghost eyes. A holeless flute. Raising the head, wearing horns.Mud pellets, felt clappers. Arrowheads braced against each other.
Xuefeng lowered his head and returned to the hut. Later the monks went toThorns in rotten mud: like a dragon without feet, like a snake with horns—hard to deal with in the middle of it.
Yantou. Yantou asked, Where have you come from? The monks said, From Lingnan. Yantou said, Have you been to Xuefeng? The monksYou still must have asked before it will do; only then will fellow travelers know.You also must be an adept for it to work. This fellow
often takes in flaws and defeat. If you are not a fellow practitioner, when it comes to letting it pass,what news could you transmit? You must also get through
this bit of news—have you met Xuefeng yet?It has been seen through long ago—do not say you have not arrived.
said, We have. Yantou said, What words did he say? The monksA truly solid person is hard to find. Split him into two piecesand off he goes like that.
brought up the previous exchange. Yantou said, What did he say? The monks said, He was silent, lowered his head, and returned to the hut. YantouOff he goes like that, piling flaw upon flaw.Better to strike him straight in the mouth; you will lose
your nostrils otherwise.Another flaw taken in. Tell me—what is he?
said, Alas! I regret that at the time I didn’t tell him the final phrase.Vast surging waves, white breakers flooding the sky.
If I had told him, people everywhere would have been unable to do anything with old man Xue. At summer’s end the monks again raised the earlier exchange to ask Yantou. Yantou said, Why didn’t you ask sooner? The monksA leper dragging companions—no need. Even Sumeru must
be ground to dust. Tell me then: where does he fence and paint it in?Already not awake,
not awake. The real thief left long ago; after the thief has passed, you draw the bow.Better to flip over the meditation bench. It is past.
said, I didn’t dare be casual. Yantou said,This blow was originally for this monk to take. It pierces through the nostrils; detaining the prisoner grows wisdom—already a double case.
Though Xuefeng and I are born on the same branch, we do not die on the same branch. If you want to knowA net over the sky, a net over the earth.
the final phrase, it is just this.It dupes a whole boatload of people. I do not believe it either. When it comes to failing to sort it out,

In general, to uphold and establish the teaching of the school, you must discern what fits the moment, and know advance and retreat, right and wrong.
Be clear about killing and giving life, capturing and releasing. If your eyes suddenly get muddled and confused, then wherever you go, when you meet a question
you question; when you meet an answer you answer—without realizing your nostrils are in someone else’s hands. Just
like Xuefeng and Yantou, who together studied under Deshan. These monks visited Xuefeng; their understanding only
reached this point. When they came to see Yantou, they still didn’t accomplish a single thing, vainly
troubling those two old worthies—one question, one answer; one capture, one release—down to
today. People everywhere make knots and corners of garbled errors, unable to sort them out. Tell me—these knots and corners
of garbled error—where are they? Though Xuefeng traveled everywhere, in the end at the Ao
Mountain inn, Yantou provoked him, and only then did he cut it off completely and awaken greatly. Later Yantou
encountered persecution and became a ferryman by a lake. On each bank he hung a board; when someone
crossed, they struck the board once. Yantou would say, You cross to that side—then from among the reeds he would
swing the oar and come out. Xuefeng returned to Lingnan to live in a hut. These monks too were long-time
practitioners. When Xuefeng saw them come, he braced the hut door with his hand, stuck his body out, and said, What
is this? Nowadays some, when asked like this, go and chew on his words. These
monks too found it strange, and only replied to him, What is this? Xuefeng lowered his head and returned to the hut. People often
call this understanding as silence. The monks then couldn’t grasp it. Some
say, Xuefeng was pressed by the monks’ question and had to return to the hut in silence. They do not
know that Xuefeng’s intent has a poisonous bite. Though Xuefeng gained the advantage, how could he avoid
hiding his body yet revealing his shadow? Later the monks took leave of Xuefeng, carried this case, and asked Yantou to judge it.
When they arrived there, Yantou asked, Where have you come from? The monks said, From Lingnan. Yantou
said, Have you been to Xuefeng? If you want to see Xuefeng, just this one question is enough—and it’s also good to
look sharply. The monks said, We have. Yantou said, What words did he say? This line
also does not pass by in vain. The monks didn’t understand and simply followed the drift of his words. Yantou said,
What did he say? The monks said, He lowered his head, was silent, and returned to the hut. These monks truly
didn’t know that Yantou, wearing straw sandals, had already walked around inside their bellies several times over.
Yantou said, Alas! I regret that at the time I didn’t tell him the final phrase. If I had
