told him, people everywhere would have been unable to do anything with old man Xue. Yantou too supports the strong and does not
support the weak. These monks remained in total darkness, unable to distinguish black from white, carrying a bellyful
of doubts. They truly said, Xuefeng doesn’t understand. At summer’s end they again raised the earlier exchange
to ask Yantou. Yantou said, Why didn’t you ask sooner? This old fellow—calculations
arise. The monks said, I didn’t dare be casual. Yantou said, Though Xuefeng and I are on the same branch
in birth, we are not on the same branch in death. If you want to know the final phrase—just this is it. Yantou
was utterly reckless, not sparing his eyebrows. Ultimately, how will you understand it? When Xuefeng was
under Deshan, he served as head cook. One day after the evening meal, Deshan carried his bowl down to
the Dharma hall. Xuefeng said, The bell hasn’t rung, the drum hasn’t sounded—where is this old man carrying his bowl
going? Deshan, without a word, lowered his head and returned to the abbot’s quarters. Xuefeng told this to Yantou.
Yantou said, Great Deshan doesn’t understand the final phrase. When Deshan heard this, he had an attendant summon
him to the abbot’s quarters and asked, You won’t approve the old monk—then privately reveal your words. Deshan
When he ascended the hall the next day, it was unlike the usual. In front of the monks’ hall, he stroked
his palms and laughed loudly, saying: Good—this old fellow understands the last phrase. Later, people throughout the world
will be unable to do anything about him. Even so, he only lasted three years. In this public case, it is like
Xuefeng seeing Deshan left speechless and thinking he had gained an advantage—without realizing he had been robbed by a thief
already. Because he had once been robbed by a thief, later he also knew how to be a thief. Therefore
the ancients said: Only the last phrase reaches the solid barrier. If someone says Yantou surpasses
Xuefeng, then they have misunderstood. Yantou often used this device to instruct the assembly, saying: A clear-eyed
person has no ruts; turning away from things is above, chasing things is below. This last phrase—
even if you personally met the patriarchs coming, you still could not grasp it. One evening after the meal, old
Deshan himself carried his bowl down to the Dharma hall. Yantou said: Big Deshan still has not understood
the last phrase. Xuedou raised it, saying: I had heard of a one-eyed dragon—turns out
it really only has one eye. Little did he know: Deshan is a toothless great beast. If
Yantou had not seen through it, how would one know that yesterday and today are different? All of you,
do you want to understand the last phrase? Only allow the old barbarian to know; do not allow the old barbarian to understand.
From ancient times to the present, public cases differ in ten thousand ways, like a thicket of brambles. If you
can pass through, people throughout the world cannot do anything about you; the Buddhas of the three times stand downwind.
If you cannot pass through, Yantou said: Though Xuefeng and I were born on the same branch,
he does not die on the same branch as I. Just this one line naturally has its own place to come forth. Xue-
dou composed a verse:

lg.l:
I speak the last phrase for you.it is already before the words. You think it is real—look at it and you go blind.The tongue drops away; you cannot say it. With a head and no tail, with
a tail and no head.
At the time when light and dark come as a pair,An old vine-tangler. Like an ox without horns, like a tiger with horns—both sides are like this.

born on the same branch, they know each other together; not on the same branch,What sort of breed is it? Neither side has anything to do with the other. You go to Xiaoxiang, I go to Qin.
their dying is still utterly different.The staff is in my hand—how could you blame this mountain monk? Why are your nostrils in someone else's hands?

Still utterly
different.Still want to take a beating? What is there to grope for?

Yellow heads and blue eyes must be sorted out.Everyone under heaven is blunted and tongue-tied. I
am like this too. Others are not like this. Only allow the old barbarian to know; do not allow the old barbarian to understand.

North, south, east, and west—returning and coming,Gather it in. The heels
underfoot still have the five-colored thread on them. I beg you for a staff.

deep in the night, together they watch the snow on a thousand crags.Still short by half a month's journey. Let him
snow blanket the earth far and wide; filling ditches and blocking ravines, no one understands—he is just a blind fellow. Do you recognize the final line? Then strike!

cb:div.p:
I speak the last phrase for you. Xuedou versified this last phrase; his intent is very much in making company down in the weeds,
but a verse is just a verse—it only praises a bit of surface color. If you want to penetrate
and see through, it still is not there. Yet he dares open his mouth wide and say: At the time when light and dark come as a pair,
it opens a single thread of a road for you—and with that one line it also kills you. Finally,
he gives you another explanation. For instance, one day Zhaoqing asked Luoshan: Yantou said
so-and-so, so-and-so; not so-and-so, not so-and-so—what is the purport? Luo-
shan called: Great Master! The master responded. Luoshan said: Both bright, and also both dark. Zhaoqing
bowed in thanks and left. Three days later he asked again: The other day I received the master’s compassion, but
I still cannot see through it. Luoshan said: I have told you fully. Zhaoqing said: The master
is like carrying fire as he walks. Luoshan said: If that is so, then take the place where you doubt and ask it forth.
Zhaoqing said: What is both bright and also both dark? Luoshan said: Born together, also die together.
Zhaoqing at that time bowed in thanks and left. Later a monk asked Zhaoqing: When born together and also
dying together, how is it? Zhaoqing said: Shut the dog’s mouth. The monk said: Great Master, gather up your mouth
and eat your rice. That monk then came and asked Luoshan: When born together but not dying together, how
is it? Luoshan said: Like an ox without horns. The monk said: When born together and also dying together, how
is it? Luoshan said: Like a tiger wearing horns. The last phrase is precisely this principle.
In Luoshan’s assembly there was a monk who used this idea to question Zhaoqing. Zhaoqing
said: Each side knows the other. Why? If I speak one line in the Eastern Victorious Body Continent, the Western
Aparagodaniya Continent also knows. If I speak one line in the heavens, the human realm also knows. Mind knows mind,
and eye illuminates eye. Being born on the same branch is still easy to see; not dying on the same branch
is still utterly different—Shakyamuni and Bodhidharma cannot grope it out. North, south, east,
and west—returning and coming—there is a bit of fine scenery here: deep in the night, together watching the snow on a thousand crags.
Now tell me: is it both bright and both dark? Is it born on the same branch, is it dying on the same branch? A clear-eyed
patched-robed monk, try sorting it out and see.

Case 52. Raised: A monk asked Zhaozhou: For a long time I have heard of Zhaozhou’s stone bridge; coming here I only see
a plank bridge. Zhaozhou said: You only see the plank bridge; you still do notSomeone also comes to stroke the tiger's whiskers—this too is the proper work of a patch-robed monk.
see the stone bridge. The monk said: What is the stone bridge? Zhaozhou said: It lets donkeys cross and lets horses crossUsed to having it easy, this old fellow sells himself off.He has taken the bait. Indeed
so.One cast of the net and it is done—so that no one under heaven has any place to breathe. Once dead, never alive again.
over.Truly urgent.

Zhaozhou has a stone bridge, built by Li Ying, and to this day it is famous throughout the world. A plank
bridge is a single-log bridge. That monk intentionally tried to diminish his majesty and asked
him: For a long time I have heard of Zhaozhou’s stone bridge; coming here I only see a plank bridge. Zhaozhou then
said: You only see the plank bridge; you still do not see the stone bridge. Judging from where he asked, it also
seems like ordinary talk. Zhaozhou used it to fish him; and indeed this monk
bit the hook. Right after, he asked: What is the stone bridge? Zhaozhou said: It lets donkeys cross and lets
horses cross. It is not amiss—within the words there is a place to come forth. Zhaozhou is not like Linji or Deshan,
wielding staff and shout; he only uses phrases to kill and enliven. Look well at this public case—
it seems like an ordinary contest of sharpness. Even so, it is
not amiss that it is hard to bring together. One day he was looking at the stone bridge with the head monk; Zhaozhou asked the
head monk: Who built it? The head monk said: Li Ying built it. Zhaozhou said: When he built it, where
did he set his hand to it? The head monk had no answer. Zhaozhou said: You usually talk about the stone bridge, but when asked
where it began, you do not even know. Another day, as Zhaozhou was sweeping the ground, a monk asked: Master,
you are a good spiritual friend—why is there dust? Zhaozhou said: It comes from outside. He asked again:
In a pure monastery, why is there dust? Zhaozhou said: There is another bit of it too. Again
a monk asked: What is the Way? Zhaozhou said: It’s outside the wall. The monk said: I am not asking about
that Way; I ask about the Great Way. Zhaozhou said: The Great Way passes through Chang’an. Zhaozhou particularly used this
device. When he arrived at plain, solid, safe ground, in guiding people he did not further injure with sharpness or offend by force; naturally
he was solitary and steep. He used this device wonderfully. Xuedou composed a verse:

lg.l:
If you do not set up solitary peril, the Way is then lofty; entering the sea,You must reach this ground for it to work. The words are still in your ears—give him his proper fodder.
you must still fish for a giant sea turtle.Sitting and cutting off the vital pass, letting neither ordinary nor sage through. Shrimp, clams, snails, and mussels are not worth asking about. A real man cannot come in twos and threes.

Worthy of
laughter is that old man Guanxi of the same time;There are also such people who have come like this; there are also such hands and feet that work the mechanisms.

even if he could say “split”
arrows, it would still be in vain.Still short by half a month's journey. It seems like it, seems right, yet it is not.

cb:div.p:
If you do not set up solitary peril, the Way is then lofty. Xuedou is praising how Zhaozhou usually guided people:
he did not set up mystery, did not set up solitary peril. He was not like those around the land who say they smash empty space,
shatter Mount Sumeru, raise dust on the ocean floor, and make Sumeru beat up waves—only then calling it the Patriarchs’
Way. Therefore Xuedou said: If you do not set up solitary peril, the Way is then lofty. A cliff standing ten
thousand fathoms shows the Buddha-Dharma’s extraordinary efficacy. Even so, solitary peril and steepness are not as good as not
setting up solitary peril. Simply in the ordinary, it naturally rolls along; not set up, yet it sets itself up;
not lofty, yet it is lofty. When the mechanism comes forth from solitary peril, then you see the mystery. Therefore Xuedou
said: Entering the sea, you must still fish for a giant sea turtle. Look—an accomplished master casually drops
one line, uses one device: he does not fish for shrimp, clams, snails, or mussels—he directly fishes for a giant sea turtle. This too
shows a master’s hand. This one line is used to illuminate the earlier public case. Worthy of laughter is that of the same time,
old man Guanxi. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Guanxi: For a long time I have heard of Guanxi; when I finally arrive,
I only see a retting-hemp pond. Guanxi said: You only see the retting-hemp pond; you still do not see
Guanxi. The monk said: What is Guanxi? Guanxi said: Split arrows—swiftly! Again, a monk
asked Huanglong: For a long time I have heard of Huanglong; when I finally arrive, I only see a red-spotted snake.
Huanglong said: You only see the red-spotted snake; you still do not see Huanglong. The monk said: What
is Huanglong? Huanglong said: Dragging along. The monk said: If I suddenly meet a golden-winged bird, then
what? Huanglong said: Life is hard to keep. The monk said: Then I would be eaten
by it. Huanglong said: Thanks for the offering. All of this is setting up solitary peril; it may be so, yet
it is not without effort. In the end it is not as good as what Zhaozhou ordinarily used. Therefore Xue-
Xuedou said, To explain it is like splitting an arrow—also futile labor. As for Guanxi and Huanglong, let that be for now;
Zhaozhou said, A wave-ass ferries a horse. How will you understand that? Try to discern it.

Instruction said: Throughout the whole realm there is no hiding; the whole mechanism alone is revealed. Whatever road you touch, there is no obstruction; step by step
there is a way to emerge. Under the phrase there is no partiality; in each and every point there is an intent that can kill. Tell me,
where did the ancients finally rest and settle? Try to raise it and see.

Case 53. Master Ma was walking with Baizhang. They saw wild ducks fly past. Master Ma said, What is that?Two
outlaws rolling in the grass—why suddenly look back?The master should know; this old fellow does not even know his own nostrils.
Baizhang said, Wild ducks. Master Ma said,His nostrils are already in someone else's hands, yet he keeps on confessing. The second ladle of foul water is even more poisonous.
Where have they gone? Baizhang said, They’ve flown past.The first arrow is still light, the next arrow sinks deep. The second pecking and biting—you should know it yourself.
Master Ma then twisted Baizhang’s nose. Baizhang cried out in pain. Master Ma said, When did they ever fly away?You just keep turning after him and miss it right in front of your face.The nostrils you were born with
are instead in someone else's hands. Twist the spearhead around; wrench the nostrils back.It is only right here. Can you still call it a wild duck
or not? Do you still know pain and itch?Do not hide it from people. This old fellow has all along been doing business only in a ghost cave,
making his living.

Seen with the true eye, it is Baizhang who has the right cause; Master Ma stirred waves without any wind.
If you all want to take the Buddhas and Patriarchs as your teachers, study Baizhang. If you want to be unable to save yourselves,
study Master Mazu. Look at those ancients: through the twelve hours of the day, they were never
not right here. Baizhang left the dust while still young, fully trained in the three trainings. When Great
Ji was teaching in Nanchang, he devoted himself and attached himself to him, serving as attendant for twenty years. When
he came again to study, it was only under a shout that he finally had great awakening. Yet now some say there is fundamentally no
place of awakening, and that establishing a gate of awakening is setting this matter up. If you see it like that, it is like
worms within a lion’s body, eating the lion’s flesh themselves. Have you not seen the ancients say: if the source is not deep,
the stream is not long; if wisdom is not great, vision is not far. If you take it as something to be established and understood,
how could the Buddha Dharma have reached us today? Look at Master Ma walking with Baizhang: they saw
wild ducks fly past. Would the master not know they were wild ducks? Why, then,
did he ask like that? Tell me, where does his intent fall? Baizhang simply
followed along behind him. Mazu then twisted his nostrils. Baizhang cried out in pain. Mazu
said, When did they ever fly away? Baizhang immediately understood. But now there are those who misunderstand: as soon as
they are questioned, they make a cry of pain. Luckily, they cannot jump out of it. In the house of a lineage master, to help people
one must teach through. Seeing that they do not understand, one cannot avoid bruising the edge and hurting the hand; one only wants to make
them clearly understand this matter. Therefore it is said: if you understand, you make use of it on the road; if you do not understand, it spreads as
worldly truth. If Mazu had not seized him by twisting, it would only have become worldly-truth circulation.
You must meet circumstances and conditions and, by turns, teach it back to oneself. Throughout the twelve hours,
there is no gap. This is called clarity on the ground of nature. If you only cling to grass and attach to wood, recognizing
some donkey-before, horse-behind, what use is it? Look at how Mazu and Baizhang used it like this:
though it seems bright and numinous, it does not abide in the bright and numinous. Baizhang
cried out in pain. If you see it like that, throughout the whole realm there is no hiding, and everywhere it is made
manifest. Therefore it is said: penetrate in one place, and a thousand places, ten thousand places are penetrated at once. The next day Mazu
ascended the hall; as soon as the assembly gathered, Baizhang rolled up the mat and bowed at the seat. Mazu then
descended from the seat. Returning to the abbot’s quarters, he asked Baizhang, Just now I went up to the hall and did not speak
the Dharma. Why did you roll up the mat? Baizhang said, Yesterday the master twisted
my nostrils so they hurt. The patriarch said, Where did you set your mind yesterday? Baizhang said, Today
my nose no longer hurts. The patriarch said, You deeply know today’s matter. Baizhang then
made a bow and returned to the attendants’ quarters, crying. A fellow attendant asked, Why are you crying
like that? Baizhang said, Go ask the master. The attendant then went to ask Mazu.
Mazu said, Go ask him and see. The attendant returned to the quarters and asked Baizhang. Baizhang
burst into hearty laughter. The attendant said, You were crying just now—why are you laughing now?
Baizhang said, I was crying just now; now I am laughing. Look at him after awakening:
rolling and rolling—no net can cage him. Naturally, he is clear and perfectly formed. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Who knows where the wild ducks are? When Mazu sees them,Forming into flocks and teams, and there is yet another one.What use is it? Like hemp, like millet.
they speak together,What end is there to vine-tangling? What are you even saying? Only Mazu recognized the one who was sharp.

and the talk exhausts mountain clouds and sea
moon-feelings.The east household's ladle-handle is long; the west household's ladle-handle is short. Who knows how much vine-tangling he has done?

Still not understanding as before, they fly off again.
Do not say he cannot speak—where did it fly off to?
About to fly off,The nostrils are in someone else's hands—already you have supplied him with a footing.

he seizes them and says, say!With an old woman's anxious heart, what more is there to say?What saying? You cannot even make this mountain monk say it. Do not make it cry like a wild duck.
Blue sky, blue sky. Under your heels you deserve thirty blows—you do not know where you are going.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou, right at the start, versified: Who knows where the wild ducks are? Tell me, how many are there?
When Mazu sees them, they speak together. This versifies Mazu asking Baizhang, What is that?
Baizhang said, Wild ducks. The talk exhausts mountain clouds and sea moon-feelings. The verse then asks again
where they have gone. For Master Ma, his intent naturally drops away from the body.
Baizhang still does not understand as before, and says, They flew past. Twice he misses it. About to
fly away, he seizes them. Xuedou decides the case on the evidence, and again says, Say, say! This
is where Xuedou turns around. Tell me, how do you say it? If you make a cry of pain,
you are wrong. If you do not make a cry of pain, then how do you understand it? Although Xuedou
versified it wonderfully, he still cannot jump out of it.

Instruction said: Penetrate birth and death; flip the mechanism; casually cut iron and sever nails.
Wherever you are, it covers heaven and covers earth. Tell me, whose tracks are these? Try to raise it
and see.

Case 54. Yunmen asked a monk, Where have you just come from?Do not say "West Chan," do not probe with pole and shadow the grass; do not speak of east, west, south, or north.
The monk said, Xichan. Yunmen said, Recently at XichanSure enough—damnably solid. At that time, it was best to give him his proper fodder.
what sayings are there? The monk spread both hands. Yunmen struck one palm. The monk said,Wanting to bring it up, he fears startling the master. He discerns the incoming wind deeply; it is like the master talking in his sleep.A blunder.
It is ruined. Luring thieves to wreck the house—no wonder it makes people suspicious.Act according to the order. A fine beating—such swift decisiveness is hard to meet.
My words are with you, master. Yunmen again spread both hands. The monk had nothing to say, and Yunmen struck.You want to overturn the verdict, but you seem to have the moves to snatch the banner and seize the drum.A perilous ride.
Even with a green dragon, he does not know how to ride.What a pity.He must not be let off. This staff should land on Yunmen. Why? When it should be cut off,
if you do not cut it off, you invite disorder. How many blows should the senior monk take? Letting him off once—if you do not let him off, then what?

Yunmen asked this monk, Where have you just come from? The monk said, Xichan. This is talk right
in your face, like a flash of lightning. Yunmen said, Recently what sayings are there? That is also just
ordinary talk. This monk, too, is not without being a capable one—he turned it around to
test Yunmen, and spread both hands. If an ordinary person met such a test,
they would immediately be flustered and scrambling. But Yunmen had the opportunity of stone-spark and lightning-flash, and struck
one palm. The monk said, Striking is indeed so, but what about my words being here? This monk had
a place to turn around. Therefore Yunmen loosened up and again spread both hands. The monk had nothing to say.
Yunmen then struck. See how Yunmen is truly a master of the craft: with each step he knows where it lands;
he can look ahead and also look back, never losing the trail. This monk only knew how
to look ahead and could not look back. The verse says:

lg.l:
Tiger head and tiger tail gathered in at once—chillingA knife that kills, a sword that brings to life—only this monk will do. A thousand soldiers are easy to get; one general is hard to find.
majesty, wind over four hundred prefectures.Sitting, he cuts off the tongues of everyone under heaven; covering heaven and covering earth.

He asks back, not knowing why it is so
perilous.Do not use a blind cangue and a blind staff. Xuedou, it turns out, still does not know where it is; the senior monk is hit in turn as well.

The master said, Let one move pass.If you do not
let him off, then what? Everyone under heaven loses the beat at once. He strikes the meditation bench once

cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s verse on this is extremely easy to understand: the main point is simply to praise Yunmen’s edge and devices.
Therefore he says, Tiger head and tiger tail gathered in at once. The ancients said, Seize the tiger head and gather
the tiger tail. Under the first line the central meaning is made clear. Xuedou only decides the case on the evidence, delighting
that Yunmen can seize the tiger head and also gather the tiger tail. The monk spread both hands and Yunmen struck—this is
seizing the tiger head. Yunmen spread both hands; the monk had nothing to say; Yunmen struck again—this is
gathering the tiger tail. Head and tail gathered together, eyes like shooting stars—naturally like striking stone
for sparks, like a flash of lightning. It reaches the point of chilling majesty over four hundred prefectures; it reaches the point that the whole
world is swept by wind. Yet he asks back, Not knowing why it is so perilous. It is no harm
that there is a perilous place. Xuedou said, Let one move pass. Tell me, if one does not let it pass now,
then what? Everyone on the great earth would have to eat the staff. Nowadays Zen monks
all say that when he spreads his hands, you should return his own proper fodder to him. It seems so,
and it also seems so, but though it is so, it is not yet so. Yunmen cannot just teach you to rest in that way; there
must be something else in it.

Instruction said: Steady and close, wholly genuine—take proof right on the spot. Entering among the shifting and turning things, straightaway
shoulder it. In the sparks-from-stone and lightning-flash, sit and cut off error and confusion. As for seizing the tiger
head and gathering the tiger tail—stand like a wall a thousand fathoms high; set that aside for now. Let out a single thread of a path—still
is there a place to help people or not? Try to raise it and see.

Case 55. Daowu and Jianyuan went to a household to offer condolences. Yuan slapped the coffin and said,
Alive, or dead? I said: I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death.and says what? How very alert. This fellow is still caught on both ends.
Yuan said: Why notWhen the dragon chants, mist rises; when the tiger roars, wind is born. Buying appearances—an old woman's anxious heart.
speak? I said: Not speaking, not speaking.He has slipped past it. Indeed, he has misunderstood.Nasty water splashes over the head. The earlier arrow was still light; the later arrow goes deep.
On the way back, Yuan said: The master, quickly tell me. IfToo alert.
you do not tell me, I will beat the master. I said: If you beat me, then beat me; if you want me to speak, then I will not speak. Yuan then struck him.Yet he falls short by a bit. Rare to meet a guest who pierces the ear; often you meet those who notch the boat. A fellow like this cannot speak a word.
Entering hell like an arrow.Again and again, it must be made weighty—right on the body
he is robbed. This old fellow is covered head to toe in mud and water; his first intention does not change.A fine beating—now tell me, why beat him? It turns out someone is taking the unjust staff.
Later Daowu passed away. Yuan went to Shishuang and brought up the previous words. Shishuang said: I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death. Yuan said: Why not speak? Shishuang said: Not speaking, not speaking.Knowing and yet deliberately offending—do you know whether it is
so or not? If it is, then it is very strange indeed.Damnably fresh. This
kind of tea and rice—turns out someone is eating it.Though the words are the same, the meaning is not twofold. Tell me: compared with the earlier
question, is it the same or different?In heaven and on earth—Caoqi's waves seem alike. Countless ordinary people are stranded and sink.
At those words Yuan had an insight. One day Yuan took a shovel and,Blind fellow—do not try to fool this mountain monk.
in the Dharma hall, walked from east to west and from west to east. Shishuang said: What are you doing? Yuan said: Seeking the formerIt is also getting life in the midst of death—good for venting for the former master.
Do not ask him. Just watch this fellow's whole bout of muddleand the mess that follows.
teacher’s sacred bones. Shishuang said: Vast floodwaters,Behind the funeral cart hangs a medicine bag. Regret for not being careful at the start. What do you say?
white waves soaring to the sky—what former teacher’s sacred bones are you seeking? Xuedou added a remark: Blue heaven, blue heaven.You must still acknowledge him as a true expert. Forming into groups, forming
into teams—what for?Too late. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—better to bury it in one pit.
Yuan said: Now is exactly the time to apply effort. Taiyuan Fu said: The former teacher’s sacred bones are still here.Tell me, where did it land? What did the former master ever say to you? This fellow, from start to finish, right up to now—
he cannot get free.Assembly, do you see it? Like a flash of lightning. What
kind of broken straw sandal is this? Still a bit better than that.

Daowu and Jianyuan went to a household to offer condolences. Yuan patted the coffin and said: Alive, or
dead? I said: I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death. If you enter right under the phrase,
then you enter. Right at the words you know where to return. This right here is the key point for passing through life and death.
If it is not so, you often miss it head-on. Look at how the ancients, in walking,
standing, sitting, and lying down, kept this matter in mind. As soon as they arrived at a home to offer condolences, Jian-
yuan patted the coffin and asked Daowu: Alive, or dead? Daowu did not shift
by a hair, and replied to him: I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death. Jianyuan, right
in front of him, missed it, chasing after the words and saying: Why not speak? I
said: Not speaking, not speaking. I could be said to have a heart of pure sincerity, going along with the mistake to meet the mistake. Yuan
still did not wake up. On the way back he again said: The master, quickly
tell me. If you do not tell me, I will beat the master. What does this fellow know of what is good or bad?
So-called: a good heart does not get a good return. Daowu still had an old woman’s anxious heart and again
said to him: If you beat me, then beat me; if you want me to speak, then I will not speak. Yuan then struck him. Although it was
like this, he still gained a point. Daowu did this for him with blood-dripping earnestness,
yet Jianyuan took it without even a glance. After Daowu was struck by him, he then said to
Jianyuan: You go for now; I fear the monastery officials will find out and bring trouble upon you.
He secretly sent Jianyuan out. Daowu was far too compassionate. Later Yuan came to a
small temple and heard an attendant reciting the Guanyin Sutra, saying: If one should be saved by the body of a monk,
then one manifests the body of a monk and teaches the Dharma. Suddenly he had a great awakening and said: At that
time I wrongly blamed my former teacher. How could I have known this matter is not in words and phrases? The ancients
said: A boundless great person gets turned around within the pulse of words. Some with emotional interpretations say: Daowu’s
Not speaking, not speaking—this is already speaking. They call it striking the back and turning the tendons,
making people unable to grope it out. If you understand it like that, how will you ever be steady and secure?
If your feet tread the real ground, not separated by a hair, then you see the Seven Worthy Ladies roaming
the charnel ground. They pointed to a corpse and asked: The corpse is here—where is the person?
The eldest sister said: What, what! The whole assembly together realized the patience of the unborn Dharma. Tell me,
how many is that? A thousand, ten thousand—just one. Later Jianyuan arrived at Shishuang
and raised the earlier words. Shishuang as before said: I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death. Yuan
said: Why not speak? Shishuang said: Not speaking, not speaking. Then he awakened.
One day he took a shovel and, in the Dharma hall, went from east to west and from west to east.
He meant to present his own understanding. Shishuang indeed asked: What are you doing? Yuan said: Seeking
the former teacher’s sacred bones. Shishuang then cut off his heels, saying: Here with me the floodwaters
are vast, the white waves surge to the sky—what former teacher’s sacred bones are you seeking? Since he was seeking
the former teacher’s sacred bones, why did Shishuang speak like that? When you get here, if
at the place of I will not speak of life; I will not speak of death, you can be confirmed right at the words—only then do you know from
beginning to end you can fully use the whole function. If you make it into doctrine, weighing and thinking, then it truly
is hard to see. Jianyuan said: Now is exactly the time to apply effort. See how after awakening he spoke with natural
strangeness: Daowu’s skull was like golden color; when struck it made a bronze sound.
Xuedou added a remark: Blue heaven, blue heaven. The meaning fell on two sides. Taiyuan
Fu said: The former teacher’s sacred bones are still here—naturally spoken with steadiness. With this one slip,
it is all taken to one side at once. Tell me, what is the essential point of insight here? What
is the place to apply effort? Haven’t you heard: when one place is penetrated, a thousand places, ten thousand places are penetrated all
at once. If you can penetrate through at the place of not speaking, not speaking, then you cut off the tongues of all
people under heaven. If you cannot penetrate, you still must investigate and awaken by yourself; you cannot
let the days pass casually. What a pity, all that time. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Rabbits and horses have horns; oxen and sheep have no horns.Cut! Damnably extraordinary; damnably fresh.Cut! What kind of shape does it become? You can fool other
people, sure.

Cut off hair and down entirely—like mountains, like peaks.In heaven and on earth, only I alone am honored—where are you groping for it?
Where is it? Waves arise on level ground—right to your nostrils.

The golden sacred bones are still here today.Cut off the tongue; block up the throat.
Toss it to one side—only afraid no one will recognize it.

With white waves surging to the sky, where can you set it down?Letting it off once: it slips right under your heel; in your eyes, in your ears—
it cannot be lodged.

There is nowhere to set it down; the single sandal going back to the West was once lostIndeed. Yet it falls short by a bit—indeed, it drowns in a deep pit.
altogether.The ancestors are not finished, and it burdens children and grandchildren. He strikes and says: why is it here?

cb:div.p:
Xuedou especially knows how to plant the foot. He is a descendant in Yunmen’s line. In every single phrase
there are the tongs and hammer of three phrases. He speaks through what is hard to say; he pries open what cannot be pried
open. He takes its crucial point and chants it out, saying straight: rabbits and horses have horns; oxen
and sheep have no horns. Tell me, why do rabbits and horses have horns? Why do oxen and sheep
instead have no horns? If you penetrate the earlier words, only then do you know Xuedou has a way of working for people. Some
mistakenly understand it and say: not speaking is speaking; having no phrase is having a phrase. Rabbits and horses have no
horns, yet they say they have horns; oxen and sheep have horns, yet they say they have no horns. That is still irrelevant.
They do not realize that the ancients, transforming in a thousand ways, manifest such spiritual powers only to
smash your goblin cave of cleverness. If you can penetrate through, there is no need for a single finished
word. Rabbits and horses have horns; oxen and sheep have no horns. Cut off hair and down entirely—like mountains, like peaks.
These four lines are like a single mani jewel. Xuedou, all in one lump,
spits it out right in front of you. At the end it is all deciding the case by the evidence. The golden
sacred bones are still here today; with white waves surging to the sky, where can you set it down? This verses Shishuang’s
words together with Taiyuan Fu’s. Why is there nowhere to set it down? The single sandal going back to the West was once lost
altogether. The spirit tortoise drags its tail—this is where Xuedou turns around to work for people. The ancients
said: he investigates the living phrase, not the dead phrase. Since it was lost, why does he in one blaze
still scramble and contend?

The pointer said: the Buddhas never came forth into the world, and there is not a single dharma given to people. The patriarchs
never came from the West, and never transmitted mind to mind. It is only that people of the time do not understand, and
run outward in search, not realizing that beneath their own heels lies a great matter of causes and conditions.
A thousand sages also cannot grope it out. Right now, do you see or not see, hear or not hear—tell me,
do you speak or not speak, know or not know—where does it come from? If you cannot yet penetrate it, then
understand it within the vine-tangle cave. Try raising it and see.

Case 56. The Chan traveler Liang asked Qinshan: When a single arrowhead smashes through the three barriers, how is it? Qinshan said: Release the master within the barriers and see. Liang said: If so, then knowing one’s faults one must correct them. QinshanPerilous, yet
no hindrance to being extraordinary. No hindrance: he is a fierce general.He comes straight at your face. Everyone should know it. The main
peak is high; it presses the mountains low.He acts upon seeing the moment—already fallen into the second head.
said: What are you waiting for? Liang said: A fine arrow cannot be loosedThere is capture and there is release; when the wind moves, the grass lies down.
at the right place. Then he left. Qinshan said: Come here, acarya.Indeed. He means to overturn the verdict, but the second staff does not hurt.
Liang turned his head. Qinshan grabbed him and said:To call is easy; to send off is hard. If you can call him back to turn his head, what is it fit for?Indeed, he cannot hold it. Hit.
As for one arrowhead smashing through the three barriers, set that aside for now. Try shooting an arrow at Qinshan. Liang started to deliberate, and Qinshan struck him sevenSideways in the tiger's mouth,
his body—waves against the current. To see what is right and not do it is lack of courage.Indeed, he cannot grope it out. He strikes and says: what a pity.
blows, saying: Let this fellow doubt for thirty years.The order should be like this: with a beginning and an end, head straight and tail straight. This
staff should land on Qinshan.

The Chan traveler Liang was indeed a fighting general. In Qinshan’s hands, he turned left
and spun right, dropping the whip and darting away. In the end—what a pity—his bow broke and his arrows were spent. Although
it was so, General Li still had his good reputation; not being granted a marquisate is
of no concern. This case: one exit, one entry; one capture, one release—meeting face-to-face at the moment,
he raises it. Face-to-face, the moment is swift. It does not fall into being or non-being, gain or loss—this is called the mysterious pivot.
If you are short even a little strength, you will stumble. This monk also was a spirited
patch-robed one. He brought up a question that could startle the crowd. Qinshan is a master hand, a lineage
teacher, and immediately knew where the question landed. An arrowhead is the head of an arrow. When one arrow shoots
through the three barriers, how is it? Qinshan’s intent is: even if you shoot through, set it aside for now.
Try letting out the lord within the passes and see. Liang said: If so, then one knows one’s faults and will surely correct them; it
is not without its strangeness. Qinshan said: What are you waiting for? Look at how he answers like that.
In what Qinshan asked, there was not the slightest gap. But afterward the Zen guest Liang instead
said: A fine arrow cannot find a place to land. He flicked his sleeves and went out. As soon as Qinshan saw him
speak like that, he called out: Come here, venerable. The Zen guest Liang indeed could not
hold himself and turned his head. Qinshan seized him and said: One shaft breaks the three barriers, fine, stop there.
Try shooting an arrow for Qinshan to see. Liang started to deliberate, and Qinshan immediately struck him seven times with the staff, and then
afterward recited a spell for him, saying: Now listen to this fellow doubt for thirty years.
Nowadays Zen fellows all say: Why not hit eight times? And why not hit
six times, only hit seven? Or else, wait for him to ask, Try shooting an arrow for Qinshan
to see, and then hit him. It seems like it, and it is kind of like it, but it is not yet it. This koan
requires that in your breast you do not harbor even a bit of principle-chasing and calculation, and that you go beyond
words, only then can you have one line that breaks the three barriers at a stroke, and only then do you have a place to shoot the arrow.
If you keep hold of right and wrong, in the end you cannot find it. If at that time this monk had been
a real man, then Qinshan was also very perilous. Since he could not carry out this command, he could not avoid turning it
around on him. Tell me: the lord within the passes, who is he really? Look at Xuedou’s verse,
which says:

lg.l:
I will let out for you the lord within the passes; you archers, do notHit. He slips past it right at the front. Step back, step back!
act recklessly.Once dead, he does not live again. A great falsehood has passed.

Take one eye, and the ears will surely go deaf.Left eye: half a catty. Letting it off once.
The left side does not go forward; the right side does not go back.

Give up one ear, and both eyes will go blind.Right eye: eight taels. Only one road. Go forward and you fall into a pit and a ditch; retreat
and you are under a fierce tiger's feet.
Pitiful: one shaft breaks the three barriers,When the whole functioning comes like this, what then? Say something. Broken. Fallen.

and plainly, distinctly, there is the road behind the arrow. Do you not seeDead fellow. Hah! He strikes and says: do you see?A leprous child dragging companions—strike!
Off with the vines and creepers!

Xuansha’s saying: a great man takes what is before heaven as the mind’s ancestor.Which one is not Xuansha?
One phrase cuts off the current; ten thousand workings are put to rest and pared away. Your nostrils are in my hand. Before heaven, earth, and the world existed, where did you settle body and life?

cb:div.p:
These few lines of verse borrow phrases from Guizong’s verse. In the past, because Guizong composed this
verse, he was called Guizong. In the Zen school it is called a statement of the central meaning. Later, Tong’an
heard of it and said: Elder Liang is good at shooting arrows, but in the end he does not understand hitting the mark. A
monk then asked: How do you hit the mark? Tong’an said: What kind of person is the lord within the passes?
Later a monk brought this up to Qinshan. Qinshan said: If Elder Liang is like that, he still
cannot escape Qinshan’s mouth. Even so, Tong’an did not mean well. Xuedou
said: I will let out for you the lord within the passes. With eyes open it hits; with eyes closed it hits. With form or without form,
all are cut into three pieces. You archers, do not act recklessly. If you are good
at shooting arrows, then you are not reckless; if you are not good at shooting, then your recklessness is obvious. Take
one eye and the ears will surely go deaf; give up one ear and both eyes will go blind. Tell me, if you take
one eye, why do the ears go deaf? If you give up one ear, why do both eyes go blind? These
words have no taking and no giving up, only then can you penetrate them. If there is taking and giving up, it is hard to see. Pitiful
one shaft breaks the three barriers, and plainly, distinctly, there is the road behind the arrow. The Zen guest Liang asked: When
one shaft breaks the three barriers, what then? Qinshan said: Let out the lord within the passes and see. And so
down to the final Tong’an case, all of it is the road behind the arrow. Ultimately, how is it?
Do you not see? There is Xuansha’s saying: a great man takes what is before heaven as the mind’s ancestor. Ordinarily
one takes mind as the ultimate ancestor, so why here does it instead place it
before heaven and earth were born, as still the ancestor of this mind? If you break through and recognize this moment,
only then do you recognize the lord within the passes, and plainly, distinctly, the road behind the arrow. If you want to hit the mark,
behind the arrow there is clearly a road. Tell me, what is the road behind the arrow? You also must
bring out your own brilliance for it. A great man takes what is before heaven as the mind’s ancestor. Xuansha
often used these words to instruct the assembly. But this verse actually belongs to Guizong; Xuedou mistakenly
used it as Xuansha’s words. Now, among those who study, if they take this mind as the ancestor,
even if they study until Maitreya Buddha descends, they still will not understand. For a real great man,
mind is still a child or grandchild. Once heaven and earth are not yet divided, that is already the second head. Tell me, right
at such a time, what is before heaven and earth?

Pointer: Before you have penetrated, it is like a silver mountain and iron wall. Once you have penetrated,
you realize you yourself were originally the iron wall and silver mountain. If someone asks, then how
is it? Just tell him: If right here you can reveal one mechanism and see one
realm, cutting off the vital pass so that neither ordinary nor sage gets through, that is not yet extraordinary. But if it is not so,
look at how the ancients were.

Case 57. A monk asked Zhaozhou: The supreme Way is not difficult, it only dislikes choosing and selecting. What is
not choosing and selecting? Zhaozhou said: In heaven above,This iron caltrop—how many cannot swallow it. Plenty of people get stuck in doubt; their mouths full of frost.
on earth below, I alone am honored. The monk said: ThisA heap of bones rises on level ground. The patched monks' nostrils are pierced all at once. A vajra casts an iron bond.
is still choosing and selecting. Zhaozhou said: You field-warehouse slave, whatIndeed, he has been turned along with it. Pressing this old fellow tight—
place is choosing and selecting? The monk had no words.mountains rise high and rocks split.I let you off with thirty blows, until your eyes stare and your mouth gapes.In the dead of night,
every sound is held within.

A monk asked Zhaozhou: The supreme Way is not difficult, it only dislikes choosing and selecting. In the Third Patriarch’s Inscription on Faith in Mind,
it opens by stating these two lines. How many people misunderstand them. Why? The supreme Way
originally has no difficulty, and also no not-difficulty; it is only that it dislikes choosing and selecting. If you understand it like this,
then for ten thousand years you still will not even dream of it. Zhaozhou often used these words to question people.
This monk took these words and turned them around to question him. If you seek it in the words, then this monk is
shocking heaven and moving earth. If it is not in words and phrases, then how are you to study for another
thirty years? This little hinge-point must be turned before it is understood. To stroke the tiger’s
whiskers also requires a proper hand. This monk did not even heed danger or death;
he dared to stroke the tiger’s whiskers and said: This is still choosing and selecting. Zhaozhou immediately blocked him, saying:
You field-warehouse slave, what place is choosing and selecting? If he were asked something else, you would see
feet in a rush and hands in a mess. But this old man was an adept: he moved where one cannot move,
and turned where one cannot turn. If you penetrate all venomous words and phrases,
even the thousand differences and ten thousand forms of worldly chatter, all of it is the finest ghee. If
you reach the solid ground, only then do you see Zhaozhou’s red heart in pieces upon pieces. Field-warehouse slave is
a Fuzhou-Tang saying, a local insult. It sounds as if one has no wits at all. When the monk said: This is still
choosing and selecting, Zhaozhou said: You field-warehouse slave, what place is choosing and selecting? A lineage master’s
eye and pupil must be like this, like the golden-winged bird splitting the sea and directly seizing a dragon to swallow. Xuedou
versified it, saying:

lg.l:
Deep as the sea, firm as a mountain,What measure is this? The source is hard to fathom—you have not even gotten half of it.Who can shake
it? Still halfway along.
a gnat and horsefly playing with a fierce wind in empty space.There are also ones like this. Indeed, he misjudged his strength—damnably lacking in self-measure.

Ants shaking an iron pillar, choosing and selecting, choosing and selecting,Same pit, no different dirt. For now, it is of no concern. The senior monk studies along with him.
Carrying water to sell by the riverbank—what are you saying, that Zhaozhou has come?

a cloth drum hung in the open.It was already buried in a pit before the words. Like hemp, like millet, he strikes and says: plug up your throat—
your gullet.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou commented on two lines: Deep as the sea, firm as a mountain. The monk said: This
is still choosing and selecting. Xuedou said: This monk is like a gnat and horsefly playing with a fierce
wind in empty space, like ants shaking an iron pillar. Xuedou praised his boldness. Why? This is
what the people above use, and he dared to speak like that. Zhaozhou refused to let him go and
said: You field-warehouse slave, what place is choosing and selecting? Is this not the fierce wind and the iron pillar?
Choosing and selecting, choosing and selecting, a cloth drum hung in the open. In the end Xuedou brings it up to enliven it. If
you understand it clearly, then you have fully brought it forth yourself. Why? Do you not see it said:
If you want it intimate, do not take a question to ask a question. Therefore: a cloth drum hung in the open.

Case 58. A monk asked Zhaozhou: The supreme Way is not difficult, it only dislikes choosing and selecting. At that time,
was he in the nest or den? Zhaozhou said:A double case—also a place that makes people doubt. Step on the scale-weight and it is hard as iron. Still there is this: do not use yourself to hinder others.
Someone once asked me, and for a full five years I could not brush it off.A red face is not as good as straight words. The barbarian monkey eats
hairy worms. A mosquito bites an iron ox.

All his life Zhaozhou did not wield the staff and shout, yet what he used surpassed staff and shout. This monk’s question
was also very strange. If it were not Zhaozhou, it would be hard to answer him. For Zhaozhou
was an adept; he simply told him: Someone once asked me, and for a full five
years I could not brush it off. Where he asked was a cliff of a thousand fathoms; where he answered, he also did not belittle him.
If you understand it just like this, it is right on the spot. If you do not understand, then do not make principle-calculations.
Do you not see Touzi Zongdao? Under Xuedou he served as a scribe. Xuedou
had him investigate The supreme Way is not difficult, it only dislikes choosing and selecting. From this he gained understanding. One day
Xuedou asked him, “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and picking—what do you make of that?” Zong
said, “Beast, beast!” Later he lived in seclusion at Touzi. Whenever he went to take up abbacy, he wrapped his kashaya
around straw sandals and sutra texts. A monk asked, “What is the household style of a person of the Way?” Zong
said, “A kashaya wrapped around straw sandals.” The monk said, “I don’t yet understand the meaning—how is it?” Zong said,
“Barefoot down in Tongcheng.” Therefore it is said: offering to the Buddha is not a matter of much incense. If you can penetrate
through and cast it off, granting and seizing are in my hands. Since it is a single question and a single answer, it is plainly present—why
then did Zhaozhou say, “For five years I couldn’t get it out”? Tell me—was that the pits and holes of the people of the time
or not? Zhaozhou answered him from within the pits and holes; he answered him from outside the pits and holes. You must know
this matter is not in words and phrases. If there is someone who penetrates to bone and marrow and can believe
it through, it is like a dragon gaining water, like a tiger leaning on a mountain. The verse says:

lg.l:
The elephant king frowns and groans; the lion roars and howls.Riches among riches—who would not shudder? What news indeed.A master among masters—
a master. The brains of a hundred beasts split—what a fine way in.

Tasteless talk,Trading insults, I’ll allow you to talk back. Like an iron stake—what is there to chew on?
Separated for no less than over five years. In a one-leaf boat, it carries Great Tang. Vast and still, waves suddenly rise—who knows there is another fine consideration.

blocking people’s mouths.Spitting at each other.
I’ll even let you splash water. Hey—Acarya, what are you saying?

North, south, east, west—crows fly and rabbitsIs there? Is there? Above heaven and below heaven—blue heaven, blue heaven!
run.From ancient times to now, all buried alive at once.

cb:div.p:
Zhaozhou said, “Someone once asked me, and for five years I couldn’t get it out.” It’s like
the elephant king frowning and groaning, the lion roaring and howling—tasteless talk, blocking people’s mouths. North,
south, east, west—crows fly and rabbits run. If Xuedou didn’t have the final line, where else
would there be any Xuedou? Since it is “crows fly and rabbits run,” tell me—Zhaozhou, Xuedou, and this mountain monk
ultimately fall in what place?

The introductory instruction says: It covers heaven and encloses earth, surpasses sages and transcends the ordinary; on the tips of a hundred grasses it points out
the wondrous mind of nirvana; amid a thicket of spears and halberds it fixes the lifeline of patch-robed monks. Tell me—receiving
whose grace and power is it that one can be thus? Try raising it and see.

[59] Raised: A monk asked Zhaozhou, “The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and picking. As soon as there are words, that is choosing and picking. Master, how do youHaul it forward again. Say
what? A triple-layered koan.A mouth full of frost.
meet people?” Zhaozhou said, “Why don’t you quote the whole saying?” The monk said, “I only recite up to here.” Zhaozhou said, “Just this: the supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and picking.”Pressing in on this old fellow.A thief is a petty man whose wit surpasses a gentleman. A bare-handed thief.
Riding the thief’s horse to chase the thief.Two fellows playing with mud clods meet a thief—base and root
hard to match.In the end it is because of this old fellow—he got
his eyes swapped out by him, and was caught and defeated.

Zhaozhou said, “Just this: the supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and picking.” Like striking flint
fire, like the flash of lightning—capturing and releasing, killing and giving life—he attained such freedom. The monasteries everywhere all say,
“Zhaozhou has discourse that outstrips the crowd.” In Zhaozhou’s usual instruction to the assembly he has this passage:
“The supreme Way is not difficult; it only dislikes choosing and picking. As soon as there are words, it is choosing and picking; it is
clarity. This old monk is not within clarity. Do you people still cherish it or not?”

At that time a monk asked, “If you are not within clarity, what is there to cherish?” Zhaozhou
said, “I don’t know either.” The monk said, “Since the master doesn’t know, why do you say
you are not within clarity?” Zhaozhou said, “You got it by asking.” The monk bowed and withdrew. Later
this monk just seized on the crack in it and went to question him. The questioning is indeed not without wonder,
but it is only the working of mind. If it were someone else, there would be no way to deal with him; but
Zhaozhou is a genuine hand, so he immediately said, “Why don’t you quote the whole saying?” This monk too
knew how to turn around and let out his breath, and said, “I only recite up to here.” It’s as if he had
set it up. Zhaozhou took it up with his very sound and answered at once—no need to calculate. The ancients
called this “continuation,” and it is very hard. He distinguishes dragons from snakes and separates fortune from misfortune—he deserves his
rightful name as a true hand. Zhaozhou swapped out this monk’s eyes; he did not touch the blade’s edge, he did not get caught in
calculation, and it is naturally just right. You cannot call it having a phrase; you cannot call it having no
phrase; you cannot call it a phrase that is neither having nor not having. It is beyond the four phrases
and cuts off the hundred negations. Why? If we speak of this matter, it is like striking flint fire, like lightning
flashing—only by snapping your eyes to it do you see it. If you speculate and hesitate, you cannot avoid losing body
and life. Xuedou’s verse says: Hesitation.The upper tone hesitates, the lower tone removes—still wavering and not advancing.

lg.l:
Water can’t splash it; wind can’t enter it.What talk of being too deep and distant? What is there to speak of together?Like empty space.
Stiffly and bluntly, making a report to the void.

Tiger steps, dragon strides; ghosts howl and spirits weep.In his house he is at ease—no harm if it is unusual.
The assembly covers its ears; the grass bends and the wind moves. Acarya, could it be you practiced together with him?

A head three feet long—who would know who it is?A strange thing.
What quarter’s sage is this? Do you see, do you see?

Facing each other without words, standing on one leg alone.Tsk! Pull your head back. Let one move pass—mountain goblin, let it pass.
That will not do. Then he strikes.

cb:div.p:
Water can’t splash it; wind can’t enter it. Tiger steps, dragon strides; ghosts howl and spirits weep. There is
nowhere for you to peck and bite. These four lines praise Zhaozhou’s answer—it is very much like dragons galloping and tigers
rushing. This monk only got a single “mo.” Not only this monk—ghosts as well
howl and spirits weep. It’s like wind moving and grass bending. As for the last two lines, it can be called a single
son personally attaining it: “A head three feet long—who would know who it is? Facing each other without words, standing on one leg alone.”
Haven’t you seen a monk ask an ancient worthy, “What is Buddha?” The ancient worthy said, “Head three
feet long, neck two inches long.” Xuedou cites it. I wonder—do you people recognize it? This mountain
monk does not recognize it either. In one stroke Xuedou stripped the body away and painted off Zhaozhou—truly he is
right there. You all must look closely and carefully.

The introductory instruction says: Buddhas and sentient beings are originally no different—how could mountains and rivers themselves have
gradations? Why then does it all turn into two sides? If you can flip the phrase-head
around, you sit and cut off the vital crossing. If you let it pass, it won’t do; if you don’t let it pass, the whole great
earth won’t take even a pinch. So how is the place where you flip the phrase-head around? Try raising it
and see.

[60] Raised: Yunmen held up his staff before the assembly and said,Teaching and transforming lies in the moment. A knife that kills, a sword that brings to life—your eyes have been swapped out.
“The staff turns into a dragon and swallows heaven and earth—where do the mountains, rivers, and great earth come from?”Why all the circling about? What will you use transformation for?Under heaven,
the patched-robed monk’s very life is not preserved—does it still stick in the throat? Acarya, where will you settle your body and establish your life?
Coming.In the ten directions there are no walls; on all sides there is no gate. East west south north, the four quarters and above and below—yet what can be done about this one, how

Now take Yunmen’s saying: “The staff turns into a dragon and swallows heaven and earth.
Where do the mountains, rivers, and great earth come from?” If you say there is, you are blind; if you say there is not, you
Dead. Do you still see where Yunmen worked for people? Give me back my staff. Nowadays
people do not understand Yunmen’s uniquely revealed point, and instead say, “Using form to illuminate the mind,” “attaching to things”
to manifest principle.” Take Shakyamuni for example: for forty-nine years he preached the Dharma; one cannot fail to
know this line of talk. Why? Then they bring up the holding up of a flower and Kashyapa’s faint smile. This old fellow
then smears on a beard and says, “I have the Treasury of the True Dharma Eye, the wondrous mind of nirvana; I entrust it to
Mahakashyapa.” What need is there, then, to speak of a separate transmission of the mind-seal? Since you are all guests under the Patriarchs’
gate, do you understand the mind that is singly transmitted? If there is a single thing in your chest,
mountains, rivers, and the great earth abruptly appear before you. If there is not a single thing in your chest, then outwardly there is
not the slightest trace. What talk is there of principle and wisdom, dark merging, or spirit meeting? Why? One
understanding is all understandings; one clarity is all clarity. Changsha said, “Students of the Way do not
recognize the real, only because from the start they have recognized the spirit.” The root of birth and death from measureless kalpas—fools
call it the original person. If you suddenly smash through the realm of the aggregates, body and mind are one; within the body
and outside it there is nothing left over—still you have not gotten even half of it. What talk is there of “using form to illuminate the mind,” “attaching to things”
to manifest principle”? The ancients said, “As soon as a single mote arises, the whole earth is gathered in.” Tell me: what
is that single mote? If you recognize this single mote, then you recognize the staff. The moment you
raise the staff, you immediately see its wondrous functioning in every direction. Talking like this is already
tangled vines. How much more if you go on and turn it into a dragon. Storekeeper Qing said, “In the five thousand four
hundred and eighteen fascicles, was there ever talk like this?” Yunmen always, with the staff,
would pick up the whole device and the great function, working for people in a vividly alive way. Bajiao addressed the assembly
and said, “The nostrils of patch-robed monks are all on the tip of the staff.” Yongjia also said, “It is not
an empty matter of outward marks; the Tathagata’s jeweled staff is his own trace.” In the past, when the Tathagata was with
Dipankara Buddha, he spread out his hair and covered the mud to await that Buddha. Dipankara said, “In this place there should
be built a monastery.” At that time a deva prince took a single blade of grass and marked it, saying, “Build a monastery
right here.” Tell me, everyone: where did this news come from? The Patriarch said,
“Take realization at the end of the staff; take it up under the shout.” Tell me, what is it that you take up? Suddenly
someone asks, “What is the staff? Is it doing a somersault? Is it
a single clap of the hands?” All of that is just playing with spirits—and luckily it has nothing to do with it. Xuedou
versified: Stripping away.the sound races.

lg.l:
The staff swallows heaven and earth—mere talk, like peach-blossom waves surging.What are you saying? Only use it to beat the dog.Push open upward
one opening. A thousand sages all stand downwind. It is not in grasping clouds and snatching mist. Saying it a thousand, ten thousand times is not as good as using hands and feet to corral it once.

The one with a scorched tail does not
lie in seizing clouds and snatching mist.Left and right. This old monk just keeps watching—it is only a piece of dry firewood.

As for the one with puffed cheeks—what then?
will surely lose your courage and your very soul.Everyone has the bearing of a king. That is your thousand miles, ten thousand miles—yet what of this sudden shudder?

He has brought it up, too.Thanking for compassion,
and pity. The old woman’s heart is urgent.

Did you hear or not? You must go straight on open and unencumbered,You cannot avoid falling into the weeds. What use is hearing?Leftover broth and sour rice.
Qian and Kun—where does the great earth come from?

and do not get tangled up in a mass of fuss and confusion.The one who raises the command commits first. Next it comes to your head. He strikes, saying: If you let it pass, then
it will not do.

Seventy-two blows are still leniency; a hundredThis mountain monk has never carried out this command. Act according to the command—thanks to this mountain monk.
and fifty would not let you off.When the true command is in force, how could it end with just this? Even if you strike three thousand in the morning and eight hundred at night, what would it amount to?

The master suddenly
picked up his staff and came down from the seat; the whole assembly scattered at once.Why does Xuedou make a dragon head and snake tail?

Exposed.
The sound is pu-ri-qian. It is generally taken as bao. Also bo-bao-qie.

cb:div.p:
Yunmen helps people with roundabout means; Xuedou helps people by cutting straight through. So he brushes aside the turning-into
a dragon: no need to say it that way. It is only that the staff swallows heaven and earth. Xuedou
in the main point spares people sentimental interpretations; he further says it is mere talk of the peach-blossom waves surging. There is even less need
to speak of turning into a dragon. Yu Gate has three tiers of waves; every third month, when the peach blossoms
bloom, the waves rise. If a fish can swim against the current and leap over the waves, it immediately turns into a dragon. Xuedou
says: even if it turns into a dragon, it is still mere talk. The one whose tail is scorched is not about grasping clouds and seizing
mist. When the fish passes Yu Gate, there is naturally heavenly fire that scorches its tail; then it grasps clouds and seizes mist
and goes off. Xuedou's meaning is: even if it turns into a dragon, it still is not in grasping clouds and seizing mist
either. Why must one who exposes his gills lose courage and soul? The Preface to the Qingliang Commentary says: those who have amassed
practice as bodhisattvas still expose their gills at Dragon Gate. The general meaning is to clarify that the Avatamsaka realm
is not something small virtue and small wisdom can reach. It is like a fish passing Dragon Gate: if it cannot
get through, it bumps its forehead and turns back, trapped in dead water and sandy flats, exposing its gills
there. Xuedou's meaning is: once it bumps its forehead and turns back, it will surely lose courage and soul. He has brought it up
too. Did you hear or not? He adds another footnote, and in one stroke sweeps it all clean for you. You all
must go straight on open and unencumbered; do not get tangled up in a mass of fuss and confusion. If you get
tangled up again, you will lose the staff. Seventy-two blows are still lenient;
Xuedou sets aside the heavy and takes the light for your sake. The ancients spoke of seventy-two blows, and turned it
into one hundred and fifty. People nowadays misunderstand and only count the numbers. It should be
seventy-five blows. Why? They only see seventy-two blows, not realizing the ancients'
meaning is beyond the words. Therefore it is said: This matter is not in words and phrases, lest later
people go drilling and chiseling at it. That is why Xuedou cites it. Even if you truly are open and unencumbered,
it would be just right to give you seventy-two blows; that is still leniency. Even if you had it all,
a hundred and fifty would not let you off. With one verse he has finished it, yet he again picked up
the staff, helping in layer upon layer. Even so, not a single one of you has blood under the skin.
blood.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Six, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu, ends.

cb:div.p:
The recluse Ding of Yuzhong has engraved and printed Yuanwu's Blue Cliff Record; fortunately the work is now completed. If Zen friends in the four
directions happen to obtain rare printed editions of Ancestors' Courtyard Matters Grove, Record of Myriad Virtues Returning Together, or writings of the Zen
school, which the world seldom has in print, I beg you to show them to me, and I will have them finely
carved and printed, to broaden Zen study. This too is one expedient means of guiding and drawing people in. I respectfully
ask you not to withhold such a treasure. Respectfully submitted.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Seven, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.

cb:div.p:
Instruction said: Raising the Dharma banner and establishing the school's intent belongs to a true master in his proper role. To distinguish dragons
from snakes and tell black from plain requires a seasoned knowing one. On the sword edge discuss killing and giving life; on the staff
tip discern the pivot of opportunity. Set that aside for now: tell me, how will you discuss the single phrase about solely occupying the world?
Try bringing it up and see.

Case 61: Fengxue offered a saying: If you establish a single mote of dust, the household and the nation flourish; if you do not establish a single mote of dust, the household and the nation perish. Xue-Raising clouds and bringing rain—you must also be host and be guest.I am the Dharma king,
at ease in the Dharma. Flowers in clusters, brocade in clusters.It is not something inside his house.Sweeping away traces and erasing tracks—losing
the eyes, and losing the nostrils too.Everywhere is light. What use are state and home? It is entirely his household business.
dou picked up his staff and said: Are there any patch-robed monks who live together and die togetherYou must stand like a sheer cliff a thousand fathoms high—Bodhidharma has come.
here?Give me back my topic of inquiry. Even so, to level what is not level, you must consult with Xuedou—do you know? If you know,
I grant you freedom at ease; if you do not know, three thousand strikes in the morning, eight hundred at night.

Take Fengxue instructing the assembly: If you establish a single mote of dust, the household and the nation flourish. If you do not establish
a single mote of dust, the household and the nation perish. Tell me: is establishing a single mote of dust it, or is not establishing a single mote of dust
it? When you reach here, great functioning must appear before you can. Therefore it is said: even if you
awaken to it in front of words, you still cling to the shell and are deluded by the seal. Even if you thoroughly understand beneath the phrase,
you still cannot avoid wild views whenever you meet it. He is a venerable elder under Linji, who directly uses his
proper fodder. If you establish a single mote of dust, the household and the nation flourish: the old rustic frowns, for the intent is
to found the state and pacify the realm; you must rely on scheming ministers and fierce generals, and only then do qilin appear and phoenixes
soar, as auspicious signs of great peace. He is a man of some backwater hamlet; how would he know
such a thing? If you do not establish a single mote of dust, the household and the nation perish: the wind rustles and whistles; why would the old
rustic come out singing? Only because the household and the nation perish. In the Caodong house this is called a place of
transformation: there is no Buddha and no sentient beings, no right and no wrong, no good and no
evil, cutting off sound and traces. Therefore it is said: though gold dust is precious, if it gets in the eye it becomes
a cataract. And it is also said: gold dust in the eye is a cataract; the pearl in the robe is dust upon the Dharma. If your own spirit is still not
weighty, who are Buddhas and patriarchs? Seven penetrations and eight openings, spiritual powers and wondrous function, are not taken as
extraordinary. When you reach here, cover your head with your patch robe and all affairs cease. At this point, this mountain monk does not
understand at all. If you further speak of mind and speak of nature, speak of the mysterious and the subtle, none of it is of any use. Why
is that? Their own house has its realm of immortals. Nanquan instructed the assembly, saying: At Huangmei there were seven hundred
eminent monks, all of them people who understood Buddhadharma; yet none obtained his robe and bowl. Only the layman Lu
did not understand Buddhadharma, and so obtained his robe and bowl. He also said: the buddhas of the three times do not know it is;
the civet cat and the white ox do know it is. The old rustic may frown, or
may sing; tell me, how do you understand it? Tell me, what kind of eyes does he have, that he is like
this? You must know: in front of the old rustic's gate there are other statutes. Xuedou picked up both.
Then he picked up his staff and said: Are there any patch-robed monks who live together and die together? At that time,
if there had been a fellow who came forth and spoke a single sentence, making guest and host for each other, it would have spared old
Xuedou from, behind, pointing at his own chest.

lg.l:
Let the old rustic refuse to unfurrow his brow; for now I scheme for household and nationThree thousand li away there is a person—fine food does not satisfy him when he eats.
to set up a mighty foundation.One song of Great Peace is known to all. If you want to go, then go; if you want to stay, then stay. The whole heaven-and-earth, the great ground, is a gate of liberation. How will you stand

Scheming
ministers and fierce generals, where are they now?Is there? Is there? The land is vast, people are few—those who meet are rare. For now, do not point

Ten thousand miles of clear wind
only I myself know.as if no one were there. Who will be told to sweep the ground? It is also the Luohan of Yunju.

cb:div.p:
Just now he raised both. Here he only lifts one side and sets the other aside, trimming
what is long to patch what is short, setting aside the heavy and taking the light. Therefore he says: Let the old rustic refuse to unfurrow
his brow; for now I scheme for household and nation to set up a mighty foundation. Scheming ministers and fierce generals, where are they now? Xue-
dou picked up his staff and said: Are there any patch-robed monks who live together and die together? It is as if he were saying,
are there any scheming ministers and fierce generals? In one mouth he swallowed everyone.
Therefore it is said: the land is vast and the people are few; those who meet are rare. Is there anyone who truly knows?
Come out and be buried in a single pit. Ten thousand miles of clear wind, only I myself know it: that is where Xuedou
points to his own chest.

Instruction said: With wisdom without a teacher, bring forth the wondrous function of non-doing; with compassion without conditions, make
an uninvited excellent friend. Under a single phrase there is killing and there is giving life; within one device there is
releasing and there is capturing. Tell me, what person ever came like this? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 62: Yunmen instructed the assembly, saying: Within heaven and earth, within the uni-The land is vast, people are few—the six directions cannot contain it.
verse, there is one treasure hidden in the mountain of form. He picked up a lantern and went into the Buddha hall, and took the threeDo not make a living in a ghost cave—you have already missed it.Where is it? Light arises too. On no account look toward the ghost cave
to seek it.Pressing and checking—it can still be discussed.
gate and put it on the lantern.Master Yunmen—though it is so, distortions do no harm; it is still a bit better. If you examine it carefully, you cannot avoid a stench of dung.

Yunmen said: Within heaven and earth, within the universe, there is one treasure, hidden in
the mountain of form. Tell me, was Yunmen's intent on the tip of the fishing pole, or on the lantern? This
is several lines from Master Zhao's Treatise on the Treasury of Treasures; Yunmen picked them up to show the assembly. Master Zhao,
in the Later Qin, composed the treatise at Xiaoyao Garden and wrote out the Vimalakirti Sutra; only then did he realize Zhuangzi and Laozi
had not exhausted its subtlety. Zhao then paid respects to Kumarajiva as his teacher, and also visited the Waguansi with
the bodhisattva Bhadrapala, transmitting the mind-seal from the twenty-seventh patriarch of the Western Heavens.
Zhao deeply entered its inner chambers. One day Zhao met with calamity; at the time of execution, he begged for seven
On a certain day, Jia wrote the Treatise on the Precious Treasury. Yunmen then picked up four lines from the treatise and presented them to the assembly. The general
meaning says: how can a priceless treasure be hidden within the realm of the skandhas? The language in the treatise
all accords with the way the school speaks. Do you not see Jingqing ask Caoshan,
"As for the principle of pure emptiness, what is it like when ultimately there is no body?" Caoshan said, "The principle is just
like that." "Then what about the matter?" Jingqing said, "As the principle, so the matter." Caoshan said, "Deceiving Caoshan alone is
fine—what can you do about the eyes of all the sages?" Jingqing said, "If there were no eyes of the sages, how
would you know it is not like this?" Caoshan said, "In public, not even a needle is allowed; in private, carts and horses pass through." Therefore
it is said: within heaven and earth, within the universe, there is a single treasure, hidden in the body-
mountain. The general meaning makes clear that everyone is fully endowed, each one perfectly complete. Yunmen then picked it up
and presented it to the assembly—already completely ready-made. Do not be like a seat master again, going to
annotate it. Out of compassion, he even added a footnote for you, saying: carry the lantern
into the Buddha hall. Bring the three gates onto the lantern. Now tell me—when Yunmen speaks like this,
what is his meaning? Do you not see the ancients say: the true nature of ignorance is buddha-nature.
The illusory, empty body of transformation is the dharma body. And they also say: within the ordinary mind one sees the buddha mind. The body-
mountain is the four elements and five skandhas. Within it there is a single treasure, hidden in the body-mountain. Therefore
it is said: the buddhas are in the mind; deluded people seek outward. Within you carry a priceless
treasure and do not recognize it—your whole life is wasted. And it is also said: buddha-nature stands forth plainly manifest; those who abide in appearances,
sentient beings, find it hard to see. If you awaken to sentient beings having no self, how is my face any different from the Buddha's face? Mind
is the original mind; face is the face born of mother. Even aeon-stones can be moved, yet within this there is no
change. Some only take that bright and numinous awareness as the treasure, but they do not
get its function, nor do they get its subtlety. Therefore they cannot move or turn; they cannot open or set it in motion.
The ancients said: when pressed to the limit, you change; when you change, you come through. Carry the lantern into the Buddha hall—if
it were something ordinary feelings could measure, then bringing the three gates onto the lantern—could you still measure
it? Yunmen, all at once, smashes your emotional consciousness, your imaginings, your gain and loss, right and
wrong. Xuedou said: I love Shaoyang's newly set mechanism—his whole life he has been pulling nails and drawing out wedges for people.
He also said: how much can a crooked piece of wood know, sitting in the seat? A sharp blade cuts it off,
and makes people love it. He said: carry the lantern into the Buddha hall—this one line already cuts
it off. And then: bring the three gates onto the lantern. If you speak of this matter, it is like striking
sparks from flint, like the flash of lightning. Yunmen said: if you are to meet it, first find
a way in. Buddhas as numerous as motes of dust are under your heels; the holy teachings of the Tripitaka are on your
tongue. Better to awaken. You students of the master, do not indulge in deluded thoughts. Heaven is
