heaven; earth is earth. Mountains are mountains; waters are waters. Monks are monks; laypeople are laypeople. After a long
while he said, "Bring me the mountain right in front and set it here for me to see." Then a monk came forward and asked,
"When a student sees mountains as mountains and waters as waters, how is it?" Yunmen said, "Why do the three gates
pass through here?" Fearing you would die, he drew a stroke with his hand and said,
"When you recognize it, it is the finest flavor of ghee. If you do not recognize it, it instead becomes poison."
Therefore it is said: when it is thoroughly clear, there is nothing left to clear up; in the most mysterious place, you must directly give a shout.
Xuedou again picked it up, saying: within heaven and earth, within the universe, there is a single treasure
hidden in the body-mountain. It hangs on the wall. For nine years Bodhidharma did not dare look at it straight on,
yet now if a patch-robed monk wants to see, he gets a staff across the back. Look at that master of the essential ground—
in the end he never ties people up with any real dharma. Xuansha said: penned in, it will not stay; called to,
it does not turn its head back. Even so, it is still the spirit turtle dragging its tail. Xuedou's verse
says:

lg.l:
Look, look—on the ancient bank, who is holding the fishing rod?Hold your eyes high—what use is looking? The black dragon plays with the pearl.Solitary and perilous—how solitary and perilous.
Sheer cliff—how sheer a cliff. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed. Seeing the cheeks from behind the head—do not associate.

Clouds drift slowly,Only by breaking it off will it do. A hundred turns, a thousand layers. Roasted fat
hawk—stinking cloth shirt.

waters spread far; bright moon and reed-flowers—you yourself must look.Left and right; blocked in front and pressed from behind.If you look at it,
you go blind. If you recognize Yunmen’s words, then you see Xuedou’s final line.

cb:div.p:
If you recognize Yunmen's words, then you see where Xuedou meets people. In Yun-
men's final two lines to the assembly, he gives you a footnote, saying: look, look.
You then take it as glowering and staring—fine, it has nothing to do with it. The ancients said: the numinous
light shines alone, far removed from sense faculties and sense objects. The essence reveals true constancy, not bound by words. Mind-nature
is unstained, originally complete. Only depart from deluded conditions, and you are thusness itself, Buddha. If you only
sit yourself to death in the place of glowering and glaring, how could you ever escape sense faculties and objects? Xuedou said: look,
Look: Yunmen is like one holding a fishing rod on an ancient bank. The clouds drift slowly; the waters
spread vast and wide. The bright moon reflects the reed blossoms; the reed blossoms reflect the bright moon. Right at such a
time, tell me: what realm is this? If you see it straightaway, then before and after are only
one phrase alike—stare!Chou-geng-qie: to stare straight.

The pointer says: When the paths of thought do not reach, it is just right to lift and guide. When words and explanations do not suffice, you should urgently
set your eye on it. If it is like lightning turning and stars flying, then you can overturn pools and topple peaks. Among the assembly, is there
anyone who can discern it? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 63. Nansen, one day, when the east and west halls were fighting over a cat, saw it and then lifted it up, saying, “If you can speak, I won’t cut it.” The assembly had no reply, and NansenNot that today should have made such a clamor—still, it is one bout
of leakage.When the true command is in force, the ten directions are cut off while sitting.
This old fellow has steady dragon-and-snake hands and feet.What a pity to let it pass. What can a whole band of lacquer tubs do? Fabricated Zen fellows like hemp and millet.
cut the cat into two pieces.Splendid, splendid. If not like this, all are fellows playing with mud clods. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—already the second head.
Before it is raised, it is good to strike.

In the household of a true master—watch his every move and stillness, his going out and coming in. Tell me: what is the
intent? This story of cutting the cat—throughout the monasteries under heaven, people discuss it in a great flood.
Some say, “It was right at the lifting.” Others say, “It was in the cutting.” In any case, it is all
irrelevant. If he had not lifted it up, they would still have spun around and around, making up every kind of reasoning.
They do not realize that those ancients had eyes that could settle heaven and earth, and a
sword that could settle heaven and earth. Tell me: in the end, who cut the cat? Just consider: when Nansen lifted it and
said, “If you can speak, I won’t cut it,” if at that moment someone had spoken—tell me, would Nansen
cut or not cut? Therefore it is said: When the true command is carried out, the ten directions are cut off as you sit; go out beyond the heavens
and look—who is the person within this? In truth, at that time he never cut it. This talk
is also not found in cutting or not cutting. This matter is plainly known—so clear—do not
seek it in emotional dust and opinions. If you seek it in emotional dust and opinions, you will
betray Nansen. Just look on the edge of the sword where you meet it head-on: “is” also works, “is not” also works,
neither is nor is not also works. So the ancients said: When pressed to the limit, you change; when you
change, you pass through. People today do not understand adaptation; they only keep running after phrases. When Nansen
lifted it like that, it was not to make people utter some particular line on the spot; he only wanted to make
people present themselves. Each uses it for himself and knows it for himself. If you do not understand it like this, in the end you will grope
and not find it. Xuedou, right on the spot, composed this verse:

lg.l:
Both halls are blocked-up Chan monks, stirring up smokeA personal word comes from a personal mouth. One sentence cuts it off. By the confession, the case is closed.
and dust, unable to do anything about it.Let’s see what settlement you make. A ready-made koan—there is also a bit of it.

Luckily Nansen could raise
the command.He raised the fly-whisk and said: Just like this. Master Wang is still a bit better. A fine vajra king’s jeweled sword—use it to cut through mud.

With one knife, two
pieces—let it be lopsided as it may.A hundred fragments. Suddenly someone presses down on the blade—see what he does. Do not let it pass. Then he strikes.

Lopsided.Pu-he.
Qie-pian-ye: pronounced po.

cb:div.p:
“Both halls are blocked-up Chan monks.” Xuedou does not die under the phrases, nor does he take
“in front of the donkey and behind the horse” as it. Where there is a turning point, he says, “stirring up smoke and dust, unable to do anything about it.”
Xuedou and Nansen walk hand in hand; in one line it is said. The head monks of the two halls,
having no resting place, only go around stirring up smoke and dust everywhere—unable to do anything about it.
Luckily Nansen cut through this case for them, collecting it cleanly to the end. How could they
help it—before they don’t reach the village, behind they can’t catch the inn. Therefore he says, “Luckily Nansen could raise
the command. With one knife, two pieces—let it be lopsided.” Straightaway one knife, two pieces, with no further concern
for lopsidedness. Tell me: by what authority does Nansen issue the command?

Case 64. Nansen again brought up the previous story and asked Zhaozhou.It must be with the same mind and the same intent. Only those on the same path will know.
Zhaozhou immediately took off his straw sandals and put them on his head and went out. Nansen said, “If you,You cannot avoid dragging mud and hauling water.
my son, had been there, you would have saved the cat.”Singing and clapping follow each other. Few are those who know the tune. Take wrong to serve wrong.

Zhaozhou was truly Nansen’s son: he understands the head and meets the tail; once it is raised, he knows where it lands.
In the evening Nansen again brought up the earlier story and asked Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou was an old hand, so he
took off his straw sandals, put them on his head, and went out. Nansen said, “If you had been there, you would have saved the
cat.” Tell me: is it truly like this or not like this? Nansen said, “If you can speak, I won’t
He cut it down—like striking sparks from stone, like a flash of lightning. Zhaozhou then took off his straw sandals and,
putting them on his head, went out. He studied living phrases, not dead phrases. Day after day new, moment after moment new.
A thousand sages cannot shift it by even a hair. You must bring forth your own family treasure.
Only then do you see his whole functioning and great use. He said, I am a Dharma king, free within the Dharma.
Many people misread it, saying Zhaozhou temporarily took the straw sandals as a cat. Some say,
Since he said, If you can speak, I will not cut, he then wore the straw sandals on his head and went out, meaning,
You cut the cat; it has nothing to do with me—so he stays uninvolved. This is just playing tricks with the spirit.
They do not realize the intent of the ancients: like heaven universally covering, like earth universally supporting. That father and son
matched each other; their pivot-points and edge met. The moment one raised the head, the other understood the tail. Students today
do not recognize where the ancients turn; they vainly speculate along the road of ideas. If you want to see it,
just go to where Nanquan and Zhaozhou turn, and you will see. Verse says:

lg.l:
When the case was concluded, he came to ask Zhaozhou—within Chang’anThe words are still in the ear—no need to cut again. Behind the funeral cart hangs a medicine pouch.
city, he may wander at leisure.Gaining such delight, gaining such ease—casually picking up weeds. I cannot but teach you to go like this.

Straw
sandals worn on the head—no one understands.There are also one or half of one—another household style. In brightness it fits, in darkness it fits.

Returning
to his home mountain, he immediately rests.Under your heels, it is good to give thirty blows. Tell me—where is the fault? Only because you raise waves without wind. Both sides put it down, only
I fear it’s not like that. Yet like that is also too strange.
Thump.“Ce-liu” cut: sai, also read se.“Ke-he” cut: a stone-sound, same pronunciation as ke.

cb:div.p:
The case was concluded and he came to ask Zhaozhou. Steward Qing said it was like someone closing a lawsuit:
eight blows are eight blows, thirteen are thirteen—already decided. Yet he picked it up again
to ask Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou was one of his own household; he understood Nanquan’s intent. He was someone thoroughly
penetrating to the bottom; the moment it struck, he turned. He had the true adept’s eyes and brains, and as soon as
he heard it raised, he picked it up and went. Xuedou said, In Chang’an city, he may wander at leisure.
Not a bit was leaked or fumbled. The ancients said, Though Chang’an is pleasant, it is not a place to stay long. Also
they said, Chang’an is very noisy; my country is at peace. You must recognize the right moment and stop blaming
before it will do. Straw sandals worn on the head—no one understands. As for the wearing of straw sandals, these
fellows, though there is not all that much going on—so it is said, Only I can know; only I can
certify. Only then do you see where Nanquan, Zhaozhou, and Xuedou share the same attainment and the same use. Tell me,
how do you understand it now? Returning to the home mountain, he immediately rests. What place is
the home mountain? If he did not understand, he surely would not speak like that. Since he did understand, tell me, the home
mountain is in what place? He strikes.

Raising a pointer, it says: Formless yet having form, it fills the ten directions of space and is vast. Mindless yet responding,
it pervades the ocean of lands without weariness. Raise one and illuminate three; the eyes weigh the pivot down to the ounce. It goes so far that the staff
falls like raindrops and shouts rush like thunder. Even so, it still does not match the conduct and footing of an above-it person.
Tell me, how is it the matter of an above-it person? Try to raise it.

Case sixty-five is raised: A non-Buddhist asked the Buddha, I do not ask about words that affirm; I do not ask about words that deny. The World-Honored One remained silent for a long time. The non-Buddhist praised him, saying, World-Honored One, of great compassion and great pity, you have opened my deluded
Even so, in the house
the people still have a bit of fragrance. Twin swords lean on empty space and fly. Luckily, I do not ask.Do not slander the World-Honored One. His voice is like thunder; those sitting and those standing
cannot move him at all.
cloud and allowed me to enter. After the non-Buddhist left, Ananda askedA sharp fellow—one flick and he turns. A bright pearl in the tray,
the Buddha, What did the non-Buddhist realize that he says he entered? The Buddha said, It is like a fine horse in the world that moves when it sees the shadow of the whip.it’s fine to let people fall into doubt. Everyone should know. Clamped
and shackled—raw iron.Tell me, what do you call the shadow of the whip?
He strikes once with the whisk. The staff’s tip has an eye, bright as the sun. If you want to know true gold, look in fire. The one who’s got it eats his fill.

If this matter were in words and phrases, how could the three vehicles and the twelve divisions of teaching be wordless
phrases? Some say wordlessness is it—then what need was there for the patriarch to come from the West at all?
And as for all those cases from of old, in the end how do you see their landing place?
There are not a few who can talk about understanding this case. Some call it a long silence; some
call it sitting firm; some call it silent and not answering. Fortunately, none of that is relevant—
when have they ever actually grasped it? In truth, this matter is not on words and phrases.
Yet it is not apart from words and phrases. If there is the slightest conjecturing, you are off by thousands upon thousands of miles.
Look—after the non-Buddhist awakened, only then did he know it is not here, and not
there; not in yes, and not in no. Tell me, what is it? Venerable Tian
yi Huai the master versified it: Vimalakirti was not silent, not long in silence; sitting firm and deliberating
becomes a fault. In the hair-splitting sword case, cold light is chill; non-Buddhists and heavenly demons all fold their hands.
Venerable Baizhang Chang visited Fayan; Fayan had him look into these words. One day Fayan
asked, What case are you looking into? Chang said, The non-Buddhist asking the Buddha. Fayan said,
Try raising it. Chang was about to open his mouth. Fayan said, Stop, stop. You are trying to understand it in the long-silence
place, aren’t you? At those words, Chang suddenly had a great awakening. Later he instructed the assembly, Baizhang
has three tips: Drink tea—take care. Rest. If you speculate and think it over again, I know you still have not
penetrated. Cuiyan Zhen, patting his chest, said: In the six directions there are nine; there are blue, yellow, red, and white. Each
one is interwoven. The non-Buddhist understood the four Vedas and treatises, and said of himself, I am an all
knowing person. Everywhere he went he sought people to debate. He brought up this question to cut off
Shakyamuni the old fellow’s tongue. The World-Honored One did not spend the slightest effort, and he immediately awakened.
He praised him, saying, World-Honored One, of great compassion and great pity, you have opened my deluded cloud and allowed me to enter.
Tell me, how is this the place of great compassion and great pity? The World-Honored One, with a single eye, penetrates the three
times; the non-Buddhist, with both pupils, spans the five heavens. Weishan Zhenru raised it and said, The non-Buddhist carried
a supreme treasure within; the World-Honored One personally lifted it high. The myriad things appear; all phenomena are clearly distinct.
But in the end, what did the non-Buddhist awaken to? It is like chasing a dog up against a wall—when it reaches the extreme,
there is no road; it must turn back. Then it becomes lively and nimble. If you calculate
right and wrong, at once set it down—feelings exhausted, views removed—naturally it becomes thoroughly clear. After the non-Buddhist
left, Ananda asked the Buddha, What did the non-Buddhist realize that he says he entered?
The Buddha said, It is like a fine horse in the world that moves when it sees the shadow of the whip. Later, all sorts of expedient talk
got blown into a different tune. And it said, Dragon head, snake tail. Where is
the World-Honored One’s whip-shadow? Where is the place of seeing the whip-shadow? Xuedou said, Not distinguishing
crooked and correct—the fault is due to the whip-shadow. Zhenru said, Ananda strikes the golden bell again; the four
assemblies hear together. Even so, it is very much like two dragons fighting over a pearl, increasing the wise one’s
fierce authority. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
The pivot-wheel has not yet turned—once it turns, it surely runs to both ends.Right here—sure enough, it doesn’t move in the slightest.It does not fall into “there is,” it surely
falls into “there isn’t.” If not east, then west. Left eye half a jin, right eye eight liang.

A bright mirror suddenly comes before the stand;Do you still see old Shakyamuni? One flick and it turns—broken too, broken
too. Ruined—ruined, ruined.

right on the spot it distinguishes fair and foul.The whole great earth is a gate of liberation. He deserves thirty blows. Do you still see old Shakyamuni?
When fair and foul are distinguished, the deluded cloud opens.I let out a single line and allow you a place to turn around—yet what can you do, you’re still just an outsider.

From the gate of compassion, where
could dust and grime arise?Throughout the whole realm it has never been hidden. Back up, back up—Bodhidharma is coming!

Therefore think of the fine horse that spies the whip
shadow—I have my staff; I don’t need you to give it to me. Tell me, where is the place of the whip’s shadow? Where is the place of the fine horse?

a thousand miles chasing the wind,
called back.Ride the Buddha hall and go out through the three gates. Turn around and you’re wrong; let it pass and you can’t. Then he strikes.

Called back—snap the fingers
three times.Ahead you don’t reach a village; behind you don’t make the inn. Snap the staff in two—where will you go? Xuedou’s thunder is very loud, but there are no raindrops at all.

cb:div.p:
The pivot-wheel has not yet turned—once it turns, it surely runs to both ends. The pivot is the numinous pivot of a thousand sages; the wheel
is the lifeline of all people since the very beginning. Have you not seen the ancients say, The numinous pivot of a thousand sages
is not easy to approach; when a dragon gives birth to a dragon’s child, do not dally. Zhaozhou seized the matchless
jade of a whole city; the King of Qin and Xiangru both lost themselves. The non-Buddhist, however, could hold it fast and take
charge of it, without ever being moved. Why? He said, I do not ask about words that affirm; I do not ask about words that deny.
Is this not the whole functioning? The World-Honored One knew how to read the wind and set the sail, to meet the illness with
the medicine. Therefore he remained silent for a long time, raising the whole functioning. The non-Buddhist understood it with his whole body. The pivot-wheel
then turned with a rumbling roll. It does not turn toward affirmation, and does not turn toward denial. It does not
fall into gain and loss, and is not bound by ordinary or sage. Both sides are cut off at once. As soon as the World-Honored One fell silent
for a long time, he bowed. People today mostly fall into denial; otherwise they fall into affirmation.
They keep running to both ends in affirmation and denial. Xuedou said, A bright mirror suddenly comes before the stand.
Right on the spot it distinguishes fair and foul. This never moved at all; it only took a single long silence, like
a bright mirror before the stand—ten thousand forms cannot escape their shape and substance. The non-Buddhist said,
World-Honored One, of great compassion and great pity, you have opened my deluded cloud and allowed me to enter. Tell me,
what place is this? It is the place where the non-Buddhist entered. When you come here, each person must
investigate for himself, awaken for himself, understand for himself—only then. Then in every place, walking, standing, sitting
Lying down—without asking of high or low—it is all complete at once, not shifting or altering by even the slightest bit. The moment you
start calculating, if there is even the slightest trace of reasoning, it blocks you up and kills you—there is no
way in at all. Later, the verse says, The World-Honored One’s great compassion and great mercy open my cloud of delusion and let
me enter. Right then, suddenly, beauty and ugliness are distinguished; when beauty and ugliness split apart, the cloud of delusion opens.
From where could dust arise in the gate of compassion? The whole great earth is the World-Honored One’s gate of great compassion and great mercy.
If you penetrate it, it won’t take even a pinch—this too is a gate that has been thrown wide open.
Haven’t you seen how, during the twenty-one days, the World-Honored One contemplated such matters: I would rather
not teach the Dharma and quickly enter nirvana. Then he thought of the fine horse that glimpses the shadow of the whip—over a thousand miles
it chases the wind and can be called back. The wind-chasing horse, seeing the whip’s shadow, immediately goes a thousand miles.
Told to return, it returns. Xuedou’s intent is to praise this: only if you find a swift one can it turn with a single
flick, come back with a single call. If you can call it back, then snap your fingers three times. Now tell me,
is that exposing it, or is it throwing sand?

Instruction: Meeting the moment face to face, raise the stratagem for trapping tigers. Press straight on and lift from the side, laying out
the tactics for catching thieves. Openly matching and secretly matching, letting go twice and gathering in twice—you can handle even a dead snake and make it work. Return it
to the adept.

Case 66. Yantou asked a monk, Where have you come from? The monk said, From the Western Capital. Yantou said, After Huang Chao passed through, did you stillBefore he even opens his mouth, he’s already taken in the flaw. It pierces through the skull—if you want to know where it comes from,
it’s not hard.Sure enough, a petty thief.
recover the sword? The monk said, I recovered it.All his life he never played bandit; he’s not afraid of losing his head, so he asks like this—what nerve!
Yantou stretched out his neck and came close. The monk said, Master, your head has fallen off. Yantou laughed loudly, heh heh. Later the monk went to Xuefeng. Xuefeng asked, WhereRuined. Not yet knowing where to turn around. The wide world is full of fellows, like hemp and millet.You also must know the timing
and what fits. A trap-for-tigers opportunity—what mind and conduct is that?You only see the awl’s point is sharp; you don’t see the chisel’s head is square. What do you know
of like and dislike? You’ve fallen into it.Patchrobed monks all under heaven can do nothing about it. He deceives everyone in the world—seek where this old fellow’s head would fall,
and you cannot.As before—stupid and muddled. This monk again and again fully takes in the flaw.
have you come from? The monk said, From Yantou. Xuefeng said,
You can’t not speak of where it comes from. It must also be examined through.Sure enough, he takes in the flaw.
What words were spoken? The monk raised the earlier exchange. Xuefeng gave him thirtyIf you bring it up, you can’t avoid taking the stick.Then it’s fine to drive him out.
blows and drove him out.Though it’s cutting nails and slicing iron, why strike only thirty blows? The staff hasn’t even been broken yet—still not the proper measure. Why is it three thousand in the morning
and eight hundred at night? If you weren’t fellow practitioners, how could you discern the real point? Even so, tell me—where do Xuefeng and Yantou land?

In general, shouldering your bag and carrying your bowl, pushing aside grass and watching the winds, you must have the eye of a pilgrim
before it will do. This monk’s eyes were like shooting stars, yet Yantou still saw through him and strung him up
in one chain. If he had been a real fellow then—whether killing or giving life—he would have used it the moment it was raised. This
monk was sloppy and careless, yet said, I recovered it. Traveling like this, if old King Yama asks you,
Where’s the money for your meals? Who knows how many straw sandals you’ve worn through. When he got
to Xuefeng, if he had even a bit of eye-muscle, he would have known to vanish in a flash—wouldn’t that
have been a delight? In this set of circumstances there are knots and corners of garbled error. Though in this matter
there is no gain or loss, the gain and loss are very great. Though there is no choosing, when you come here you still must
choose with eyes. Look at how Longya traveled and brought up this question to Deshan: A student wields the Moye sword,
and is about to take the master’s head—what then? Deshan stretched
out his neck and came close. Longya said, Master, your head has fallen off. Deshan then returned to the abbot’s
quarters. Later Longya brought this up to Dongshan. Dongshan said, What did Deshan say at the time?
Longya said, He was silent. Dongshan said, Put his silence aside for now—lend me Deshan’s fallen
head so I can have a look. At these words Longya had a great awakening. He then burned incense, gazed from afar
toward Deshan, bowed, and repented. A monk carried the news to Deshan. Deshan said, Dongshan—
that old fellow—doesn’t know good from bad. This man has been dead for who knows how long; saving him,
what use is it? This case is just like Longya’s. Deshan
returning to the abbot’s quarters is, in the dark, most wonderful. Yantou’s loud laughter—within his laughter there is poison.
If someone can discern it, they can stride across the world. If this monk had discerned it then,
for ages to come he would have been spared blame. Under Yantou’s gate it was already one great
misstep. See how old man Xuefeng was a fellow student—he knew where it landed, yet
did not explain it for him; he only gave thirty blows and drove him out of the monastery. That can illuminate the past and cut off
the future. This is the method of grasping an adept patchrobed monk by the nostrils and helping people—further
not doing anything else to him, letting him awaken on his own. A true master in helping people
sometimes encloses and covers, not letting him show his head; sometimes lets him go along dead and sloppy—
yet there must be a way to step out. Great Yantou and Xuefeng were instead seen through by this
meal-eating Chan fellow. Just take Yantou’s words: After Huang Chao passed through, did you still
recover the sword? Now tell me, what phrase fits right here, so you avoid
his laughter, and also avoid Xuefeng’s staff driving you out? Here is the garbling: if
you have not personally realized and personally awakened, then even if your tongue is sharp, in the end you still cannot penetrate and break free
from birth and death. This mountain monk usually teaches people to look at where this mechanism turns; if
you speculate, you are far, far away. Haven’t you seen Touzi ask Yanping’s monk, After Huang
Chao passed through, did you recover the sword? The monk pointed to the ground with his hand. Touzi said, For thirty
years I played at riding horses; today I got thrown by a donkey. Look at this monk—he’s not
bad; he may well be an adept. He neither says recovered nor says not recovered. Compared to the monk from
the Western Capital, it’s like separated by an ocean. Zhenru commented, Those ancients—one makes
the head, one makes the tail—settled. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
After Huang Chao passed through—did you ever recover the sword? Loud laughter stillWhat use is that Meng Balang fellow? Just a single tin knife.
answers; only the adept knows.One point personally grasped—how could there be any opening? If it weren’t that man, how could anyone gain freedom?

Thirty mountain vines—still,
be lenient.On the same branch you live, on the same branch you die—three thousand in the morning, eight hundred at night. People in the east house die; the west house helps with mourning—yet it saves them alive.

Getting the advantage
is falling into advantage.Citing the terms to close the case, regretting not being careful at the start—there’s a bit of that too.

cb:div.p:
After Huang Chao passed through—did you ever recover the sword? Loud laughter still answers; only the adept knows. Xuedou then
praises the place where this monk and Yantou laughed loudly. This little bit—people everywhere cannot
find it. Tell me, what are they laughing at? Only an adept can know. Within this
laughter there is provisional and real, illumination and function, killing and giving life. Thirty mountain
vines—still, be lenient. The verse praises how later this monk came before Xuefeng; the monk was still
reckless and crude. Xuefeng then acted according to the rule: he gave thirty blows and drove him out. Tell me,
why was it like this? Do you want to understand these words completely? Getting the advantage is
falling into advantage.

Case 67. Emperor Wu of Liang invited Layman Fu to lecture on the Diamond Sutra. Layman Fu, on the seat,Bodhidharma’s brothers are coming. Fish go by in the wine
shop—it’s not that there is none. But under the patchrobed monks’ gate, it won’t do. This old fellow, so old and grown, still makes such comings and goings.
struck the table once and then stepped down. Emperor Wu was astonished.Straight enough to make sparks fly. It seems like it, yet it is not. No need to tangle up with vines.
Zhigong asked, Does Your Majesty understand? The emperor said, I do not. Zhigong said, The layman
Twice, three times, he’s been fooled by others—still he can’t grope his way to it.Take the side of principle, not the side
of feelings. Keep your elbow from turning outward. He also deserves thirty blows.What a pity—
has finished lecturing on the sutra.he must be driven out of the country. Back then, with Zhigong, they drove him out of the country as well—that’s being a genuine hand. Two fellows in the same pit, no different dirt.

Emperor Wu, Gaozu of Liang, of the Xiao clan, personal name Yan, style name Shuda, established great merit, and
even received the abdication of Qi. After taking the throne he wrote separate notes and lectures on the Five Classics, and was deeply devoted to Huang-Lao,
and by nature extremely filial. One day he wished to obtain a supramundane Dharma to repay his parents’ toil.
Thus he set aside Daoist ways and served the Buddha, receiving the bodhisattva precepts from Dharma Master Louyue.
Donning the Buddha’s kasaya, he personally lectured on the Radiant Prajna Sutra to repay his parents. At that time
the layman Zhigong, by displaying marvels and bewildering the crowd, was confined in prison. Zhigong then
divided his body and traveled to teach in towns and cities. One day the emperor learned of it, was deeply moved and awakened, and esteemed him greatly. Zhigong
often went about protecting him; his hidden and manifest workings were beyond measure. At that time, in Wuzhou there was a layman
who lived on Yunhuang Mountain. With his own hands he planted two trees, calling them the Twin Groves, and claimed he would be
the future Layman Shanhui. One day he wrote a letter and ordered his disciple to submit a memorial to the emperor. At the time
the court refused it because it lacked the proprieties of ruler and minister. Layman Fu was about to enter Jinling
city to sell fish. Around then, Emperor Wu at times invited Zhigong to lecture on the Diamond Sutra. Zhigong
said, This poor monk cannot lecture; in the marketplace there is Layman Fu, who can lecture on this
sutra. The emperor issued an edict summoning him into the palace. When Layman Fu arrived, on the lecture seat
he struck the table once and then stepped down. Right then it was turned over for you, sparing you from seeing a
whole mess—yet Zhigong said, Does Your Majesty understand? The emperor said, I do not.
Zhigong said, The layman has finished lecturing on the sutra. One makes the head, one makes
the tail. With Zhigong talking like this, have you even dreamed of Layman Fu? It’s all the same—just playing
with spirits. This is what is truly peculiar: though it is a dead snake, if you know how to handle it, it comes alive. Since
it is lecturing on sutras, why not clearly split it into two, like an ordinary lecturer
would say: The substance of vajra is firm; nothing can destroy it. Because it is sharp and effective, it can
smash all things. Explaining it like that is what’s called lecturing on sutras. Even so, everyone
does not know that Layman Fu only picked up the pivotal point of what’s above, slightly revealing the edge
of the blade, letting people know where it lands—straight and direct for you, like a sheer cliff ten thousand fathoms high, exactly enough to be
Master Zhi does not discern good and bad, yet he says the Great Being has finished preaching the sutra; truly, it is good intention
that will not receive a good reward. Like a cup of fine wine, yet Master Zhi diluted it with water.
Like a pot of soup, Master Zhi soiled it by dropping in a pellet of mouse dung. Tell me, since it
is not preaching the sutra, in the end what do you call it? Verse says:

lg.l:
Not lodging this body in the Twin Groves, instead in the land of LiangOnly because he can’t hold it down—how can you hide an awl in a bag?
he stirred up dust and grime.If you don’t enter the weeds, how will you see the real point? Even where there’s no elegance, it’s elegant.

If at that time he had not met Master Zhi,
the old master,To be a thief you don’t need capital. A leper with companions
he too would have been a restless man driven from the country.is just right to take it in one receipt—then he strikes.

cb:div.p:
Not lodging this body in the Twin Groves, instead in the land of Liang he stirred up dust and grime. Layman Fu
and the old fellow with no front teeth met in the same way. When Bodhidharma first arrived in Jinling, he met
Emperor Wu. The emperor asked, What is the first meaning of the holy truths? Bodhidharma said, Vast and empty, no
holiness. The emperor said, Who is it that faces me? Bodhidharma said, I do not know. The emperor did not accord, so he crossed
the river to Wei. Emperor Wu raised this and asked Master Zhi. The master said, Does Your Majesty recognize this person
or not? The emperor said, I do not recognize him. Master Zhi said, This is the Great Being Avalokiteshvara, transmitting the Buddha’s
mind-seal. The emperor regretted it, and then sent envoys to fetch him. Master Zhi said, Do not say Your Majesty
sent envoys to fetch him; even if the whole country went, he still would not return. Therefore Xuedou said:
If at that time he had not met old Master Zhi, he too would have been a restless man driven from the country. If at that time
it were not Master Zhi giving Layman Fu an outlet, he would have had to be driven out of the country.
Master Zhi was indeed long-tongued, and Emperor Wu was thoroughly hoodwinked by him. Xuedou’s great
intent says: no need for him to come to the Liang court, preaching the sutra and pounding the table. So it says, why not
lodge this body in the Twin Groves, eat gruel and eat rice, pass the time according to circumstances? Instead he came to the Liang
court; like this, he pointed and pounded the table once, then stepped down from the seat. This is exactly where he stirred up dust
and grime. If you want something extraordinary, then gaze into the clouds; above you do not see any Buddha.
Below you do not see any sentient beings. If you speak of matters beyond the world, you cannot avoid a head of ash and a face of dust,
taking what is not and making it be, taking what is and making it not, taking what is right and making it wrong, taking what is coarse and making it
fine. A fish moving through a wine shop, grabbing sideways and using upside down, making everyone understand this matter.
If you do not let it go like this, then even until Maitreya is born in the world, there will not be a single half
one. Since Layman Fu was dragging mud and trailing water, it is fortunate there was someone who understood the tune. If he had not
met old Master Zhi, he nearly would have been driven out of the country. Tell me, right now, where is it
located?

Instruction says: to lift heaven’s barrier and overturn earth’s axle, to seize tigers and rhinos and distinguish dragons from snakes, it must be
a lively, nimble fellow; only then can every phrase hit the mark and every move respond. Now then, from
all that has come so far, what person fits this? Please raise it and see.

Case 68. Yangshan asked Sansheng, What is your name? Sansheng said,
Name and reality snatch from each other—hooking thieves, wrecking households.
Huiji. Yangshan said, Huiji is me. SanshengSit and cut off tongues; seize the banners and take the drums.Each guards his own borders.
said, My name is Huiran. Yangshan burst into hearty laughter.Snatch it in the noisy marketplace; leave off “you and me” and instead keep to your proper share.
。This can be called such a season—flowers spread on brocade. People under heaven don’t know where it lands. Why? The land is vast and people are sparse; those who meet are few. It’s like Yantou’s laugh—yet it is not Yantou’s laugh.
It’s the same kind of laugh—why then make it into two sections? Only one who has eyes can decisively see it rightly.

Sansheng was an honored elder under Linji. From early on he had outstanding ways, with great capacity
and great function. Among the assembly he carried himself proudly and concealed himself deeply; his name was known in all directions. Later he took leave of Linji
and traveled throughout Huai and Hai. Wherever he went among the monasteries, all treated him as an honored guest. From
the north to the south, he first visited Xuefeng and asked, A golden-scaled fish that passes through the net, what do you
take as food? Xuefeng said, Wait until you come out of the net, then I will tell you. Sansheng said,
A teacher of fifteen hundred people, and you do not even recognize the topic of conversation. Xuefeng said, This old monk
has many duties as abbot. Xuefeng went to the temple estate; on the road he met monkeys and said, These monkeys
each wear an ancient mirror. Sansheng said, Through kalpas without a name, how can it be displayed as
an ancient mirror? Xuefeng said, A flaw has arisen. Sansheng said, A teacher of fifteen hundred people, and you do not recognize
the topic of conversation. Xuefeng said, My fault. This old monk has many duties as abbot.
Later he went to Yangshan. Yangshan greatly loved his sharp brilliance and received him beneath a bright window. One
day an official came to visit Yangshan. Yangshan asked, What office do you hold? He said, Investigating officer.
Yangshan held up his whisk and said, Can you investigate this? The official had no words. The assembly
offered remarks, but none matched Yangshan’s intent. At the time Sansheng was ill in the Yanshou Hall.
Yangshan had an attendant take these words and ask him. Sansheng said, The master has business.
Again he had the attendant ask, what business is it exactly? Sansheng said, A second offense will not be tolerated.
Yangshan deeply approved. In Baizhang’s time, he entrusted the meditation board and sitting cushion to Huangbo;
the staff and whisk to Guishan; Guishan later entrusted them to Yangshan. Since Yangshan greatly
approved of Sansheng, one day Sansheng took leave. Yangshan gave the staff and whisk to Sansheng.
Sansheng said, I already have a teacher. Yangshan questioned him about why, and it turned out he was truly Linji’s
heir. Now, when Yangshan asked Sansheng, What is your name, he could not
not have known his own name. So why ask like this? Because a genuine adept wants to test a person and find
out precisely. It seems casual, asking, What is your name, as if there were no calculation;
so why did Sansheng not say Huiran, but instead say Huiji? Look at him, a man with eyes,
naturally different. Sansheng was like this, yet it was not craziness. He was always disrupting banners
and snatching drums; his intent lay outside Yangshan’s words. These words do not fall into ordinary sentiment, hard to
grasp. The methods of such a fellow bring people to life. Therefore it is said, he studies living
phrases, not dead phrases. If you follow ordinary sentiment, you cannot stop people. See how the ancients
thought like this, exhausting their spirit, only then able to awaken greatly. Once awakened, when they use it
it is still like someone not yet awakened. According to circumstance, with a word or half a phrase, they must not
fall into ordinary sentiment. Sansheng knew where Yangshan would land, so he said to him, My name is
Huiji. Yangshan wanted to seize Sansheng, but Sansheng instead seized Yangshan. Yangshan only
managed to rob him on the spot, saying, Huiji is me. That is the releasing point. Sansheng said,
My name is Huiran. That too is release. Therefore Xuedou later composed this verse: double
gathering, double releasing, how can it be the principle? In a single line he versified it all at once. Yangshan
laughed heartily. There is expedient and there is real; there is illumination and there is function. Because he was
crystal-clear on all sides, his functioning attained great freedom. This laughter is different from Yantou’s laughter.
Yantou’s laughter has poison. This laughter, through a thousand ages and ten thousand ages, is a clear wind
sharp and bracing. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Double gathering, double releasing, how can it be the principle? Riding a tiger,How many know it? Shining from eight sides—you’d think there really was such a thing.
from the start you must cut off achievement.If you didn’t have an eye on the crown of your head and a talisman under your elbow, how would you reach here? Riding is no problem—only I fear you can’t get down.
If you’re not that sort of person, how could you make such a matter clear?

After laughing, not knowing where he went,Search through all four hundred military prefectures for such a person.
Hard to find. The words are still in the ears. For a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, there is a pure breeze—

it only makes the mournful wind stir through a thousand ages.where is it now? Tsk!
Since it is a great laugh, why does it stir a sorrowful wind? The great earth is pitch-black.

cb:div.p:
Double gathering, double releasing, how can it be the principle? In release, they alternate as host and guest. Yangshan said, You
what is your name? Sansheng said, My name is Huiji, that is double release. Yangshan said, Huiji
is me. Sansheng said, My name is Huiran, that is double gathering. In truth it is the pivot of mutual exchange.
If gathering, everyone gathers; if releasing, everyone releases. Xuedou, all at once, versified it completely
as well. His intent says: if you do not release and gather, if you do not exchange, you are you and I
am I. Altogether just four characters. Why then within it do you come and go, roll
and unroll? The ancients said: if you stand, I sit; if you sit, I stand.
If you sit together and stand together, both are blind men. This is double gathering and double releasing; it
can be taken as the essential principle. Riding a tiger has always required cutting off achievement. With such lofty style
and the supreme pivot, ride when you want to ride, dismount when you want to dismount. Grasp the tiger’s head
and it works; seize the tiger’s tail and it works. Sansheng and Yangshan both had this style. After laughing,
he did not know where he went. Tell me, what was he laughing at? It made the clear wind
sharp and bracing—why? In the end it says, it only makes the mournful wind stir through a thousand ages. That too
is dying without mourning. All at once I have annotated it for you. But what can be done? People under heaven
cannot bite into it, do not know where it lands. Even this mountain monk does not know where it lands.
Do you all know?

Instruction says: there is nowhere to bite in. The Patriarchs’ mind-seal resembles the mechanism of an iron ox.
Passing through a thicket of thorns, for a patch-robed monk it is like a speck of snow on a red-hot furnace. On level ground
seven piercings and eight holes can be set aside; not falling into causal conditions, then what? Try to raise it
and see.

Case 69. Nansen, Guizong, and Mogu went together to pay respects to National Teacher Zhong. When they reached
the middle of the road, Nansen drew on the groundWhen three walk together, one must be my teacher—what’s strange about that? You still must discern the real point.
a circle and said, “Speak, and we go.”Raising waves with no wind—yet you want people to know. Throwing away the ship that sinks on dry land—if you don’t test it through, how will you discern the real point?
Guizong sat within the circle; Mogu then acted like a womanOne person strikes the gong; only those on the same path know.
and bowed. Nansen said, “If so, then we won’t go.” Guizong said, “What kind of mind-conduct is that?”One person beats the drum; three are enough as well.Pulling back halfway is a good person. What a fine
performance in a crooked tune—genuine hands, genuine hands!Luckily it was seen through. At that time he deserved a slap. Meng Ba-
lang fellow.

At that time Mazu’s influence flourished in Jiangxi; Shitou’s way was carried out in Hunan; National
Teacher Zhong taught in Chang’an. He had personally seen the Sixth Patriarch come. In those days in the south,
none of those who “raised their heads and wore horns” failed to want to ascend his hall and enter his room; if they did not,
they would be shamed by others. These three old fellows wanted to go pay respects to National Teacher Zhong.
Halfway there, they staged this botched affair. Nansen said, “If so, then we won’t go.”
Since each has spoken, why then say, “we won’t go”? Tell me: what were the ancients’
intentions? If at that moment, when he said, “If so, then we won’t go,” you were to
slap him right across the ear—see what tricks he has. The teaching that shakes the lineage for ten thousand ages is just
these key points. Therefore Ciming said, “If you want to lead it, it’s right at the end of the rope.”
Touch it and it turns; it is like pressing down a gourd on water. Many people
call it words of non-acceptance. They do not realize that when this matter reaches the ultimate point, you must leave
mud and leave water, pull out the wedge and draw the nail. If you understand it as mind-conduct, then it has nothing to do with it.
The ancients were good at turning and changing. When you reach here, you cannot but be this way. There must be
killing and there must be giving life. Look: one person sits inside the circle; one person acts like a woman
and bows—also quite fine. Nansen said, “If so, then we won’t go.” Guizong said,
“What kind of mind-conduct is that?” That Mengbalang fellow went and did it that way too. When he said it like that,
his main intent was to test Nansen. Nansen usually says, “Call it suchness.” It has already
changed. Nansen, Guizong, and Mogu were, after all, people of one household. One
Capture one, release one; one kill, one let live—no harm in its strangeness. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Youji’s arrow shoots the gibbon—circling the tree, why so straight?Right in front on a single road—who dares step forward? Everywhere it touches it’s wondrous; before it’s released, it hits.
If you didn’t take responsibility, how would you dare be like this? East, west, south, north—one family wind. It has covered all around for a long time.

A thousand, and ten thousand—Like hemp and millet. A band of wild
fox spirits—what can they do to Nanquan?

who has ever truly hit the mark?One or half of one, add another—none of them can be used.
Calling to each other, they shout: come back, go home.A whole crew of mud-ball fellows—better to go home. That would be a bit better.

On the Caoxi road,
do not climb.Great toil, giving rise to calculating thoughts. Not a guest under Caoxi’s gate. In low places he levels them with ease; in high places, viewing is not enough.

cb:div.p:
Again,
he says: the Caoxi road is level—why do you say do not climb?Not only did Nanquan pull back halfway; Xue-
dou also pulled back halfway. Better no good thing than one. Xuedou too suffered this kind of illness.

“Youji’s arrow shoots the gibbon; circling the tree, why so straight?” Youji was a man of Chu in ancient times.
His surname was Yang, personal name Shu, style name Youji. When King Zhuang of Chu went hunting, he saw a white
gibbon and had someone shoot it. The gibbon caught the arrow and toyed with it. He ordered the assembled ministers to shoot, but none
could hit it. The king then asked his ministers, and they replied: Youji is skilled at archery.
So he commanded him to shoot. As Youji began to draw his bow, the gibbon clung to the tree and cried mournfully. When the arrow
was released, the gibbon circled the tree to evade it, and the arrow also circled the tree and struck, killing it. This was a miraculous
arrow. Why, then, does Xuedou say “too straight”? If it were too straight, it would not hit.
Since it circled the tree, why say “too straight”? Xuedou borrows the idea—no harm in using it
well. This story comes from the Spring and Autumn records. Some say that circling the tree is a circle emblem. If
it were really so, they simply do not understand the point of the words; they do not know where the “too straight” lies. The three
old fellows take different roads yet return to the same principle—together, all “too straight.” If you
recognize where they go, then seven times loose, eight times free, you do not leave a square inch of mind. The hundred rivers flow differently,
yet all return to the great sea. Therefore Nanquan said: if it is so, then I will not go. If
a patched-robed monk looks with the true eye, it is only playing with the spirit. If you call it playing with the spirit,
then it is not playing with the spirit. The former master, Fifth Patriarch, said: those three are the Samadhi
of the torch of wisdom, and the Samadhi of the Adornment King. Even so, if you take it as a woman’s bow, in the end
they will not understand it as a woman’s bow. Though a circle emblem is drawn, they will never understand it as a circle emblem.
If you do not understand it that way, then how will you understand it? Xuedou says: a thousand, and ten
thousand—who has ever hit it? How many could there be? A hundred shots, a hundred hits—calling to each other,
they shout: come back, go home. This praises Nanquan’s “if it is so, then I will not go.” From then on Nanquan
did not go; hence it says: on the Caoxi road, do not climb—wipe out the thornbush grove.
Xuedou cannot quite pin it down, and again says: the Caoxi road is level—why do you say do not
climb? The Caoxi road is beyond dust and beyond tracks—bare and open, stark and unadorned, level and
free and easy—why, then, say do not climb? Each of you, look under your own feet.

The pointer says: a swift man—one word; a swift horse—one whip. Ten thousand years—one thought, thought
is ten thousand years. If you want to know what is direct, before it is raised—tell me, before it is raised, how do you
grope for it? Please raise it and see.

Case 70. Raised: Weishan, Wufeng, and Yunyan were attending Baizhang, standing by his side. Baizhang asked Weishan: seal up your throat and your lips—how will youAh ha ha! From start to finish—confused and wrong. You
go to Western Qin, I to Eastern Lu.
speak? Weishan said: I ask the master to speak. Baizhang said:One general is hard to find.Borrowing the road to pass through,
I do not refuse to tell you, but I fear that later I will lose my sons and grandsons.you can’t avoid an old woman’s anxious heart—thick-skinned
by three inches. Mixing mud with water, you rob a person on the spot.

Weishan, Wufeng, and Yunyan were attending Baizhang, standing by his side. Baizhang asked Weishan: seal up
your throat and your lips—how will you speak? Weishan said: I ask the master to speak. Baizhang
said: I do not refuse to tell you, but I fear that later I will lose my sons and grandsons. Baizhang
added: even so, the pot has already been seized by someone else. Baizhang again asked
Wufeng. Wufeng said: the master must seal it up too. Baizhang said: in a place with no one, I break my brow
and look to you. He also asked Yunyan. Yunyan said: does the master have it or not? Baizhang said: I have lost
my sons and grandsons. The three men each make a school. The ancients said: on level ground, the dead are
beyond number; the one who can pass through the thornbush grove is a good hand. Therefore, in the house of the lineage masters, they use
the thornbush grove to test people. Why? Under phrases of ordinary feelings, you cannot test people.
In the patched-robed family, you must reveal the pivot within the phrase and discern the target within the words. If you are a plank-shouldered
fellow, you mostly die inside the phrase, and then say: seal up the throat and lips—there is no
place to open your mouth. But if you are someone who can adapt, you have waves against the current—right in the question
head there is a road; you do not nick the blade or cut the hand. Weishan said: I ask the
master to speak. Tell me, what is his intent? Here it is like striking sparks from stone, like
a flash of lightning—pressing at the place of the question, he answers at once, and has his own way out.
It does not cost the slightest bit of effort. Therefore it is said: he studies living phrases, not dead phrases.
Yet Baizhang does not accept him; he only says: I do not refuse to tell you, but I fear that later I will lose
my sons and grandsons. In general, when lineage masters deal with people, they pull nails and extract wedges. If you were like
people today, you would say: this answer does not approve of him, it does not take up the words. You do not realize that here
there is a whole living road—sheer cliffs a thousand fathoms high; guest and host exchange places, lively and
unrestrained. Xuedou loved the style of this phrase—flexible and free, and also able to hold
the boundary. Therefore his verse says:

lg.l:
I ask the master to speak: horns grow from a tiger’s head, emerging from wild grass.The case covers heaven and earth—already the blade has hurt and cut the hand.
Terrifying enough to startle the crowd—no harm in being singular.

When spring ends on the ten isles, blossoms wither and fall; the coralWherever it touches is cool and refreshing—praise can’t reach it.
grove stands bright in the blazing sun.A thousand layers, a hundred turns—yet on the tips of a hundred grasses you can’t find it. Where it answers, it covers heaven and covers earth.

cb:div.p:
In how these three answered, each was different. There is sheer cliff like a thousand fathoms; there is
illumination and function at once; there is also one who cannot even save himself. “I ask the master to speak”—Xuedou
right within this single phrase already presents the pivot. Then within it he lightly presses, making it
easy to see: “horns grow from a tiger’s head, emerging from wild grass.” Weishan’s answer is like
fixing horns onto the head of a fierce tiger—where is there anywhere nearby? Haven’t you seen a monk ask Luo
shan: when living together but not dying together, how is it? Luoshan said: like an ox with no horns. The monk
said: when living together and dying together, how is it? Luoshan said: like a tiger wearing horns. Xuedou
finishes it with a single line of verse. He has extra talent for turning and shifting, and adds: when spring on the ten isles
ends, flowers wither and fall. In the sea there are three mountains and ten isles; a hundred years counts as one spring.
Xuedou’s words carry style and flair, winding and ranging freely. At spring’s end, hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands
of blossoms wither at once. Only the coral grove does not know how to wither and fall; it
contends with the great sun, their lights reflecting each other. Right at such a time, no harm in its strangeness.
Xuedou uses this to illuminate “I ask the master to speak.” The ten isles all belong to various lands beyond the seas.
First, Zuzhou: it produces soul-returning incense. Second, Yingzhou: it grows magic fungi and herbs.
Its jade-stone spring tastes like wine. Third, Xuanzhou: it produces elixir; take it and you live long. Fourth,
Changzhou: it yields wood-melon jade essence. Fifth, Yanzhou: it yields fire-washed cloth. Sixth, Yuanzhou:
it yields a numinous spring like honey. Seventh, Shengzhou: it has mountains and rivers, with no cold or heat. Eighth, Fenglinzhou:
people take phoenix beaks and qilin horns, boil them to make glue to mend broken strings. Ninth, Juku Zhou: it produces
beasts like lions with bronze heads and iron foreheads. Tenth, Tanzhou produces Kunwu stone; made intoOne version reads “Liuzhou.”
swords, it cuts jade like mud. In Miscellaneous Traditions of Foreign Lands on coral it says: southwest of Great Qin, in the vast
sea, some seven or eight hundred li out, you reach Coral Isle. The bottom of the isle is bedrock; coral
grows upon that stone, and people take it with iron nets. And Records of the Ten Isles says: coral
grows on the seabed of the Southern Sea, like a tree two or three feet high, with branches but no bark, like jade and
red and glossy. It grows in response to the moon; every branch tip has a lunar halo.This case should be read together with the first koan of volume eight.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Seven, by Chan Master Foguo YuanwuEnd.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Eight, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu

cb:div.p:
Case 71. Raised: Baizhang again asked Wufeng: seal up your throat and lips—how
will you speak? Wufeng said: the master must seal it up too. Baizhang said: in a place with no one, I break my brow and look to you.Ah ha ha! An arrow flies past Silla.Seize the banner, snatch the drum—one phrase cuts off the stream.
The ten thousand workings are quieted and pared away.The land is vast and people are few; those who meet are rare—this case, together with the end of volume seven,
should be viewed as one.

Weishan holds the boundary firm; Wufeng cuts off all streams. These fellows—what they require is
a man who lifts it up right in your face, like wrestling in front of a horse—no room for conjecture, directly
applying it tight, swift, perilous, and steep. Not like Weishan, winding on and on. Like
today’s Zen companions, who only walk under the rack and cannot get their head above it. Therefore
it is said: if you want intimacy, do not take a question and ask it as a question. Wufeng’s answer cuts it off right
where it stands—no harm in how sharp and swift it is. Baizhang said: in a place with no one, I break my brow and look to you. Tell me,
is he approving him or not approving him? Is it killing or is it letting live? Seeing him rolling along
like that, he gives him just a single point. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
The master too has swept it all away; on the dragon-and-snake battle line, watch the strategy.It is already before the words; it cuts off all streams.
It takes golden fangs to understand the seven things that follow the body—an adept seasoned in battle.

It makes one long remember General Li.Skilled hands are not many; a single horse
and a lone spear—over a thousand miles, ten thousand miles; a thousand people, ten thousand people.

At the edge of the sky ten thousand miles away, a single hawk flies.Do you all see it? Tell me, where does it land? In the middle
as well. He strikes and says: It has flown past.

cb:div.p:
The master too has swept it away. Within one line, Xuedou presses once, saying: Dragon and snake
on the battle line, watch the strategy—like setting out two armies, breaking out and charging in, seven advances and eight crossings.
There are the moves of a dueling general, and there are people of great strategy—one horse, one spear, heading toward
the dragon-and-snake battle line, coming and going freely. How could you possibly surround him? If
he were not this sort of person, how would he know such strategy? These three verses of Xuedou
all go straight to the inside; the words he draws out are like this—just like Li Guang’s miraculous arrows, ten
thousand miles to the sky’s edge a single hawk flies—one arrow drops one eagle for sure, and he does not let it slip away.
Xuedou’s verse: Baizhang’s asking is like a hawk; Wufeng’s answering is like an arrow.
This monk only keeps praising Wufeng, and without noticing, my whole body has entered mud and water
already.

Case 72. Raised: Baizhang again asked Yunyan: Set aside throat, lips, and mouth—how
will you speak? Yunyan said: Master, do you have it or not? Baizhang said: You’ve killed my sons and grandsons.Coming out of a shrimp hole—what are you saying?Skin stuck to bone, dragging mud and trailing water.
Ahead it does not reach a village; behind it does not catch a shop.Clearly there is such an answer—half ahead, falling behind.

Yunyan served as Baizhang’s attendant for twenty years. Later, along with Daowu, he went to Yaoshan.
Yaoshan asked: While you were in Baizhang’s assembly, what was it for? Yunyan
said: To transcend birth and death. Yaoshan said: Have you transcended yet or not? Yunyan said: It has no
birth and death. Yaoshan said: Twenty years with Baizhang, and your habits still aren’t gone. Yunyan took leave
and went to see Nanquan. Later he returned to Yaoshan and only then accorded and awakened. Look at the people of old—twenty
years of investigation, and still half green, half yellow—skin stuck to bone, unable to break free
cleanly. It is so, yes, but: ahead he did not reach a village, behind he did not make it to an inn. Don’t you see it said:
If words do not leave their ruts, how can one escape the covering tangle? White clouds stretch across the valley mouth, confusing
the source for how many people. The Dongshan line calls it breaking through by contact. Hence it is said: Leaping past the immortal guard,
you reach Phoenix Tower. People of the time disliked such “contact” and called it a present-day style. Therefore it is said: A thicket of thorns must
be passed through. If you do not pass through, from start to finish you brush against trifles, unable to cut them off.
Just now I said: ahead he did not reach a village, behind he did not make it to an inn. Yunyan just kept going, checking
other people. Seeing him like this, Baizhang at once seized him and killed him off
on the spot. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Master, do you have it or not? The golden-haired lion does not crouchThe case is ready-made: following the waves, chasing the surf, mixing mud and blending water.
on the ground.Clearly—what use is it? A pity, indeed,

In pairs and in threes, they walk the old road.to block off throat, lips, and mouth—how
can you speak? Turn around and exhale—already you have slipped past beneath your heels.

Below Great Hero Mountain, he snaps his fingers in vain.Once dead, you will not live again. Pitiful and
painful—adding more grievance and suffering under the blue sky.

cb:div.p:
Master, do you have it or not? Xuedou lays out the charge and closes the case: it is so, yes—but as for the golden-
haired lion, what can you do that it does not crouch? When a lion seizes prey, it hides its fangs and crouches its claws; when it crouches
on the ground it flings it back. Whether the thing is large or small, it uses full power. Wanting the full achievement, Yunyan
said: Master, do you have it or not?—he was only walking along the old road. Therefore Xuedou
said: Baizhang below Great Hero Mountain snaps his fingers in vain.

In his instruction he said: As for one who speaks the Dharma, there is no speaking and no showing; and for one who hears the Dharma, there is no hearing
and no attaining. If speaking is already without speaking and without showing, how is that compared to not speaking? If hearing is already without hearing and without attaining,
how is that compared to not hearing? Yet with no speaking and also no hearing, it still falls short a bit. For example,
all of you now are listening to this monk speak here—how will you avoid this fault?
Whoever has eyes that penetrate the barrier, try to raise it and see.

Case 73. Raised: A monk asked Master Ma: Leaving the four propositions and cutting off the hundred negations, I ask the master to directly
point out the meaning of the Patriarch’s coming from the West. Master Ma said: Today IFrom what place did you get this topic of talk? From where did you get this news?
am weary and cannot speak for you. Go ask Zhizang. The monk asked Zhizang. ZhizangStep back three paces and you slip past it without knowing.
Hiding the body yet showing the shadow—no harm if this old fellow shoves it off; but as for others,you must also give them a squeeze; they slip past it without knowing.
said: Why not ask the master?From the grass comes a scorched-tailed great beast. What are you saying? It goes so far as to bind itself with a rope of grass—go die, all the way.
The monk said: The master told me to ask. Zhizang said: Today ICherishing people by dealing with them: the first arrow is light, the later arrow sinks deep.
have a headache and cannot speak for you. Go ask Elder Hai. The monk asked Elder Hai. Hai said: When it comes to this,It is no harm if there are eighty-four good knowing advisors.
They all suffer the same illness.Hand it off to others—hugging stolen goods and crying injustice.
I do not understand. The monk reported this to Master Ma. Master Ma said: Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black.No need for endless chatter; let a thousand ages, ten thousand ages be pitch-black.This monk, though, has a bit of
eyes.Within the realm, the Son of Heaven's edict; beyond the passes, the general's command.

This public case—back in the day, when this monk studied Zhenjue in Chengdu, Zhenjue said: Just
look at Mazu’s first line, and naturally you will understand it all at once. Now tell me—this
monk—did he come asking with understanding, or asking without understanding? This question can be quite deep and far-reaching. “Leaving the four
propositions” means: is, is not, not is, not is not, not not is, not not is not. Leaving these
four propositions, cutting off its hundred negations—he only keeps making it into principle, not recognizing the topic, seeking a head
and not seeing a brain. If it were this monk, once Mazu had spoken, I would immediately spread out my sitting
cloth and bow three times, to see how he would speak. At that time, if Mazu saw this
monk come asking: Leaving the four propositions and cutting off the hundred negations, please directly point out the meaning of the Patriarch’s coming from the West—
he should have split his back with the staff, beaten him, and chased him out, to see whether he woke up or not. Master
Ma only kept tangling vines with him, so that this fellow slipped past right in front of him.
Then he sent him to ask Zhizang. Little did he know Master Ma’s incoming breeze was deeply discerning; this
monk was muddled and ran off to ask Zhizang. Zhizang said: Why not ask the master? The monk
said: The master told me to ask. Look at this—pressed, he turns immediately, with not
a spare moment. Zhizang said: Today I have a headache; I cannot speak it for you.
Go ask Elder Hai. This monk then went to ask Elder Hai. Elder Hai said: When it comes to this,
I do not understand. Now tell me—why? One says “headache,” one
says “don’t understand.” In the end, how is it? This monk then came back and reported it to Master
Ma. The master said: Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black. If you measure it by the route of interpretation, it is called
mutual deception. Some say it is merely shoving it off onto each other. Some say all three
knew the point of his question, so they did not answer. All of that is just slapping at the dark. It takes the ancients’ finest ghee-flavor
and mixes poison into it. Therefore Mazu said:
When you swallow the waters of the West River in one gulp, then I will tell you. It is just like this public case:
if you understand “Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black,” then you will understand the talk about the West River’s water.
This monk took a shoulder-load of confusion and traded it for unrest, further troubling three
venerable elders to enter mud and water. In the end, this monk did not catch it in a flash. Even so,
in this way, these three lineage masters were instead seen through by a plank-shouldered fellow. People nowadays only
work at it in language, saying: White means it matches the bright head;
black means it matches the dark head—drilling and calculating nonstop. Not realizing that the ancients, with one
line, cut off the root of intention—you must look for yourself within the true pulse to be secure.
Therefore it is said: Only the last phrase reaches the solid barrier, blocking the crucial ford, not allowing passage
to ordinary or holy. Speaking of this matter, it is like pressing a sword point at the gate—if you speculate,
you lose body and life. It is also said: It is like throwing a sword and slashing empty space—do not argue whether it reaches
or does not reach; just understand it where it is crystal-clear on all eight sides. Haven’t you seen the ancients say: This
lacquer bucket—or some say wild-fox spirit, some say blind fellow—tell me, compared with one blow
and one shout, is it the same or different? If you know the thousand differences and ten thousand distinctions are only the one, then
naturally you meet enemies on all sides. Want to understand “Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black”? The Fifth Patriarch, former
master, said: Mister Fenghou. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black—clear-eyed patched-robed monksHalf closed, half open; one hand lifts, one hand grips—golden tones, jade-like resonance.
cannot understand it.Travel on foot another thirty years, and in the end someone will still pierce your nostrils. This mountain monk's mouth is indeed like a flat eave.

The colt tramples to death
the people of the world.In the monasteries it still has to be this old fellow. Let this old fellow loose—

Linji still wasn’t a barehanded thief.a leper child
dragging companions. Even if you have good hands, you still get caught.

Leave the four propositions, cut off the hundred negations—What are you saying? You must examine it yourself.
Granddad is like dad.

in heaven above and among humans below, only I know.What use am I? Snatch away the staff. If there is no person, no self, no gain
and no loss, with what will you know?

cb:div.p:
Zang’s head is white, Hai’s head is black—now tell me, what does it mean? This little matter—monks under heaven
cannot jump out of it. Look at Xuedou—how well he closes it off at the end, saying: Even
a clear-eyed patched-robed monk cannot understand it. This little bit of news is called
an immortal’s secret formula, not passed from father to son. Old Shakyamuni preached for a whole lifetime,
and in the end transmitted the mind-seal separately; it is called the Vajra King’s jeweled sword, it is called the true
position. All this vine-tangling is already a matter of necessity; the ancients only slightly revealed a bit of it.
edge and point. If you are someone who has pierced through, then you pierce seven times and bore eight holes, gaining great freedom
and ease. If you have not pierced through, from the start there has been no place to awaken or enter, and the more you talk the farther you go. The colt
tramples everyone under heaven to death. In the West, Prajñātāra prophesied; Bodhidharma foretold, saying: Though the land of
China is vast, there is no other road; it must be walked under the feet of sons and grandsons. A golden rooster can carry in its beak
a single grain of millet and make offerings to the arhat monks of the ten directions. And the Sixth Patriarch said to Master Rang,
In the future, the Buddha Dharma will go out from your side. Later there will arise a colt that will trample
everyone under heaven to death. After that, the Dharma heirs of Jiangxi spread throughout the world, and he was called
Mazu. Bodhidharma and the Sixth Patriarch both foretold Mazu in advance. Look at his conduct—indeed
it was different. Just say, The mountain head is white, the sea head is black, and you will see the place where he tramples everyone under heaven to death.
Just this one black-and-white line—thousands upon tens of thousands cannot bite through it. Linji was not yet
a daylight robber. One day Linji addressed the assembly, saying: Within this red lump of flesh there is
a true person of no rank, always coming and going through the gate of your face. Those who have not verified it,
look! At that time a monk came forward and asked, What is the true person of no rank? Linji
got down from the meditation seat, grabbed him, and said, Speak, speak! The monk had no words. Linji shoved him away and said, The true person of no rank—
what kind of dried shit-stick is that! Later Xuefeng heard of it and said, Linji really seems like
a daylight robber. Xuedou wanted Linji to meet him, to observe Mazu’s edge and devices.
They surpass Linji even more. This truly is a daylight robber; Linji was not yet a daylight
robber. Xuedou pierced it through in one go, and then versified this monk’s saying: Depart from the four
propositions, cut off the hundred negations—through heaven and earth, only I know. Do not set up shop in a ghost cave.
The ancients said, The question is in the answer; the answer is in the question—already extraordinary. How will
you depart from the four propositions and cut off the hundred negations? Xuedou said,
This matter, only I can know. Even the Buddhas of the three times cannot see it. Since
it is known by me alone, what are you all still coming up here to seek? Great Weishan Zhenru
raised it and said: The monk asked like this; Mazu answered like this—depart from the four propositions, cut off
the hundred negations—and even Zhizang and Hai the elder brother do not know at all. Want to understand? Have you not seen it said: the colt
tramples everyone under heaven to death—tsk!At the start, it is especially tightly restrained.

Instruction said: The Mo-ye sword is held crosswise; before the edge it snips off the nest of tangled vines. A bright mirror is hung
high; within the phrase it draws out Vairocana’s seal. Where the ground is steady and close, you put on clothes and eat food.
Where spiritual powers play as a game, how do you approach and settle? Do you understand? Look at what follows.

Case 74. Master Jinniu, whenever it was mealtime, would carry his own rice bucket, and
dance in front of the monks’ hall, laughing loudly, saying, Bodhisattva children, come eat! Xuedou said,A thread line at the tip of the pole.
Play with it as you please; without troubling the clear waves, the intent is naturally distinct. Ghee and poison travel together; yes, it is seven treasures and eight gems spread out at once—yet those who meet it are few.
Although this is so, Jinniu is not acting from a good heart. A monk asked Changqing, The ancients said, Bodhisattva children, come eat—Thieves recognize thieves; spirits recognize spirits.
Whoever comes to speak of right and wrong is a person of right and wrong.
what is the intent? Changqing said, It is very much likeNo harm in doubting. From the start you do not know where it lands. What does Changqing say?
celebrating and praising because of the meal.Over shared seats they strike the command; by the evidence they close the case.

Jinniu was a venerable elder under Mazu. Whenever it was mealtime, he would carry his own rice bucket and,
in front of the monks’ hall, dance, laughing loudly, saying, Bodhisattva children, come eat! He did this
for twenty years. Tell me, where was his intent? If you just call it
eating, ordinarily striking the wooden fish and beating the drum already announces it—so why would he need
to bring the rice bucket himself and perform so many tricks? Was he crazy? Or was he
proclaiming and setting this up? If he was proclaiming this matter, why not go up to the jeweled-flower king
seat, strike the bench and raise the whisk? Why must he do it like this? People today truly do not
know: the ancients’ intent is beyond words. Why not first look at what the patriarch’s original topic was when he first came:
it plainly says, A separate transmission outside the teachings, a direct transmission of the mind
seal. The ancients’ expedients only taught you to take it up directly. Later
people vainly guessed and divined, saying there are not so many things: when cold, go to the fire;
when hot, enjoy the shade; when hungry, eat; when tired, sleep. If you explain it in this way with
ordinary feelings, interpreting and glossing by meanings, Bodhidharma’s lineage would be swept away like dust. You do not know that the ancients,
through the twelve hours, never let it go, thought after thought, wanting to make this matter clear. Xuedou
said, Although this is so, Jinniu is not acting from a good heart. Just this one line—how many
people misunderstand it! What is called the finest flavor of ghee is treasured by the world, yet when it meets such people,
it turns into poison. Since Jinniu descended into the weeds to help people, why did Xuedou
say, not acting from a good heart? For what reason did he say it like that? A patch-robed monk must
have living potential, only then does it work. People today do not reach the ancients’ ground, and only keep saying, What mind
is there to see? What Buddha is there? If you hold such a view, you ruin old Jinniu
the seasoned master. You must look carefully for it to work. If you only talk today and tomorrow,
making your mouth quick, there will be no end. Later Changqing ascended the hall; a monk asked, The ancients
said, Bodhisattva children, come eat—what is the intent? Changqing said, It is very much like celebrating
and praising because of the meal. The venerable elder was extremely compassionate, leaking and revealing quite a lot. It is so, yes it is so:
celebrating and praising because of the meal. But tell me, what is it that is being celebrated and praised? Look at Xuedou’s verse:
It says:

lg.l:
In the shadow of white clouds, laughing ho-ho, both handsA knife hidden in a smile. What does the fever flare up for? Patch-robed monks throughout the land do not know where it lands.
bring it and hand it to him.How could there be such a thing? Do not slander the Golden Ox. Can you call him a rice bucket? If you are a proper patch-robed monk, you do not drink this kind of tea
and rice.

If you are a golden-haired lion cub, three thousandYou must grant that he is exceptional. Allow that he has eyes—only fear the eyes are not straight.
miles away you see error and confusion.Not worth half a coin. One whole spill and leak—where is the distortion? Blind fellow.

cb:div.p:
In the shadow of white clouds, laughing ho-ho. Changqing said, celebrating and praising because of the meal. Xuedou said,
both hands bring it and hand it to him. Tell me, is it merely giving him food to eat, or
is there something else extraordinary? If within this you truly know it, you are a golden-haired
