lion cub. If you are a golden-haired lion cub, there is no need for Jinniu to bring the rice
bucket and dance and laugh. Straight from three thousand miles away, you would know his point of defeat
and flaw. The ancients said, The mirror is before the pivot; it does not take a single pinch. Therefore a patch-robed monk
ordinarily must function beyond the rules, only then can he be called a proper lineage master. If
you rely only on words and language, you cannot avoid leakage and revelation.

Instruction said: The numinous edge, the precious sword, is always bared before you; it can kill people and it can
bring people to life. There and here, the same gain and the same loss. If you want to raise it up and hold it, raise it up and hold it;
if you want to lay it out level, lay it out level. Tell me, when it does not fall into guest and host, not constrained by
back-and-forth, how is it? Try to raise it and see.

Case 75. A monk left the assembly of the master of Dingzhou and came to Wujiu. Wujiu
asked, How is Dingzhou’s Dharma way compared to here?There is an echo in the words. If you want to tell shallow from deep, probe with pole and shadow the grass—far too much deceiving of people.
The monk said, Not different. Wujiu said, IfAmong dead fellows there are living ones—one or half of one. Like iron stakes, they step on solid ground.
it is not different, then turn back there. And he struck. The monk said, The staff tipClearly—true command is in force.
has eyes; you must not strike people carelessly. Wujiu said,It also requires this kind of adept; it is a lion's cub.
Today I’ve hit one. And he struck three more times. The monkWhy say one? A thousand, ten thousand.
then went out. Wujiu said, A bent staff turns outIt turns out he is one of the household. He can only suffer the injustice—he merely acts upon the opportunity.
to have someone eating it. The monk turned around and said, What canA mute eating bitter melon. Let it go, then bring it back and dot it—what use is it when you get it back?
be done? The ladle handle is in the master’s hand. Wujiu said,Just as before, three hundred sixty days—yet he is a clever patch-robed monk.
If you want this mountain monk to give it back to you—then the monk came forward, seized the staff from Wujiu’s hand, and struck Wujiu three times. Wujiu said, Bent staff, bent staff! The monkWho knows which is ruler and which is minister? He dares to throw himself across a tiger's mouth—far too much not knowing good
from bad.He is also a capable Chan traveler,
only then. Guest and host exchange; giving and taking, free and sudden,dot. This old fellow—why such deadly urgency?
said, Someone is eating it. Wujiu said, You carelessly hitHeh heh. How many ladle-handles are in this monk's hands?
this fellow. The monk then bowed. Wujiu said, TheNot falling to the "Bing" side—who knows who he is?Unchanging in the face of danger—only then is he a true man.
master goes off just like that? The monk laughed loudly and went out. Wujiu said, It fits like that, it fitsDot.A capable Chan traveler is naturally there. A fierce tiger must
have a clear wind follow; only then do you know from start to finish. People under heaven cannot lay a hand on it.
like that.A pity to let it pass. Why not strike straight down the spine? You think it has run off somewhere.

A monk left the assembly of the master of Dingzhou and came to Wujiu. Wujiu too was a master of the craft.
If you all, here, recognize these two people—one going out and one coming in—then a thousand
and ten thousand are just one. Acting as host is like this; acting as guest is like this. The two
people, in the end, form one household. In one round of examination, guest-and-host questions and answers, from
beginning to end, are the work of masters. Look: Wujiu asked this monk, How is Dingzhou’s Dharma way compared to
here? The monk immediately said, Not different. If at that time it had not been Wujiu, it would have been hard to deal with
this monk. Wujiu said, If it is not different, then turn back there—and he struck. But what can be done?
This monk was a capable one, so he said, The staff tip has eyes; you must not strike people carelessly.
Wujiu carried out his command straight through, saying, Today I’ve hit one—and he struck three more times.
The monk then went out. Look at the two of them rolling and turning—both are masters of the craft.
To settle this one matter, you must distinguish monk from lay, and discern blame from innocence. Though this monk went out,
this case was still not finished. From start to finish, Wujou wanted to test him and see where he was real,
to see how he would be. But this monk was like bracing the door and propping the house, so his true face was not yet seen.
Wujou then said: So it turns out someone has taken a beating from this staff. This monk wanted to turn around
and breathe out, yet he did not contend with him; he lightly turned it, saying: But what can you do, the ladle-handle is in the
master’s hand. Wujou was a lineage teacher with eyes atop his head; he dared to throw himself across a fierce tiger’s
mouth and say: If you want this mountain monk to hand it back to you. This fellow was one
who carried a tally under his elbow; as the saying goes, to see what is right and not do it is to lack courage. Without
the least hesitation, he stepped up, snatched the staff from Wujou’s hand, and struck Wujou three times. Wujou said: Bent
staff, bent staff! Tell me, what is the meaning? Earlier he said: So it turns out
someone has taken a beating. Yet when this monk struck him, he instead said: Bent staff, bent staff.
The monk said: Someone has taken a beating. Wujou said: Carelessly you struck this fellow. Earlier he said:
Carelessly you struck one. In the end he himself took the staff—why did he also
say, Carelessly you struck this fellow? If at that time it were not this monk standing firm and direct,
there would have been no way to deal with him. The monk then bowed. This bow was the most venomous; it
was not from a good heart. If it were not Wujou, he would not have recognized it and broken through. Wujou said:
And you go off like that? The monk burst into loud laughter and left. Wujou said: It can digest
like this, it can digest like this. See how master hands meet: from start to finish host and guest are clearly
distinguished; cut off yet able to continue. In truth it is only the pivot of mutual exchange. When he came to
here, he also did not say there was some place of exchange. Such are the ancients: cutting off emotional dust
and ideation. Both sides were master hands, and neither spoke of gain or loss. Though it was a single bout
of words, the two were lively and quick, each with bloodlines and stitches. If
you can see it here, then throughout the twelve double-hours it is vividly clear.
This monk’s leaving is a double release; what follows is a double gathering. This is called mutual exchange. Xue-
dou chanted it just like this.

lg.l:
Calling is easy; sending off is hard.Everyone under heaven keeps doubting. Stinking flesh draws flies; patch-robed monks throughout the land still do not know where it lands.No harm in wiping it out. On the sea
there is the eminent one, outstanding and refined.

Look closely at the sharp pivot of mutual exchange.One goes out, one comes in—both are adepts. One staff, two people supporting it. Tell me, on whose
side is it?

Though the aeon-stone is solid, it can still be worn away; in the deep of the vast sea,How do you discern the golden hammer in the sleeve? A thousand sages do not transmit it.
it would have to dry up at once.Where can you place it? The tip of the staff has eyes; only he is allowed to obtain it personally.

Old Wujou, old Wujou,A pity
indeed. This old fellow does not recognize good and bad.

what is it, after all, to hand him the ladle-handle soHe is also a pointless fellow, a hundred, a thousand, ten thousand layers
without cause?already before the words. He should have smashed Caizhou—best to give him thirty blows. Tell me, where is the fault?

cb:div.p:
Calling is easy; sending off is hard. Either way, it is falling into the weeds. Xuedou was far too compassionate.
They commonly say: calling a snake is easy; sending a snake off is hard. Now he brings a gourd ladle in by blowing.
To call the snake is easy; to send it away is hard. It is like handing him the staff is easy,
but then snatching the staff back and sending him off is hard. You must have fundamental hands and feet, only then
can you send him off. Wujou was a master hand: he had the skill to call the snake, and also
the means to send the snake away. This monk was no sleepyhead either. Wujou asked: How is
Dingzhou’s Dharma Way compared to here? That was calling him in. Wujou then struck him—that was sending
him off. The monk said: The staff-tip has eyes; you must not carelessly strike people. That turned it over onto this
monk—this was calling him in. Wujou said: If you want this mountain monk to hand it back to you.
The monk then stepped forward and snatched the staff, and also struck three times—this was the monk sending him off. Then
this monk burst into loud laughter and left. Wujou said: It can digest like this, it can digest like
this. This clearly is sending him off just right. Look at these two, their sharp points mutually
exchanging—thread coming, line going—beating it into one piece. From start to finish host and guest are clear. Sometimes
the host becomes the guest; sometimes the guest becomes the host. Even Xuedou could not praise it enough.
Therefore he said: the pivot of mutual exchange—people must look closely. Though the aeon-stone is
solid, it can still be worn away. This aeon-stone is said to be forty li long, and eighty-four thousand
yojanas wide, and eighty-four thousand yojanas thick. Every five hundred years a heavenly being
comes down and brushes it once with a sleeve of a six-zhu robe, then leaves; after another five hundred years, again
comes to brush it like this. When this stone is brushed away entirely, that counts as one aeon. This is called the light-robe brushing-
stone aeon. Xuedou said: though the aeon-stone is solid, it can still be worn away. Though the stone is hard, it still
can be ground down to nothing. But the sharp exchange of these two people, for a thousand ages, ten thousand ages, has no
end. In the deep of the vast sea, it would have to dry up at once. Even if it is the vast sea, with floodwaters broad and
white waves surging to the sky, if you let these two stand firmly within, then this vast sea too
would have to run dry. When Xuedou got to this, he chanted it all at once. At the end he added: Old Wujou
old Wujou old—what is it, after all? Sometimes capturing, sometimes releasing; sometimes killing, sometimes enlivening—ultimately
what is it? To hand him the ladle-handle was too uncaused. This staff: all Buddhas of the three times use it;
patriarchs of successive generations also use it; lineage teachers’ households also use it. It is used to
pull nails and remove pegs for people, to undo sticking and remove bonds—how could one lightly hand it over to others?
Xuedou’s intent was to use it alone. Luckily, at that time this monk only laid it out evenly with him.
If suddenly thunder rose from dry ground, see how he would meet it. Wujou passed the ladle-handle
off to another—was that not too uncaused?

The pointer said: fine as rice bran, cold as ice and frost; filling heaven and earth; apart from brightness
and cutting off darkness. Seen from low places it is more than enough; leveled from high places it is not enough. Grasp it
and release it—everything is right here. Is there still a way out or not? Try raising it
and see. Fu.Clapping and pressing—tight and full.

Case 76. Danxia asked a monk: Where have you come from?Indeed you cannot entirely lack a place it came from. To know the source is not hard.
The monk said: From below the mountain.Put on straw sandals and walk through your belly. You just do not understand. There is an echo in the words, coming hidden and familiar—how will you know whether it is yellow or green?
Danxia said: Have you eaten yet? The monk said:Pour the first ladle of foul water—why insist on a fixed north star? If you want to know precisely,
I have eaten. Danxia said: The one whosure enough you crash into a bare pillar, yet someone else pierces your nostrils. It turns out to be a holeless iron hammer.
brought the food for you to eat—does he still have eyes? The monk had no words. Changqing asked Baofu: Bringing food for people to eat, there is a share in repaying kindness; whyThough it relies on power to bully people, it is still closing the case by the evidence. At that
time it was best to flip over the meditation bench—why act for no reason?Sure enough, he cannot run. If this monk were an adept, he would say to him: You have eyes like the master's,
the same.
is it that he does not have eyes? BaofuEven then, that only says half. The whole body is it, every part is it—one knife, two sections; one hand lifts, one hand grips.
said: Giver and receiver are both blind fellows. Changqing
Act in accord with the command. One sentence says it all—rare to meet such a person.
said: If one exhausts the whole function, is one still blind? BaofuWhat do you know of good and bad? Still you will not agree—what bowl are you begging for?
said: Tell me, am I blind?Both are grass-dwelling fellows: dragon head, snake tail. If at that time you waited for him to speak out his whole mechanism, would you still end up blind? Only
tell him "blind," and you still say only half. Both are of the same rank as adepts—so why does it not reach a village ahead, and not catch a shop behind?

Chan Master Danxia Tianran of Dengzhou—no one knows where he was from. At first he studied Confucian learning.
He was going to Chang’an to take the examinations, and stayed at an inn. Suddenly he dreamed of white light filling the room.
A diviner said: An auspice of understanding emptiness. By chance a Chan traveler asked: Worthy one, where are you going?
He said: To be selected as an official. The Chan traveler said: How does selecting officials compare to selecting Buddhas? Danxia said: To select
Buddhas, where should one go? The Chan traveler said: Now in Jiangxi, Master Ma has appeared in the world; that is
the place for selecting Buddhas. Worthy one, you can go there. So he went straight to Jiangxi. As soon as he saw Master Ma,
he lifted up the feet of Ma’s head-wrapped cap with both hands. Master Ma looked back and said: I am not
your teacher. Go to Shitou at Nanyue. He hurried to Nanyue and again offered his former intent.
Shitou said: Go to the work shed. The master bowed in thanks, entered the practitioners’ hall, and together with the
assembly labored for three years. One day Shitou told the assembly: Tomorrow, clear the grass
in front of the Buddha hall. When the next day came, the great assembly each prepared shovels and hoes to clear the grass. Danxia alone
brought a basin of water to wash his head, knelt before the teacher, and Shitou saw this and smiled.
He then shaved his hair and also explained the precepts to him. Danxia covered his ears and left, then went to Jiang-
xi to visit Mazu again. Before paying respects, he went into the monks’ hall and sat astride the neck of the holy monk
statue. The great assembly was startled and rushed to report to Mazu. The patriarch personally entered the hall and looked
at him, saying: My son Tianran. Danxia then came down and bowed, saying: I thank the master for granting me a Dharma name.
Thus he was named Tianran. Those ancients were naturally like this—so outstanding and unbound. This is what is meant by: selecting officials
is not as good as selecting Buddhas. The Transmission of the Lamp records his sayings; they are truly like sheer cliffs
a thousand fathoms high. Every line has the skill to pull nails and remove pegs for people. It is like asking this
monk: Where have you come from? The monk says: From below the mountain. Yet this monk did not understand his coming
place at all—like someone with eyes going in upside down to assess the household. If at that time it were not
Danxia, it would have been hard to clean up. Danxia then said: Have you eaten yet? From the start
he still had not been seen through. This was the second round of testing him. The monk said: I have eaten.
The dullard never understood. Danxia said: The one who brought the food for you to eat—
person. Do you still have eyes? The monk had no words. Danxia meant: With fellows like you,
you eat rice, but what is it good for? If this monk were a real man, try giving him one jab
and see what he does. Even so, Danxia still would not let you off. This
monk just blinked and said nothing. Baofu and Changqing were together in Xuefeng’s assembly
and often brought up the ancients’ cases to discuss. Changqing asked Baofu: Giving rice to people to eat,
there is a share of repaying kindness; why is it that one does not have eyes? There is no need to ask every detail of the case;
broadly speaking, he borrows these words as a topic, to test where it truly hits.
Baofu said: Giver and receiver are both blind men. Excellent, right here. Just
speak of what meets the situation; at home there is a road of emergence. Changqing said: If you exhaust his mechanism
and it comes forth, does it still become blind? Baofu said: Are you saying I am blind? Baofu’s intent
was: I have eyes like this, I have told you, and you still say I am blind?
Even so, it is half closed and half open. If it had been this mountain monk back then, when he said,
If you exhaust his mechanism and it comes forth, does it still become blind? I would only tell him: Blind. What a pity. If Baofu
back then could have delivered that one word blind, it would have spared Xuedou all this much vine and
creeper. Xuedou also versified only using this intent.

lg.l:
Exhausting the mechanism does not become blind; pressing the ox’s head to make it eat grass.You have only managed to say half of it. It still must be tested through. The words are still in the ear.Losing money,
you incur punishment. Half Henan, half Hebei—without realizing you have nicked the blade and cut the hand.

Four, seven, two, three: the various patriarchs and masters.Having a branch, you cling to a branch; dragging
down the former sages—not merely dragging down one person.

Bringing the precious vessel turns into a fault.Everyone on earth switches hands and pounds their chests: return my staff!
It even drags this mountain monk down so I cannot lift my head.

The fault is deep, with nowhere to search.Damnably deep. Patch-robed monks throughout the land cannot jump out. Tell me—how deep is it?
Right under your heel—you cannot grope it out.

In heaven above and among humans, all sink on dry land.Bury all the patch-robed monks in one pit—are there still any living people
left? Letting it off once—blue heaven, blue heaven!
Blink, blink.Horse-stall without walls.The eyes move.

cb:div.p:
Exhausting the mechanism does not become blind. Changqing said: If you exhaust his mechanism and it comes forth, does it still become blind? Bao-
fu said: Are you saying I am blind? It is like pressing the ox’s head to make it eat grass. You must wait
until it eats by itself. How could you press its head and teach it to eat? Xuedou versified it like this
and naturally you see Danxia’s intent. Four, seven, two, three: the various patriarchs and masters. Bringing the precious vessel
turns into a fault. It not only implicates Changqing, it even buries the twenty-
eight patriarchs of the West, and the six patriarchs of this land, all at once. Old Shakyamuni, for forty-nine
years, spoke the entire canon; in the end he only transmitted this precious vessel. Yongjia said:
It is not an empty show of forms; the Tathagata’s jeweled staff has its own trace. If you take Baofu’s
understanding, then bringing the precious vessel all turns into a fault. The fault is deep, with nowhere to search.
This cannot be explained to you. Just go sit quietly, and check it within his lines,
and see. Since the fault is deep, why is there nowhere to search? This is no small
fault. It sinks the great affair of the patriarchs all at once, sinking it flat on dry land. Therefore
Xuedou said: In heaven above and among humans, all sink on dry land.

Pointer: Turn upward and you can pierce the nostrils of everyone under heaven, like a falcon
snatching a dove. Turn downward and your own nostrils are in someone else’s hand, like
a turtle hiding in its shell. If someone suddenly comes out here and says: Originally there is no upward or
downward, so what use is turning? Just tell him: I also know you are making a living in a ghost cave.
Tell me, how do you distinguish black and white? After a long pause he said: There is
a vine, you climb a vine; no vine, you climb a pattern. Try raising it and see.

Case 77. A monk asked Yunmen: What is talk that surpasses Buddhas and transcends patriarchs? Yunmen said: A sesame cake.Open. On dry ground,
sudden thunder. Pressingthe tongue against the upper palate—passed.

This monk asked Yunmen: What is talk that surpasses Buddhas and transcends patriarchs? Yunmen said: A ses-
ame cake. Do you feel your hair stand on end? In the house of patched-robes they ask about Buddha, ask about patriarchs, ask about Zen,
ask about the Way, ask upward and downward, until there is nothing left to ask, and yet they still produce a question
point, asking about talk that surpasses Buddhas and transcends patriarchs. Yunmen was an adept; as the water rises
the boat floats high, as the mud grows the Buddha grows big, so he answered: A sesame cake. It can be said the Way is not practiced in vain,
and effort is not applied wastefully. Yunmen again instructed the assembly, saying: You must not make up things. When you hear
someone speak of the patriarchs’ meaning, you then ask about the principle of surpassing Buddhas and transcending patriarchs. You,
first call what Buddha, call what patriarch, and then you speak of surpassing Buddhas and transcending patriarchs.
Then you ask about leaving the three realms. Bring the three realms and look: what
seeing, hearing, awareness, and knowing is obstructing you? What sounds, forms, Buddha-dharma are there for you
to settle? Settle what bowl? Taking that as a view of differences, the ancient
sages could do nothing with you. You throw yourself sideways to become a thing. Say: the whole body is entirely real, thing after thing
meets it directly, and it cannot be gotten. I tell you: what matter is there right here? It is already
buried. If you understand these words, then you recognize the sesame cake. The Fifth Patriarch said:
Donkey dung is compared to musk. As the saying goes: directly cutting to the root is what the Buddha attested; picking leaves
and searching branches, I cannot. When you reach here and want it intimate, do not take a question to ask a question.
Look at this monk asking: What is talk that surpasses Buddhas and transcends patriarchs? Yunmen said: A ses-
ame cake. Do you still know shame? Do you still sense leakage? There are some people who fabricate,
saying: Yunmen saw a rabbit and released the hawk, so he said sesame cake. If you take sesame cake like that
as the view of talk that surpasses Buddhas and transcends patriarchs, how could there be a living road? Do not take it as a
A cake banquet. And if you do not make it a surpass-Buddhas-and-outdo-Patriarchs affair, then it is the living road. Like hemp
the three pounds of hemp that can beat a drum: though it is only said as sesame cake, in truth it is hard to see.
Later people often make doctrines of it, saying: coarse words and subtle words all return to the first meaning.
If you understand it like that, then go be a lecturer; for a whole life you win much knowing and much explaining.
Nowadays Zen fellows say: When you surpass Buddhas and transcend patriarchs, all Buddhas are trampled under
your heels, and the patriarchs too are trampled under your heels. Therefore Yunmen only told
him: sesame cake. Since it is sesame cake, how could it transcend Buddhas and patriarchs? Go investigate
it carefully. There are extremely many verses in various places, all making words at the edge of the question. Only Xue-
dou’s verse is the best. Try raising it and see. The verse says:

lg.l:
Zen guests asking about surpassing talk ask far too much; do you see the seams and gaps torn open,Each one that comes out at once makes this kind of understanding—like hemp, like millet.
do you?It was already before the words. Open. One's own filth does not smell to oneself.

Even when the sesame cake comes, you still will not stop.Taking wooden
soapberries to swap out your eyes.

To this day, under heaven there are distortions.He draws a circular emblem and says: do you understand it like this? Words that bite people—
what end is there to them? The great earth is vast and desolate, sorrowing people to death. Then he strikes.

cb:div.p:
Zen guests asking about surpassing talk ask far too much. Zen fellows especially love to ask this. Do you not see
Yunmen saying: You people shoulder your staffs crosswise and say you study Zen and learn the Way, and then
you go looking for some principle of surpassing Buddhas and transcending patriarchs. Let me ask you: throughout the twelve hours, in walking
standing sitting lying, in shitting and pissing, even for the bugs in the latrine and in the market buying
and selling at the mutton stall, is there still any principle of surpassing Buddhas and transcending patriarchs? Speak it
out if you can. If not, do not hinder me from going east or west, and then he stepped down from the seat. Some
do not even know good from bad and make an enso, adding mud on dirt, adding cangues and chains. Do you see the seams
and gaps torn open? Where he brings up the question, there are great and small seams and gaps. Yun-
men saw that his question was torn open, so he used a sesame cake to block the seam and plug it tight. This
monk still would not stop, and asked again. Therefore Xuedou said: Even when the sesame cake
comes, you still will not stop. To this day, under heaven there are distortions. Nowadays Zen fellows only
go on interpreting it from the sesame cake, or else they make doctrines in the place of surpassing Buddhas and transcending patriarchs.
Since it is not in either of these two ends, where is it ultimately? After thirty years,
wait until this mountain monk changes bones and comes out, then I will tell you.

Case 78. In ancient times there were sixteen enlightened ones who, while bathingForming into groups and teams—what use is it? This whole band cannot utter a word.
with the monks, entered the bath according to custom and suddenly awakened to the water as the cause.Bumping into a bare pillar—what is the use of a lacquer bucket?Nasty water splashes over the head.
How do you Zen worthies understand this? He said: Wondrous contact reveals illumination; even abiding as a Buddha-son still requiresIt has nothing to do with anyone else. How
will you understand it? The falling and scattering are nothing else.Patch-robed monks throughout the land cannot grope it out here. Why make two ends into three?
seven piercings and eight holes before it works.One blow, one stripe. Do not let this mountain monk down. Bumping and knocking—have you ever seen Deshan and Linji?

In the Shurangama assembly, the bodhisattva Bhadrapala and the sixteen enlightened ones each cultivated
pure conduct, and each spoke of the cause by which they realized the perfect penetration dharma gate. This too is one among the twenty-
five perfect penetrations. He, because while bathing with the monks he entered the bath as usual, suddenly
awakened to the water as the cause, and said: Since it does not wash dust, and also does not wash the body, tell me, what does it
wash? If you can understand it, then in the middle it is at ease, gaining nothing at all; a thousand, ten thousand
cannot even come near. As is said: taking no attainment as true prajna; if
there is attainment, it is only similar prajna. Do you not see Bodhidharma saying to the Second Patriarch: Bring
me your mind and I will set it at rest for you. The Second Patriarch said: When I searched for mind, it could not be found. Here
is the very life-root of a patch-robed monk. In the end there is no need for so many entangling vines
at all. Just say a sudden awakening to the cause of water, and it is naturally clear and settled. Since one does not wash away dust,
one also does not wash the body. Tell me, awaken to what? When you reach this stage, not a
speck can be attached. Even saying the word Buddha must be avoided. He said: wondrous contact
makes it manifest, and the child of Buddha abides. Proclaim means reveal; wondrous contact is clarity. Once
you awaken to wondrous contact, the child of Buddha abides, that is, abides on Buddha-ground. People nowadays also enter
the bath and also wash with water, and they touch just like that; why then do they not awaken? They are all confused and obstructed by dusty
objects and conditions, stuck to the skin and clinging to the bones, so they cannot at once go clear and awake. If in this
place, washing gains nothing, touching gains nothing, and the cause of water gains nothing.
Tell me: is it wondrous contact making it manifest, or is it not wondrous contact making it manifest? If right here
you see it directly, that is wondrous contact making it manifest: the child of Buddha abides. People nowadays
also touch; do they still see the wondrous place? Wondrous contact is not ordinary contact: when contact and the one who contacts unite, it
is contact; when they separate, it is not. Xuansha crossing a ridge, stubbing his toe, and even
Deshan's staff: are these not wondrous contact? Even so, you still must have seven penetrations
and eight openings for it to work. If you only grope around on the body, what does it have to do with it? If you
go with seven penetrations and eight openings, why need to enter a bath? Right on the tip of a single hair you manifest
the jeweled king's land; within a mote of dust you turn the great Dharma wheel. If you penetrate in one place, in a thousand places
and ten thousand places you penetrate at once. Do not merely guard one nest or one cave; everywhere is
the gate by which Guanyin enters principle. The ancients too had awakening to the Way on hearing sound, and clarifying
the mind on seeing form. If one person awakens, that is so; why did the sixteen great ones awaken
all at the same time? Therefore the ancients practiced together and realized together, awakened together and understood together. Xuedou picked up
the teaching's intent, making people go and understand at the place of wondrous contact. He brought forth his teaching eye in verse, to spare
people from being netted by the teaching and caged, half drunk and half awake. He wants people to go directly
open and unencumbered. The verse says:

lg.l:
A patch-robed monk who has finished the matter needs only one.Right now there is one: three thousand blows in the morning, eight hundred at dusk. Leap out of the vajra ring—yet not a single one
can digest it.

Stretched out on a long connected bed, he lies with legs extended; in a dream he onceIndeed he is a dozing fellow—talking about kalpas, not about Zen.
spoke of awakening perfect penetration.Already dozing, he goes on to speak dreams; and you are even allowed to dream it. What use is sleep-talk?

When the fragrant water is used to wash, suddenly
spit in his face.Hah! Adding mud atop earth—another layer. Do not come to a clean place to shit.

cb:div.p:
A patch-robed monk who has finished the matter needs only one. Tell me, what matter is finished? A seasoned
Zen traveler, just hearing it raised, picks it up and goes. A patch-robed monk like this needs only
one; why form crowds and teams? Stretched out on a long connected bed, he lies with legs extended. The ancients
said: clearly there is no Dharma of awakening; once you awaken, it still confuses people. Stretch both legs out long and sleep.
No false and no true; therefore in the breast there is not a single affair. When hungry, eat; when tired,
sleep. Xuedou's meaning is: if you say that by entering the bath you awaken to wondrous contact making it manifest,
as far as this kind of no-affairs patch-robed monk is concerned, it is only like speaking of a dream within a dream. Therefore
he says: In a dream you once spoke of awakening perfect penetration; when the fragrant water is used to wash, suddenly spit in your face. Like this,
it is merely filthy water suddenly poured over your head; what more is there to say about perfect penetration? Xuedou
says: a fellow like this is just right for sudden spitting straight on his head and face. This mountain monk says: on dirt
you add mud, another layer.

Instruction said: When great functioning appears, it keeps no rules. Seize alive and capture living, without
any extra effort. Tell me, what person ever came like this? Try bringing it up and see.

Case 79: A monk asked Touzi: Are all sounds the Buddha's sound or not? Touzi said, Yes. The monk said, Master, do not take the sound of a boiling bowl. Touzi immediately struck him. The monk then asked: Rough speech andHe also knows how to stroke the tiger's whiskers. The blue sky
booms with thunderclaps. One's own filth does not smell to oneself.He swindles a whole boatload of people to death. He has sold himself to you. He picks it up and sets it aside—what kind of mind
activity is that?You only see the awl point is sharp; you do not see the chisel head is square. Say what
you will. Indeed, he takes in the blunder.Hit. A fine beating. Letting him off is not allowed.
subtle speech both return to the first meaning, is that so? Touzi said, Yes. The monk said,A second time stroking the tiger's whiskers. Why hold stolen goods and cry injustice? East, west, south, north—
there are still echoes and traces.Again he has sold himself to you. A device that traps the tiger—what kind of mind activity is that?
can I call the master a donkey? Touzi immediately struck him.You only see the awl point is sharp; you do not see the chisel head is square. Though there are waves against the current, it is only
that there are no horns on the head—spitting blood at people.Hit. He must not be let off. A fine beating. The staff has not yet reached the breaking—why stop so soon?
。Buttocks.

Touzi, that plain and solid fellow, had an eloquence that surpassed the crowd. Whenever there was a question put to him, as soon as he opened his mouth he
showed his guts. Without spending extra effort, he simply cut off their tongues while seated. It can be called planning within the curtain,
deciding victory a thousand miles away. This monk took his views about sound and form and Buddhadharma
and pasted them on his own forehead, asking everyone he met. Touzi was a seasoned hand, with sharp discernment;
he saw through it deeply. This monk knew Touzi was a solid fellow, so from the start he made a trap, to make
Touzi step into it, and so there was the follow-up line. But Touzi instead used a tiger-trapping mechanism,
to fish out the monk's follow-up words. The monk, catching at Touzi's reply, said: Master, do not
take the sound of a boiling bowl. Sure enough, one cast and he bit. If it were someone else, they could do nothing
against this monk. Touzi had eyes; right after it he struck. Hands and feet that bite pigs and dogs
must belong to a seasoned hand. Turning left, he rolls along with him; turning right, he rolls along with him.
This monk, having made a trap, wanted
to stroke the tiger's whiskers. He did not know that Touzi was even above his trap. Tou-
zi immediately struck. Pity this monk: a head with no tail. At the time, if he had waited for Touzi
to pick up the staff, then overturned the Zen bench, even if Touzi had the whole functioning, he would still have had to retreat
three thousand miles. He asked again: Rough speech and subtle speech both return to the first meaning, is it
so? Touzi also said yes. It was no different from the earlier line. The monk said, Can I call the mas-
ter a donkey? Touzi struck again. Although this monk made nests
and caves, it was not without something strange. If it were some old fellow on a crooked wooden bed, with no eyes on the crown
of his head, it would be hard to break him down. Touzi had a place to turn around. This monk, having made
a piece of reasoning, wanted to elbow into his trade; but when it came to it, he still could not do anything to old Touzi.
Haven't you seen Yantou say: when it comes to battle, each one stands at a turning point.
Touzi let him go too late, and pulled him in too fast. If at that time this monk had known how to turn
his body and breathe out, would he not have made himself into a fellow with a mouth like a basin of blood? Patch-robed monks
do it once and do not stop at twice. Since this monk could not throw it back, he was instead
led by Touzi with his nostrils pierced. The verse says:

lg.l:
Touzi, Touzi, the wheel of function has no obstruction.Plain as day. Under heaven there is no such stubborn old fellow—he corrupts the sons and daughters of other households.What can be
done about him? Still, there is a bit of something.

Release one and get two, the same there and the same here.Swap out your eyes—where do you see Touzicoming like this?
Take the staff. If he does not come like this, he also takes the staff. The senior monk takes his place—then strike.

Pitiable are the countless people who play with the tide,Letting out one and a half
fellows in the monasteries—letting out these two men, patch-robed monks throughout the land will want to go like this.

in the end they still fall and die within the tide.What a pity. But how can one get out
of this painted circle? A sorrowful person should not speak to a sorrowful person.

Suddenly, living, a hundred streamsThe meditation bench shakes, startling this mountain monk to death; I too retreat three thousand li.
roar as they flow backward.Perilous. Waiting and thinking is futile. This mountain monk dares not open his mouth. That old Touzi—one must twist and break the staff before it will do.

cb:div.p:
Touzi, Touzi, the wheel of function has no obstruction. Touzi would often say: you all always say Tou-
zi is solid and plain. Suddenly he goes three steps down the mountain; if someone asks you, say, what is
Touzi's solid-plain place, how will you respond? The ancients said: where the wheel of function turns,
the author is still deluded. His wheel of function turns rumbling without the least obstruction. Therefore
Xuedou said: release one and get two. Haven't you seen a monk ask: What is Buddha? Tou-
zi said, Buddha. He asked again: What is the Way? Touzi said, the Way. He asked again: What
is Zen? Touzi said, Zen. He asked again: when the moon is not yet full, how is it? Tou-
zi said, swallow three and four. After it is full, how is it? Spit out seven
and eight. Touzi received people and always used this device. Answering this monk, it was only a single
word yes. This monk was struck twice, so Xuedou said: the same there and the same here.
In four lines at once he finished versifying Touzi. In the end he versified this monk, saying: pitiable
are the countless people who play with the tide. This monk dared to seize the banner and snatch the drum, saying: Master, do not
take the sound of a boiling bowl. And he also said: can I call the master a donkey? This is exactly
where he plays with the tide. This monk exhausted his tricks, yet still died in Touzi's phrase
as before. Touzi struck immediately. This monk is precisely one who in the end still falls and dies within the tide. Xue-
dou spoke for this monk: if suddenly living, then overturn the Zen bench; Touzi too
would have had to retreat three thousand miles, until a hundred streams roar as they flow backward. Not only would the
Zen bench shake, but mountains and rivers would loom jagged; heaven and earth would suddenly darken. If each and every one
were like this, this mountain monk would beat the retreat drum. You all: where would you go to settle your body
and establish your life? Jagged, looming.Against.Against.

Case 80: A monk asked Zhaozhou: does a newborn child still possess the six consciousnesses or not?
Zhaozhou said: hitting a ball on swift water. The monk again asked Touzi: hitting a ball on swift water, what is the meaningThe moment of a flash of lightning—why talk about a newborn baby?Passed. A swift hawk cannot
catch up. It too must be tested through.
of it? Touzi said: thought after thought flows on without stopping.Experts also test it together. Do you understand? Passed.Beat the vine-and-creeper fellow.

These six consciousnesses are established by the teaching schools as the proper root. Mountains and rivers, the great earth, sun and moon and stars.
Because of how it arises: coming, it serves as the vanguard; going, it serves as the rearguard. The ancients said: the three
realms are only mind; the ten thousand dharmas are only consciousness. If one realizes the Buddha-ground, the eight consciousnesses are transformed into the four
wisdoms. The doctrinal schools call this changing the name without changing the substance. Root, dust, and consciousness are three; the external dust
originally cannot discriminate. The supreme root faculty can give rise to consciousness; consciousness can manifest form and discriminate
distinctions—this is the sixth, the mind-consciousness. The seventh consciousness, the manas consciousness, can grasp and hold
all shadowy things of the world, causing people affliction and preventing freedom and ease. All of this
is the seventh consciousness. As for the eighth consciousness, it is also called the alaya consciousness, and also called
the storehouse consciousness, containing all wholesome and unwholesome seeds. This monk knew the doctrinal meaning,
so he came to ask Zhaozhou: does a newborn child still have the six consciousnesses or not?
A newborn child, though possessing the six consciousnesses—eyes can see, ears can hear—has not yet
discriminated the six sense-objects: liking and disliking, long and short, right and wrong, gain and loss. At that time,
it does not know any of it. A person learning the Way should return to being like an infant: honor and disgrace, merit and fame, adverse
feelings and favorable circumstances cannot move him. Seeing forms with the eyes is the same as being blind; hearing sounds with the ears
is the same as being deaf. Like a fool, like a stump, the mind does not move, like Mount Sumeru. This
is where a patched-robed monk truly gains strength. The ancients said: with the patched robe covering your head, ten thousand
matters cease. At such a time this mountain monk understands nothing at all. If you can be like this, only then is there a small measure of
accord. Even so, you cannot hide even a bit from it. Mountains are still
mountains, waters are still waters. No contrivance, no calculating thought—like the sun and moon
moving through vast space without ever pausing. It does not say, I have so many names and marks.
Like heaven universally covering, like earth universally supporting—because it is without mind, it nurtures and sustains
all beings. It does not say, I have so many merits and practices. Heaven and earth, because they are without mind,
endure for a long time. If there is mind, then there are limits. A person who has attained the Way is likewise
like this: within no-doing, he applies doing. All adverse feelings and favorable circumstances are
embraced with a compassionate heart. When you reach here, the ancients still scolded, saying: when it is utterly clear,
there is nothing to be made clear. In the most mysterious place, you must scold straight through. They also said: in every matter it is open,
in every thing it is bright; the one who has arrived hears this and is startled in the dark. And again: entering sagehood and surpassing the ordinary,
it makes no sound; the crouching dragon is long feared, the green pool is clear. If a person could always be like this,
how could the great earth keep even a single name? Yet even so, you must still leap out of the nest and the den
before it is so. Have you not seen the doctrine say: the bodhisattva of the eighth, Immovable Ground, with no-doing
wisdom, turns the great Dharma wheel within a single speck of dust, and in all times
walking, standing, sitting, and lying down, is not bound by gain and loss, and naturally flows into the ocean of omniscience. In the patched-robed monk’s
house, when you reach here, you must not cling even to this—just be free according to the time. Meet tea,
drink tea; meet rice, eat rice. This matter of going beyond cannot be pinned with the word fixed,
and cannot be pinned with the word not-fixed either. Venerable Shishi Shandao instructed the assembly,
saying: do you not see that when a child comes out of the womb, it never says, I can read doctrine. At
such a time, it does not know the meaning of having buddha-nature, or not having buddha-nature. When it grows
up, it then learns all sorts of knowledge and interpretations, and then says, I can, I understand—without knowing
these are guest-dust afflictions. Among the sixteen contemplative practices, infant conduct is best. Babbling
and cooing are metaphors for a person learning the Way leaving behind the discriminating mind of grasping and rejecting. Therefore one praises
the infant, and takes it as a metaphor. If one claims the infant is the Way, people today mis
understand. Nanquan said, When I was eighteen and up, I knew how to make a living. Zhaozhou said, When I was
eighteen and up, I knew how to break up a household and scatter the home. He also said, I was in the South for twenty years;
aside from the two times of gruel and rice, it was mixed, busy mind. Caoshan asked a monk: in bodhisattva concentration,
hearing a fragrant elephant crossing the river, vividly and clearly—what sutra is it from? The monk said, The Nir
vana Sutra. Caoshan said, Heard before concentration, heard after concentration? The monk said, The master is flowing.
Caoshan said, Catch it below the shoals. Also, the Shurangama Sutra says: the deep enters and merges; the deep enters into the boundary of consciousness.
And the Lankavatara Sutra says: from marks arises grasping obstruction; from thought arise deluded thoughts; the flowing
stream gives rise to becoming, and one follows delusion in turning. Even if you reach the no-doing ground, you are still within the flowing-stream
phase; you must get out of the third phase of flowing-stream becoming before you are at ease and
free. Therefore Weishan asked Yangshan, How is Jizi? Yangshan said, If the mas
ter asks him about understanding, ask him about practice-understanding. If you ask about his practice-understanding, I do not
know. If it is understanding, it is like pouring one bottle of water into one bowl of water. If it is like
this, then he can serve as a teacher in one region. Zhaozhou said, Hitting a ball atop rushing water
already rolls and spins. If you hit it again atop rushing water, in the blink of an eye it has
passed by. It is like what the Shurangama Sutra says: like a swift current, it appears calm when seen from afar. The ancients
said: like a fast-flowing stream, the water’s flow has no fixed stop; each does not know the other. The dharmas
are also like this. In Zhaozhou’s reply, the intent is wholly like this. That monk again asked
Touzi: hitting a ball atop rushing water—what is the meaning? Touzi said, Thought after thought does not
stop flowing. Naturally it fits his question exactly. The ancients’ conduct was seamless and close-knit; their answers
came out as if from a single one, with no need for calculation. The moment you asked him, he already knew where you
would land. The child’s six consciousnesses, though without deliberate doing, still—thought after thought—
do not stop, like hidden water flowing. Touzi answered like this—one could say he deeply discerned the wind that was coming.
Xuedou’s verse says: Swift.Very swift.

lg.l: The six consciousnesses, without deliberate doing, extend a single question;With eyes as if blind, with ears as if deaf. A bright mirror on its stand; a luminous pearl in the palm. One sentence says it all.

adepts once together discerned the source—vast, vast, rushing water,Why bother? Still you must distinguish black-robed from white-robed. Only by realization do you know.
hitting a ball.Beginning to end, one thread runs through—passed. Say what?

Its landing never stops—who can see it?Look, and you pass by blindly.
Receive it below the shoals.

cb:div.p:
The six consciousnesses, without deliberate doing, extend a single question. The ancients learning the Way, nurturing it to here, called it
the doing of no-doing. It is like an infant: though it has eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body, and mind,
it cannot discriminate the six sense-objects—this is no deliberate doing. Once you reach this sort of field,
then you subdue dragons and tame tigers, shed the body sitting, depart standing. People today only need to have the
ten thousand present conditions cease all at once—why must it be only above the eighth ground for it to be so?
Even at the place of no deliberate doing, mountains are still mountains and waters are still waters. Earlier Xuedou
versified: In the living there are eyes, yet it is still like death; why must medicine-taboo use the mirror to assess adepts?
Because Zhaozhou and Touzi were adepts, he said: adepts once together discerned the wind that
came. Vast, vast, rushing water—hitting a ball. Touzi said, thought after thought does not stop flowing. All of you,
do you still know the landing place? In the end Xuedou taught people to set their own eyes and see; thus
he said: its landing never stops—who can see it? This is Xuedou’s living phrase. Tell me,
where does it land?

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Eight, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.End.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Nine, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.

cb:div.p:
Raising a pointer, it says: Snatching flags and seizing drums—no thousand sages can fathom it. Sitting cuts off twisted error; ten thousand
functions cannot reach it. It is not miraculous powers or wondrous uses, nor is it the original body as-it-is. Tell me, relying on
what does one get to be so extraordinary?

Case eighty-one is raised: A monk asked Yaoshan, On flat fields with shallow grass, the deer and the musk-deer gather in herds—how
do you shoot the musk-deer among musk-deer? Yaoshan said, Watch the arrow. The monk let his body go and fell. YaoshanGrab the topknot and throw him to the yamen. Lift the head and come out with horns. Pull the arrow from the back of the head.Rob him right on the spot.
Going downhill and yet not running—such swift decisiveness is hard to meet. Hit.Plainly different. Once dead, he does not live again. A fellow who toys with spirit and soul.
said, Attendant, drag out this dead fellow. The monkAct according to the order; no need to investigate again. The earlier arrow was still light; the later arrow goes deep.
then ran off. Yaoshan said, You mud-ball fellow, whatStaring with eyes wide in the coffin. Getting life in the midst of death—there is still breath in him.
limits have you? Xuedou raised it and said, Three steps and you may live,What a pity to let him off. Act according to the order—frost added atop snow.
five steps and you must die. Musk-deer—stareOne hand lifts, one hand grips. Even if you run a hundred paces, you still lose body and life. Again he says: watch the arrow. Tell me—where does Xuedou's intent land?
In what place? If it is to die together and live together, Yaoshan will only stare, mouth agape—always like a hammer with no hole. What use is it?Main.
blankly.Staring straight ahead with angry eyes.The appearance of an open mouth.

This case, in the Dongshan line, is called borrowing an affair to ask; it is also called a host-discerning question, used
to illuminate what is fit to the moment. A deer and a musk-deer are ordinarily easy to shoot; only the musk-deer among musk-deer is
the king among deer, the hardest to shoot. This musk-deer often sharpens
its horns on cliff rocks, like a keen blade. With its body it protects and cherishes the herd; even tigers cannot
come near. This monk too seemed alert, coming to ask Yaoshan, to illuminate the first
pivot. Yaoshan said, Watch the arrow. A true adept teacher is indeed remarkable—like striking sparks from stone,
like a flash of lightning. Have you not seen how Sanping first visited Shigong? As soon as Shigong saw him
coming, he made the posture of drawing a bow and said, Watch the arrow. Sanping opened his chest and said, Is this
an arrow that kills people, or an arrow that brings people to life? Shigong flicked the bowstring three times, and Sanping bowed.
Gong said: Thirty years. One bow, two arrows. Today I only shot half
a sage, so I snapped the bow and arrows. Later Sanping brought it up to Dadian. Dadian said:
If it is an arrow that brings living people to life, why do you discriminate on the bowstring? Sanping had no words.
Dadian said: Thirty years from now, to find someone who can raise this saying will be hard. Fadeng has
a verse: In old times there was Master Shigong, who sat with bow and arrows set. Thus for thirty years.
Not one true knower. Sanping hit the mark. Father and son met in harmony. Carefully
reflect again: from the start it was shooting at a target. Shigong's methods were just like Yaoshan's.
Sanping had eyes in the crown of his head; under a single phrase he hit it at once. Just like Yaoshan
saying "Watch the arrow"—that monk then made himself the deer and threw his body down. This monk too seemed like a capable hand.
Only he had a head but no tail. Since he set the snare to trap Yaoshan, how could it help that Yaoshan
was a true adept, pressing him on relentlessly. Yaoshan said: Attendant, drag out this dead
fellow. It was like deploying the ranks and advancing. It was fine that the monk ran—fine, it is so.
But he was not cleanly free, sticky-footed and sticky-handed. Therefore Yaoshan said: This mud
ball fellow—what limit does he have? If Yaoshan at the time had no further words, for a thousand ages
he would be picked over by people. Yaoshan said: Watch the arrow. The monk then fell. Now tell me: is this
understanding or not understanding? If you say it is understanding, why did Yaoshan then speak like this?
Mud-ball fellow—this is the worst. It is just like when a monk asked Deshan: A student wields
the Moye sword—what if he intends to take the master's head? Deshan stretched out his neck, leaned forward, and said...
The monk said: The master's head has fallen. Deshan lowered his head and returned to the abbot's room. Later Yantou asked
the monk: Where do you come from? The monk said: From the Western Capital. Yantou said: After Huang Chao passed through,
did you ever retrieve the sword? The monk said: I retrieved it. Yantou stretched out his neck, leaned forward,
and said... The monk said: The master's head has fallen. Yantou laughed loudly. Cases like this
are all mechanisms for trapping tigers, exactly like this. It was precisely because Yaoshan did not bother with him,
only because he saw through it, that he kept pressing him. Xuedou said: This monk in three
steps may live, but in five steps he must die. Though this monk really understood "watch the arrow," he simply threw his body
down. Yaoshan said: Attendant, drag out this dead fellow. The monk then ran. Xuedou said:
I only fear that beyond three steps he would not live. If at that time he had leapt beyond five steps, then under heaven
no one could have done anything to him. When adepts meet, host and guest must from start to finish
exchange roles without a break—only then is there a share of free ease. Since this monk at the time
could not carry it through to the end, he was inspected by Xuedou. Later he also used his
own words. The verse says:

lg.l:
Deer among deer—sir, look closely:Lift your eyes and look high. Lift the head and go off wearing horns.Better to grow a second head and run. If you want to shoot, then shoot. What are you taking it for—
what?

With the next arrow, run three steps—fiveHit. You must know Yaoshan has good hands.Lively and spry—yet only three steps. Dead for a long time.
steps, if you live,What for? Jump a hundred paces. Suddenly, what if there is someone who gets life in the midst of death?

a crowd will chase the tiger.Both shine together. You must
make him retreat, only then. Patch-robed monks throughout the land let him show his head—yet it is only in a grass nest.

The true eye has always been entrusted to the hunter.But Yaoshan
would not accept these words. If Yaoshan is thus, then what about Xuedou? It has nothing to do with Yaoshan, nothing to do with Xuedou, nothing to do with this mountain monk, nothing to do with the head
seat matter.

Xuedou cried out loudly: Watch the arrow!Take it all in one stroke. You still must make him retreat, only then. He strikes and says: I have already plugged up your throat.
Choked up too.

cb:div.p:
Deer among deer—sir, look closely. A patched-robed monk must have the eye of deer among deer.
Have the bearing of deer among deer; have mechanisms and tactics. Even if it is a fierce tiger with wings
or a great beast with horns, it can only preserve its body and keep far from harm. This monk at that time
threw himself down and said to himself: I am the deer. With the next arrow, run three steps. Yaoshan
said: Watch the arrow. The monk then fell. Yaoshan said: Attendant, drag out this dead fellow. This
monk running was also very good. But he only managed three steps. If five steps were alive,
a crowd would chase the tiger. Xuedou said: I only fear that in five steps he must die. If at that time
he had leapt beyond five steps and lived, then he could gather a crowd to chase the tiger. That deer
among deer has horns sharp as a spear; the tiger sees it and fears it and flees. The deer is the king among deer,
often leading the herd of deer, chasing the tiger into another mountain. Later Xuedou's verse on Yaoshan
also has a point where he steps forth in the moment: The true eye has always been entrusted to the hunter. If Yaoshan
can shoot the hunter, that monk is like the deer. At that time Xuedou, because he was giving a sermon, raised this
saying and bound it into a single bundle of talk, crying out one line: Watch the arrow! Those sitting and those standing
all at once could not get up.

The instruction says: Only with eyes can one know the thread at the tip of the pole; only an adept can discern the exceptional mechanism beyond rules.
Now tell me, what is this exceptional mechanism of the thread at the tip of the pole? Try
to raise it and see.

Case 82. A monk asked Dalong: When the physical body decays, what is the solid dharma
body? Dalong said: Mountain flowers bloom like brocade; the water is deep and blue like indigo.Split the words into two chunks—fine, split them.
。A holeless flute bumps into a felt clapper board—solid through and through, you cannot split it. A person comes from Chenzhou, yet goes off to Xuzhou.

If you seek this matter in words, it is like swinging a stick to strike the moon—you will get nothing
to do with it. The ancients plainly said: If you want intimacy, do not take a question to ask. Why?
Because the question is in the answer, and the answer is in the question. This monk carried a load of crudity, and exchanged it
for a load of muddle, bringing forth a questioning point, with no small flaws. If it were not Dalong,
how could it cover heaven and cover earth? He asked like that; Dalong answered like that. In one fitting
of forms, not shifting a hair's breadth. Like seeing a rabbit and releasing the hawk, seeing the hole
and driving the wedge. In the three vehicles and the twelvefold teachings, is there still such a moment? It is not
that it cannot be wondrous—only the words are tasteless, blocking people's mouths. Therefore it is said: A
piece of white cloud lies across the valley mouth; how many returning birds at night lose their nests. Some say it is just
an offhand answer. If you understand it that way, you are all fellows who wipe out the barbarian tribes.
You do not know that the ancients, with one device and one situation, knocked the cangue and struck the lock—one phrase, one word,
pure gold and uncut jade. If you have the eyes and brains of a patched-robed monk, sometimes you seize, sometimes you release;
illumination and function are simultaneous; person and environment are both taken away; both released and both gathered; at the moment you adapt
with ease. Without great function and great mechanism, how could one understand such a net over heaven and canopy over earth? It is like
a bright mirror on its stand: barbarians come, barbarians appear; Chinese come, Chinese appear. This case is like the talk of flowers
and the medicine railing, yet the intent is different. The place where this monk asked was unclear; Dalong's
answering was just right. Do you not see a monk asking Yunmen: When the trees wither and the leaves fall, how
is it? Yunmen said: The body reveals the golden wind. This is called arrow points bracing each other. This monk asked
Dalong: When the physical body decays, what is the solid dharma body? Dalong said: Mountain
flowers bloom like brocade; the water is deep and blue like indigo. Just like you go to Western Qin and I to
Eastern Lu. Since he goes that way, I do not go that way. Compared to him, Yunmen
turns it back twice over. That one who goes that way is easy to see; this one that does not
go that way is hard to see. Dalong's three inches are extremely tight. Xuedou's verse
says:

lg.l:
Asked—he never knew; answered—he still did not understand.Not distinguishing east from west, handling things without knowing their names—buying a head for appearances.Not distinguishing south from north. Trade
away the skull. South of the river, north of the river—
The moon is cold, the wind is high.how could it compare to living? Today is exactly this season: people under heaven have eyes yet have never seen, have ears yet have never heard.

On an ancient cliff, cold cypress—laughable, meeting a realized person on the road,Better when it does not rain. A holeless flute bumps into a felt clapper board.
You must personally arrive here for it to work. Give me back my staff. In crowds and squads you come like this.

not responding with speech or silence.In what place do you see Great
Dragon? What will you bring to face him?

Hand holding a white jade whip, he smashes all the black pearls.
From one to seven, snapped and broken.
Leave it for later people to see—what a pity.

If you do not smash them, flaws increase.Let one move pass, and again it goes like this.What are you doing fiddling with a mud ball?
The more you turn, the more sloppy it gets; the offense fills the heavens.

A nation has three thousand statutes.Those who know the dharma are afraid. In the morning strike three thousand, in the evening strike eight hundred.
Guilty.You only speak half of it. For eighty-four thousand immeasurable kalpas, falling into the Avici karma—you still have not returned even half of it.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou's verse is the most skillful. Earlier, when he versified Yunmen's saying, he said: The question
has a lineage; the answer accords with it. But this one is not like that. Here he says: Asked—he never
knew; answered—he still did not understand. Dalong's answer is a sideways glance—truly wondrous. Clearly
who is it that asks like this? Before he even asked, he had already taken in the flaw. As for his
answering, it can bow down and be just right, responding appropriately: Mountain flowers bloom like brocade.
The water is deep and blue like indigo. All of you now, how will you understand Dalong's intent? The answer
is a sideways glance—truly wondrous. Therefore Xuedou brought forth this verse to let people know:
The moon is cold, the wind is high—further colliding with ancient cliff and cold cypress. Now tell me, how is his intent
to be understood? Thus it was just said: A holeless flute runs into a felt clapper board. It is only
these four lines of verse. Xuedou also feared people would make doctrine of it, so he said: Laughable—
meeting a realized person on the road, not responding with speech or silence. This matter is not a matter of seeing
Hearing, awareness, knowing—none of it is thinking or discriminating. Therefore it is said: plainly, it has no admixture.
When it moves alone, what can it rely on? Meeting a realized person on the road, you do not answer with either speech or silence. This is
what Xiangyan praised, which Xuedou quoted. Haven’t you seen a monk ask Zhaozhou: You do not answer with either speech
or silence—so what will you answer with? Zhaozhou said: Present a lacquer vessel. This is the same as
what was just said: it does not fall into your emotional dust and mental imagining. Like what? Holding a white
jade whip, you smash the black pearl to bits. Thus the ancestral command is to be carried out: in the ten directions, sit and cut off.
This is business on the sword’s edge. You must have such a tactic; if not,
you wholly betray all the sages of old. When you reach here, there must not be the slightest thing; then there is
a good place. This is precisely where a person of the Way walks. If you do not smash it, you will surely add
blemishes and flaws, and then leaks will show. Ultimately, how can it be right? A state has statutes
with three thousand articles of crime; the five punishments have three thousand categories—none is greater than unfiliality. The statute
is the law; the chapter is the article. Even if you violate all three thousand crimes at once—why is it so
? Only because you do not meet people with the fundamental matter. If it were a great dragon, it would not be like
this.

Case 83. Raised: Yunmen addressed the assembly, saying: An ancient Buddha communes with a pillar—this is the
what numbered pivot? He answered for himself:Three thousand miles away, it has nothing to do with it. Shattered into seven flowers and eight splits.The east neighbor dies; the west neighbor helps mourn. A single united form cannot be obtained.
South Mountain raises clouds; North Mountain sends down rain.Heaven and earth cannot be seen; a knife chop cannot enter.Not a drop is applied—half Henan, half Hebei.

Great Master Yunmen produced more than eighty good spiritual guides. More than seventy
years after his passing, they opened the stupa and saw him—solemn as before. His ground of seeing was clear; his pivots and situations
were swift. In general, his pointing phrases, separate phrases, and substitute phrases were directly solitary and steep. This very
public case is like striking stone for sparks, like a flash of lightning—truly appearing and vanishing like spirits and ghosts. Qingzang
the abbot said: Does the whole canon even have talk like this? Nowadays people mostly
make their living in emotional interpretations, saying the Buddha is the guide of the three realms, the compassionate
father of the four kinds of birth. If he is an ancient Buddha, why then does he commune with a pillar? If you understand it
like this, in the end you will not be able to find it. Some call it singing out of nothingness. But they do not
know that the words of the masters cut off consciousness and cut off emotional measuring; cut off birth and death and cut off dharma
dust. Entering the proper position, not a single thing is retained. The moment you start reasoning and calculating, you
bind your feet and hands. Tell me: what was the intent of the ancients? They only made mind and
environment one suchness; likes and dislikes, right and wrong—none can shake it. Then saying there is is fine;
saying there isn’t is fine; having pivots is fine; having no pivots is fine. Here, a slap is
the command. The ancestral master Wuzu said: Big and small, Yunmen was originally timid. If it were this
mountain monk, I would just tell him: the eighth pivot. He said: An ancient Buddha communes with a pillar—
what numbered pivot is it? For the moment, wrap it up right before your eyes. A monk asked: I wonder
what is the purport? Yunmen said: One cord, bought for thirty coins. He has an eye that settles heaven
and earth. Since no one understood, later he answered for himself: South Mountain raises clouds; North
Mountain sends down rain—just to give later students a way in. Therefore Xuedou only picks up
his place of settling heaven and earth to let people see. If you so much as fall into calculation and show a bit of sharpness,
you miss it right in front of your face. You only need to trace Yunmen’s central aim and understand his steep pivot.
Therefore the verse says:

lg.l:
South Mountain clouds, North Mountain rain—four seven,Heaven and earth cannot be seen; a knife chop cannot enter.Not a drop is applied—half Henan, half Hebei.
two three, faces meeting face to face;In several places you search and cannot find it, implicating those beside you. A dew pillar hangs a lantern.

In Silla it once
ascended the hall;It surges in the east and sinks in the west. Going east you do not see the benefit of going west. Where do you get this news?

In Great Tang the drum has not yet been struck.
Delay one moment—give me back my topic. The first move does not arrive; the last is too much.

Joy within bitterness, bitterness within joy—Who are you going to make know?A double-layered case—make
who raise it? Bitter is bitter, joyful is joyful—where do two heads and three faces come from?

Who says gold is like dirt and dung?Those with eyes discern it. Try
to brush it and test it. A-la-la! What a pity. Now tell me—is it an ancient Buddha or a dew pillar?

cb:div.p:
South Mountain clouds, North Mountain rain—Xuedou buys a hat to match the head, watching the wind and setting the sails. On
the sword’s edge he gives you a footnote, straight to four seven, two three, faces meeting
face to face. Don’t misunderstand: this only praises that an ancient Buddha communes with a pillar—what numbered
pivot it is. After that he splits open a road; he tangles vines to make you see the intent. In
Silla it once ascended the hall; in Great Tang the drum has not yet been struck. Xuedou, at
the place where lightning turns and stars fly, then says: Joy within bitterness, bitterness within joy. Xuedou seems to heap
up a pile of seven treasures and eight jewels right here. Therefore at the end there is this line:
Who says gold is like dirt and dung? This line comes from Chan Yue’s poem The Road is Hard
and Xuedou borrows it. Chan Yue said: Mountains are high, seas are deep—people cannot be fathomed.
From ancient times to now they turn blue-green. Don’t associate with the shallow and flighty. Low ground only
knows how to grow thorns. Who says gold is like dirt and dung? Zhang Er and Chen Yu cut off and
ended the news. The road is hard, the road is hard—see for yourself. Also, don’t say the land is vast and people are few. Yun
Ju, the arhat.

The pointing instruction says: Saying right is right—there is nothing that is not right; saying wrong is wrong—there is nothing that is not wrong. Right and wrong
have already gone; gain and loss are both forgotten. Clean and naked, bare and bright. Tell me: what is
in front and what is behind? Perhaps a patched monk comes forward and says: In front is the Buddha
hall and the three gates; behind is the sleeping hall and the abbot’s quarters. Tell me: does this person have eyes
or not? If you can discern this person, I will allow you to personally meet the ancients.

Case 84. Raised: Vimalakirti asked Manjushri, whatThis fellow is far too much—he should make a ruckus once. He should shut his mouth.
is a bodhisattva’s entry into the Dharma gate of nonduality? Manjushri said: As I see it, toward all dharmas there is no speech, noKnowing it and deliberately offending.Say what
? It comes to the point you cannot sort it out. Carry the cangue and confession; tie up your hair and surrender at the yamen.What do you call all dharmas?
speaking, no pointing, no knowing; apart from all question and answer—this isSay what?If you fool others, it works.Say what?
entry into the Dharma gate of nonduality. Then ManjushriWhat use is it to bring it in? What use is all these entangling vines?
asked Vimalakirti: We have each spoken; you, sir, should speak—what
is a bodhisattva’s entry into the Dharma gate of nonduality? Xuedou said: What did Vimalakirti say? And again: It has been seen through.With this one leaning, do not say even the Tathagata Golden Millet—suppose all Buddhas of the three times—none can open their mouths.
Turning the spearhead around. Stab and kill one person—being hit by an arrow is still like when you shot someone.Bah! Ten thousand
arrows crowd the heart—explaining doctrine for him.Not only then—right now it is also like this. Xuedou too is like drawing a bow after the thief has passed.
Though he exhausts himself for the assembly, disaster comes out of a private gate. Tell me, has Xuedou ever seen where it lands? He has not even dreamed it—what talk of seeing through? Perilous! Golden-maned
even a lion cannot grope it out.

Vimalakirti had the great bodhisattvas each speak of the Dharma gate of nonduality. At the time, thirty-
two bodhisattvas all used dual views—conditioned and unconditioned, true and conventional, the two truths—combining them into
one view, as the Dharma gate of nonduality. Then he asked Manjushri. Manjushri said: As I see it,
toward all dharmas: no speech, no speaking; no pointing, no knowing; apart from all
question and answer—this is entry into the Dharma gate of nonduality. The thirty-two people used words to drive out
words; Manjushri used wordlessness to drive out words—sweeping everything away at once, wanting none of it. This is
entry into the Dharma gate of nonduality. Yet he did not know: the spirit tortoise drags its tail; brushing away traces makes a mark. Also
it is like sweeping away dust: though the dust is gone, the traces remain. In the end, in
accordance with what came before, remove even the tracks. Thus Manjushri again asked Vimalakirti: We each
have spoken; you, sir, should speak yourself—what is a bodhisattva’s entry into the Dharma gate of
nonduality? Vimalakirti remained silent. If you are a living person, you will never go soak in dead water.
If you make such an interpretation, it is like a mad dog chasing a clod. Xuedou also does not say
after a long time; he also does not say, silently sitting there. He only goes to the urgent spot and says: Vimalakirti
said what? Just as Xuedou says it like this—have you seen Vimalakirti? Not even in a
dream have you seen him. Vimalakirti is an ancient Buddha of the past, and also has a retinue, assisting
the Buddha in proclaiming the teaching. He possesses inconceivable eloquence and inconceivable states.
He has inconceivable spiritual powers and wondrous functions. In the abbot’s room, he accommodated thirty thousand
two thousand lion-thrones and eighty thousand in the great assembly—yet it was neither cramped nor spacious. Tell me:
what principle is this? Can you call it spiritual powers and wondrous function? Don’t misunderstand.
If it is the Dharma gate of nonduality, then only by attaining together and realizing together can you mutually confirm and know it.
Only Manjushri can respond in kind. Even so, can he avoid Xuedou’s
scrutiny and blame or not? When Xuedou speaks like this, he also wants you to meet these two people
face to face, saying: What did Vimalakirti say? And again: It has been seen through. Tell me: what
place is the place of seeing through? Just this much: not bound by gain and loss, not falling into right
and wrong—like a sheer cliff of ten thousand fathoms. Upward, you must be able to cast away your life and leap across; then I
permit you to personally meet Vimalakirti. If you cannot let go, it is like a flock of sheep butting into a fence. Xuedou
indeed is a person who can cast away his life. Therefore he versifies:

lg.l:
Bah! This old Vimalakirti—so pitiful,Why scold him? In the morning strike three thousand, in the evening strike eight hundred—scolding is useless. Better give him thirty blows.
troubled by empty notions,Why pity him? There is already the Diamond King precious sword. For his idle affairs, ignorance grows long—labor with no achievement.

lying sick,
VaiśālīWho brought it about? It drags down everyone.

His whole body is utterly withered.Set the illness aside for now—why is the mouth like a shoulder pole?
You cannot even eat, and you cannot even catch your breath.

The patriarch of the Seven Buddhas arrives.When guests come, you must watch; when thieves come, you must strike. Forming crowds and squads—you must also be an adept.
Only then will it do.

In a single room, swept again and again—pray ask about the gate of nonduality.This is still here. It turns out he has been making a living in a ghost cave.
If there is anything that can be spoken, he has already spoken it. Striking, he says: And, acarya—still you cannot find it.
Right then he collapsed.Blue heaven, blue heaven—say what?

If he does not collapse, there is nowhere to seek the golden-maned lion.Gaining life within death—there is still breath in it.Bah! Do you still see?
Blue heaven, blue heaven!

cb:div.p:
Xuedou said, Bah, this old Vimalakīrti—why throw out a Bah right at the start?
With the vajra-king jeweled sword, cut straight down on the head; you must strike three thousand in the morning and strike
eight hundred in the evening before it will do. In Sanskrit he is called Vimalakīrti; here it means Spotless Renown; also called
Pure Name. He is the past Golden-Grain Tathāgata. Did you not see a monk ask Yunju Jian,
the master, Since he is the Golden-Grain Tathāgata, why does he nonetheless, in Śākyamuni Tathāgata’s
assembly, listen to the Dharma? Jian said, He does not contend over self and others. A person of great liberation is not bound by
becoming a Buddha or not becoming a Buddha. If you say his practice aims at attaining Buddhahood, that only makes it irrelevant.
For example, the Perfect Enlightenment Sutra says: with a mind of rebirth, you give rise to views of rebirth; entering
the Tathāgata’s great sea of quiescent extinction, in the end you cannot arrive. Yongjia said: whether it is or whether it is not,
people do not recognize it. Inverting the course or going with it—heaven cannot fathom it. If you go with the course, you head toward the Buddha
fruit and its rank; if you invert the course, you enter the realm of sentient beings. Chan Master Shou said, Even if
you grind and temper yourself until you reach this ground, it still will not follow what you have in mind. Only
once you realize the stainless, holy body may you invert or go with the course. Therefore Xuedou said,
Compassion arises from emptiness, and affliction. The Vimalakīrti Sutra says: because sentient beings are sick, I
too am sick. When there is affliction, compassion is cut off. Lying ill in Vaiśālī—Vimalakīrti displayed
illness in the city of Vaiśālī. In the Tang, the envoy Wang Xuance passed by his
dwelling and measured the room lengthwise and crosswise with a hand-board, finding it ten cubits; thus it was named the abbot’s quarters.
His whole body was utterly withered. Because of bodily illness, he broadly preached the Dharma, saying: this body is
impermanent, without strength, without power, without firmness; a law of quick decay, not to be trusted. It is suffering,
it is vexation, a gathering of many illnesses, even to the point that it is formed from the aggregates, realms, and sense-fields combined. The Seven
Buddhas’ patriarch arrives. Mañjuśrī is the patriarch of the Seven Buddhas; receiving the World-Honored One’s command, he went there
