to inquire after the illness. In a single room, swept again and again: inside the abbot’s quarters, everything was cleared away, leaving only
one couch, waiting for Mañjuśrī to arrive—so as to ask about the Dharma gate of nonduality. Therefore Xuedou
said, Pray ask about the gate of nonduality. Right then he collapsed. Vimalakīrti’s mouth was like a flat eave.
Nowadays the Chan crowd then says, Having no words is collapsing. Do not mistake it and fix it as
the Pole Star. Xuedou pressed them to the edge of a ten-thousand-fathom cliff, then said, He does not collapse. With one
hand he lifts, with one hand he grips. He has such hands and feet—truly he uses them with perfect finesse.
Before this verse he brought it up, saying, What did Vimalakīrti say? There is nowhere
to seek the golden-maned lion. Not only then—right now it is also so. Do you still see the old Vimalakīrti? The whole
mountains and rivers, the great earth, grasses and forests, all transform into golden-maned lions—yet you still cannot
lay hold of it.

The instruction said: Grasp the world firmly and do not let a single hair leak; people throughout the whole earth lose their edge
and tie up their tongues—this is the patch-robed monk’s true command. Light shines from the crown of the head, illuminating the four worlds—this is
the patch-robed monk’s vajra eyes. Touch iron and it becomes gold; touch gold and it becomes iron. Now seize, now
let go—this is the patch-robed monk’s staff. Sitting, he cuts off the tongues of all under heaven, until there is no
place to breathe out; retreating three thousand miles—this is the patch-robed monk’s bearing. Tell me, when it is not
like this at all, what sort of person is it, after all? Try to bring it up and see.

Case 85. A monk went to Master Tongfeng of the hermitage and asked, When you suddenly meet a great beast here,
what then? The hermitage master immediately made a tiger’s roar.Shadow-playing fellows of the adepts—one or half a one in a grass nest.
The monk at once struck a frightened pose. The hermitage master laughed loudly. The monk said, You old bandit! The hermitage master said, WhatTake error as error—yet there are teeth and claws. Live together, die together. If you take up words, you must understand the lineage.Two mud-ball fellows—seeing the opportunity and
acting. It seems like it, indeed it seems; if you say it is, it is not.Still a bit off. In the smile there is a knife. It can also release and it can also
gather in.You must also see through it. Ruined. Let both go through.
can you do about this old monk? The monk left. Xuedou said, Yes, they are two evil bandits, who only know how to coverA slap right across the ear—what a pity to let it pass. Frost on top of snow, one more layer.Let it rest like this. The two
both do not finish it. Blue heaven, blue heaven!
their ears and steal the bell.The words are still in the ear, yet he is inspected by Xuedou. Tell me, what should have been done then to avoid inspection? Patch-robed monks of the world cannot reach it—

In the Great Hero’s lineage there appeared four hermitage masters: Damei, Baiyun, Huxi, and Tongfeng.
Watch how those two, with their own eyes and hands, made the distinctions. Tell me, where is the error
located? The ancients—one device, one situation; one word, one phrase—though it emerged on the spot,
if your eyes are proper, it is naturally lively and nimble. Xuedou picked this up to teach people
to recognize crooked and straight and to distinguish gain and loss. Even so, for one who has arrived, even though
he deals in gain and loss, there is no gain and loss. If you look at the ancients through gain-and-loss, then
it has nothing to do with it. People today must each press through to the place of no gain and loss, and then
use gain and loss to assess others. If you always apply your mind to picking and choosing in phrases and sentences,
when will you ever finish? Did you not see Great Master Yunmen say: Pilgrims
do not merely wander idly through prefectures and counties. You only want to grab hold of idle talk, waiting for the old
master’s mouth to move, then you ask about Chan and ask about the Way—upward, downward, how this and
how that. You copy off big volumes and take them away, divining with them in your belly. Everywhere, by the furnace
you gather in threes and fives, heads together, mouths going, mumbling and saying, This one is
words of public talent; this one is words struck out from the body; this one is words spoken on the matter;
this one is words of principle; this one is words from within the essence. Essence—your old man and old woman at home have eaten
their meal; you just keep talking in dreams, then say, I understand the Buddha-Dharma now. You should know
that traveling like this, you will rest only in the year of the donkey. The ancients would toy with it for a moment—
how could there be views of victory and defeat, gain and loss, right and wrong? Tongfeng met Linji; at that time
he was in the deep mountains at a hermitage. This monk went there and asked, When you suddenly meet a
great beast here, what then? Feng made a tiger’s roar. Fine—he acted right on the matter.
This monk also knew how to turn mistake into mistake, and struck a frightened pose. The hermitage master laughed loudly.
The monk said, You old bandit! Feng said, What can you do about this old monk? Yes, yes—
neither of the two understood. For a thousand ages they have been inspected by others. Therefore Xuedou said,
Yes, they are two evil bandits, who only know how to cover their ears and steal the bell. Though those two
are both bandits, when the moment came they did not use it; thus, covering the ears and stealing the bell. These two old fellows
are like deploying an army of a million—yet they only fought with broom and dustpan. If you speak of this matter, you must have
hands and feet that kill without blinking. If you only let go and never seize, if you only
kill and never give life, you will inevitably be mocked. Even so, those ancients
had no such excess. Look at those two as they are—altogether it is acting upon the opportunity,
doing. The Fifth Patriarch said: the samādhi of supernormal play; the samādhi of the torch of wisdom; the
samādhi of the Adornment King. It is simply that later people’s heels do not touch the ground; they only go inspecting the ancients
and say, There is gain, there is loss. Some say, Clearly the hermitage master fell into a cadence. Even so,
it has nothing to do with it. Xuedou said, When those two met, each had a place of letting go.
The monk said, When you suddenly meet a great beast here, what then? Feng then made
a tiger’s roar—this is precisely the letting-go. And then, What can you do about this old monk? This
too is letting-go. Step after step, they fell into the second device. Xuedou said, If you want
to use it, then use it. People today hear this and say, At the time it would have been good to carry out
the command. Do not be a blind cangue and a blind stick. Consider Deshan—enter the gate and he strikes; Linji—
enter the gate and he shouts. Tell me, what was the ancients’ intent? After this, Xuedou then just
put forth the verse like this. Tell me, after all, how do you avoid covering your ears and stealing the bell
and leaving it at that? The verse says:

lg.l: Seeing it, do not take it; thinking of it is a thousand miles.they have missed it already—already a thousand miles, ten thousand miles.Regret not being careful at the start. Blue heaven, blue heaven!

What a fine mottled one—yet its claws and fangs are not fully formed.The acarya carried it out himself, but he still did not understand how to use it.I only fear the place of use is unclear. Wait
until claws and fangs are ready, then I will tell you.
Do you not see? Beneath Great Hero Mountain they suddenly meet,If there is a vine, cling to the vine; if there is no vine, cling to the rule.

clear and bold—sound and light both shake the earth. Great man!This great beast goes off like this—still a bit off. How many men are true men?
The fellow sees nothing at all.Old-woman mind is urgent. If you can open your eyes and live together and die together—Xuedou tangles vines

Gather up the tiger’s tail, and pluck
the tiger’s whiskers.and suddenly breaks out—how will you gather it in? Gather all patch-robed monks right here. If one suddenly comes out, give him a squeeze. If you have no gathering and releasing, you get thirty blows, making you turn
your body and spit out breath. Shouting and striking, he says: Why not call this old thief

cb:div.p:
Seeing it, do not take it; thinking of it is a thousand miles. Right at the perilous place, you cannot make use of it at all. Wait until
he says, What can you do about this old monk?—then give him his proper fodder. If at that time you could have put down
these hands and feet, he would surely have had a follow-up. The two only know how to let go, not how to
gather in. Seeing it, do not take it, already it is white clouds for ten thousand miles—what more is there to say about thinking of it
A thousand li. What a fine dappled one—its claws and fangs not yet fully formed. It is, to be sure, a great beast, yet
it knows how to hide its fangs and tuck its claws; but it cannot bite people. Have you not seen? Below Great Hero Mountain,
they suddenly met—clear and ringing, the sound and brilliance shook the earth. One day Baizhang asked Huang-
bo: Where have you come from? Huangbo said: From below the mountain, gathering mushrooms. Baizhang said:
Did you see the great beast? Huangbo then made a tiger’s roar. Baizhang drew an axe from his waist and made
a chopping gesture. Huangbo grabbed him and slapped him. That evening Baizhang ascended the hall and said: Below Great Hero Mountain
there is a tiger. All of you, when going in and out, must watch carefully. Today this old monk
personally took a bite. Later Weishan asked Yangshan: How about Huangbo’s tiger talk—
how was it? Yangshan said: What is the master’s honored intent? Weishan said: Baizhang at the time should have
chopped him dead with one axe. Why did it come to this? Yangshan said: Not so. Weishan
said: Then what would you do? Yangshan said: Not only would I ride the tiger’s head, I would also know how to gather
the tiger’s tail. Weishan said: Jizi has a phrase from a sheer cliff. Xuedou cites this
to illuminate the earlier case: the sound and brilliance, clear and ringing, shook the earth. This little bit
turns and changes with ease, wanting a way out within the phrase. Does a true man see it
or not? Do you see it? Gathering the tiger’s tail, stroking the tiger’s whiskers—this too must be fundamental.
Even if you gather the tiger’s tail and stroke the tiger’s whiskers, you still cannot avoid, for a moment, having your nose pierced through.

The pointer said: Hold the world firm without leaking a hair; cut off all streams without leaving
a single drop. Open your mouth and you are wrong; think it over and you miss. Tell me, what is the eye that passes
through the barrier? Try to say it.

Case 86. Yunmen held up a saying: Everyone has their own brightness; when you look,a black lacquer bucket?
you do not see it—dark and murky. What is all of your brightness? He answered for himself: The kitchen and storeroom, the three gates. He alsoLook at it and you go blind.Mountain is mountain, water
is water—washing black sap in a lacquer bucket.Old-woman mind is urgent—what are you doing tangling vines?
said: Better to have no good thing.You yourself know it is off by half—still a bit off.

In Yunmen’s room he held up sayings to receive people. Under the heels of all of you, each and every one
has a stretch of brightness, blazing through past and present, far beyond seeing and knowing. Though there is brightness,
when you are asked, you do not understand—how is that not dark and murky? For twenty years
he held it up, and no one understood his intent. Later Xianglin asked for a substitute phrase. Yunmen said:
The kitchen and storeroom, the three gates. And he said: Better to have no good thing. Usually a substitute phrase is just one
line—why are there two lines here? The first line slightly opens
a single thread of a path so you can see. If you are a real fellow, hearing it raised, you pick it up and
go. He feared people would get stuck here, so he added: Better to have no good thing, and as before
swept it away for you. People today, as soon as they hear brightness raised, stare their eyes wide and say:
Where is the kitchen and storeroom? Where are the three gates? That is entirely irrelevant. Therefore
it is said: Recognize the meaning at the hook’s tip; do not take the fixed star as certain. This matter is not in the eyes.
Nor is it in objects. You must cut off views and forget gain and loss—clean, naked,
bare and bright. Each person must investigate it on their own share. Yunmen said: In daylight
you come and go, in daylight you recognize people. Suddenly at midnight there is no sun, moon, or lamplight—of the places you have
been to, of course they are so; but from a place you have never been to, take one thing—can you take it? The Cantongqi says:
Within brightness there is darkness; do not take darkness as what you see. Within darkness there is
brightness; do not meet it by means of brightness. If you cut off brightness and darkness, tell me, what is it?
Therefore it is said: the mind-flower blossoms in illumination, shining on the lands of the ten directions. Panshan said: Light is not what
illuminates objects; objects are not what remain. When light and objects are both forgotten, what is it then? He also said: This
seeing and hearing are not seeing and hearing; there are no remaining sounds or colors to present to you. If within this you understand,
there is nothing at all. Essence and function—what harm is there in dividing or not dividing? Only grasp the very last line,
then go back and play in the earlier parts; in the end you are not making your living inside them. The ancients
said: Taking non-abiding as the root, one establishes all dharmas. You must not go here and toy with light
and shadow, toy with spirit. Nor must you take it as an understanding of nothing-to-do. The ancients said: Better to raise
a view of being as big as Mount Sumeru than to raise a view of non-being as small as a mustard seed. People of the two vehicles
people often fall one-sidedly into this view. Xuedou's verse says:

lg.l:
Self-illumination sets out a solitary brightness, opening oneThe myriad phenomena: host and guest interpenetrate. Nostrils turn in a row. What is a blind fellow doing?
thread for you.More than a single thread: ten suns shine together. Release one thread of the way and it is obtained.

The flowers fall; the trees have no shadow.What end is there to tangling vines? Toward what
place are you groping? A black lacquer bucket holds black sap.

When you look, who does not see? See, notBlind. You must not always cling to the fence and feel the wall—two blind, three blind.
see.Both ends are cut off and sat dead. Blind

Riding the ox backward, enter the Buddha hall.within blindness. At the three gates, palms together. Give me back my topic. Strike
and say: Where are you going? Xuedou too only makes a living in a ghost cave—do you understand? At midnight the sun comes out; at noon, you strike the third watch.

cb:div.p:
Self-illumination sets out a solitary brightness. Beneath your own heels, there has always been this stretch of brightness.
It is only that in ordinary use it becomes dark. Therefore Master Yunmen set out
this brightness right in front of you. Now what is all of your brightness? The
kitchen and storeroom, the three gates—this is where Yunmen sets out the solitary brightness. Panshan said: The mind-moon,
solitary and round, its light swallows ten thousand forms. This is true constancy uniquely revealed. Then it opens
one thread for you. He also feared people would stick to the kitchen and storeroom, the three gates. So the kitchen and storeroom,
the three gates—set them aside for now. Morning flowers fall and trees have no shadow; the sun sets and the moon
grows dark. All of heaven and earth, the great land, is pitch-black. Do you still see? When you look,
who does not see? Tell me, who is it that does not see? When you get here, within brightness there is
darkness; within darkness there is brightness—each can be seen by the earlier and later steps. Xuedou said: See,
not see—he chanted Better to have no good thing. It should be seen, yet not seen; it should be bright, yet not
bright. Riding the ox backward, enter the Buddha hall—go into a black lacquer bucket. You must
yourself ride the ox into the Buddha hall and see what kind of principle it is.

The pointer said: A clear-eyed fellow has no ruts. Sometimes, atop a lone peak, grass is wild
and sprawling; sometimes, in the busy marketplace, bare and bright. Suddenly like wrathful Nata, manifesting
three heads and six arms; suddenly like Sun-Face and Moon-Face, releasing universally embracing compassionate light. Within a single
speck of dust, manifest all bodies. To suit the type of person, mixing mud and blending water. Suddenly, if it is tapped
into the upward aperture, even the Buddha-eye cannot spy it. Even if a thousand sages came forth,
they would have to retreat three thousand li. Is there anyone who attains the same and verifies the same? Try raising it
and see.

Case 87. Yunmen instructed the assembly, saying: Medicine and illness cure each other; throughout the great earthA single united form cannot be obtained.
is medicine—what is the selfBitter gourd, bitter to the root—set it off to one side.Sweet melon, sweet through the stem—where do you get this news?
that is cured?Cheng's note: qie. It means to attack the principle. Pronounced chi.

Yunmen said: Medicine and illness cure each other; throughout the great earth is medicine—what is the self?
Do you people still have a way out? In the twelve hours of day and night, you will surely be like a sheer wall a thousand
fathoms high. Deshan’s staff is like raindrops; Linji’s shout is like rushing thunder—set those aside. Shakya is
Shakya; Maitreya is Maitreya. Those who do not know where it lands often
take it as an understanding of medicine and illness fitting together. For forty-nine years, the World-Honored One, in more than three hundred
assemblies, set up teachings in response to capacities—all are medicine given in response to illness, like trading honeyed fruit for a bitter
gourd. Having washed out the roots of your karmic habits, making you open and unbound, all
the great earth is medicine—where will you stick in your mouth? If you can stick in your mouth, then I allow
you a place to turn around and breathe out; then you personally see Yunmen. If you look back and hesitate,
you will surely be unable to stick in your mouth. Yunmen is right beneath your heels. Medicine and illness curing each other
is also just ordinary talk. If you cling to being, I tell you non-being. If
you cling to non-being, I tell you being. If you cling to neither being nor non-being, I take you to
a dung-and-sweepings heap and manifest a sixteen-foot golden body—head appearing, head disappearing. Right now, all
the great earth’s dense array of myriad forms, up to and including yourself, are at once medicine. At such a
time, what do you call the self? If you only keep calling it medicine, even if Maitreya Buddha descends,
you still have not even dreamed of Yunmen being here. Ultimately, how is it? Recognize the meaning at the hook’s tip;
do not take the fixed star as certain. One day Manjushri told Sudhana to gather medicine, saying:
Gather what is not medicine and bring it back. Sudhana gathered everywhere—there was nothing that was not medicine. He came back
and reported: There is nothing that is not medicine. Manjushri said: Gather what is medicine and bring it back. Sudhana
then picked up a blade of grass and handed it to Manjushri. Manjushri lifted it up and showed the assembly, saying:
This medicine can also kill people; it can also bring people to life. This talk of medicine and illness curing each other is the most
difficult to see. Yunmen ordinarily used it to receive people in his room. Elder Jine arrived one
day to visit Xuedou. He was a master hand, a venerable elder under Linji’s line, and with Xue-
Xuedou discussed this talk of medicine and illness curing each other all night until daybreak, and only then could it be fully perfected.
When you reach here, learned understanding, thinking, and calculating are of no use at all. Later Xuedou
sent him off with a verse, saying: To see medicine and illness curing each other is hardest; ten thousand layered barriers and locks are far too
boundless. The golden-goose man of the Way comes to visit; in one night the waves of the sea of learning dry up. Xuedou
in the later part of his verse shows the most workmanship. His intent is in the guest and also in the host; it can
be seen for yourself. The verse says:

lg.l:
The whole great earth is medicine, how terribly wrong from ancient times to now.Who can discern it? Scattering sand and dirt, setting it up on a high rack.In the words
there is an echo. In a single stroke, under the line—bah!

With the door shut, do not build a cart.Big and small, Xuedou strains himself for the crowd; calamity comes from the private gate. Open and level, not hanging by a single
hair—who has spare time to make a living in a ghost den?

The open road is naturally vast and wide.With one step you enter the weeds; mount the horse and you see the road. Freely,
you pick it up at hand—it is fine to be strange.

Wrong, wrong, even nostrils reaching the sky are pierced through.Twin swords fly leaning on empty space; one arrow brings down two hawks.
The head drops. He struck and said: It has pierced through.

cb:div.p:
The whole great earth is medicine, how terribly wrong from ancient times to now. If you call it an understanding of medicine, then
from of old until now, in one stroke it is all mistaken. Xuedou said: There are fellows who do not know how to cut off
Taimei at the heels, and only keep saying he was too greedy and hurried. He knows how to cut off Yunmen
at the heels, because Yunmen’s one line confuses people under heaven. Yunmen said: The
staff is a wave. I allow you seven lunges and eight crosses. The whole great earth is a wave. Watch you
bobbing up and down. With the door shut, do not build a cart; the open road is naturally vast and wide. Xuedou said:
I have opened a single thread of a road for you. If you build a cart behind closed doors and when you go out it fits the ruts, what
does that accomplish? Here I also shut the door and do not build a cart; when I go out, it is naturally vast
and wide. Here he slightly reveals a seam and gap for people to see, and then he hurriedly
says: Wrong, wrong. The front is also wrong, the back is also wrong. Who would know that when Xuedou opens a
thread of a road, that too is wrong? Since the nostrils reach the sky, why are they also pierced through?
Want to understand? Then investigate for thirty years. If you have a staff, I will give you a
staff. If you have no staff, you cannot avoid having your nostrils pierced through by others.

Pointer: As for the gatehouse arrangements, let it be so. Break two and make three; enter principle and speak deeply,
and you still must have seven piercings and eight holes. Striking and pointing in response to the situation, you smash the dark gate of golden locks.
Acting in accord with the command, you sweep away tracks and erase traces. Tell me, where is the distortion?
Those who have the eye at the crown of the head, please try bringing it up and see.

Case 88. Xuansha instructed the assembly, saying: The old worthies in all quarters all speak of meeting beings and benefiting
life. Suddenly, if three kinds of sick people come, how do you receive them?Spread the mat according to your share; each household with its own plenty or thrift.
If one is blind, you lift the hammer and raise the whisk;Beating the grass only aims to startle the snake. This mountain monk is left staring, mouth agape—surely you will retreat three thousand miles.
but he does not see. If one is deaf, you use the speech samadhi,Truly blind. If so, in meeting beings and benefiting life, it is not that he necessarily does not see.
but he does not hear. If one is mute,Truly deaf. If so, in meeting beings and benefiting life, it is not that he is necessarily deaf—what is it that has not been heard?
you tell him to speak, but he cannot speak. SoTruly mute. If so, in meeting beings and benefiting life, it is not that he is necessarily mute—what is it that has not been spoken?
how do you receive them? If you cannot receive such people, the Buddha-dharma has no efficacy. A monk asked Yunmen for instruction, and Yun-Indeed!
Those words are true. This mountain monk folds his hands and surrenders—already received. Then strike.And you must let all quarters know. Hit.
men said: You, bow. The monk bowed. When the monk rose, Yun-When the wind moves, the grass bends—bah!This monk snaps the staff in two.
men struck him with his staff. The monk stepped back. Yunmen said: You are not blind.
He called him close again. The monk came near. Yunmen said: You are not deaf. Yunmen thenTruly blind. Don’t say this monk’s blindness is good.The second ladle pours foul water. Guanyin arrives
too. At that time, he deserved a shout.Truly deaf. Don’t say this monk’s deafness is good.
said: Do you understand? The monk said: I do not understand. Yunmen said: You are not mute. The monkWhy not give him his proper fodder? At that time, best not to make a sound.A double-layered public case. Blue
heaven, blue heaven!Truly mute. His mouth gapes and babbles—don’t say this monk’s muteness is good.
from this had an insight. Stare. Gape. Strike.Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—what bowl are you begging for?Held fast: glaring eyes, staring straight ahead.Cut away.
The look of opening the mouth.Struck and choked: the sound jams and crashes into emptiness.

Xuansha studied until feelings, dust, and thoughts were cut off, reaching a place utterly naked and completely bare.
Only then could he speak like this. At that time, monasteries in all quarters faced one another; in ordinary instruction to the assembly
he would say: The old worthies in all quarters all speak of meeting beings and benefiting life. Suddenly, if three kinds of sick people
come, how do you receive them? If one is blind, you lift the hammer and raise the whisk, but he does not see.
If one is deaf, you use the speech samadhi, but he does not hear. If one is mute, you tell him to speak,
but he cannot speak. So how do you receive them? If you cannot receive such people, the Buddha-dharma
has no efficacy. If people nowadays take it as blind, deaf, mute understanding, in the end they cannot find it
at all. Therefore it is said: Do not die inside the lines; you must understand Xuansha’s intent
for it to work. Xuansha often used these words to meet people. There was a monk who had long been with Xuansha.
One day in the hall, the monk asked the master: Regarding the talk of the three kinds of sick people, do you still allow a student
to speak doctrine or not? Xuansha said: I allow it. The monk then said farewell and went down. Xuansha
said: No, no. This monk had understood Xuansha’s intent. Later Fayan
said: I heard Dizang the master cite this monk’s words, and only then did I understand the talk of the three kinds of sick people.
If you say this monk did not understand, then why did Fayan speak like that? If
you say he understood, then why did Xuansha say no, no? One day Di-
zang said: I have heard that the master has talk of the three kinds of sick people, is that so? Xuansha said:
It is. Dizang said: Guichen now has eyes, ears, nose, and tongue. How do you receive him?
Xuansha then stopped. If you understand Xuansha’s intent, how could it be in words and phrases? What he
understood is naturally distinct. Later a monk brought it up to Yunmen, and Yunmen immediately understood his
intent and said: You, bow. The monk bowed and rose. Yunmen struck with his staff. This monk
stepped back. Yunmen said: You are not blind. He called him close again. The monk came
near. Yunmen said: You are not deaf. Then he said: Do you understand? The monk said: I do not understand.
Yunmen said: You are not mute. From this the monk had an insight. If at that time he had been
a real man, the moment Yunmen said bow, he would have flipped over the meditation bench. How could you see
so much vine and creeper? Tell me, where Yunmen and Xuansha understood, is it the same or different?
Where those two understood is all just the same. Look at the ancients coming forth, making a thousand
and ten thousand kinds of expedient means; the intent is on the hook point. How many bitter words, only to make everyone
each clearly understand this one matter. The old master of the Fifth Patriarch said: One person can speak it, yet
does not understand; one person understands, yet cannot speak it. If the two come to study, how do you discern
them? If you cannot discern these two, then you can be sure you cannot remove others’ sticking and undo their binding.
If you can discern them, only then do you see entry. I will put on straw sandals and
walk through your belly several times, and still you will not awaken. Why go out begging for a bowl?
Just do not take it as blind, deaf, mute understanding. If you calculate like that, therefore it is said:
Eyes seeing forms are like the blind, and so on; ears hearing sounds are like the deaf, and so on. It also says: With eyes full you do not
see forms; with ears full you do not hear sounds. Manjushri constantly meets what the eyes touch; Guanyin blocks the ear faculty.
When you reach here, eyes seeing is like the blind, ears hearing is like the deaf; only then can you
be not far from Xuansha’s intent. Do you all recognize where the blind, deaf, mute fellow
lands? Look at Xuedou’s verse:

lg.l:
Blind, deaf, mute, cut off from all timing and appropriateness.Already before words. The three apertures are all clear—already made into a complete piece.Where will you grope for it?
Can you still make calculations? What does it have to do with anything?

In heaven above and below heaven, fit to laugh, fit to grieve.The right principle is free. I am also like this—
what are you laughing at, what are you grieving over? Half bright, half dark,
Li Lou cannot distinguish true colors;a blind fellow. A clever artisan leaves no trace. Truly blind.

how could Shi Kuang recognize the mysterious strings? Better to sit alone in an emptyA deaf fellow. Great merit sets up no reward. Truly deaf.
window’s shade,It must be like this to work. Don’t make a living in a ghost den—smash the lacquer tub in one go.

as leaves fall and flowers bloom, each has its
season.What season is it right now? On no account take it as a no-matter affair. Today it is from morning to night; tomorrow it is from morning to night.

cb:div.p:
He added: Do you understand
or not? A holeless iron hammer.Repeating the verse again:Take it out yourself. A pity to let it slip by. Then strike.

Blind, deaf, mute, cut off from all timing and appropriateness. All your seeing and not seeing, hearing and not
hearing, speaking and not speaking, Xuedou sweeps away for you at once. Until
blind-deaf-mute understandings, timing and appropriateness calculations, are all at once cut off and gone. None of it is
of any use. This upward matter can be called true blind, true deaf, true mute, with no timing
and no appropriateness. In heaven above and below heaven, fit to laugh, fit to grieve. Xuedou lifts one hand and
grips with one hand. Tell me, what are you laughing at, what are you grieving over? Fit to laugh: mute yet not
mute; deaf yet not deaf. Fit to grieve: clearly not blind yet blind; clearly not
deaf yet deaf. Li Lou cannot distinguish true colors; he cannot distinguish blue, yellow, red, and white; that
is truly blind. Li Lou was a man of the Yellow Emperor’s time; from a hundred paces away he could see the tip of an autumn hair.
His eyes were extremely clear. When the Yellow Emperor roamed at the Red Water and sank a pearl, he ordered Li Zhu to search for it
but he could not find it; he ordered Chi Gou to search and also could not obtain it. Later he ordered Xiangwang to search, and only then
did he get it. Therefore it is said: When Xiangwang arrives, the light is dazzling; where Li Lou walks, the waves
billow to the sky. At this one move from the heights, even Li Lou’s eyes cannot distinguish
true colors. How could Shi Kuang recognize the mysterious strings? In the Zhou era, in Jiangzhou, Duke Jing of Jin
son. Shi Kuang, styled Ziye, was skilled at distinguishing the five tones and the sixOne says: It was Jin Pinggong’s music master.
pitch-pipes. He could hear ants fighting across a mountain. When Jin and Chu were at war, Shi Kuang only played the zither.
Plucking the wind-strings, he knew that fighting Chu would surely achieve nothing. Even so, Xuedou said,
he still had not recognized where the mysterious string is. Not deaf, yet he is a deaf person. This lofty
mysterious sound at this place: even Shi Kuang could not recognize it. Xuedou said: I also do not
play Li Lou, and I also do not play Shi Kuang. Better to sit alone beneath an empty window; leaves fall
and flowers bloom each in their own time. If you reach this realm, though you see it is as if not seeing, though you hear
it is as if not hearing, though you speak it is as if not speaking. Hungry, you eat; tired, you sleep. Let it be
leaves fall and flowers bloom: when leaves fall, it is autumn; when flowers bloom, it is spring. Each and every
thing has its own season. Xuedou has, in one stroke, swept it all clean for you. And again he let
one thread of the Way loose, saying: do you understand or not? Xuedou is spent and weary, and can only say
a holeless iron hammer. You must look with urgent eyes to see this line; if you form an idea, you
miss it. The master raised his whisk and said: do you see? Then he struck the Zen bench once and said,
do you hear? He stepped down from the Zen bench and said: can you speak it?

Instruction said: Your whole body is eyes, yet you cannot see; your whole body is ears, yet you cannot hear up to it.
Your whole body is mouth, yet you cannot speak it; your whole body is mind, yet you cannot illuminate it. Whole body, for now
set it aside. Suddenly, if there were no eyes, how would you see? If there were no ears, how would you hear? If there were no
mouth, how would you speak? If there were no mind, how would you illuminate? If right here you can flip
a single thread of the Way, then you participate together with the ancient buddhas. Participation aside for now, tell me: you participate
with what person?

Case 89: Yunyan asked Daowu: The Great Compassion Bodhisattva uses so many hands and eyes
for what? Daowu said: like a personAt that time, best to give him his proper fodder. What are you usually running up and down for? The acharya asks: For what?
in the middle of the night, reaching back with the hand to feel for a pillow. Yunyan said: IWhy not use your proper fodder? One blind person leading a crowd of blind.
understand. Daowu said: how do youTaking wrong as right, tricking a whole boatload of people to death. Same pit, no different dirt. You cannot avoid nicking the edge and cutting the hand.
understand? Yunyan said: the whole body is hands and eyes. Daowu said: you have said far too much; you only said eightyWhy bother asking again? Yet you still must ask it through. Give him a squeeze.What does it have to do with anything?
Making a living in a ghost den; washing a clod of dirt in the mud.
percent. Yunyan said: then how do you, elder brother? Daowu said: the entire body is hands and eyes.Same pit, no different dirt. A servant sees the maid and fawns; a leprous child drags along a companion.How could you get the authority to judge people?
Give him a squeeze as well.A shrimp cannot jump out of the dipper. Swap out your eyes, move away your tongue—still
do you get the full ten parts or not? Calling dad “sir.”

Yunyan and Daowu studied together under Yaoshan; for forty years their ribs never touched a mat. Yao-
shan produced the Caodong school. There were three whose Dharma and Way flourished: from Yunyan came Dong-
shan; from Daowu came Shishuang; from Chuanzhi came Jiashan. The Great Compassion Bodhisattva has eighty-
four thousand mudra arms. Great Compassion has so many hands and eyes; do you all have them or not?
Baizhang said: all language and writing all, by winding turns, return
to oneself. Yunyan often followed Daowu to consult, inquire, and decide. One day he asked him,
saying: what does the Great Compassion Bodhisattva use so many hands and eyes for? At the start it would have been good to
split his back with a staff right then, sparing us from seeing so many entangling vines afterward. Daowu was compassionate
and could not do that, so he explained the principle to him instead, intending to make him understand right away. So he
said: like a person in the middle of the night, reaching back with the hand to feel for a pillow. In the deep of night with no lamplight,
you reach with your hand to feel for the pillow; tell me, where are the eyes? Yunyan then said: I
understand. Daowu said: how do you understand? Yunyan said: the whole body is hands and eyes. Daowu
said: you have said far too much; you only said eighty percent. Yunyan said: then you, elder brother,
how do you say it? Daowu said: the entire body is hands and eyes. Tell me: is whole body it, or is entire
body it? Though it seems like rotten mud, it is clean and free. People nowadays mostly go and make
sentimental interpretations, saying: whole body is not it, entire body is it. They keep chewing on the ancients'
words and phrases, and die beneath the ancients' words. They do not realize that the ancients' meaning is not
in words and phrases. All of this was used only because matters left no choice. People now add
footnotes and set up rules, saying: if you penetrate this koan, then you can stop investigating and understanding.
They use the hand to feel the whole body, feel the lantern and the exposed pillar, and make it all into an understanding of entire-body talk. If
you understand like that, you ruin the ancients quite a lot. Therefore it is said: they investigate living phrases, not
dead phrases. You must cut off emotional dust and imagining; clean, naked, bare, and open.
Only then can you see the Great Compassion saying. Haven't you seen Caoshan ask a monk: responding to things and manifesting form
like the moon in water, how is it? The monk said: like a donkey looking into a well. Caoshan said: you have said it to death,
you only said eighty percent. The monk said: then how do you, master? Caoshan said:
like a well looking at a donkey. It is the same meaning. If you go to see it in the words, you will never
escape the traps of Daowu and Yunyan. Xuedou, a seasoned hand, does not die under the phrases either;
he goes straight over your head. The verse says:

lg.l:
Whole body is, entire body is; pick it upFour limbs, eight joints—this is not the ultimate standard of a patch-robed monk.On the crown of the head there is half a side—still in the den. Blind.
and it is still a hundred thousand miles off.You must not let it pass—why only a hundred thousand miles?

Spreading its wings, the roc soars through the six directions,
cloudsA little bit of a realm, taken to be strange. A point

beating the wind, drumming up the waters of the four seas.of dust, taken to be that people under heaven
cannot do anything to you. Pass.

What dust and grime is this, suddenly arising,Again, for the Zen person, he adds a footnote—where will you cut it off and pick it up?
that tiny hairbreadth that has not yet stopped?Separate, separate—blown apart. Cut.

Do you not see the net-pearlsAnd off it goes like this again.
hanging down as a model, reflections layered upon layers?Big and small, Xuedou makes this coming and going—what a pity. Still tangling vines as before.

At the tip of the staff, hands
and eyes: from where do they arise?Bah! Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—you cannot be let off. All over the earth people have no place to breathe out. If you are let off, you still must eat the stick. Again he struck and said: Bah!
Tell me: is it this mountain monk’s, or is it Xuedou’s?
Bah!After three shouts, four shouts—what then?Covered over: dust also can be read as a dam.

cb:div.p:
Whole body is, entire body is. If you say that reaching back to feel for a pillow is it, and that using
the hand to feel the body is it, then with such a view you do business entirely in a ghost cave.
In the end, whole body and entire body are both not it. If you want to use feelings and cognition
to see the Great Compassion saying, it is truly still a hundred thousand miles off. Xuedou worked up
one living phrase: pick it up and it is still a hundred thousand miles off. The later lines verse Yunyan and Daowu's
marvelous place, saying: spreading its wings, the roc soars through the six directions, clouds; beating the wind, drumming up the waters of the four
seas. The great roc swallows dragons, and with its wings beats wind and drums waves; the water opens three thousand
miles, and then it takes the dragons and swallows them. Xuedou said: if your great roc can beat the wind
and drum the waves, it is indeed too heroic. But if you look at it with Great Compassion's thousand hands and eyes, it is only
like a bit of dust suddenly arising, and also like a tiny hairbreadth of wind that has not yet
stopped. Xuedou said: if you use the hand feeling the body as hands and eyes, what use can it
possibly be? As for the Great Compassion saying, you are simply not there. Therefore it says: what
dust and grime is this, suddenly arising; that tiny hairbreadth that has not yet stopped. Xuedou, calling himself
a seasoned hand, in one stroke wiped away the traces. Yet afterward he still leaks and hints, speaking
a metaphor; as before it is still inside the trap. Do you not see: the net-pearls hang down as a model,
reflections layered upon layers. Xuedou brings up Indra's net bright pearls to serve as a model. Tell me: hands and eyes
fall where? In the Huayan school they establish four dharma realms: first, the realm of principle,
clarifying that it is one flavor of equality; second, the realm of phenomena, clarifying that the whole principle becomes phenomena;
third, the realm of nonobstruction between principle and phenomena, clarifying that principle and phenomena interpenetrate, with no obstruction of large and small;
fourth, the realm of nonobstruction among phenomena, clarifying that one phenomenon pervades all phenomena, and all
phenomena wholly contain all phenomena, mutually interpenetrating at the same time without obstruction. Therefore it is said: as soon as
one speck is raised, the whole earth is gathered in; each and every speck contains boundless dharma realms. Since one speck
is like this, all specks are likewise. The net-pearls are before the Hall of Good Dharma of the Heavenly Lord Shakra.
He uses mani jewels as a net: within a single jewel appear hundreds and thousands of jewels, and the hundreds
and thousands of jewels all appear within one jewel, mutually reflecting in layers, host and companion without end. This is used
to clarify the realm of nonobstruction among phenomena. In the past, National Teacher Xianshou set it up as the mirror-and-lamp
metaphor: arrange ten mirrors in a circle with a lamp in the middle. If you look at the eastern mirror, then in the nine mirrors the mirrors
and lamp appear distinctly all at once; if you look at the southern mirror, each mirror is likewise. Therefore the World-Honored
One, when he first attained perfect awakening, without leaving the bodhi site, universally ascended among the Trayastrimsa heavens,
and even to all places, in seven locations and nine assemblies, taught the Avatamsaka Sutra. Xuedou
uses Indra's net pearls to point out the realm of nonobstruction among phenomena. Yet the meaning of the six aspects is very
clear: namely whole and parts, namely sameness and difference, namely formation and destruction. Raise one aspect,
and all six are included. But sentient beings use it every day and do not know it. Xuedou picks up
Indra's net bright pearls as a model; how much more this Great Compassion saying is exactly like this. If you
If you can truly, within this jeweled net, make clear the staff—its miraculous powers and wondrous function—going
in and out without obstruction, only then can you see the hands and eyes. Therefore Xuedou said: on the end of the staff,
where do hands and eyes arise? He has you take verification at the staff’s end and take it up under the shout. Just like
Deshan—enter the gate and he strikes. Tell me, where are the hands and eyes? Linji—enter
the gate and he shouts. Tell me, where are the hands and eyes? And tell me, at the end, why did Xuedou
still add that tut as something to investigate?

Raising a pointer, it says: a line before sound—no thousand sages transmit it. A single thread right before your face—through long ages
without interruption. Clean nakedness, bare and plain; hair tousled, ears sticking out. Tell me,
how is it? Try to raise it and see.

Case ninety is raised: A monk asked Zhimen, What is the substance of prajna? Zhimen said, A clam holds the bright moon. The monk said, What is the function of prajna?The whole body has no shadow or image. Sitting cuts off the tongues of people under heaven
entirely. What use is the substance?Light swallows the ten thousand forms—let that be for now. How is the matter of the true eye on the tip of the staff? The crooked cannot hide
the straight. Frost on top of snow—another layer.Retreat three thousand miles—what do you want to use it for?
Zhimen said, A rabbit carries a fetus.Steep. Bitter gourd, bitter down to the root; sweet melon, sweet through the whole vine. Making a living amid light and shadow, you cannot get out of the
den at the gate of wisdom. If someone comes out, tell me: is it prajna as substance or prajna as function? And still you want to add mud on top of dirt.

Zhimen’s A clam holds the bright moon, a rabbit carries a fetus both employ the meaning of mid-autumn. Even
so, the ancients’ intent is not on clam and rabbit. He was a venerable elder in Yunmen’s assembly;
in one phrase he must have three phrases: the phrase that encloses and covers heaven and earth,
the phrase that cuts off all streams, and the phrase that goes along with waves and follows the current. There is no need to arrange them; naturally
it is just right. He went straight to the steep place to answer this monk, slightly revealing a bit of edge. It is
indeed remarkable. Even so, the ancients never went to toy with light and shadow; they only
point out some route for you so people can see. This monk asked, What is the substance of prajna?
Zhimen said, A clam holds the bright moon. Clams come from the Han River; within the clam there is a bright
pearl. When the mid-autumn moon rises, the clam floats to the surface, opens its mouth and takes in moonlight; stirred
by it, it produces a pearl—these are the Hepu pearls. If there is moon at mid-autumn, there are many pearls; if there is no
moon, there are few. What is the function of prajna? Zhimen said, A rabbit carries a fetus. This
meaning is also no different. The rabbit belongs to yin; when the mid-autumn moon is born, it opens its mouth and swallows its light, and then
it becomes pregnant, giving birth from its mouth. Again, if there is moon there are many; if there is no moon there are few.
In the ancients’ reply there is not all that much; they only borrow that meaning to answer the prajna
light. Even so, their intent is not on words and phrases. It is only
later people who make a living on words and phrases. Have you not seen Panshan say: the mind-moon, solitary
and round, its light swallows all forms. Light does not illumine objects, and objects do not abide; when both light and objects are gone,
what then is it? People today only stare their eyes and call it light, and then
generate interpretations from feelings—driving a peg into emptiness. The ancients said: all of you, at the gateways of the six faculties,
day and night emit great light, shining through mountains, rivers, and the great earth—not only the eye faculty
emits light; nose, tongue, body, and mind also all emit light. When you reach here, you must simply
fold up the six faculties so there is not the slightest thing. Clean nakedness, bare and plain—only then do you see
where these words land. Xuedou versified it exactly like this.

lg.l:
A single piece, empty and congealed, cuts off what is called feeling; humans and devas fromIf you shape it in the mind, you miss; if you stir a thought, you are separated. Even the Buddha-eye cannot see it.
this see emptiness arise.Subhuti deserves thirty blows—what use is this old fellow? Even if Subhuti also retreated three thousand miles,

The clam holds
the dark rabbit’s deep, deep meaning;it still must be the very person himself. What is the point? Why use some even deeper intention?

it once with the Chan folk
made battle.Spears and halberds have ceased; all under heaven is at peace. Do you understand? He struck and said: Acharya, how much can you take?

cb:div.p:
A single piece, empty and congealed, cuts off what is called feeling—Xuedou, with one line, versified it well. Naturally
you can see the ancients’ intent. The six faculties are clear and still—what is it? Just this single piece,
empty-bright and congealed-still. No need to seek it in heaven, and no need to ask it from others.
Naturally constant light appears before you; everywhere is a sheer cliff a thousand fathoms high. What is called feeling is precisely cut off—
that is, the dust of feeling. Fayan’s verse on the perfected real nature says: when principle reaches the utmost, one forgets feeling
and speech—how can it be explained evenly? At the end, the frosty night moon falls naturally into the stream before you. Fruit
ripens and the monkeys grow heavy; the mountains are far and the road seems lost. Raise your head and the remaining glow is there—it was
always dwelling in the west. Therefore it is said: mind is the root, dharmas are the dust—these two are like a stain upon a mirror.
When dust and grime are exhausted, light first appears; when mind and dharmas are both forgotten, the nature is true. Also
it says: in three bays of thatched hut I have always lived; one path of numinous light and ten thousand conditions are at ease. Do not take
right and wrong to judge me; the meddling of this floating life is not connected. In this very verse too you see
that single piece, empty and congealed, cutting off what is called feeling. Humans and devas from this see emptiness arise. Have you not seen
how Subhuti sat in silent ease in a cave, and the devas rained flowers in praise? The venerable one said, In emptiness
flowers rain and praise—who is it? The deva said, I am Brahma. The venerable one
said, How do you praise? Brahma said, I esteem the venerable one for well expounding prajnaparamita. The venerable one said,
I have never spoken a single word about prajna—how do you
praise? Brahma said, The venerable one has no speaking; I therefore have no hearing. No speaking and no hearing
are true prajna. And again the earth shook and flowers rained down. Look at how Subhuti well expounded
prajna—without even speaking of substance and function. If you see it here, then you can see Zhimen
saying, A clam holds the bright moon, a rabbit carries a fetus. Though the ancients’ intent is not in words and phrases,
still the reply carries a deep, deep purport, which drew out Xuedou’s saying: the clam holds
the dark rabbit’s deep, deep meaning. Coming to this, it has once made battle with the Chan folk. The Chan crowd under heaven
makes a huge racket debating, yet not one has ever even dreamed of it.
If you want to investigate together with Zhimen and Xuedou, you must set your own eyes to it first.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Nine, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.End.
Blue Cliff Record, Volume Ten, by Chan Master Foguo Yuanwu.

cb:div.p:
Raising a pointer, it says: Transcend feeling and depart from views; remove bonds and loosen what clings; lift up the vehicle of going beyond.
Support and raise the treasury of the true dharma eye. It also requires the ten directions responding in unison and eight sides bright and clear. Only when you reach
such a field, tell me, is there anyone who attains together, verifies together, dies together, lives together
with it? Try to raise it and see.

Case ninety-one is raised: One day Yanguan called to an attendant, Bring me the rhinoceros-horn fan.
The attendant said, The fan is broken. Yanguan said, If the fan is broken, return my rhinoceros to me. The attendant had no reply.There is no shortage of tangled vines—how about this fine news?What a pity. Fine news indeed—say
what?Plenty of leaking and letting out. You
zhou is still acceptable; the bitterest is Silla. What is the master doing using a little rhinoceros?It truly is a holeless iron hammer—what a pity.
Touzi said, I do not refuse to bring it out, but I fear the head and horns are not whole. Xuedou raised it and said, I want head and horns that are not whole. Shishuang said, If it is returned to the master, then there is none. XuedouIt seems like it, but what can be done about two ends and three
faces? It is still talking about principles.What use is it? Taking
wrong as wrong,what are you saying? Smacking right into the nostrils.
raised it and said, The rhinoceros is still here. Zifu drew a circularSteep. When it comes to misrecognition, draw your head back and go.
form, and within it wrote the character ox. Xuedou raised it and said, JustNo need to pluck the grass out. Shadow-juggling fellows
now, why did you not bring it out? Baofu said, The master iscannot tell gold from brass—also fellows in the weeds.
advanced in years—better ask someone else. Xuedou raised it and said, What a pity—Cursing officials in some backwater—why speak of hardship and bitterness?
labor with no achievement.Their own bodies are in it too—they also deserve thirty blows. Plainly so.

One day Yanguan called to an attendant, Bring me the rhinoceros-horn fan. This matter
though it is not on words and phrases, still tests a person’s lifelong spirit and tactics. Also,
it must be made manifest by relying on words like this. On the thirtieth day of the twelfth lunar month, you must gain real strength
and be able to take charge. Though ten thousand conditions crash and clatter, you see them without moving—this is called
the achievement of no-achievement, the strength of no-strength. Yanguan was Chan Master Qian of Qi’an. In ancient times they used rhinoceros
horn as a fan. At that time, how could Yanguan not know the rhinoceros fan was broken? Therefore he asked the attendant.
The attendant said, The fan is broken. Look at the ancients—through the twelve hours they are always
right there, colliding and striking. Yanguan said, If the fan is broken, return my rhi
noceros to me. Tell me, what does he want the rhinoceros for? He only wants to test
whether a person knows the landing place or not. Touzi said, I do not refuse to bring it out, but I fear the head and horns
are not whole. Xuedou said, I want head and horns that are not whole—also, right under the phrase he
met the pivot. Shishuang said, If it is returned to the master, then there is none. Xuedou said, The rhinoceros
is still here. Zifu drew a circular form and wrote within it the character ox—because he
inherited Yangshan, and throughout his life loved using circumstances to meet people and make this matter clear. Xuedou
said, Just now, why did you not bring it out?—again piercing his nostrils. Bao
fu said, The master is advanced in years—better ask someone else. These words were spoken steadily. The previous
three replies are easy to see; this one sentence has a far-reaching intent. Xuedou also shattered
it. This mountain monk once, at Steward Qing’s place, worked it out, saying: The master
is advanced in years and senile—getting the head and forgetting the tail. Just now he asked for the fan; now he asks for the rhi
noceros. It is hard on those serving him, so he said, Better ask someone else. Xuedou said, What a
pity—labor with no achievement. These are all patterns of giving a concluding phrase. The ancients, seeing through this
matter, though each different, could speak it forth—one hundred shots, one hundred hits. There must be a way out
the road of one’s very body. Sentence after sentence never loses the living lineage. Nowadays when people are asked, they only keep
making doctrinal calculations. Thus, through the twelve hours of the day, they make people chew it over until drops of water
freeze drop by drop, searching for some place of realization. Look at how Xuedou strung his verse together, saying:
。Striking and colliding.

lg.l: The rhinoceros fan has been used a long time;Meeting summer it is cool; meeting winter it is warm. Everyone fully has it—why do they not know? Who has never used it?

when asked, it turns out none of us knows at all—boundlessKnowing is knowing; understanding is not understanding. Don’t try to fool people—nor can you blame others.
pure breeze, along with the horn and the ridge,Where is it? If you do not understand it in yourself, where will you understand it? Above heaven, below earth—horns sprout again. What
is it? Raising waves without any wind.
all go off with clouds and rain, hard to chase back.Blue sky, blue sky—also losing money and taking the blame.

cb:div.p:
Xuedou again
said: If you want the pure breeze to return and the horn to be born again, I ask each Zen traveler to give a turning word. He asked, The fan is already broken—return my rhinoceros to me. At the time a monk came forward and said, Everyone, go to the hall for practice. Xuedou shouted, Casting a hook to fish for a great whale.Everyone has a rhinoceros fan; through the twelve hours
you rely entirely on its power. Why is it that when asked, you never know? Can you speak it or not?Yanguan.
Still there—already turned three times.There is also
one and a half. Bah! It is also fine to topple the meditation couch.After the thief
has passed, you draw the bow. Your spear has been snatched away; ahead you cannot reach a village, behind you cannot make the inn in time.
He only caught a shrimp. Then he stepped down from the seat.You have brought it on yourself to this point. Drawing the bow after the thief has passed—Foguo cited this phrase himself, saying: again
I ask you all directly: the monk said, Everyone, go to the hall to practice. Is that understanding or not understanding? If it is not understanding, how can he say it like that? If you say it is understanding, Xuedou again says: casting a hook
to fish for a kun-whale, he only hooks a shrimp—then he steps down from the seat. Tell me: where is the error? Please examine it and see.

The rhinoceros fan has been used a long time; when asked, it turns out none of us knows at all. Everyone has
a rhinoceros fan; through the twelve hours of the day we wholly rely on its power. Why is it that when asked
we always go on not knowing? The attendant, Touzi, and even Baofu as well—none of them
knew either. Tell me, did Xuedou know or not? Haven’t you seen how Wuzhuo visited Manjushri, and while
drinking tea, Manjushri raised a glass cup and said, In the south, is there still this
or not? Wuzhuo said, No. Manjushri said, What do you ordinarily use to drink tea? Wuzhuo was silent.
If you know where this case lands, then you know that the rhinoceros fan has
boundless pure breeze, and you also see the rhinoceros horn rising rugged and sharp. Those four old fellows spoke
like that, as if morning clouds and evening rain—once gone, hard to chase. Xuedou again said, If you want
the pure breeze to return and the horn to be born again, I ask each Zen traveler to give a turning word. He asked,
The fan is already broken—return my little rhinoceros to me. At the time a Zen traveler came forward
and said, Everyone, go to the hall for practice. This monk seized the householder’s authority; what he said
was deadly indeed, yet he only got it to eighty percent. If you want one hundred percent, then you must overturn the meditation bench.
Now tell me, did this monk understand the little rhinoceros or not? If he didn’t understand, how could he
say it like that? If he did understand, why didn’t Xuedou approve him? Why did he say, Casting a hook
to fish for a great whale, he only caught a shrimp? Tell me, in the end, how is it? You
all, if you have nothing to do, try picking it up and bringing it forth.

Instruction: A different tune from a moved string—hard to meet in a thousand years. Seeing the hare, you loose the hawk, and at once
you take the swift. Gather all language into a single phrase; gather the great thousand worlds of sand into a single
mote of dust. Dying together, living together, piercing through seven times, burrowing eight holes—do you have any proof? Try presenting
it.

Case 92. One day the World-Honored One ascended the seat. Manjushri struck the gavel and said,Host and guest both are lost—not just one slip.
Contemplate well the Dharma of the Dharma King; the Dharma of the Dharma King is just so. The World-Honored One then stepped down from the seat.Get it firsthand.
。A sorrowful person should not speak to a sorrowful person; speak to a sorrowful person and sorrow kills people. Beating drums and plucking lutes—two who understand meet face to face.

Before the World-Honored One ever held up a flower, this message was already there. From the Deer Park
at the beginning to the Bhadrā River at the end, when did he ever need the vajra-king precious sword? At that time,
if among the assembly there had been a fellow with the breath of a patchrobed monk, he would have snatched it away, sparing us
the later holding up of the flower and the whole messy affair. When the World-Honored One sat silent for a long time, Manjushri pressed him
with a shove, and he stepped down from the seat. At that moment too there was this message. Shakyamuni closing the room, Vimalakirti
sealing his mouth—both are like this: it has already been spoken. It is like Emperor Suzong asking
National Teacher Zhong about making a seamless stupa; and like an outsider asking the Buddha for a word that is not asked with words
and not asked with silence. Look at how those above tread and conduct themselves—when did they ever enter
a ghost cave to make a living? Some say the intent lies in silence; some say
it lies in the long pause. With words they clarify what is beyond words; without words they clarify what is within words—
and Yongjia said, Silent while speaking, speaking while silent. If you understand it all like that, for three lives
and sixty kalpas you still won’t even dream of it. If you can straightaway take it up and accept it,
you will no longer see any ordinary or any sage. This Dharma is equal, with no high or low. Day after day
you walk hand in hand with all buddhas of the three times. Later, look at Xuedou and you will naturally see
the verse emerge.

lg.l: Among the array of sages, only the adept knows.Do not slander old man Śākyamuni. Give it back to Linji and Deshan—among thousands and tens of thousands, it is hard to find even one and a half.
The Dharma King’s decrees are not like this.Those who just follow him are like hemp and millet—three heads and two faces. Clearly, how many can reach here?

If in the assembly there were a sandalwood guest,Among them it is hard to find a nimble person. Mañjuśrī is not a master hand; Venerable, you certainly are not either.
why would Manjushri need to strike the gavel?Strike one more blow—what harm? But the second and third blows you do not want at all. In the moment, how do you speak a single sentence? Perilous!

cb:div.p:
Among the array of sages, only the adept knows. The eighty thousand at Vulture Peak are all an array of sages.
Manjushri and Samantabhadra, up to Maitreya—host and companions gathered together. It must be skill within skill,
marvel within marvel, to know where it lands. Xuedou’s intent is that among the array of sages
not a single one knows it is there. If there is an adept, only then does he know it is not like
that. Why did Manjushri strike the gavel and say, Contemplate well the Dharma of the Dharma King; the Dharma King’s Dharma
is just so? Xuedou says, The Dharma King’s decrees are not like this. Why is it like this? At that
time, if in the assembly there had been a fellow with eyes on the crown of his head and a talisman behind his elbow, before the World-
Honored One even ascended the seat he would have seen through it—what need would there be for Manjushri to strike the gavel? The Nirvana
Sutra says: Xiantuopo is one name with four realities: first, salt; second, water; third,
a vessel; fourth, a horse. There was a wise minister who understood these four meanings well. If the king wished to sprinkle and
wash and asked for xiantuopo, the minister would present water. If food was requested, he would present salt. After eating, he would present
a vessel to drink broth. If the king wished to go out, he would present a horse. Responding according to intent without error—clearly it requires
a sharp fellow. For example, a monk asked Xiangyan, What is it like when the king asks for
xiantuopo? Xiangyan said, Come over here. The monk came over. Xiangyan said, You are so dull it kills
people. He also asked Zhaozhou, What is it like when the king asks for xiantuopo? Zhaozhou stepped down from the meditation bench
and bowed, hands folded. If at that time there had been a xiantuopo in the assembly, before the World-Honored One ascended
the seat he would have passed through—still somewhat better. But the World-Honored One ascended the seat and immediately stepped down—
that already misses the mark. How much more when Manjushri also strikes the gavel—it does not hinder, but it
makes that one lifting-and-proclaiming of the World-Honored One dull. Now tell me, what is the place where it is made dull?

Case 93. A monk asked Daguang, Changqing said, Because of the meal, he offered a congratulatory chant—what is the meaning
of it? Daguang danced. The monk bowed.Chongguang, this lacquer bucket—no harm in doubting it. Without asking, you do not know.Do not cheat people to death. As before, it comes like this,
Daguang said, Seeing what, do you bow?and as before it goes like this. Yes, yes—only fear mistaken understanding.
The monk danced. Daguang said, ThisIt is also good to press once—only after you have discerned through will it do.Drawing a cat by copying a model—indeed mistaken understanding. A fellow who plays with light and shadow.
wild-fox spirit.This kindness is hard to repay. The thirty-two patriarchs only transmitted this.

In the West, four seven; in the Tang land, two three—only this little bit is transmitted. Everyone, do you
know where it lands? If you know, you avoid this fault; if you don’t know, you are still just
a wild-fox spirit. Some say it is ripping and twisting his nostrils to deceive people. If it were truly
like that, what sense would it make? Daguang was skilled at helping people; within his phrase there is
a road for stepping out. In general, a master of the school must pull nails and draw wedges for people, remove sticking,
untie bonds—only then is he called a good friend. Daguang danced; the monk bowed. In the end
the monk danced instead. Daguang said, This wild-fox spirit. It isn’t that he turned
the monk—ultimately the monk didn’t know what was correct. You just keep dancing; passing it back and forth like this, when
will it ever come to rest? Daguang called him wild-fox spirit—these words cut through a golden bull.
It is indeed peculiar. Therefore it is said: he studies the living phrase, not the dead phrase.
Xuedou only loved that he said, This wild-fox spirit, so he brought forth a verse. But tell me, this
wild-fox spirit—compared with The river’s head is white, the sea’s head is black—is it the same or different? You lacquer bucket,
he also says, Good master and monk—tell me, is it the same or different? Do you know? Wherever you touch, you meet
him. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
The first arrow is still light; the later arrow goes deep—who says yellow leaves areA hundred shots, a hundred hits—where can you dodge to?
gold?For now, pretend to stop the crying—you can fool a child, but it is useless.

If Caoxi’s waves were like that,A fellow kneading mud-balls—what
limit is there? Drawing a cat by copying a model—letting one road go through.

boundless ordinary people would be drowned on dry land.If you meet a living person, you burden all the patch-robed
monks—unable to grope it out. You burden the Venerable, unable to raise your head.

cb:div.p:
The first arrow is still light; the later arrow goes deep. Daguang dancing is the first arrow; then he says, This
wild-fox spirit, is the later arrow. These are fangs and claws handed down from above. Who says yellow
leaves are gold? Yangshan instructed the assembly, All of you, each return the light
and reflect it back; do not memorize my words. From beginningless kalpas you have turned your backs on the bright and thrown yourselves into darkness; delusive
thoughts have deep roots and are hard to pull out all at once. Therefore I set up provisional means to take away your coarse knowing.
It is like using yellow leaves to stop a child’s crying; like using sweet fruit to trade for bitter gourds.
Like this. The ancients provisionally set up skillful means for people; once the crying stops, yellow leaves are not gold.
The World-Honored One’s teaching throughout a lifetime was also just talk to stop the crying. This wild-fox
goblin only wants to swap out their karmic consciousness. Within it there is also provisional and real, and also illumination and function.
Only then do you see the snout of a patch-robed monk. If you understand it, it is like a tiger with wings; the Caoxi waves
are like that as well. But if, from all directions, students suddenly just keep everyone
dancing like this—always like this—countless ordinary people get sunk. What
is there to save them?

Instruction says: one phrase before sound is not transmitted by a thousand sages. A single thread right before your eyes, for a long time
without gap. Clean nakedness, bare and free. The white ox on open ground—eyes upright and bold, ears upright
and bold. The golden-maned lion, set that aside for now. Tell me: what is the white ox
on open ground?

Case 94. The Surangama Sutra says: When I do not see, why do you not see the place of my not seeing?
If you see not seeing, then it naturally is notWhat fine news! What use is seeing for? Old man Śākyamuni leaked and let slip quite a lot.
the appearance of that not seeing. If you do not see the place of myBah! What leisure is there? Do not make this mountain monk become two ends and three faces.
not seeing, then it naturally is not a thing—how is it not you?Where are you going? Like a nailed iron stake. Bah!Press down the ox’s head to make it eat grass—what more
talk of mouth, sound, and color?Saying you, saying me, has nothing to do with it. He struck and said: Do you still see old man Śākyamuni? But the ancients refused to take responsibility.
He struck and said: Look for yourself beneath your heel. Do you understand?

The Surangama Sutra says: When I do not see, why do you not see the place of my not seeing? If
you see not seeing, it naturally is not the appearance of that not seeing. If you do not see the place of my not seeing,
it naturally is not a thing—how is it not you? When Xuedou reaches here, he does not quote the sutra text
to the end; quoting it in full makes it clear. The sutra says: If what is seen is a thing, then you also
can see my seeing. If you see the same, it is called seeing me. When I do not see,
why do you not see the place of my not seeing? If you see not seeing, it naturally is not that
appearance of not seeing. If you do not see the place of my not seeing, it naturally is not a thing. How
is it not you? Too many words to record. Ananda’s intent was this: the world, lanterns, pillars—everything
can have a name; he also wanted the World-Honored One to point out this subtle essence, original clarity—what is it
called, what thing is it—so that I may see the Buddha’s intent. The World-Honored One said, I see the incense stand. Ananda
said, I also see the incense stand; that is the Buddha’s seeing. The World-Honored One said, If I see the incense stand,
then you can know. If I do not see the incense stand, how do you see? Ananda
said, When I do not see the incense stand, that is seeing the Buddha. The Buddha said, When I say I do
not see, that is my own knowing. When you say you do not see, that is your own knowing. Another person’s not seeing—
how can you know it? The ancients said: when you reach here, you can only know it yourself.
You cannot speak of it to others. Just as the World-Honored One said: When I do not see, why do you not
see the place of my not seeing? If you see not seeing, it naturally is not the
appearance of that not seeing. If you do not see the place of my not seeing, it naturally is not a thing—how is it not you?
If you say you take seeing to be a thing, you still cannot brush away traces. When I do not see, it is like an antelope
hanging its horns—sound and traces, even its breath, are all cut off. Where will you go to grope
for it? The sutra’s intent first lets go to break through, then snatches away to break through. Xuedou, bringing forth a verse with an eye for the teaching, does not
versify a thing; nor does he versify seeing and not seeing. He only versifies seeing the Buddha.

lg.l:
The whole elephant, the whole ox—no different from an eye-film; fromA half-blind fellow—half open, half shut. Why hold the fence and grope the wall? One knife, two pieces.
the beginning, all who do it are together named gropers.In the West, four times seven; in Tang lands, two or three. Old masters under heaven are like hemp and millet—still too few.

If
you now want to see the yellow-headed old fellow,Bah! This old barbarian—blind fellow—is beneath your heel.

in every instant, in every speck of dust, it is
halfway along the road.Beneath your heel you have already missed it. What more can you have this mountain monk say? Have you ever dreamed of it in the year of the donkey?

cb:div.p:
The whole elephant, the whole ox—no different from an eye-film. The blind crowd gropes the elephant, each speaking a different view. It comes from the Nirvana
Sutra. A monk asked Yangshan, When the master sees people ask about Zen and ask about the Way, you make a
circle, and inside it you write the character ox; what is your intent in this? Yangshan said, This too
is an idle matter. If you suddenly understand, it does not come from outside. If you suddenly do not understand, you definitely
do not recognize it. Let me ask you: the old worthies of all directions point to you and
indicate which is your buddha-nature— is it the speaking one, the silent one? Could it be
the one that neither speaks nor is silent? Or is it all of them? Or is it none of them? If you
take the speaking one to be it, you are like a blind person feeling the elephant’s tail. If you take the silent one to be it, you are like
a blind person feeling the elephant’s ear. If you take the one that neither speaks nor is silent to be it, you are like a blind person
feeling the elephant’s trunk. If you say every single thing is it, you are like a blind person feeling the elephant’s four
legs. If you say none of it is, you throw away the elephant itself and fall into empty views. Such is what the blind crowd
sees: they only name vague differences upon the elephant. If you want it good, absolutely do not grope the elephant.
Do not say seeing and awareness are it; also do not say they are not. The Patriarch said, Bodhi originally has no
tree; the bright mirror also has no stand. Originally there is not a single thing—how could dust and grime cling? And he also
said, The Way originally has no form or appearance; wisdom is the Way. One who makes this understanding is
called true prajna. A clear-eyed person sees the elephant and gets its whole body; as the Buddha sees nature,
so it is. The whole ox comes from Zhuangzi: Butcher Ding carved an ox, never once seeing its whole
ox. Following the pattern, he cut; the blade moved freely, at ease. There was no need to force the hand. When he only
lifted his eyes, horns, hooves, and flesh all at once resolved themselves. Thus for nineteen years, his knife
stayed sharp as if freshly honed on the whetstone—this is called the whole ox. Even though it is so extraordinary, Xue-
dou said, Even if you achieve this, the whole elephant and whole ox are still no different from an eye-film.
From the beginning, all who do it are together named gropers. Even a genuine adept—go in there and grope
and you will not find it. From Kashyapa on, down to the Patriarchs of India and this land, the old
masters under heaven are all merely named gropers. Xuedou bluntly said: now if you want
to see the yellow-headed old fellow—therefore it is said, if you want to see, then see right away. If you still want to search, only then
you will see—then it is a thousand miles, ten thousand miles. The yellow-headed old fellow is the yellow-faced old one. You
now want to see: in every instant, in every speck of dust, it is halfway along the road. It is commonly said: one mote, one
land; one leaf, one Shakyamuni. Throughout the three-thousand great-thousand worlds, all the fine
dust—see it only within a single mote. Even then, you are still halfway along the road. Over there
there is still another halfway along the road. Tell me, where is it? Even old Shakyamuni himself
does not know—how could this mountain monk possibly speak it? Eye-film.One scheme cuts off the eye disease.

Instruction says: where there is Buddha, you must not stay; if you stay, horns and a head grow. Where there is no Buddha,
hurry past. If you do not pass, the grass is a fathom deep. Even if you are clean nakedness, bare and free,
outside of affairs there is no function, outside of function there are no affairs—still you cannot avoid guarding a stump waiting for a rabbit. Tell me,
if it is not like this at all, how do you walk it? Try to raise it and see.

Case 95. Changqing once said, Better to say an arhat has the three poisons than to say the Tathagata has two kinds of speech; it is not that the Tathagata has noBurnt grain does not sprout
shoots.This is already slandering old man Śākyamuni.
speech, only that there are not two kinds of speech.Still you remain muddleheaded—already riddled through seven times and holed through eight.A complete mess. What talk of a third and fourth kind?
Baofu said, What is Tathagata speech? Changqing said, DeafA fine press—what do you say?
people—how could they hear it? Baofu said, I know you have gone to theMaking proclamations to empty space—shattered into seven blossoms and eight cracks.
second head. Changqing said, What is
How could you fool a clear-eyed person? Split and twist the nostrils—far beyond merely the second head.
Tathagata speech? Baofu said, Go drink tea.Wrong—yet it is a bit closer.Take it up. Again he said: Do you understand? You have missed it.

Changqing and Baofu were in Xuefeng’s assembly, often raising things with each other for reflection and discussion. One
day he said as usual, Better to say an arhat has the three poisons, not
to say the Tathagata has two kinds of speech. In Sanskrit, arhat; here it means slayer of thieves. It is named by
