its function and capability: it can cut off the nine times nine, eighty-one grades of afflictions. All outflows have already
ended; the holy conduct has been established. This is the position of a no-more-learning arhat. The three poisons are
greed, anger, and delusion—the root afflictions. Even the eighty-one grades are cut off; how much more
the three poisons. Changqing said, Better to say an arhat has the three poisons than to say the Tathagata
has two kinds of speech. The main intent is to reveal that the Tathagata has no untrue speech. The Lotus Sutra
says, Only this one matter is true; the other two are not real. It also says, There is only one vehicle
dharma, not two and not three. Across more than three hundred assemblies, the World-Honored One observed capacities and applied teachings, matching
sickness with medicine, teaching the dharma in ten thousand ways—yet in the end there are not two kinds of speech. His
intent reaches here—how do you all see it? The Buddha expounds the dharma with a single sound;
that is not absent. But Changqing has not even dreamed of Tathagata speech yet—why?
It is like someone talking about food and never becoming full. Baofu saw him preaching on level ground and
so asked, What is Tathagata speech? Changqing said, Deaf people—how could they hear it?
This fellow—who knows how long he has been making a living in a ghost cave. Baofu
He said: I knew you would head for the second point—sure enough you hit the mark. Then he asked the senior brother,
What is the Tathagata’s speech? Baofu said: Go drink tea. Instead, the spear point was seized by someone else
and taken away. Big Changqing—losing money and incurring punishment. Now I ask all of you:
How many kinds of Tathagata’s speech are there? You must see it like this to see these two
fellows’ defects. If you examine it carefully, they both deserve a beating. Letting
a single thread out so they can understand—some say Baofu spoke rightly and Changqing spoke
wrongly. They just keep forming interpretations from the words, saying there is gain and there is loss—without realizing
that the ancients were like striking sparks from stone, like the flash of lightning. People today do not look where
the ancients turned; they only run under the phrases, and then say that Changqing at the time
did not use it well, so he fell into the second point, while Baofu’s “Go drink tea” is
the first point. If you only look at it this way, even when Maitreya descends you still will not see
the ancients’ intent. A master would never make such an understanding; he would leap out of this
nest and pit—above there is naturally a road. If you say, how could a deaf person hear,
what is not the case? When Baofu says “Go drink tea,” what is the case?
Turn it and there is no connection. Therefore it is said: He studies living phrases, not dead phrases. This causal
nexus is the same as “cause-and-conditions through the whole body.” There is no place for you to calculate right
and wrong. You must stand on your own heels, completely bare, and only then see where the ancients met
each other. The Fifth Patriarch, the old master, said: It is like grappling in front of a horse—you must have eyes
to discern and hands that are intimate. If you view this public case with the true eye, there is no place of gain or loss at all.
If you distinguish gain and loss, there is no place of closeness or distance; if you divide closeness and distance, Changqing too must
bow to Baofu to make it so. Why? This little bit is used well at the skillful point,
like lightning turning and stars flying. Baofu is not hindered from growing teeth on teeth, claws on
claws. The verse says:

lg.l:
First and second points—crouching dragon,In my king’s treasury there is no such thing. A model for past and present—why follow the crooked and chase the evil?
does not mirror itself in still water.Only those on the same path know.

Where there is no dragon, moon-waves are clear.Across the four seas, a lone boat travels by itself—divining and calculating is vain labor.
What bowl are you looking for?

Where there is a dragon, windless waves rise—edgy Chan traveler,It frightens people to death. Do you still feel your hairs stand on end? He struck and said: Here it comes!
edgy Chan traveler,Colluding with thieves ruins the household. In a noisy market do not stick your head out—lose money and take the blame.

in the third month, at Yu Gate, his forehead is marked.Step yourself back,
yield to others—one in ten thousand. You can only swallow your breath and swallow your voice.

cb:div.p:
First and second points—people only manage to understand first and second; that is exactly
making a living in dead water. This mechanism—if you only take it as first and second,
you still cannot grope it out. Xuedou said: The crouching dragon does not mirror itself in still water. In dead
water how could there be a dragon’s lair? If it is first and second, that is exactly making a living in
still water. You must be in vast flooding waves, white breakers reaching heaven—only then is there a dragon’s
lair. Just like earlier it said: A clear pool does not allow an azure dragon to coil. Haven’t you seen it said: Dead
water does not hide a dragon. And it is also said: The crouching dragon always fears the clear blue pool. Therefore it is said:
Where there is no dragon, moon-waves are clear—wind calm and waves still. Where there is a dragon, without wind waves rise.
It is just like Baofu saying “Go drink tea”—exactly windless waves rising. Here Xuedou
at this point, all at once packs up your emotional interpretations and versifies it. He has lingering
resonance, forming it into coherent writing; as before, he stays within it and sets one eye there—also not amiss,
extraordinary—yet he says: edgy Chan traveler, edgy Chan traveler, in the third month at Yu Gate his forehead is marked.
Although Changqing is a dragon that passed through Dragon Gate, he was suddenly marked on the forehead by Baofu.
Case 96. Raised: Zhaozhou presented the assembly with three turning phrases.Say what? Three sections are not the same.

After Zhaozhou presented these three turning phrases, at the end he said: Sit in the house of the true Buddha.
That line is far too casual. The ancients opened one eye and, with hands hanging down, received
people. Borrowing this phrase a bit, he passed a message through, wanting to guide people. If you always
fully raise the true command, the grass in front of the Dharma hall will be one zhang deep. Xuedou disliked his final
line for leaking and dangling, so he cut it off and only versified the three lines. If a clay Buddha crosses water,
it rots away. If a golden Buddha crosses through a furnace, it melts away. If a wooden
Buddha crosses fire, it burns up. What is hard to understand? In Xuedou’s one
hundred verses on old cases, full of calculations and vine-tangling, only these three verses straightaway have the breath of a patched-robed monk.
Even so, these verses are still not easy to understand. If you can penetrate these three
verses, then I allow you to stop your wandering study.

lg.l:
A clay Buddha does not cross water; Shen Guang illuminates heaven and earth.Soaking the nostrils till they rot—raising waves without wind.What does it have to do with him? Seeing the rabbit,
you release the hawk.

If standing in snow had not ceased, who would notOne person passes on the empty, ten thousand pass on the solid—turning mistake into mistake. Who has ever seen you come
carve and counterfeit?into the monastery to look at the plaque? In the twelve two-hour periods, what is this running up and down? Venerable, that is you.

cb:div.p:
A clay Buddha does not cross water; Shen Guang illuminates heaven and earth. This line of verse is clear enough.
But tell me, why does it bring in Shen Guang? When the Second Patriarch was first born, a divine light shone
through the room and reached the sky. And one night a spirit appeared and said to the Second Patriarch: Why have you long
stayed here? The time for you to attain the Way has arrived; you should go south at once. The Second Patriarch, meeting the divine,
was thus named Shen Guang. He long lived in the Yi and Luo region, exhaustively studying many books, often sighing: The teachings of Confucius
and Lao only transmit the models and rules of the ancestors. Recently I heard that Master Bodhidharma resides at Shaolin, so
I went there morning and evening to inquire. Bodhidharma sat upright facing the wall, and no instruction was heard. Guang
thought to himself: People of old seeking the Way knocked bone to bring out marrow, pricked blood to relieve hunger, spread their hair
to cover mud, threw themselves off cliffs to feed tigers—if the ancients were like this, what about me? That year, on the ninth
night of the twelfth month, there was heavy snow. The Second Patriarch stood below the steps; by dawn the snow had piled up
past his knees. Bodhidharma pitied him and said: You stand in snow here—what matter do you seek? The Second
Patriarch wept and said: I only beg compassion to open the gate of sweet dew and widely ferry all beings. Bodhi-
dharma said: The Buddhas’ wondrous Way requires diligence over vast kalpas—doing what is hard to do, enduring what is not to be endured. How could you,
with small virtue and small wisdom, with a frivolous mind and arrogant mind, hope for the true vehicle? There is no
such place. Hearing this admonition, the Second Patriarch’s resolve for the Way grew more urgent. He secretly took a sharp knife and cut off
his left arm, placing it before Bodhidharma. Bodhidharma knew he was a vessel of the Dharma and asked: You
stood in snow and cut off your arm—what is it for? The Second Patriarch said: My mind is not at peace; I beg
the master to pacify my mind. Bodhidharma said: Bring me your mind, and I will pacify it for you. The patriarch said: Searching for the mind,
it cannot be found. Bodhidharma said: I have pacified your mind for you. Later Bodhidharma changed his
name to Huike. Later he received the Third Patriarch, Master Can. After transmitting the Dharma, he hid away at
Wangong Mountain in Shuzhou. During the time when Emperor Wu of Later Zhou destroyed Buddhism and purged monks,
the master traveled to and from Sikong Mountain in Taihu County, living without a fixed place, for more than ten years,
with no one knowing. In Master Xuan’s Biographies of Eminent Monks, the Second Patriarch’s affairs are not detailed. The Third
Patriarch’s record says: The Second Patriarch’s wondrous Dharma was not transmitted in the world; only because at the end one still relied on the former
did he awaken to that standing in snow. Therefore Xuedou said: If standing in snow had not ceased, who
would not carve and counterfeit? If standing in snow had not ceased, people of excessive deference and flattery would all imitate it,
and all at once it would only become carved counterfeit—mere flattery and fraud. Xuedou’s verse
says: A clay Buddha does not cross water—why then does it bring in this causal story to use? He investigated
until beneath the root of intention there was not a single speck of matter; only then could he versify like this, on naked, bare ground.
The Fifth Patriarch often taught people to look at these three verses. Haven’t you seen that early on, Master Dongshan
also had a verse instructing the assembly, saying: On Mount Wutai, clouds steam rice; before the old Buddha hall,
the sky is dog piss. On top of the temple flagpole, a frying pan; three barbarian brats at night winnow
coins. And Master Dushun said: In Huaizhou, cattle eat grain; in Yizhou, horses’ bellies swell.
Searching the world for a doctor, you moxa a pig’s left foreleg. And Layman Fu’s verse says: Empty-
handed, he holds a hoe; walking, he rides a water buffalo. People pass over the bridge; the bridge flows
but the water does not flow. And he also said: The stone man’s mechanism is like yours; he too can sing Ba songs. If you are like
the stone man, you should harmonize with the snow song. If you can understand these words, then you will understand Xuedou’s
verses completely.Turning the tree upside down.

lg.l:
A golden Buddha does not cross a furnace; someone comes to visit Zihu.Singeing off your eyebrows. Above heaven and below earth, I alone am honored.Off you go again like this,
only fearing you will lose your body and lose your life.

A few characters on a signboard,Even a cat that cannot read has no place to understand the words. Patch-robed monks under heaven cannot get a word in—only fearing you will lose
your body and lose your life.

where is there no pure breeze?And off you go again like this. Endless above the head, endless under the feet. Again he said: Here it comes.Door-plank.
Cutting clearly into the face.

cb:div.p:
A golden Buddha does not cross a furnace; someone comes to visit Zihu. This one line is also versified.
Why then bring in “someone comes to visit Zihu”? Only a master’s furnace and bellows will do
it. Zihu once set it up: at the monastery gate a signboard was erected, and on it were written the characters: Zihu
There is a dog: above it takes a man's head, in the middle it takes a man's waist, below it takes a man's feet. If you speculate,
you lose your body and your life. Whenever he saw a newly arrived monk he would shout: Watch the dog! As soon as the monk turned
his head, Zihu would return to the abbot's room. Tell me, why could it not bite Zhaozhou?
Zihu again, one night deep in the dark, called out from behind the racks: Catch the thief, catch the thief!
In the pitch dark he met a monk, grabbed him across the chest and said: Caught him, caught him!
The monk said: Venerable, it is not me. Hu said: It is, it is—only you refuse
to take responsibility. If you can understand this saying, I will allow you to bite to death everyone. Everywhere
the pure wind is sharp and bracing. If it is not so, a few words on a placard will certainly not
do anything. If you want to see it, only when you penetrate it completely will you see. The verse says:

lg.l:
A wooden Buddha does not pass through fire; always think of breaking the stove and letting it collapse.Burn it up as well—only I can know.Going east, going west—what
is not allowed? Leprous children tugging companions.

When the staff suddenly strikes, only then you knowIn this mountain monk’s hands—the mountain monk uses no one. In whose hands is there not
you have betrayed me.someone like you? You cannot grope it out—what use is it? Blue sky, blue sky—only after thirty years will it do. Better to sink for endless kalpas than
seek liberation from the sages. If you can realize it here, you still cannot avoid betraying it. How do you manage not to betray it? The staff inevitably ends up in someone else’s hands.

cb:div.p:
A wooden Buddha does not pass through fire; always think of breaking the stove and letting it collapse. This one line is also versified. Xue
Dou, because of this saying, A wooden Buddha does not pass through fire; always think of breaking the stove and letting it collapse, tells of Songshan's Stove-Breaking
Venerable, who did not give his surname or personal name. His words and actions were unfathomable; he lived in seclusion on Mount Song. One day
he led his disciples into a mountain hollow where there was a very efficacious shrine. In the hall there was only a single stove installed. From far
and near the sacrifices never ceased, and they cooked and killed many living creatures. The master entered the shrine and with his
staff struck the stove three times, saying: Hey! You are originally made of brick and clay—where does your numinous power come from?
Where does your holiness arise? You have them cook and kill living creatures like this! Then he struck it three more times, and the stove
toppled over and broke, collapsing. After a short while there was a person in green robes with a tall cap, who suddenly stood
before the master, bowed, and said: I am the stove spirit. I have long borne karmic retribution. Today I have received the master's
teaching of the unborn dharma; I have already been freed from this place, and am born in the heavens. I have come especially to give thanks. The master
said: Your nature was originally so; it is not something I forced with words. The spirit bowed again and vanished. The attendant
said: We have long attended and studied under the master, yet have not received instruction. By what direct path did the stove spirit gain
the essential point, and immediately be born in the heavens? The master said: I only said to it: You are originally made of brick and clay—
where does your numinous power come from? where does your holiness arise? The attendant monks all had no reply. The master
said: Do you understand? The monk said: I do not understand. The master said: Keep bowing. The monk bowed. The master
said: Broken, broken; collapsed, collapsed. The attendant suddenly had a great awakening. Later there was
a monk who brought this up to National Teacher Anguo. The teacher sighed and said: This fellow fully understood that things and self are one thus.
The stove spirit awakening to this is of course so. That monk is a body formed of the five aggregates, and he too said: broken
and collapsed. Both opened awakening. Now, are the four elements and five aggregates the same as, or different from, bricks, tiles, and mud?
Are they the same or different? Since it is like this, why does Xuedou say: When the staff suddenly
strikes, only then you know you have betrayed me? Why does it become a betrayal? It is only
that the staff has not yet been obtained. Tell me, when Xuedou versifies A wooden Buddha does not pass through fire, why
does he bring in the case of Stove-Breaking Venerable? This old monk will tell you straight: his
intent is only to cut off notions of gain and loss, emotional dust and imaginings. Naked and cleanly, you will naturally see
the intimate place.

The instruction says: Pick up one and put down one—this is not yet an adept. Raise one and illuminate three—still it deviates
from the main point. Even if heaven and earth change and the four directions fall silent; thunder runs, lightning darts, clouds
move and rain lashes; pools overturn, peaks topple; jars pour out, basins tip over—you still have not lifted even half
of it. Is there anyone who can turn the heavenly barrier and move the earth's axle? Try to raise it and see.

Case 97. The Diamond Sutra says: If one is despised by others, that personLet out a single thread—what harm is there?
in a former life had karma of wrongdoing that should fall into an evil path; because in the present life people despise him,Donkeys, camels, and horses carry it,yet it falls into a pit.
therefore the karma of wrongdoing from former lives is therebyRepaying root and branch alike—you can only endure it.Where will you grope for it? Plant grain and it will not sprout bean shoots.
extinguished.Frost added atop snow, another layer; like hot broth melting ice.

The Diamond Sutra says: If one is despised by others, that person in a former life had karma of wrongdoing that should fall
into an evil path; because in the present life people despise him, the karma of wrongdoing from former lives is thereby extinguished.
Going only by ordinary explication, this is a common discussion in the sutra. Xuedou picked it up to versify this
intent, wanting to smash the livelihoods in the doctrinal school's ghost cave. Prince Zhaoming classified this
section as being able to purify karmic obstacles. The general intent in the teachings says this sutra has efficacy. Such a
person in a former life created karma for hell; because the power of goodness was strong, it had not yet been received. Because in the present
life people despise him, the former-life karma is extinguished. Therefore this sutra can
extinguish the karma of wrongdoing from immeasurable kalpas, turning heavy into light, turning light so it is not received, and again obtain
the Buddha fruit of awakening. According to the doctrinal school, to recite these twenty-odd pages of sutra is then called
holding the sutra. What does that have to do with it? Some say the sutra itself has efficacy. If so,
then try putting a scroll in an idle place and see—does it have any response or not?
Fayuan said: To realize the Buddha ground is called holding this sutra. In the sutra it says: All
Buddhas, and the dharma of all Buddhas' unsurpassed, complete awakening, all come from this
sutra. Now tell me, what do you call this sutra? Is it the yellow scroll with red roller-ends
kind? Do not mistakenly take the fixed star for it. Diamond is a metaphor for the dharma body's solidity:
therefore things cannot destroy it. Because it is useful, it can crush all things. If you set it against a mountain, the mountain
is crushed; set it against the sea, and the sea is drained. Taking the metaphor to reveal the name, the dharma is also thus. This prajna
has three kinds: first, real-mark prajna; second, contemplative prajna; third, textual
prajna. Real-mark prajna is true wisdom—it is the great matter beneath all your heels.
It shines through past and present, utterly beyond knowledge and views. Naked and clean, bare and bright
is it. Contemplative prajna is true realm—throughout the twelve periods of day and night, it
emits light and moves the earth; hearing sounds and seeing forms is it. Textual prajna is that which can explicate
words and letters—namely, the speaker and listener right now. Now tell me, is it prajna or is it not prajna?
The ancients said: Everyone has one scroll of sutra. They also said: The hand does not hold a
sutra scroll, yet constantly turns such a sutra. If you go by the sutra's efficacy, how could it be only turning heavy to make
it light, turning light so it is not received? Even if it could match a sage, it would still not be wondrous. Do you not see
Layman Pang listening to a lecture on the Diamond Sutra, asking the seat-master: This layman dares to have a small
question—may I? The master said: If you have doubts, ask. Pang said: No self-mark
and no person-mark. Since there are no self and person marks, who is there to lecture, and who is there to listen? The seat-master
had no reply, and instead said: I interpret meaning based on the text; I do not know this intent. The layman then
had a verse: No self and also no person—how could there be distant and close? I urge you, do not go through
the seat; how could it compare to directly seeking the true? The nature of diamond prajna is beyond even the slightest speck of dust. I
hear and believe and accept—altogether these are provisional names. This verse is best; clearly in one
moment it is said. Guifeng classified the four-line verse: All forms whatsoever are
illusory; if you see all forms as no-form, you then see the Tathagata. The meaning of these four lines
is entirely the same as To realize the Buddha ground is called holding this sutra. It also says: If by form you see me,
by sound you seek me, that person walks a deviant path and cannot see the Tathagata. This too
is a four-line verse. But to take the one whose meaning is complete: a monk asked Huitang:
What is the four-line verse? Huitang said: Even if words collapse, you still do not know. Here, regarding this
sutra, Xuedou points out: If someone holds this sutra, it is precisely all of your native
scenery, your original face. If you go by the Patriarchs' command in effect, this native scenery, this original face
is also cut into three sections. The Buddhas of the three times and the twelvefold teachings
cannot dissolve even a pinch. When you arrive here, even if you had ten thousand kinds of abilities, you still could not manage
it. People today just keep reciting sutras, completely unaware what principle this is.
They just say: How many have I recited in a day? They only recognize the yellow scrolls and red roller-ends, going around
counting ink marks. They do not know it all arises from one's own original mind. This is only
a bit of the turning-place. Master Dazhu said: If you pile up several chests of sutras in an empty room
and look at them, do they emit light? It is only that a single thought from your own mind is merit
and virtue. Why? The ten thousand dharmas all come from the mind itself. One thought is numinous; being numinous, it is
unobstructed; being unobstructed, it transforms. The ancients said: The green green bamboo is entirely true thusness; the lush
yellow flowers are nothing but prajna. If you see through it thoroughly, it is true thusness. If you suddenly
have not seen it, then tell me: how do you call it true thusness? The Avatamsaka Sutra says: If
a person wishes to fully know all Buddhas of the three times, he should contemplate the nature of the dharma realm: all is only
mind-made. If you can recognize it, then meeting situations and conditions you are the host, you are the core. If you have not
yet been able to make it clear, then submit and listen to the disposition. Xuedou opens his eye and versifies the general outline, wanting to clarify
It is the scripture’s wondrous efficacy. The verse says:

lg.l:
A bright pearl in the palm—reward those with merit.Above it reaches the sky; below it penetrates the Yellow Springs. Say what? Wrong on four sides, yet nimble on eight faces.
How much is clear! Let him go. If there is suddenly no merit, how do you reward it?

When neither barbarian nor Han comes, entirelyNews cut off within and without—still not quite enough.
there are no tricks.Turned around and around, it has nothing to do with it. Where will you grope for it? Smash the lacquer bucket and come meet face to face.

Since there are no tricks,Stop, rest. Who
says it like that?

Papiyas loses the way—Gautama, Gautama,Examined through, it is broken open. This heterodox demon king—you search his tracks and cannot find them.Even Buddha-eye
cannot see it. Bah!
do you recognize me or not?Bah! Examined through, it is broken open.

cb:div.p:
Again he said: It has been seen through.One blow, one stripe
of a mark—already before the words.

A bright pearl in the palm—reward those with merit. If someone can uphold this scripture and has merit
that proves true, then reward them with the pearl. Having obtained this pearl, they will naturally know how to use it. When a Hu comes,
a Hu appears; when a Han comes, a Han appears. The ten thousand forms, dense and manifold, appear freely in all directions. This is
merit. Fayan said: One who realizes the Buddha-ground is called one who upholds this scripture. These two lines of verse—
the case is finished. When neither Hu nor Han comes, there are utterly no tricks. Xuedou twists and tears at the nostrils.
If Hu and Han do come, then he has you make them appear. But if suddenly both Hu and Han do not come,
then what? When you reach here, even the Buddha-eye cannot see it. Tell me: is it merit
or is it karma of wrongdoing? Is it Hu or is it Han? It is like an antelope hanging by its horns—don’t say there is no
sound, no trace, no breath. Where will you grope for it? This leaves the gods
holding flowers with no road, and Mara and outsiders peeking with no gate. Therefore Master Dongshan, for
his whole life residing in a monastery, could not be tracked by the local earth deity. One day, in front of the kitchen he scattered
rice and flour. Dongshan had a thought and said: These are communal supplies—how can you waste them like
this? The earth deity then managed to catch sight of him and immediately bowed in worship. Xuedou said: Since there are no tricks,
if you reach this place of no tricks, you make even Papiyas lose the way. The World-Honored One
takes all beings as his own children. If even one person arouses the resolve to practice, the palace of
Papiyas is shaken and cracked. Then he comes to harass the practitioner. Xuedou
said: Even if Papiyas comes like that, you must still make him lose the road, with nowhere nearby
to cling to. Xuedou further pointed to his own chest and said: Gautama, Gautama—do you recognize me or not?
Don’t say it is only Papiyas—let the Buddha himself come; would he recognize me or not? Even old Shakyamuni
does not see it. Where will you all grope for it? Again he said: It has been seen through.
Tell me: did Xuedou see through Gautama, or did Gautama see through Xuedou?
Those with eyes, try to decide it and see.

The pointing instruction says: All summer long, chattering and tangling vines, nearly tripping the monks from the five lakes.
The vajra precious sword cuts right across the top; only then do you realize you have never been able to do a hundred things. Tell me,
what is the vajra precious sword? Blink above the eyebrows—try showing the tip of its blade.

Case 98. Raised: When Master Tianping was traveling, he visited Xiyuan. He often said: Don’t say you
understand the Buddha Dharma; you can’t even find someone to raise a phrase.Nothing is leaked or let slip. This fellow is what he is, but what can you do—like the spirit tortoise dragging its tail,
One day Xiyuan saw him from afar and called out. Tianping raised his head. Xiyuan said: Wrong.he has already been caught by the rattle-hook and rope.Caught. A double
case.It still must be forged in the furnace. Splitting the belly, gouging the heart. The three essentials seal open, the vermilion points are tight; before there is room to deliberate, host and guest are
Tianping walked three or two steps. Xiyuan again said: Wrong. Tianping came closer. Xiyuan said: Those two wrongs just now—were they Xiyuan’s wrong, or the senior’salready half ahead and half behind. This fellow is washing a lump of dirt in mud.Splitting the belly,
gouging the heart—people all call it a double-layer case, not realizing it is like water entering water, like gold striking gold.As before, he does not know where it lands, turning around and groping
and still cannot find it.
wrong? Tianping said: From the start, wrong. Xiyuan said: Wrong. Tianping was leaving. Xiyuan said: Stay here and pass theThe first arrow is still light; the later arrow is deep.Mistaking the saddle-bridge, calling it the old man’s lower jaw. With such a patch-
robed monk, killing a thousand or ten thousand—what crime would there be?Frost added on top of snow.Mistaking the fixed
star as certain—indeed he does not know where it lands. And yet you know your nostrils are in someone else’s hands.
summer; I’ll discuss these two wrongs with you. Tianping immediately left. Later, after he took charge of a monastery, he told the assembly,The West Courtyard’s backbone is ordinarily hard as iron—why at that time did he not drive him
out?He also seems like a patch-robed monk. He seems, yes, but he is not yet.
saying: When I was traveling at first, I was blown by karmic winds and arrivedA poor child thinking of old debts—still it must be checked off.
at Elder Siming’s place. He hit me with two wrongs in a row, and even kept me over the summer to
discuss them with me. I didn’t say I was wrong then. When I set out and headed south,
I already knew I was wrong.But what can you do about these two mistakes? A thousand mistakes, ten thousand mistakes—still irrelevant. It only makes you more sloppy, worrying you to death
people.It should read: Qie Shuiwen.

Siming first studied under Dajue, and later became heir to Bao Shou. One day he asked: When you have trampled through
the city of illusions and come out, how is it? Bao Shou said: A sharp sword does not cut a dead man. Siming said: Cut.
Bao Shou then struck him. Siming said cut ten times; Bao Shou struck ten times, saying: This fellow—
why be in such a hurry to die? Taking a corpse to meet the painful stick! And he drove him out with a shout. At that time
there was a monk who asked Bao Shou: The monk who just questioned you had a lot of sense.
The master skillfully received him. Bao Shou also struck and drove this monk out. Tell me: Bao
Shou also drove this monk out—was it only because he spoke right and spoke wrong, or was there some other
principle? What was the intent? Later, both became heirs to Bao Shou. One day Siming went out
to meet Nanyuan. Nanyuan asked: Where have you come from? Siming said: From Xuzhou. Nanyuan said:
What did you bring? Siming said: I brought a Jiangxi razor, to present to the
master. Nanyuan said: If you came from Xuzhou, why do you have a Jiangxi razor?
A-la-la-la. Tian-Siming pinched the West Courtyard’s hand once. The Courtyard said: Attendant, take it up. Siming brushed it once with his sleeve and left. The Courtyard said: Ah-la-la!
ping once studied with a mountain master. Because of that, as he went around to various places, he picked up some radish-
head Zen in his belly, and everywhere he casually opened his big mouth saying: I understand Zen,
I understand the Way. He often said: Don’t say you understand the Buddha Dharma; you can’t even find someone to raise a phrase. The stink of
dung fumes people. He just kept flaunting shallowness. But tell me: before the Buddhas appeared in the world, before the ancestral
teacher came from the West, before there were questions and answers, before there were public cases—was there still Zen
and the Way? The ancients, unable to avoid it, pointed according to the situation; later people called it a public
case. Because the World-Honored One held up a flower and Kashyapa smiled; later Ananda asked Kashyapa:
Besides the robe, what Dharma did the World-Honored One transmit separately? Kashyapa said: Ananda, Ananda.
Ananda responded. Kashyapa said: Knock down the temple flagpole in front of the gate. Just as before he held up the flower,
before Ananda asked—where would you get a public case? Yet you keep getting stamped and sealed by
pumpkin seals from all directions and then say: I understand the Buddha Dharma—how extraordinary. Don’t let
people know. Tianping was exactly like this. When Xiyuan called him over, he got two wrongs in a row, leaving him
utterly flustered and frightened, unable to sort it out—before him no village, behind him no inn.
Some say: The moment you speak of the meaning of coming from the West, you are already wrong. They do not realize where
Xiyuan’s two wrongs land. Tell me: where do they land? Therefore it is said:
He studied the living phrase, not the dead phrase. When Tianping raised his head, he had already fallen into the second, fallen into the
third. Xiyuan said: Wrong. Yet he did not grasp the function right on the spot; he only
said: I’ve got Zen in my belly—don’t mind him. Then he walked three or two steps again, and Xiyuan
again said: Wrong—still pitch-dark as ever. Tianping came closer. Xiyuan
said: Those two wrongs just now—were they Xiyuan’s wrong, or the senior’s wrong? Tianping said:
From the start, wrong. Fortunately it has nothing to do with me. He was already on the seventh head, the eighth head.
Xiyuan said: Stay here and spend the summer; I’ll discuss these two
wrongs with you. Tianping left right then. It seemed like it, and yet it wasn’t; it was, and yet not quite; nor
could you say it wasn’t—he just couldn’t keep up. Even so, there was still a bit of
the breath of a patched monk. Later, after Tianping took charge of a monastery, he told the assembly: When I was traveling at first,
I was blown by karmic winds and arrived at Master Siming’s place. He hit me with two wrongs in a row, and even kept
me over the summer to discuss them with me. I didn’t say I was wrong then. When I set
out and headed south, I already knew I was wrong. This fellow really said it.
He only fell into the seventh head and the eighth head—trying to shake it off, claiming it had nothing to do with him. Nowadays when people hear
him say: When I set out and headed south, I already knew I was wrong—they then go
and speculate, saying: Before traveling, there wasn’t so much Buddha Dharma and Zen Way. But once you
travel, you get hoodwinked by the heat of the various places. If, before traveling, you called earth
heaven and called mountains water, luckily there wasn’t the tiniest matter. If you take it all in such a vulgar
way, why not just buy a hat and wear it, and everyone gets by—what use
is there? The Buddha Dharma is not this principle. If you discuss this matter, how could there be so many
tangling vines? If you say I understand and he doesn’t, carrying a shoulder-pole of Zen, you run around under
heaven—once a clear-eyed person sees through you, not a bit of it is usable. Xuedou is
exactly so, and he versifies:

lg.l:
Zen folk drift into shallow fondness, their bellies full of it;A lacquer bucket. Take it over in one strokeand there is something to it. Cursing Buddha, cursing the patriarchs—like hemp, like millet.
after all their studying, it is of no use.It should only be used where it is useful. A square peg does not fit a round hole. Acarya, you investigate along with him—

Worth grieving, worth laughing—
old Tianpingpatch-robed monks under heaven cannot jump out. Not fearing others knitting their brows, it still makes people dull and stifled.

yet said he regretted, back then, setting out to travel,
on foot.Before he even went on pilgrimage, he had already been wrong. What use is it to wear through straw sandals? Under a single stroke of the brush and a single phrase—
Wrong, wrong.what is it? Xuedou has already wrongly set down fine words.

At West Cloister, the pure breeze suddenly melts it away.Where is the West Courtyard? How is it, sir? Do not say West Courtyard—Buddhas of the three times and the old masters under heaven
must also retreat three thousand li. If you understand here, I allow you to roam freely under heaven.

cb:div.p:
Again he said: suddenly a patch-robed monk stepped out and said,
Wrong—Xuedou is wrong. How does that compare to Tianping’s wrong?Take it over in one stroke—still somewhat better.If the West Courtyard came forth in the world again, deciding by the evidence,
closing the case—it would still be irrelevant. Tell me, ultimately how is it? He strikes and says: Wrong.

Chan folk are faddish and shallow; their bellies are full of practice, yet none of it can be used. This fellow understands if he
understands, but he cannot employ it. Usually he stares up into the clouds and says, How much Chan has he
understood? Yet once he is cooked on the brazier, it turns out he cannot use even a bit of it.
The former master, the Fifth Patriarch, said: there are people who study Chan like pounding
rice cake inside a glass bottle—you cannot shift it at all; you cannot shake it out; touch it and it breaks.
If you want it lively and nimble, just study shell-and-husk, leaky-basket Chan—go straight to a high mountain
and throw yourself down; neither broken nor harmed. The ancients said: even if you can realize it before words,
you are still stuck in the shell, deluded by the seal; even if you are perfectly clear beneath the phrase, you still cannot avoid
wild views whenever you meet things. Pitiful and laughable is old Tianping, who then said he regretted traveling
on pilgrimage back then. Xuedou said: pitiful that he could not speak it to others; laughable that he understood a whole
bellyful of Chan yet could not put even a little of it to use. Wrong, wrong—these two wrongs. Some say,
Tianping not understanding is the wrong. Others say: having no words is the wrong. What
does it have to do with it? They do not know that these two wrongs are like sparks struck from stone, like a flash of lightning.
This is the course and footing of one who is above: like taking up a sword to cut a man, going straight for his throat—
only then is the life-root severed. If you can walk on this sword’s edge, then in seven releases and eight
crossings, if you understand the two wrongs, then you can see how at West Cloister the pure breeze suddenly melts it away.
Xuedou ascended the hall and raised this story, intending to say Wrong. I ask you: Xue-
dou’s two wrongs—how do they compare to Tianping’s wrong? Study it for thirty years.

The instruction said: when the dragon chants, mists rise; when the tiger roars, winds are born. The guiding principles that transcend the world ring like gold and jade,
answering each other. The strategies of one who penetrates all directions meet like arrowheads braced tip to tip. Throughout the whole realm nothing is hidden; far and near alike
stand revealed. Past and present are clearly discerned. Tell me: whose sphere is this? Try to bring it up and see.

Case 99. Emperor Suzong asked National Teacher Zhong: What is the regulating and taming of the ten bodies?
The National Teacher said: Patron, you treadA master-hand king, the Son of Heaven of Great Tang, should also know it like this: a rolled-wheel crown on the head, worry-free shoes under the feet.
atop Vairocana’s crown. The emperor said: I do notBeyond Sumeru, hand in hand we walk—this still remains.
understand. The National Teacher said: Do not takeWhy not take up the words? What a pity. Luckily it was not handed to the emperor—at that time he would have shouted; what more is there to understand?
your own pure Dharma-body for it.Though it is vine-tangles, there is still a way out. After getting drunk, sloppy behavior worries people to death.

Emperor Suzong, while still in the Eastern Palace, had already studied with National Teacher Zhong. Later, after he took the throne,
his reverence only deepened; in coming and going, in greeting and escorting, he personally attended the carriage. One day he brought up
a question and asked the National Teacher: What is the regulating and taming of the ten bodies? The teacher said: Patron,
you tread atop Vairocana’s crown. All his life the National Teacher had a spine as hard
as raw iron; yet before an emperor he was like soft mud. Even though
his reply was thin and petty, it still had something good in it. He said: if you want to understand, patron,
you must tread atop Vairocana’s crown. Yet the emperor did not awaken, and instead said,
I do not understand. After that the National Teacher was far too careless and fell into the weeds, adding a sentence
on top of it: Do not mistake your own pure Dharma-body. It is said that every person
is fully endowed; each one is perfectly complete. Watch how he lets go and gathers in—meeting enemies from eight sides.
Did you not see it said: one who is good at being a teacher sets up instruction in response to the occasion, and trims the sail to the wind. If you only
cling stubbornly to one corner, how can you turn and respond? Look at old Huangbo—how well he could receive people.
When he met Linji, after three encounters he fiercely administered sixty blows; Linji immediately
understood. But when it came to Chancellor Pei, the entanglements were far too many—how is this not being good
at being a teacher? National Teacher Zhong, with skillful expedients, received Emperor Suzong, because he
had methods for facing eight-sided attack. The ten-body regulating and taming is precisely the ten kinds of
bodies of personal use. The three bodies—Dharma, reward, and transformation—are just the Dharma-body. Why is it that reward and transformation
are not the true Buddha, nor are they the one who speaks Dharma? As for the Dharma-body, it is a single expanse of empty stillness, numinously
bright, quiescent, and illuminating. Senior seat Fu of Taiyuan, at Guangxiao Monastery in Yangzhou, was lecturing on the Nirvana
Sutra. A traveling monk—Jiaoshan’s monastic cook—was snowed in at the monastery and went to listen
to the lecture. When the talk reached the three causes of Buddha-nature and the three virtues of the Dharma-body, he spoke at length of the wondrous principle of the Dharma-body.
The cook suddenly laughed. Fu glanced over at him. When the lecture ended, he had the Chan monk invited and asked,
saying: My understanding has always been narrow and poor; I explain meaning based on the text. Just now during the lecture, I saw you
laugh. I must have fallen short somewhere—please tell me. The cook said: If the lecturer
did not ask, I would not dare speak. Since you have asked, I cannot not speak. Truly,
I was laughing that you do not know the Dharma-body. Fu said: Explained like this, what place
is not it? The cook said: Please say it again. Fu said: The principle of the Dharma-body
is like the great void: vertically it exhausts the three times; horizontally it spans the ten directions; it pervades the eight
extremes, encompassing the two polarities; following conditions and responding to stimuli, it is nowhere not all-pervading. The cook said:
I do not say your words are wrong; you only know matters at the measure-and-boundary of the Dharma-body. In truth you have not
known where the Dharma-body is. Fu said: If so, then you, Chan monk, should explain it for me. The
cook said: If so, then lecturer, suspend your lectures for ten days; in a quiet room sit
upright in calm contemplation; gather the mind and collect the thoughts; let go of all conditions, good and bad, all at once; and investigate
to the end on your own. Fu followed his words; from the first watch of night until the fifth, hearing the drums and horns
sound, he suddenly accorded and awakened. He went and knocked at the Chan monk’s door. The cook said: Who is it?
Fu said: It is I. The cook snapped: I teach you to uphold the great teaching and speak Dharma in the Buddha’s stead—
why, in the middle of the night, are you like a drunk lying in the street? Fu said: All along, in lecturing on sutras, I have been
twisting and pinching the nostrils of my living parents. From today onward, I will not dare do so
again. Look at this extraordinary fellow—how could he merely recognize some bright, numinous awareness and fall
in front of donkeys and behind horses? You must shatter karmic consciousness—there must not be the slightest trace to be found.
Even then you only get half of it. The ancients said: do not raise the slightest mind of cultivating and learning; in the formless
light you are always at ease. Only recognize what is constantly quiescent extinction; do not mistake sounds and forms. Only
recognize numinous knowing; do not mistake deluded thoughts. Therefore it is said: even if an iron wheel spins atop your head,
samādhi and wisdom, perfectly bright, are never lost. Bodhidharma asked the Second Patriarch: You stood in snow and cut off your arm—
for what reason? The patriarch said: My mind is not at peace; I beg the master to pacify it. Dharma
said: Bring me your mind, and I will pacify it for you. The patriarch said: I searched for the mind, and it cannot be found. Dharma
said: I have pacified your mind for you. The Second Patriarch suddenly understood. Tell me, right at that moment,
where is the Dharma-body? Changsha said: People who study the Way do not recognize
the true, only because from long ago they recognized the spirit. The root of birth and death through countless kalpas—fools
call it the original person. People today only recognize some bright, numinous awareness, and then
stare wide-eyed, bulging their eyes, playing with spirit and soul—what does that have to do with it? Just as he said: do not
mistake your own pure Dharma-body. And yet as for your own Dharma-body, you have not even dreamed
of seeing it—so what is there to say about do not mistake? The doctrinal schools take the pure Dharma-body as the ultimate
standard—so why not teach people to recognize it? Did you not see it said: if you recognize it, as before
it is still not it—Bah! Fine, give him a blow. Only one who understands this intent will understand his saying,
Do not mistake your own pure Dharma-body. Xuedou disliked his old-womanly, over-fond heart—yet
what can you do, there are thorns in the mud. Did you not see that Master Dongshan had three
roads for receiving people: the mysterious road, the bird path, and the outstretched hand. Beginners who study the Way should for now walk these three
roads. A monk asked the master: You usually teach students to walk the bird path—what, precisely,
is the bird path? Dongshan said: You do not meet a single person. The monk said: How do you walk it?
Dongshan said: You must go with no private footing beneath your feet. The monk said: If one walks the bird path,
is that not the original face? Dongshan said: Venerable, why are you inverted?
The monk said: Where am I inverted? Dongshan said: If you are not inverted,
why do you take a servant for the lord? The monk said: What is the original face? Dong-
shan said: Not walking the bird path—you must see inversion at this level; only then is there a small measure
of correspondence. Even if you immediately pack it up, cut off traces, and swallow your voice, you are still within the gate of patch-robed monks.
The novice’s and trainee’s views are still there; you must turn your head back through dusty toil, and only then can great function flourish
in full. Xuedou’s verse says:

lg.l:
National teacher of a whole realm—yet it is a forced name; only Nanyang alone granted approval,Why insist on empty flowers and water-moon? When wind passes, the treetops sway.
and raised a fine reputation.Indeed he sits cutting off the vital pass. Among a thousand or ten thousand, it is hard to find even one or half of one.

Great Tang upheld a true Son of Heaven.
Poor fellow. Even if you receive it, what use is it? Receiving blind patch-robed monks—what does that accomplish?

He once walked upon the crown of Vairocana.Why do all people
not go like this? It reaches heaven above and earth below. Senior, how will you step

An iron hammer strikes and shatters the golden bones.freely and joyfully?
For a whole lifetime—it is already before the words.
Between heaven and earth, what else is there?Vast, vast—the four seas have few who know the tune. Bearing it with the whole body, scattering sand and flinging dirt.

In the sea of three thousand realms, the night is deep and dark; notSet your eyes high; hold the border firm. You are waiting to enter the ghost cave—those
knowing who has entered the azure dragon’s cave.thirty blows—one blow cannot be spared. I have picked it up; do you understand? Hah. Everyone’s nostrils have been pierced by Xue-
dou. Do not mistakenly take your own pure Dharma-body.

cb:div.p:
A teacher of one nation is also a forced name. Only Nanyang was uniquely approved, his fine reputation resounding. This verse is
just like a true portrait-praise. Haven’t you heard: the utmost person has no name; calling him “National Teacher”
is also forcing on a name. The Way of the National Teacher is beyond compare; he was truly able to
receive people like this. Only Nanyang was acknowledged as a master hand. Great Tang upheld the true
Son of Heaven. He once walked upon the crown of Vairocana. If you are a patch-robed monk with eyes, with a living head,
you must walk upon the crown of Vairocana—only then do you see these ten bodies being tamed and guided. The Buddha calls it
“taming and guiding,” one of the ten epithets. One body transforms into ten bodies; ten bodies
transform into a hundred bodies, up to thousands, hundreds of millions of bodies. In the main outline it is only one body. This
verse is easy enough to speak of. The later verse says: Do not mistake your own pure Dharma-body. That verse
cannot be splashed by water—truly hard to open the mouth and speak. An iron hammer strikes and shatters the golden
bones. This verse is: Do not mistake your own pure Dharma-body. Xuedou praised him far too much—
with one hammer-blow the golden bones are shattered. Between heaven and earth, what else is there?
You must be clean and naked, bare and bright—there is not a single thing to be grasped. This is the native
scenery. It is like: in the sea of three thousand realms, the night is deep and dark. In the trichiliocosm,
within the perfumed-water ocean there are boundless realms; each realm has an ocean. Precisely when
the night is still and deep, heaven and earth at once become clear and settled. Tell me, what is it?
Absolutely avoid taking it as an understanding of closing the eyes. If you understand it that way, you fall straight into a poison sea.
Not knowing who has entered the azure dragon’s cave—stretching the leg, drawing the leg back—tell me, who is it? All of you,
your nostrils have at once been pierced through by Xuedou.

The pointer said: Gather the cause and reap the result, exhaust the beginning and exhaust the end. Face-to-face there is no private bias; originally
nothing was ever said. Suddenly someone comes out and says: All summer I sought instruction—why have you never
said it? When you awaken, I will tell you. Tell me, is this concealing it right in front of your face,
or is there some other strength? Try raising it and see.

Case 100. A monk asked Baling: What is the hair-blowing sword? Baling said:Cutting risk.
Coral branches, each one holding up the moon.Light swallows ten thousand forms; the four seas and nine provinces

Baling did not stir spear or halberd—how many tongues fell to the ground across the four seas and five lakes.
Yunmen received people exactly like this. He was Yunmen’s son, and each also had his own
strategy. Therefore it is said: I love Shaoyang’s newly set pivot; all his life he pulled nails
and removed pegs for people. This saying is exactly like that. Within a single phrase, naturally
it contains three phrases: the phrase that encloses heaven and earth; the phrase that cuts off all streams; the phrase that follows the waves and runs with the currents.
To answer it is not without strangeness. Fushan Yuanlu said: For one who has not yet penetrated,
investigating the phrase is not as good as investigating the intent. For one who has penetrated, investigating the intent is not as good as investigating the
phrase. Under Yunmen there were three venerable elders who all answered the hair-blowing sword and said “finished.” Only
Baling answered beyond the word “finished.” This is obtaining the phrase. Tell me: is the word “finished”
and “coral branches, each one holding up the moon” the same or different? Earlier it said: The three phrases
can be distinguished. One arrowhead spans the open sky. To understand this saying, you must cut off emotional dust and ideation,
and only when utterly clean do you see him say: coral branches, each one holding up the moon. If you further make it into
doctrine, you only end up unable to grope it out. These words come from Chan Yue’s poem Thinking of a Friend, which says:
Thick as the iron atop Iron Encircling Mountain; thin as embroidered gauze on a twin immortal’s body; the Shu loom, phoenix
chicks stirring, stumbling and tottering—coral branches, each one holding up the moon. In Wang Kai’s house, the hidden one is hard to
dig out. Yan Hui, a hungry fellow, worries over sky-snow. The ancient cypress stands straight; thunder does not break it. The snow-
clad stone woman lacks the coiled-peach. Wearing it into the dragon palace, the steps go slow and slow. Embroidered curtains, silver mats—
how uneven. If you do not know the black dragon has lost its pearl, do you know or not know? Baling, within the phrase,
took one line to answer the hair-blowing sword—this is swift. Blow a hair onto the sword’s edge to test it;
the hair breaks of itself. A sharp sword is called hair-blowing. Baling simply, right at the place of the question,
answered this monk’s words—his head would fall and he would not know. The verse says:

lg.l: Seeking to level what is not level—great skill seems like clumsiness.are tiny as ants. A true man must be like this—without moving voice or color, hiding the body yet revealing the trace.

Sometimes finger, sometimes palm—leaning to the sky, it shines on snow; the great forge, ah,Look—indeed this is notcutting. If you look at it, you go blind.
cannot grind it down.What need is there for further forging? Even Ganjiang cannot come

The fine craftsman, ah, has not ceased wiping and polishing.
and no person can proceed. Even if Ganjiang came out, he would still retreat three thousand li.

cb:div.p:
Distinct, distinct—coral branches, each branchHah. Where is there any other place? Praise has its share.
holding up the moon.At the third watch the moon sets; shadows shine on the cold pool. Tell me, where does it go? It goes so far that all under heaven is at peace—after getting drunk, sloppy behavior worries people to death.
An ant.Pronounce: Pifu.

Seeking to level what is not level; great skill seems like clumsiness. In ancient times there were knights-errant who, seeing injustice on the road, used
the strong to oppress the weak, then sent a flying sword to take the strong one’s head. Therefore in the lineage teacher’s house, the brows hide
a precious sword and the sleeve hangs a golden hammer, to cut off affairs of injustice. Great skill seems like clumsiness—Ba-
ling’s answer, for this matter of leveling what is not level, was so extremely skillful that it instead
looks clumsy. Why? Because he did not swing it right in front of your face, but instead went to a secluded place
and, in one hidden cut, took a person’s head—and the person did not notice. Sometimes finger, sometimes palm—leaning to the sky,
it shines on snow. If you understand it, it is like a sky-leaning long sword, stern and awe-inspiring. The ancients said:
The mind-moon is solitary and round; its light swallows ten thousand forms. Light does not illuminate objects; objects do not remain. When light
and objects are both forgotten, what is it then? This precious sword may appear on a finger, and suddenly appear
in the palm. In the past, Steward Qing spoke to this point, raised his hand and said: Do you see it?
It also need not be on the fingers. Xuedou passes by, borrowing the road, to let you see
the ancients’ intent. Tell me, is there any place that is not the hair-blowing sword? Therefore
it is said: When the third-tier waves rise high, a fish transforms into a dragon; fools still bail water from a night pond. The Record of
the Ancestor’s Court Affairs Garden records in the Filial Son Biography: The consort of the King of Chu once, in summer, seeking coolness, embraced
an iron pillar and conceived. Later she gave birth to an iron lump. The King of Chu ordered Ganjiang to forge it into a sword.
After three years a pair of swords was made: one female, one male. Ganjiang secretly kept the male and presented the female
to the King of Chu. The king hid it in a case, and often heard a mournful cry. The king asked his ministers.
A minister said: The sword has female and male; the one that cries longs for the male. The king, greatly enraged, seized Gan-
jiang and killed him. Ganjiang knew what would come, so he hid the sword inside a house pillar, and instructed
his wife Moye, saying: When the sun rises at the north door, on the south mountain is a pine; the pine grows from stone; the sword
is within it. Later his wife bore a son named Red-Brow. At fifteen he asked his mother:
Where is my father? His mother recounted what happened before. After long reflection he split the pillar and got the sword, and day and night
wished to avenge his father. The King of Chu also offered rewards and sought him, proclaiming: Whoever captures Red-
Brow will be richly rewarded. Red-Brow then fled. Soon a guest said: You
are Red-Brow, are you not? He said: Yes. The guest said: I am from Zengshan, and I can
avenge your father. Red-Brow said: My father was innocent and was wrongfully harmed. Now you kindly
remember him—what do you require? The guest said: I must obtain your head and the sword. Red-Brow then gave him the sword
and his head. The guest took them and presented them to the King of Chu. The king was overjoyed. The guest said: I wish to boil oil
and cook it. The king then threw it into a cauldron. The guest told the king: That head does not rot.
The king came forward to look. From behind, the guest used the sword to strike at the king’s head and knocked it into the cauldron. Thereupon
the two heads bit each other. The guest feared Red-Brow would not prevail, so he cut his own throat to help him.
The three heads bit each other, and soon all rotted away. Xuedou said: This sword canIn the Sichuan edition there is no such passage about the King of Chu.
lean to the sky and shine on snow. It is commonly said: the light of a sky-leaning long sword can shine on snow. This use
goes so far that: the great forge cannot grind it down. Even if the fine craftsman keeps
wiping and polishing, it does not cease. The fine craftsman is Ganjiang. The old story makes itself clear. Xuedou
after chanting, finally makes it clear: distinct, distinct—also not without strangeness. There is a special
point, different from ordinary swords. Tell me, what is the special point? Coral branches,
each branch holding up the moon—one could say it surpasses what came before and cuts off what comes after, uniquely occupying the world, with no equal
match. Ultimately, how is it? All of you—your heads have fallen too. This old monk has another short verse:
To tell; to bite.Pronounce: dai, as in deceive.Pronounce: nie; it means to bite.

lg.l: Ten thousand hu fill the boat—casually taken in hand, yet because of a single grain, a jar swallows a snake.
Lifted and turned a hundred times, an old case—scattered among people today as grit in the eyes.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Blue Cliff Record, Volume Ten, by the Chan master Foguo YuanwuEnd.

cb:div.head: Postface
cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s one hundred verses on the ancients are essential pointers for studying the Way in the monasteries. Within them he draws analogies
from sutras and treatises, or from Confucian literature and history, to illuminate this matter. Without a lineage craftsman with eyes
who at times strikes and raises it up, dissecting it for later students, there is no way to know it.

When the elder teacher Yuanwu was in Chengdu, I and others requested instruction on his explanations.
Later, when the master lived at Jiashan Daolin, we again pressed him for it as students. In all, he raised the essentials of the school three times.
Though the words differed, the meaning was one. Disciples gathered them and recorded them. It has been twenty
years now, and the master has never corrected or questioned it. As it circulated in all directions, it sometimes became confused and faulty.
In various places, because people could not trace and unfold his Way from his words, they rashly
altered and rewrote them, and then this book was ruined. Students, please carefully examine its transmission.
Recorded by Ren Guan, You Wudang, on the last days of spring in the yisi year of the Xuanhe era.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the reprinting of the Blue Cliff Collection Commentary by the Chan master Yuanwu
cb:div.head: Preface to the reprinting of the Blue Cliff Collection Commentary by the Chan master Yuanwu
cb:div.p:
Xuedou’s one hundred verses on the ancients, with Yuanwu’s added annotations, are shown plainly to the monasteries.
They are a scripture that forever transmits the school’s aim. When students’ sharp points spring forth swiftly, Dahui in the private room
examined and discerned, and knew there was no real attainment, so he destroyed the woodblocks and did not transmit it, as a provisional measure. This book is the
Buddhas’ true eye and the patriarchs’ great functioning. The two classics are tongs and hammer, without a single flaw. Now
it is desired to run alongside Dahui’s long letter, carrying out together Yuanwu’s mind-essentials,
raising the bright sun in a deluded path, and setting a compass needle in the sea of wisdom. With one swift look, it opens
that crowd of the foolish, and together brings completion. It is not without benefit. How fortunate.

Moreover, at seventeen he already awakened through Yunmen and Muzhou, which can be called a verbal
samadhi. For two hundred years there was no sight of Blue Cliff Xuedou; suddenly one is struck once by his hand,
how could one forget the bow, the forge, the fur robe, the winnowing basket? Do not cut off the descendants’ seed of weeds. Following others,
you turn after their heels. Who can cast the dragon-fishing hook? Let someone with eyes
come forth, and do not take it as a donkey-tethering stake. This matter should be like the raft parable; later on you will
naturally forget the fish trap. Every household’s gate passes through Chang’an; the one in front calls, the one behind responds. All kinds
of causes and conditions return to the great tally. What was abandoned in the past now revives; do not blame this mountain monk for his
many words, it is only an old woman’s urgent heart. If you do not read the books of this land, how will you know the meaning of the coming from the West?
Reviving a generation’s school style: though there are no geese going south, look at the fish coming from the north, and then
there is full news. Holding the seal of the shared script, reading the inexhaustible lamp, I respectfully submit this.
Written on the day of this month

When the old patriarch Yuanwu lived at Jiashan, he compiled this book, wishing that later generations under heaven
would know the mysterious depths of Buddhas and patriarchs. How could that be a small benefit? The old Miaoxi deeply worried that students
were not rooted in the Way and drowned in learned interpretations; therefore he destroyed it, saying that between father and son
there were contradictions. Could that be right? Now Layman Zhang of Yuzhong has again made woodblocks to print it. What
does that mean, really? Readers should choose for themselves. Mid-autumn in the renyin year of the Dade era, residing at Tiantong,
the seventh-generation dharma-grandson bhikshu Jingri respectfully wrote this with joined hands.

The Chan master Yuanwu commented on and sang Xuedou the master’s one hundred verses on the ancients, splitting open and deciding
the subtle and mysterious, picking out the hidden and profound, revealing the patriarchs’ functioning, and opening the mind-
source for later students. Moreover, wondrous wisdom is empty and congealed, the spiritual mechanism operates silently; crystal dawn shines and the dark gate is
thoroughly illuminated; the full moon rises and the hidden chamber becomes bright. How could shallow knowledge bring one to the ultimate?
Later the Chan master Dahui, because students entered the room and his words were quite different, doubted it and only after
examination did the crooked edge collapse on its own; after a second pressing he confessed. He humbled himself, saying: In my Blue
Cliff Collection notes, I truly had no awakening. Because I feared that later people would not understand the root,
and would prize language alone to seek quick mouths, I therefore burned it to remedy this flaw.
Yet compiling this book and burning this book come from a single intention. How could there be two? Layman Zhang of
Yuzhong, Zhang Mingyuan, happened to obtain a later volume of a manuscript copy, and also obtained the Xuetang printed edition and
a Shu edition, collated and corrected errors, and printed this book to circulate for ten thousand ages, so that those of superior faculties
and great wisdom may glance once and suddenly open their original mind, directly reaching the ground of no doubt.
How could this be called a small benefit? On the day of the Buddha-greeting assembly in the ding-si year of the Yanyou era, the resident of Jingshan,
the bhikshu Ling, bowed and wrote this as a postface.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface by the old man Haisu, Feng Zizhen
cb:div.p:
In the Confucian school, Zigong had great merit for the sage of the eastern house. If a fine horse sees
the shadow of a whip and runs, it is still like staring blankly compared to the later Yanzi. Our sacred teacher roamed
long in the heaven beyond words. At the assembly on Vulture Peak, the fourfold community gathered like a sea; the World-Honored One
held up a flower as the school’s essential point; everyone was at a loss. Only the venerable Kasyapa slightly broke into a
smile. In our teaching too, outside the single cry, mouth and ears are both lost, the same sudden penetration and sudden
awakening. If at that time there had been inquiry, but it did not directly split open and strike the secret key of loyalty and forgiveness, then not only
would the disciples’ confusion have grown worse; a thousand years later, how could one dispel the delusion of the single thread
of coherence? Later, in Chengdu, the old Chan master Foguo Yuanwu dwelled in the abbot’s room at Jiashan.
He raised and presented Xuedou’s one hundred verses on the ancients. His great disciple, Senior Kegui, feared that students
would cling to words and phrases and betray the patriarchs of old. He took the old master’s tongue,
cut off a section and threw it into fierce flames, smoking it and scattering it to an ash heap. He regarded himself as casting
a hair-tip drop into a vast void, like the old worthy Deshan showing off before the old woman selling fried cakes.
This commentary had already turned to cold ash with nothing left. Wildfire cannot burn it all away; spring wind
blows and it grows again. Flowers fall on Blue Cliff; the sunny slope is like embroidery. Passing through kalpas, dead ash
flares again. From who knows where. So many vines and creepers, each one planted by Layman Zhang of Yuzhong
by hand upon a shadowless tree, the whole body is exposed. Until prajna without
speech makes the gods rain flowers. After a hundred and seventy or eighty years, a patch-robed monk suddenly pierces the nostrils
straight through. A precious fragrance never smelled before, in one day water surges and clouds steam, and through eighty-
four thousand pores it is everywhere and universal. It can be called profoundly rare, hard to encounter,
hard to meet. After that, the layman’s two sons fell ill of the heart. Some said: Because you diligently treasured
the scriptures, Senior Kegui burned the blocks; the layman should not have gathered leftover ashes, and so by the light
of sun and moon you received such a recompense. The layman doubted this claim and asked me about it.
I said: In Yuanwu’s gate there are people upon people, and then there is Senior Kegui. Blue Cliff is blue of itself; how
could one speak about Senior Kegui? Seeing the moon, you lose the finger. So he went on to fault even the ancient Buddha, with poisonous
flames blazing across the sky, toppling the monastery pole, not letting through a single thread. Those who have never known the moon,
who will use a finger to show it to them? Others again said: Senior Kegui burned this
book, and the oath to the shrine-ghosts was heavy; the layman’s two sons’ illness is exactly because of this.
I said: When Senior Kegui clearly held the torch, he refined the old paper until it was glowing red.
How could a hidden room have a draft? Old Qinba’s vital gate and tongue root have their own
unbreakable place. One spark bursts and scatters, the bright moon over an empty mountain. Where would Layman Zhang get this
news? He took a natural stretch of Western Shu brocade loom and still wove the old
patterns of former days. Perhaps it was done by the forest’s ruling spirits in the dark, guarding and protecting it to this day. I suppose
it was also the time when this book’s conditions for appearing in the world had come. At the Clear and Cool Pool, needle and
mustard seed meet; then the merit of copying, reading, reciting, and expounding it for others should obtain exceptional
blessings. How much more so when carved in metal and stone and passed along in turn. The root of the layman’s two sons’
heart illness is not here at all. A hired-hand fellow rashly divined it with feelings and consciousness.
The layman, on account of what lay before his eyes, counted up and pulled at blessings and misfortunes, also using feelings and consciousness
to divine it. This is following each other into a fire pit. Is it not unjust? In the Record of Hidden Responses:
In Pei, the Zhou family had three sons, all mute. One day a visitor came to the gate and said: You should
inwardly examine past faults. Suddenly he strongly recalled that in childhood he had seen a swallow’s nest with three chicks, and when their mother
went out, he made each swallow a caltrop. In a short while they all died. The mother returned, cried sadly, and
flew away. He always reproached himself in regret. The visitor said: Since you know regret and blame yourself, your guilt is now forgiven.
The three sons then all could speak. Thus, as for the layman’s two sons, if their illness is wind that makes the mind lost,
could it be that there is also something to repent and regret? Those who speak of prajna, if they are to help people
despised. Such a person's past-life karmic offenses should have made them fall into an evil path, but because in this life people despise
them, their past-life karmic offenses are thereby extinguished. If the layman can gain insight into this,
then even the various deeds created since beginningless kalpas should be extinguished right then. Then the heart illness of your two
sons should, like the Zhou family's three sons, at the proper time be able to speak; you can
have no doubt. The World-Honored One dwelt in the world for forty-nine years; six hundred cases of written words cover and store up
the whole realm. If you follow Senior Seat Gao's talk, ten thousand years are one thought; what traces would you still leave
for what? In the higher Zen groves, countless venerable elders have two lines; the most incisive say:
Let you say mind itself is Buddha; I only say neither mind nor Buddha. From now on, if there are those who slander
the Tathagata's true Dharma wheel, you should only answer them: go ahead and say Senior Seat Gao's
is right; I only say Teacher Qin's is right. If you do not do so, I fear
your face will be scorched, and the four hundred and four illnesses will erupt at once. Then what of the layman's two
sons' heart illness? Have you not seen the ancients say: only when raising children do you know the kindness of parents. The layman,
studying Buddha, knows gratitude, and repents in old age. Another day, as a seasoned bellows and furnace, you will leap out of
the sixteen-foot golden body. I do not know whether you will still see Teacher Qin truly raise his brows and lift his whisk
or not. If you can realize even one sentence, you may say: Buddhas and patriarchs have a vow; offenses will not be punished too harshly.
Do not let it bring calamity upon that family's children and grandchildren. Even so, for now it has nothing to do with it.
On the Zhongyuan day of the Ding-si year of Yanyou, the old man Haisu, Feng Zizhen, inscribed this.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface by Wannian Xuanhui, Abbot of the Jade Peach Hermitage in Huayuan.
cb:div.p:
The Blue Cliff Collection circulating in the world exists in several editions, with many volumes and cases; for advanced students
carrying it in book satchels is not convenient. Therefore I wished to make a small-character edition with shortened lines to save paper and binding; it hasI.
been some years now. In the autumn of the Ding-si year of Ansei, devout donors exerted themselves and gave funds; happily
the capital was put to the blocks, and I at once ordered the engravers. When the work was completed, I gladly gave to have it printed and bound,
and sold the copies at a low price; this was indeed my original intent. As for the detailed sections of Blue Cliff, and the excellent prefaces
and postscripts of the former worthies, all is perfectly fine; what more can be said? Since this abridged engraving is completed, I therefore write theseI.
words at the end of the tablets. In the sixth year of Ansei, the year ji-wei, on the first auspicious day of the seventh month of autumn,
respectfully recorded by Wannian Xuanhui, appointed resident abbot of the Jade Peach Hermitage in Huayuan.

app.rdg: Fanqie: qiu-hou. A bird hatchling that is fed after birth.

app.lem: I say: I do not speak of life, and I do not speak of death.

lem.l: 𡎺Fanqie: ce-liu. It means blocked.磕Fanqie: ke-he. The sound of stone; same pronunciation as he.
